text: What is a computer program?
options: A set of instructions for a computer to execute
A system that manages hardware resources
A physical component inside the computer case
A protocol for transmitting network data
explanation: A computer program is a set of instructions that tells the computer what to do.

text: Which of the following is a low-level programming language?
options: JavaScript
Kotlin
Assembly
Python
explanation: Assembly language is a low-level language that closely corresponds to machine code.

text: What does a compiler do?
options: Allocates and manages runtime memory
Converts entire source code to machine code
Identifies and removes bugs from programs
Executes source code one line at a time
explanation: A compiler translates the entire source code into machine code before execution.

text: Which is NOT a programming paradigm?
options: Procedural Programming
Functional Programming
Structural Networking
Object-Oriented Programming
explanation: Structural Networking is not a recognized programming paradigm.

text: What is the purpose of pseudocode?
options: To plan program logic in plain language
To write actual machine-executable code
To compile a program into bytecode
To debug errors in existing code
explanation: Pseudocode is used to plan program logic in human-readable form before actual coding.

text: What is a syntax error?
options: A failure in the underlying hardware
A violation of the language grammar rules
A mistake in the program logic flow
A crash that occurs during runtime only
explanation: A syntax error occurs when code violates the grammatical rules of the programming language.

text: What is the difference between a compiler and an interpreter?
options: An interpreter generates standalone machine code files for later execution
They are functionally identical with no meaningful differences at all
A compiler translates all code at once; an interpreter translates line by line
A compiler always executes faster than an interpreter in every case
explanation: A compiler processes the entire source code before execution, while an interpreter processes it line by line.

text: What is an algorithm?
options: A step-by-step problem-solving procedure
A high-level programming language
A standalone software application
A structured data storage format
explanation: An algorithm is a finite sequence of well-defined steps to solve a specific problem.

text: Which of the following is a high-level language?
options: Python
Assembly
FORTRAN
Haskell
explanation: Python is a high-level language that abstracts away hardware details.

text: What does IDE stand for?
options: Integrated Development Environment
Internal Data Engine
Indexed Database Engine
Internet Development Extension
explanation: IDE stands for Integrated Development Environment, a software tool for writing and testing code.

text: What is the difference between compilation and interpretation in terms of error detection?
options: Compilation detects syntax errors before execution; interpretation at runtime
Interpretation detects every error before execution even begins
There is no practical difference between the two in error detection
Compilation detects all errors only at runtime during execution
explanation: Compilers check syntax of the entire program before execution, while interpreters find errors as each line is executed.

text: Which generation of programming languages includes languages like C and Java?
options: First generation
Second generation
Fifth generation
Third generation
explanation: C and Java are third-generation languages (3GL) which use human-readable syntax.

text: What is Just-In-Time (JIT) compilation?
options: Compiling code during execution for performance
Handling runtime errors through catch blocks
Compiling all code before shipping to users
Writing source code at the very last minute
explanation: JIT compilation converts bytecode to machine code during runtime for improved performance.

text: What is the role of a linker in program compilation?
options: It combines object files into an executable
It interprets bytecode during execution
It validates syntax in the source code
It manages runtime memory allocation
explanation: A linker combines multiple object files and resolves references to produce a final executable.

text: What is the difference between strongly typed and weakly typed languages?
options: There is no real difference between the two type systems
Strongly typed languages allow implicit type conversions freely
Strongly typed enforces strict rules and restricts conversions
Weakly typed languages enforce very strict type conversion rules
explanation: Strongly typed languages enforce strict type checking and restrict implicit type conversions.

text: What is bytecode?
options: Source code that is written using bytes
Encrypted form of the original source
Intermediate code run on a virtual machine
Binary code identical to machine code
explanation: Bytecode is an intermediate representation that is executed by a virtual machine like the JVM.

text: What is the purpose of a preprocessor in C/C++?
options: To optimize generated machine code output
To process directives like #include and #define
To execute the compiled program directly
To link external libraries to the binary
explanation: The preprocessor handles directives such as #include and #define before the compiler processes the code.

text: Which of the following best describes a runtime error?
options: An error detected during the compilation phase
An error that occurs while the program executes
A syntax mistake caught before program runs
A warning generated by the development IDE
explanation: A runtime error occurs during program execution, such as division by zero or null pointer access.

text: What is type inference?
options: A runtime error caused by wrong type assignment
The compiler automatically determining variable type
A debugging technique to inspect type values
Manually specifying every variable data type
explanation: Type inference allows the compiler to deduce the data type of a variable from its assigned value.

text: What is the call stack used for in program execution?
options: Tracking function calls and local variables
Handling incoming network data requests
Storing all global variables and constants
Managing all file input/output operations
explanation: The call stack keeps track of active function calls and stores local variables and return addresses.

text: What is the halting problem in computer science?
options: Optimizing a program execution speed through advanced compiler techniques
Determining whether an arbitrary program will finish running or loop forever
Finding and reporting memory leaks occurring during a program execution
Determining whether a program contains syntax errors in its source code
explanation: The halting problem, proven undecidable by Turing, asks whether a program will terminate or run forever for a given input.

text: In language theory, what is a context-free grammar (CFG)?
options: A grammar where production rules apply regardless of context
A grammar equivalent to standard regular expressions
A grammar where rules depend on surrounding token context
A grammar that is exclusively used in natural languages
explanation: A CFG has production rules where the left side is a single non-terminal, applied regardless of surrounding symbols.

text: What is tail call optimization (TCO)?
options: Optimizing the first function call in a chain
Parallelizing multiple function calls at once
Reusing the current stack frame for a tail call
Removing unused function calls from the code
explanation: TCO reuses the current function's stack frame when the recursive call is the last operation, preventing stack overflow.

text: What distinguishes a pure function in functional programming?
options: It returns the same output for the same input always
It cannot accept any parameters from the caller
It modifies global state and external variables freely
It must use recursion as its primary control flow
explanation: A pure function produces the same result for identical inputs and causes no observable side effects.

text: What is referential transparency?
options: A function that returns void and produces no output value
An expression replaceable with its value without side effects
A pointer that references itself in a circular data structure
A variable that can be referenced from anywhere in the code
explanation: Referential transparency means an expression can be substituted with its computed value without altering the program's behavior.

text: What is the difference between static and dynamic dispatch?
options: Static dispatch is always slower than dynamic dispatch in practice
Static dispatch resolves methods at compile time; dynamic at runtime
They are identical concepts in object-oriented programming languages
Dynamic dispatch is only available in the C programming language
explanation: Static dispatch determines which function to call at compile time, while dynamic dispatch determines it at runtime using vtables or similar mechanisms.

text: What is a closure in programming?
options: A method used to close open file handles
A function bundled with its lexical scope
A technique to terminate a running loop
A destructor that cleans up class state
explanation: A closure is a function that captures and retains access to variables from its enclosing lexical scope.

text: What is continuation-passing style (CPS)?
options: A structured method for handling runtime errors
Passing control explicitly via continuation functions
A design pattern for building user interfaces
Passing all arguments by reference to functions
explanation: In CPS, instead of returning values, functions pass results to a continuation function that specifies what to do next.

text: What is homoiconicity in a programming language?
options: All variables in the program share the same type
The code can run on any platform without changes
Code structure uses the language own data structures
Functions return the exact same type they receive
explanation: Homoiconicity means the program's source code is expressed using the language's fundamental data structures, as in Lisp.

text: What is the Curry-Howard correspondence?
options: A technique for normalizing database tables
A method for performing function currying
A link between mathematical proofs and programs
A protocol for networking between computers
explanation: The Curry-Howard correspondence establishes a deep connection between logical proofs and typed programs.

text: Which of the following is a primitive data type in most languages?
options: List
Integer
Object
Array
explanation: Integer is a primitive (basic) data type available in virtually all programming languages.

text: What data type is used to store true or false values?
options: string
float
boolean
integer
explanation: The boolean data type stores only two values: true or false.

text: What is a variable?
options: A fixed constant value in the program
A reusable block of executable code
A named storage location in memory
A control structure for iteration
explanation: A variable is a named location in memory that holds a value which can change during program execution.

text: What is the default value of an integer variable in Java?
options: null
explanation: In Java, the default value for an int instance variable is 0.

text: Which data type would you use to store a single letter?
options: String
double
boolean
char
explanation: The char data type is designed to store a single character.

text: What is the difference between float and double?
options: They are exactly the same data type
double has more precision than float
double is used for storing integers
float has more precision than double
explanation: double uses 64 bits and provides more precision than float which uses 32 bits.

text: What does declaring a variable mean?
options: Specifying its name and type
Assigning it an initial value
Printing its current value
Removing it from the memory
explanation: Declaring a variable means telling the compiler its name and data type so memory can be allocated.

text: Which keyword is used to declare a constant in Java?
options: static
fixed
final
const
explanation: In Java, the 'final' keyword is used to declare a constant whose value cannot be changed after initialization.

text: What is a string?
options: A boolean value
A single character
A sequence of characters
A numeric data type
explanation: A string is a sequence of characters used to represent text.

text: What is type casting?
options: Removing a variable from program memory
Declaring a variable with a specific name
Converting a value from one type to another
Defining a brand new composite data type
explanation: Type casting is the process of converting a value from one data type to another, such as int to float.

text: What is the difference between implicit and explicit type casting?
options: Implicit casting always causes errors while explicit casting never does
Implicit is done automatically by the compiler; explicit is done manually
Explicit casting executes much faster than implicit casting at runtime
They are exactly the same type conversion process with no differences
explanation: Implicit casting (widening) is automatic and safe; explicit casting (narrowing) requires manual intervention and may lose data.

text: What is the size of an int in Java?
options: 32 bits
16 bits
8 bits
64 bits
explanation: In Java, an int is always 32 bits (4 bytes) regardless of the platform.

text: What is the difference between value types and reference types?
options: Value types store data directly; reference types store a memory address
They are completely identical in behavior and memory representation
Value types are immutable and cannot be modified after creation
Reference types always execute faster than value types at all times
explanation: Value types hold data directly in memory, while reference types store a reference (address) to the data's location.

text: What happens when you assign a float value to an int variable without casting?
options: It works fine with no issues at all
Compilation error in strongly typed languages
The decimal part is automatically rounded up
The program crashes with a runtime exception
explanation: In strongly typed languages like Java, assigning a float to an int without explicit casting causes a compilation error.

text: What is an enumeration (enum)?
options: A looping construct for iteration
A dynamically resizable array type
A function for string manipulation
A set of named constant values
explanation: An enum defines a fixed set of named constants, improving code readability and type safety.

text: What is the difference between signed and unsigned integers?
options: There is no meaningful difference between signed and unsigned types
Signed holds only positive values; unsigned holds negative values
They have different byte sizes in memory but the same value range
Signed holds negative and positive; unsigned holds non-negative only
explanation: Signed integers can represent both negative and positive values, while unsigned integers represent only non-negative values.

text: What is a nullable type?
options: A type that is always null
A type with no default value
A type that cannot be null
A type that can hold a value or null
explanation: A nullable type can hold either a valid value of its underlying type or null, indicating the absence of a value.

text: What is the result of integer overflow?
options: The value automatically becomes null
An exception is always thrown by runtime
The program always crashes immediately
The value wraps around in most languages
explanation: In most languages, integer overflow causes the value to wrap around due to fixed bit representation.

text: What is a union type in TypeScript?
options: A built-in type for numeric calculations
A type that can be one of several types
A type that combines properties of two types
A class that extends multiple parent classes
explanation: A union type (e.g., string | number) allows a variable to hold a value of any of the specified types.

text: What is type aliasing?
options: Hiding a type from external modules
Renaming a declared variable in scope
Converting between different data types
Creating a new name for existing type
explanation: Type aliasing creates an alternative name for an existing type, improving readability (e.g., typedef in C, type in TypeScript).

text: What is the IEEE 754 standard?
options: A specification for database schemas
A standard for security encryption
A protocol for computer networking
A standard for floating-point numbers
explanation: IEEE 754 defines the representation and behavior of floating-point numbers in computing, including special values like NaN and Infinity.

text: What is a phantom type?
options: A type providing compile-time safety without storing data
A type that has been deprecated from the language standard
A type reserved for representing ghost or phantom objects
A type that only exists at runtime and not at compile time
explanation: Phantom types are type parameters that don't correspond to any runtime data but enforce constraints at compile time.

text: What is the difference between structural typing and nominal typing?
options: They are exactly the same concept with different names
Structural typing is only applicable to struct data types
Nominal typing is only applicable to numeric data types
Structural checks compatibility by shape; nominal by name
explanation: Structural typing (e.g., TypeScript) considers types compatible if their structures match; nominal typing (e.g., Java) requires explicit declarations.

text: What is type erasure in Java generics?
options: Removing type information at the compile stage
Deleting all variables of a particular given type
Generic type info removed at runtime as raw types
Converting every type to Object at compile time
explanation: Type erasure removes generic type information at runtime, replacing it with raw types and inserting casts as needed for backward compatibility.

text: What is a dependent type?
options: A type that inherits from another
A type that depends on a library
A type that is defined by a value
A type that requires garbage collection
explanation: A dependent type is a type whose definition depends on a value, allowing more precise type specifications (e.g., a vector of length n).

text: What is the bottom type in type theory?
options: A type with no values and subtype of every type
The null type representing an absence of a value
The base class that all other types inherit from
The standard integer type used for whole numbers
explanation: The bottom type (e.g., Never in Kotlin, never in TypeScript) has no values and is a subtype of all types, often used for functions that never return.

text: What is variance in type systems?
options: Random and unpredictable behavior in type inference
The size variation of different data types in memory
How subtyping of complex types relates to components
The total range of values a type can hold
explanation: Variance describes how subtyping of container/generic types relates to the subtyping of their type parameters (covariant, contravariant, invariant).

text: What is an algebraic data type (ADT)?
options: An array type that exclusively stores numeric values
A data type designed for mathematical operations only
A type used for representing algebraic math expressions
A composite type combining sum and product operations
explanation: ADTs are composite types formed via sum types (tagged unions/enums) and product types (tuples/records), common in functional languages.

text: What is the unit type?
options: A type with exactly one possible value
A type used for measurement units
The integer constant value of one
A class with a single instance only
explanation: The unit type has exactly one value (e.g., () in Rust/Haskell, Unit in Kotlin) and represents the absence of meaningful data, similar to void.

text: What is the purpose of type witnesses in advanced type systems?
options: To provide explicit proof of type relationships
To help debug complex type errors in code
To convert types dynamically during execution
To observe and log type changes at runtime
explanation: Type witnesses are explicit terms that prove type equalities or relationships, helping the compiler verify complex type-level computations.

text: What does the + operator do with two integers?
options: Concatenates them together
Compares their values
Multiplies them together
Adds them arithmetically
explanation: The + operator performs arithmetic addition when used with integer operands.

text: What is the result of 10 % 3?
explanation: The modulus operator % returns the remainder of division: 10 divided by 3 leaves a remainder of 1.

text: Which operator is used for assignment in most programming languages?
explanation: The single equals sign (=) is the assignment operator in most languages like C, Java, and Python.

text: What does the == operator do?
options: Declares a new variable in scope
Assigns a value to a variable
Performs addition of two values
Checks if two values are equal
explanation: The == operator compares two values and returns true if they are equal.

text: What is the result of 5 > 3?
options: true
false
explanation: The expression 5 > 3 evaluates to true because 5 is greater than 3.

text: Which operator performs integer division in Python 3?
explanation: In Python 3, the // operator performs floor (integer) division, discarding the decimal part.

text: What does the NOT (!) operator do?
options: Reverses a boolean value
Adds two values together
Multiplies two operands
Assigns a new value
explanation: The NOT operator (!) reverses a boolean value: true becomes false and vice versa.

text: What is the result of true AND false?
options: false
error
true
null
explanation: Logical AND returns true only when both operands are true; since one is false, the result is false.

text: What does the ++ operator do?
options: Adds two variables
Doubles the value
Increments the value by 1
Concatenates strings
explanation: The ++ operator (increment) increases the value of a variable by 1.

text: Which operator is used for string concatenation in Java?
explanation: In Java, the + operator is overloaded to concatenate strings.

text: What is the difference between ++i and i++?
options: ++i always executes faster than i++ does overall
++i increments before use; i++ increments after
There is no difference between them at all
i++ increments the value by two instead of one
explanation: ++i (pre-increment) increments the value before it is used in the expression; i++ (post-increment) uses the value first, then increments.

text: What is short-circuit evaluation?
options: Stopping evaluation once result is known
A hardware feature of modern processors
Evaluating all conditions in every case
A compiler optimization for loop bodies
explanation: Short-circuit evaluation stops evaluating a logical expression as soon as the final result can be determined (e.g., false AND x skips x).

text: What is the ternary operator?
options: An operator that takes a single operand only
A conditional operator with three operands
A bitwise operator for binary manipulation
An operator that takes exactly two operands
explanation: The ternary operator (? :) takes three operands: a condition, a value if true, and a value if false.

text: What does the << operator do in C/Java?
options: Reads input from the console
Performs a left bitwise shift
Compares two values for equality
Performs string stream insertion
explanation: The << operator shifts the bits of a number to the left by a specified number of positions, effectively multiplying by powers of 2.

text: What is operator precedence?
options: The order in which operators are defined in code
The rules determining operator evaluation order
The execution speed of a particular operator
The data type returned by a given operator
explanation: Operator precedence defines the order in which different operators are evaluated, e.g., * before +.

text: What is the result of 5 & 3 (bitwise AND)?
explanation: 5 in binary is 101, 3 is 011. Bitwise AND: 101 & 011 = 001, which is 1.

text: What is the difference between == and === in JavaScript?
options: There is no difference between them
== checks value; === checks value and type
=== is used for variable assignment
== checks type without value comparison
explanation: == performs type coercion before comparison, while === (strict equality) checks both value and type without coercion.

text: What does the XOR (^) operator return?
options: True when the operands are different
Always false regardless of the operands
True when both operands are the same
Always true regardless of the operands
explanation: XOR returns true when exactly one of the operands is true (i.e., when they differ).

text: What is the null coalescing operator (??) in JavaScript?
options: It checks the code for syntax errors at compile time
It returns the right operand if the left is null
It always returns a null value to the caller
It performs integer division on two operands
explanation: The ?? operator returns the right-hand operand when the left-hand operand is null or undefined, otherwise returns the left-hand operand.

text: What is operator associativity?
options: How operators are stored internally within the memory
The direction operators of same precedence are evaluated
The underlying data type of an operator in the language
The execution speed of evaluating a given expression
explanation: Associativity determines the evaluation direction for operators of equal precedence, e.g., assignment is right-to-left, addition is left-to-right.

text: What is operator overloading?
options: Defining custom behavior for user-defined types
An error caused by incorrect operator usage
Increasing the default operator precedence level
Using too many operators in one expression
explanation: Operator overloading allows programmers to redefine the behavior of operators for custom types, e.g., defining + for a Complex number class.

text: What is the result of ~5 in a system using 32-bit signed integers?
explanation: The bitwise NOT (~) inverts all bits. For 32-bit signed int, ~5 gives -6 because ~n = -(n+1) in two's complement representation.

text: What is the spaceship operator (<=>)?
options: An assignment operator for setting variables
A comparison operator returning -1, 0, or 1
A bitwise shift operator for binary values
A logical operator for boolean expressions
explanation: The spaceship operator (<=>) performs a three-way comparison, returning negative, zero, or positive based on the ordering of operands.

text: What is the difference between arithmetic right shift (>>) and logical right shift (>>>)?
options: There is no difference between them at all
>>> preserves the sign bit; >> fills with zeros
>> preserves the sign bit; >>> fills with zeros
>> always fills the vacant bit positions with zeros
explanation: Arithmetic right shift (>>) preserves the sign bit for signed numbers, while logical right shift (>>>) always fills vacant bits with zeros.

text: What is the Elvis operator (?:) in Kotlin?
options: Returns left if non-null, otherwise right
A type-checking operator for null safety
A standard ternary conditional operator
A bitwise operator for binary operations
explanation: The Elvis operator (?:) in Kotlin returns the left expression if it is non-null, otherwise evaluates and returns the right expression.

text: How does Python's 'is' operator differ from '=='?
options: is runs faster but produces less accurate results
is checks identity; == checks value equality
They are functionally identical in all cases
== checks identity while is checks equality
explanation: 'is' checks if two references point to the same object in memory (identity), while '==' checks if the values are equal (equality).

text: What is the safe navigation operator (?.) in Kotlin/TypeScript?
options: It forces a null pointer exception to be thrown immediately
It converts any null value to the integer zero automatically
It always returns a null value regardless of the receiver
It accesses a member only if the receiver is non-null
explanation: The safe navigation (optional chaining) operator returns null if the receiver is null instead of throwing a NullPointerException.

text: What does the >>> operator do in Java that >> does not?
options: It fills leftmost bits with 0, ignoring the sign
It operates more slowly than the >> operator
It works on floating-point numbers unlike >>
Nothing different from the >> operator at all
explanation: >>> (unsigned right shift) always fills the highest bits with zeros, while >> (signed right shift) fills with the sign bit, preserving negative values.

text: What is destructuring assignment?
options: A type of memory deallocation for objects
Deleting variables from the current scope
Unpacking values from arrays or objects into variables
Assigning null to multiple variables at once
explanation: Destructuring assignment allows extracting values from arrays or objects into separate variables in a concise syntax.

text: What is the pipeline operator (|>) proposed for JavaScript?
options: A bitwise OR operation on binary values
A comparison operator for ordering two values
It passes left result as argument to right function
A type conversion operator between data types
explanation: The pipeline operator passes the value on the left as the first argument to the function on the right, enabling readable function chaining.

text: What does an if statement do?
options: Declares a new variable in the scope
Defines a reusable function or method
Executes code only if condition is true
Repeats a block of code in a loop
explanation: An if statement evaluates a condition and executes the associated block of code only when the condition is true.

text: What is a loop?
options: A conditional branching statement
A construct that repeats code blocks
A syntax for declaring variables
A mechanism for calling functions
explanation: A loop is a control structure that repeatedly executes a block of code while a condition is met.

text: What is the purpose of the 'else' clause?
options: To terminate the entire program execution
To execute code when if condition is false
To define a reusable function or procedure
To start a new loop iteration immediately
explanation: The else clause provides an alternative block of code that executes when the if condition evaluates to false.

text: Which loop checks the condition before executing the body?
options: both of them
do-while loop
neither of them
while loop
explanation: A while loop evaluates its condition before each iteration; if false initially, the body never executes.

text: What does the 'break' statement do in a loop?
options: Restarts the loop from start
Terminates the loop entirely
Pauses the loop temporarily
Skips the current iteration only
explanation: The break statement immediately terminates the enclosing loop and continues execution after the loop.

text: How many times does a for loop 'for(int i=0; i<5; i++)' execute?
explanation: The loop runs for i = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, which is 5 iterations.

text: What is a switch statement?
options: A syntax for declaring new functions
A type of iterative loop construct
A multi-way branch on expression value
A mechanism for handling exceptions
explanation: A switch statement evaluates an expression and branches to the matching case label for execution.

text: What does the 'continue' statement do?
options: Restarts the loop from the very beginning
Skips rest of iteration and moves to next
Pauses execution until user gives input
Exits and terminates the entire loop
explanation: The continue statement skips the remaining statements in the current loop iteration and proceeds to the next iteration.

text: What is a nested loop?
options: A loop that has been terminated
A loop without any condition set
A loop that runs only one time
A loop placed inside another loop
explanation: A nested loop is a loop placed inside the body of another loop; the inner loop completes all iterations for each iteration of the outer loop.

text: What is the output of: if(false) print('A'); else print('B');?
options: BA
AB
A
B
explanation: Since the condition is false, the else branch executes and prints 'B'.

text: What is the difference between while and do-while loops?
options: do-while is faster in execution speed compared to while loop
while always runs the loop body at least one time guaranteed
while checks condition first; do-while runs body at least once
There is no meaningful difference between them at all
explanation: A do-while loop executes the body first then checks the condition, guaranteeing at least one execution.

text: What is an infinite loop?
options: A syntax error detected at compile time
A loop that has an empty body
A loop whose condition never becomes false
A loop that executes very fast
explanation: An infinite loop is one where the termination condition is never met, causing it to run indefinitely unless externally interrupted.

text: What is the purpose of the 'default' case in a switch statement?
options: It executes when no other case matches
It is required in every switch
It must always be first
It resets the switch value
explanation: The default case executes when the switch expression does not match any of the specified case labels.

text: What is fall-through in a switch statement?
options: Execution continues to next case without break
A compilation error in the switch block
The switch statement fails and throws an error
The default case gets skipped during execution
explanation: Fall-through occurs when a case does not have a break statement, causing execution to continue into subsequent cases.

text: What is a sentinel value in loop control?
options: A special value signaling loop termination
A constant defined in the loop body
A randomly generated input value
A variable used as a loop counter
explanation: A sentinel value is a special value used to indicate the end of a sequence or that a loop should terminate.

text: What is the time complexity of a nested loop where both run n times?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
explanation: When one loop of n iterations contains another loop of n iterations, the total iterations are n * n = O(n^2).

text: What is a for-each loop?
options: A loop that counts its own iterations explicitly
A loop that runs indefinitely until interrupted
A loop that skips every other element in turn
A loop iterating over each element without index
explanation: A for-each (enhanced for) loop iterates over every element in a collection or array without requiring an index variable.

text: What happens if you forget the break in a switch case in Java?
options: A compilation error is raised by the compiler immediately
Nothing happens and the switch works as normally expected
The program crashes with an unrecoverable runtime error
Fall-through: subsequent cases execute until a break is hit
explanation: Without break, execution falls through to subsequent case blocks until a break statement or the end of the switch is encountered.

text: What is a labeled break statement?
options: A break that exits a specific labeled outer loop
A break statement followed by a comment
A syntax error that the compiler will reject
A break statement that logs a debug message
explanation: A labeled break allows breaking out of a specific outer loop by referencing its label, useful for nested loops.

text: What is loop unrolling?
options: Making loops intentionally execute more slowly for debugging
Reducing loop overhead by executing multiple iterations per pass
Adding additional iterations to increase the loop precision
Removing a loop from the code entirely
explanation: Loop unrolling is a compiler optimization that replicates the loop body multiple times to reduce the overhead of loop control.

text: What is tail recursion and how does it relate to loops?
options: Recursion that uses a tail pointer data structure
Recursion without any base case for termination
A loop structure that invokes itself recursively
A tail-position recursive call optimized into a loop
explanation: Tail recursion occurs when the recursive call is the last operation; compilers can optimize it into an iterative loop, avoiding stack growth.

text: What is the difference between structured and unstructured control flow?
options: Structured control flow is slower than unstructured control
Unstructured control flow is more readable than structured
There is no meaningful difference between the two approaches
Structured uses blocks like if/while; unstructured uses goto
explanation: Structured control flow uses well-defined blocks (if, while, for) while unstructured flow uses goto/jump statements, making code harder to reason about.

text: What is pattern matching as a control structure?
options: Matching strings with regular expressions
A generalized switch that destructures data types
A repeating pattern within a loop construct
A text search algorithm for finding substrings
explanation: Pattern matching (as in Rust, Scala, Haskell) goes beyond switch by destructuring data and matching against structural patterns with exhaustiveness checking.

text: What is the difference between eager and lazy evaluation in control flow?
options: Eager evaluates expressions immediately; lazy defers until value needed
Lazy evaluation is always faster than eager evaluation in all cases
Eager evaluation is only used in purely functional language paradigms
There is no practical difference between the two evaluation strategies
explanation: Eager evaluation computes values as soon as they are bound; lazy evaluation defers computation until the value is actually required, enabling infinite data structures.

text: What is a coroutine and how does it differ from a subroutine?
options: A coroutine is a specialized type of loop iteration control structure
A subroutine can be paused and resumed at arbitrary execution points
They are the same thing with different names in different languages
A coroutine can suspend and resume; a subroutine runs to completion
explanation: Coroutines can suspend execution and be resumed later, maintaining their state between suspensions, unlike subroutines which run to completion once called.

text: What is the purpose of guard clauses?
options: To lock and protect shared resources from concurrent thread access
To guard against invalid memory allocation during program startup
To protect systems against unauthorized network access attempts
To handle edge cases early by returning at the start of functions
explanation: Guard clauses handle preconditions and edge cases at the start of a function, returning or throwing early to avoid deep nesting of the main logic.

text: What is a trampoline in the context of recursion?
options: A technique converting recursion into iterative loops
A specialized debugging tool for stack overflow
A physical device used for hardware testing
A tree-based data structure for fast lookup
explanation: A trampoline wraps recursive calls in thunks and uses a loop to iteratively evaluate them, preventing stack overflow in languages without tail call optimization.

text: What is continuation in control flow?
options: A loop continuation condition for iterations
An alternative to the break statement in loops
The next sequential statement in the code
An abstract representation of remaining computation
explanation: A continuation represents the remaining computation from a given point in the program, allowing capture and invocation of the rest of the program.

text: What is the difference between deterministic and non-deterministic control flow?
options: There is no practical difference between them at all
Deterministic follows a fixed path; non-deterministic explores many
Non-deterministic control flow always causes the program to crash
Deterministic control flow always executes more slowly than others
explanation: Deterministic control flow follows one fixed execution path; non-deterministic control flow can explore multiple execution paths.

text: What is the 'goto considered harmful' argument?
options: Goto should be used everywhere as the primary control flow method
Goto causes memory leaks and resource allocation failures in code
Dijkstra argued goto makes programs hard to follow and reason about
Goto is always the most efficient control flow mechanism available
explanation: Dijkstra's 1968 letter argued that unrestricted goto makes programs hard to understand and verify, leading to the structured programming movement.

text: What is a function in programming?
options: A reusable block performing a specific task
A loop that iterates over a collection
A named variable storing data values
A primitive or composite data type name
explanation: A function is a named, reusable block of code designed to perform a particular task, promoting modularity.

text: What is a function parameter?
options: A global variable accessible from anywhere
A variable that receives a passed-in value
The return value that a function produces
The identifier name given to the function
explanation: A parameter is a variable in the function definition that acts as a placeholder for the value passed when the function is called.

text: What does the return statement do?
options: Declares a variable in the function scope
Sends a value back and exits the function
Terminates the entire running program
Starts a new loop inside the function
explanation: The return statement exits the function and optionally sends a value back to the calling code.

text: What is the difference between a parameter and an argument?
options: A parameter is the actual value passed during the function call
A parameter is in the definition; an argument is the passed value
They are exactly the same thing with no differences at all
An argument is defined in the function formal declaration only
explanation: Parameters are variables in the function declaration; arguments are the actual values supplied when calling the function.

text: What is a void function?
options: A function that takes no input parameters
A function with an empty body and no code
A function that does not return any value
A function that is syntactically invalid
explanation: A void function performs actions but does not return a value to the caller.

text: What is a function call?
options: Writing the definition of a function
Invoking a function to execute its code
Removing a function from the program
Adding a comment above a function
explanation: A function call is the act of invoking a function by its name with any required arguments, causing its code to execute.

text: Can a function call another function?
options: Only void functions can call other ones
Only if they are defined in the same class
No, functions cannot call other functions
Yes, functions can call other functions
explanation: Functions can call other functions, enabling code modularization and reuse.

text: What is a built-in function?
options: A function provided by the language or library
A function that you must always write yourself
A function that calls itself recursively
A function defined inside a loop construct
explanation: Built-in functions are pre-defined functions provided by the language, such as print() in Python or Math.sqrt() in Java.

text: What is the main function?
options: Any function found anywhere in the program source
The last function that gets called before exiting
The entry point of execution in languages like C/Java
A mathematical function for numeric calculations
explanation: The main function is the designated entry point where program execution begins in languages like C, C++, and Java.

text: What happens if a function is defined but never called?
options: It causes an error
It runs at the end
It runs automatically
Its code is never executed
explanation: A function that is defined but never called simply exists in the code without being executed.

text: What is function overloading?
options: Calling a function too many times in a row
Same name functions with different parameter lists
A runtime error from excessive function calls
Calling a function from within itself recursively
explanation: Function overloading allows multiple functions with the same name but different parameter types or counts, resolved at compile time.

text: What is the difference between pass by value and pass by reference?
options: Pass by value copies data; pass by reference passes address
Pass by value uses pointers to access the original variable
Pass by reference is always slower than pass by value mode
They are exactly the same thing with different names only
explanation: Pass by value creates a copy of the argument; pass by reference passes the memory address, allowing the function to modify the original variable.

text: What is recursion?
options: A function calling itself
A loop construct
An error in programming
A type of variable
explanation: Recursion is a technique where a function calls itself to solve a problem by breaking it into smaller subproblems.

text: What is a base case in recursion?
options: The condition that stops recursion
The recursive call itself in code
An optional function parameter
The very first function call made
explanation: The base case is the condition that terminates recursion, preventing infinite recursive calls.

text: What is a lambda function?
options: An anonymous short inline function expression
A function that returns no value at all
A function that calls itself recursively
A function with an explicitly declared name
explanation: A lambda function is an anonymous function that can be defined inline, often used for short operations like sorting comparators.

text: What is a callback function?
options: A function that calls itself in a recursive way
A function passed as an argument for later call
A constructor that initializes object properties
A function that contacts the user directly
explanation: A callback is a function passed to another function as an argument, which is then invoked at a specific point or when an event occurs.

text: What is variable scope in the context of functions?
options: The memory size of a variable in bytes
The declared data type of the variable
The current assigned value of a variable
The region where a variable is accessible
explanation: Scope defines where a variable is visible and accessible; local variables exist only within their function, while global variables are accessible everywhere.

text: What are default parameters?
options: Parameters that are always initialized to zero
Parameters whose values cannot ever be changed
Parameters with values used when none is passed
Parameters that the caller must always provide
explanation: Default parameters have pre-assigned values that are used when the caller does not provide an argument for that parameter.

text: What is a higher-order function?
options: A function with high computational time complexity
A function defined in a higher-level parent class
A function that accepts many input parameters
A function that takes or returns other functions
explanation: A higher-order function either accepts functions as parameters or returns a function, enabling functional programming patterns like map, filter, and reduce.

text: What is method signature?
options: Only the access modifier of the declared method
The complete body of the method implementation
Only the return type of the declared method
The method name combined with its parameter list
explanation: A method signature consists of the method name and its parameter list (number, types, and order of parameters), used to uniquely identify the method.

text: What is function currying?
options: Overloading a function with additional parameter types
Caching the results of previous function calls
Optimizing a function for better performance
Transforming multi-argument function into single-argument chain
explanation: Currying transforms f(a, b, c) into f(a)(b)(c), where each function takes one argument and returns a function taking the next.

text: What is memoization?
options: Writing descriptive comments inside function body code
Caching expensive function results to avoid recomputation
Saving a function definition to persistent disk storage
Allocating additional memory for function local variables
explanation: Memoization stores previously computed results in a cache, returning the cached result when the same inputs occur again, improving performance.

text: What is the difference between early binding and late binding?
options: Early binding resolves at compile time; late binding at runtime
There is no meaningful difference between the two approaches
Early binding is always slower than late binding in all cases
Late binding only works in the C programming language alone
explanation: Early (static) binding resolves the function to call at compile time, while late (dynamic) binding defers resolution to runtime, enabling polymorphism.

text: What is a variadic function?
options: A function that has a variable return data type
A function that returns multiple values at once
A function that accepts no input parameters
A function accepting variable number of arguments
explanation: A variadic function accepts a variable number of arguments, like printf in C or functions using *args in Python.

text: What is partial application?
options: Fixing some arguments to produce a new function taking the rest
Calling a function with completely wrong argument types or values
A function that only partially works and fails on some inputs
An incomplete function definition that is missing its body code
explanation: Partial application fixes some arguments of a function, returning a new function that takes the remaining arguments.

text: What is a thunk?
options: A runtime error in the application
A wrapper delaying computation evaluation
A pre-allocated block of heap memory
A particular type of variable binding
explanation: A thunk is a parameterless function that wraps a computation to delay (defer) its evaluation until the result is actually needed.

text: What are first-class functions?
options: Functions assignable to variables and passable as arguments
Functions that have the highest execution priority level
Functions that belong to the main class of the program
Functions that are defined first in source code order
explanation: First-class functions are treated as values: they can be stored in variables, passed as arguments, and returned from other functions.

text: What is the difference between a method and a function?
options: Methods cannot return any values while functions always can
Functions cannot accept any parameters while methods always can
They are always the same thing in every programming language
A method belongs to an object or class; a function is standalone
explanation: A method is a function that belongs to a class or object and can access the object's state, while a standalone function is independent of any object.

text: What is a generator function?
options: A function that creates and returns other functions
A function that yields multiple values over time lazily
A function that generates random numbers on demand
A constructor that initializes a new class instance
explanation: A generator function uses yield to produce a sequence of values lazily, pausing after each yield and resuming when the next value is requested.

text: What is the Y combinator?
options: A fixed-point combinator enabling anonymous recursion
A popular comparison-based sorting algorithm
A well-known startup incubator company
A commonly used structural design pattern
explanation: The Y combinator is a higher-order function that enables anonymous functions to call themselves recursively in lambda calculus where functions have no names.

text: What does I/O stand for in programming?
options: Integer or Object
Index with Order
Internal Operations
Input and Output
explanation: I/O stands for Input/Output, referring to communication between a program and the outside world.

text: Which function is used to display output in Python?
options: echo()
write()
print()
printf()
explanation: In Python, the print() function is used to display output to the console.

text: What is standard input (stdin)?
options: A persistent network data connection
The default input source like keyboard
A database for storing program data
A file stored on the hard disk drive
explanation: Standard input (stdin) is the default input stream, usually connected to the keyboard for reading user input.

text: What is standard output (stdout)?
options: The default output destination like console
A database for persisting program output
A connected printer device only
A file stored on persistent disk storage
explanation: Standard output (stdout) is the default output stream, usually displaying output on the console screen.

text: Which function reads a line of input from the user in Python?
options: input()
scanf()
getLine()
readLine()
explanation: In Python, the input() function reads a line of text from standard input.

text: What is a newline character?
options: A whitespace character for horizontal spacing
A tab character for indenting text in documents
A special character that moves cursor to next line
A backspace character that deletes previous input
explanation: The newline character (\n) moves the cursor to the beginning of the next line in output.

text: What does printf() do in C?
options: Closes an open file descriptor handle
Formats and prints output to the console
Reads input from standard input stream
Allocates dynamic memory on the heap
explanation: printf() in C formats a string with specified format specifiers and prints it to standard output.

text: What is a format specifier?
options: A variable name in source code
A placeholder defining value display format
A counter used inside loop bodies
A file type extension on disk
explanation: Format specifiers like %d, %s, %f indicate the type and format of data to be printed in functions like printf().

text: What is console output?
options: Storing structured data in a database table
Sending formatted data to a connected printer
Displaying information on screen or terminal
Writing data to a file on disk storage
explanation: Console output refers to displaying text and data on the screen or terminal window.

text: What does Scanner class do in Java?
options: Compiles Java source code into bytecode
Reads input from various sources like keyboard
Scans the system for viruses and malware
Writes formatted output to the console
explanation: The Scanner class in Java is used to read input from sources like keyboard (System.in), files, and strings.

text: What is buffered I/O?
options: I/O that applies encryption to all data before transferring
I/O that exclusively works with pre-allocated buffer arrays
I/O that collects data in a buffer to reduce system calls
I/O operations performed without any processing delays
explanation: Buffered I/O accumulates data in a memory buffer and performs actual I/O operations in larger chunks, reducing the overhead of frequent system calls.

text: What is the difference between print() and println() in Java?
options: There is no difference between them at all
print() executes faster than println() does
println() only prints numeric values to output
println() adds a newline; print() does not
explanation: System.out.println() appends a newline after the output, while System.out.print() does not, keeping the cursor on the same line.

text: What is file I/O?
options: Reading from and writing to disk files
Reading data from the keyboard input
Allocating memory for program variables
Communicating over the internet network
explanation: File I/O involves reading data from files and writing data to files stored on persistent storage.

text: What is serialization?
options: Encrypting data for secure network transmission
Sorting data in ascending or descending order
Converting an object into a byte stream for storage
Compressing files to reduce their storage size
explanation: Serialization converts an object's state into a byte stream that can be stored in a file or sent over a network, and later deserialized back.

text: What does the flush() method do in I/O?
options: Deletes the entire output buffer contents
Forces buffered output to be written immediately
Clears the entire console screen display
Reads all contents from the input buffer
explanation: flush() forces any data held in the output buffer to be written to the underlying stream immediately, without waiting for the buffer to fill.

text: What is the difference between text mode and binary mode in file I/O?
options: Text mode processes files faster than binary mode does
There is no functional difference between the two modes
Text mode translates newlines; binary reads raw bytes
Binary mode is exclusively used for image file formats
explanation: Text mode performs newline translations, while binary mode reads and writes bytes exactly as they are.

text: What is standard error (stderr)?
options: A specialized tool used for debugging programs
A separate output stream for error messages only
An error that occurs in the standard input stream
It is the same stream as standard output stdout
explanation: stderr is a separate output stream specifically for error messages, allowing errors to be redirected independently from normal output.

text: What is the purpose of the 'with' statement for file I/O in Python?
options: It loops through all files in a given directory
It reads binary files into memory as byte arrays
It writes data to multiple files simultaneously
It ensures the file is properly closed after use
explanation: The 'with' statement creates a context manager that automatically closes the file when the block exits, even if an error occurs.

text: What is CSV and how is it used in I/O?
options: Comma-Separated Values, a text format for tabular data
A relational database management system for queries
A compiled programming language for systems work
A binary file format for storing structured records
explanation: CSV (Comma-Separated Values) is a simple text format where each line is a record and fields are separated by commas, commonly used for data exchange.

text: What is piping in command-line I/O?
options: A specialized type of network I/O communication
Writing output data to a physical pipe file
Redirecting one program output as another input
Processing multiple data streams in parallel mode
explanation: Piping connects the stdout of one command to the stdin of another, enabling data to flow between programs.

text: What is memory-mapped I/O?
options: Mapping files into virtual memory address space
Encrypting all I/O data during transfer
Storing I/O data in the processor cache
I/O that uses only RAM for data storage
explanation: Memory-mapped I/O maps files or devices into a process's virtual address space, allowing file access through memory read/write operations instead of explicit I/O calls.

text: What is the difference between blocking and non-blocking I/O?
options: Non-blocking I/O is always slower than blocking I/O overall
There is no meaningful difference between the two modes
Blocking I/O never waits for the operation to finish first
Blocking waits until completion; non-blocking returns immediately
explanation: Blocking I/O suspends the thread until the operation finishes, while non-blocking I/O returns immediately, allowing the program to continue and check results later.

text: What is asynchronous I/O?
options: I/O that continues without waiting, notified on completion
I/O that is identical to synchronous blocking I/O
I/O operations that execute in strict sequential order
I/O operations that are always extremely fast
explanation: Asynchronous I/O initiates an operation and continues executing; the program is notified when the operation completes.

text: What is the purpose of the select() system call?
options: Selecting a record from a database table query
Selecting files to delete from the file system
Monitoring file descriptors for I/O readiness
Choosing which thread the scheduler should run
explanation: select() allows a program to monitor multiple file descriptors simultaneously, waiting until one or more become ready for reading, writing, or have errors.

text: What is zero-copy I/O?
options: I/O operations that have zero financial cost
I/O operations that transfer no data at all
Deleting data as it is being transferred out
Transferring data without copying between buffers
explanation: Zero-copy I/O transfers data directly between kernel buffer and the destination without intermediate copies to user space, improving performance.

text: What is the reactor pattern in I/O?
options: A nuclear-physics computing simulation pattern
An event-driven dispatcher that demultiplexes I/O events
A chemical reaction simulation programming model
A simple loop-based sequential processing pattern
explanation: The reactor pattern uses an event loop to demultiplex I/O events from multiple sources and dispatch them to appropriate event handlers, as used in Node.js.

text: What are I/O multiplexing mechanisms like epoll and kqueue?
options: Command-line utilities designed for copying files efficiently
Multi-threaded I/O libraries for parallel data processing
Mechanisms that duplicate I/O devices for redundancy
Scalable kernel interfaces for monitoring many file descriptors
explanation: epoll (Linux) and kqueue (BSD) are efficient kernel mechanisms for monitoring large numbers of file descriptors, overcoming the scalability limits of select().

text: What is the difference between character devices and block devices?
options: Character devices transfer byte by byte; block in fixed chunks
Block devices are exclusively used for text file operations
There is no functional difference between device types
Character devices are always faster than block-based devices
explanation: Character devices handle data one byte at a time, while block devices handle data in fixed-size blocks with buffering.

text: What is Direct Memory Access (DMA) in the context of I/O?
options: Hardware allowing device-to-memory transfer without CPU
A software programming technique for data transfer
A debugging method for diagnosing hardware I/O issues
A specialized type of dynamic memory allocation method
explanation: DMA enables hardware devices to transfer data directly to/from main memory without CPU involvement, freeing the CPU for other tasks during I/O operations.

text: What is scatter-gather I/O (vectored I/O)?
options: Reading/writing multiple non-contiguous buffers at once
Distributing I/O operations across network nodes
Random I/O patterns across scattered disk sectors
Fragmented file storage across multiple disk drives
explanation: Scatter-gather I/O uses readv/writev to perform I/O with multiple buffers in one system call, avoiding the need to concatenate buffers or make multiple calls.

text: How are strings typically stored in memory?
options: As boolean value sequences
As integer values in memory
As an array of characters
As floating-point numbers
explanation: Strings are stored as sequences (arrays) of characters in memory.

text: What does the length/len function return for a string?
options: The index of the last character
The memory size in bytes
The number of characters in the string
The ASCII value of the first character
explanation: The length function returns the total number of characters in a string.

text: What is string concatenation?
options: Splitting a string into parts
Reversing a string order
Joining two or more strings
Deleting a string entirely
explanation: Concatenation combines two or more strings into a single string.

text: What is the index of the first character in a string in most languages?
explanation: In most programming languages (C, Java, Python), string indexing starts at 0.

text: What does the toUpperCase() method do?
options: Converts all characters to lowercase
Converts all characters to uppercase
Reverses the character order fully
Removes all whitespace characters
explanation: toUpperCase() returns a new string with all alphabetic characters converted to uppercase.

text: What is a substring?
options: The name of a string variable only
A string that contains no characters
A contiguous portion of the string
A complete copy of the entire string
explanation: A substring is a contiguous sequence of characters within a string.

text: What is an empty string?
options: A string with spaces only inside it
An uninitialized variable with no type
A null value with no reference set
A string with zero characters in it
explanation: An empty string is a string containing no characters, having a length of zero.

text: What does the trim() method do?
options: Removes leading and trailing whitespace
Converts all characters to lowercase form
Removes every character from the string
Splits the string at every space character
explanation: trim() removes whitespace characters from both the beginning and end of a string.

text: How do you compare strings in Java?
options: Using the == operator directly
Using the + concatenation operator
Using the > comparison operator
Using the equals() method call
explanation: In Java, equals() compares string content, while == compares object references.

text: What is a character in programming?
options: A string of exactly two characters
A numeric whole number data type
A single symbol like a letter or digit
A boolean true or false data value
explanation: A character is a single symbol, typically represented by the char data type.

text: What is the difference between mutable and immutable strings?
options: Mutable strings always execute faster than immutable string types
Immutable strings can dynamically grow in size without any limit
There is no meaningful difference between the two string types
Mutable strings can be modified in place; immutable ones cannot
explanation: Mutable strings (e.g., StringBuilder) can be modified in place, while immutable strings (e.g., Java String) create a new object for any change.

text: What is a regular expression?
options: A standard normal string literal value
An algorithm for sorting string collections
A pattern for matching and manipulating text
A mathematical expression using operators
explanation: A regular expression (regex) is a sequence of characters defining a search pattern for matching, finding, or replacing text.

text: What is string interpolation?
options: Converting strings to their numeric equivalents
Splitting strings by a delimiter character
Comparing two strings for equality or ordering
Embedding variables directly within a string
explanation: String interpolation allows embedding variable values or expressions directly in a string, e.g., f-strings in Python or template literals in JavaScript.

text: What is the String Pool in Java?
options: A thread pool dedicated to string processing
A mutable buffer for building string values
A collection of string utility methods
A heap area that caches string literals for reuse
explanation: The String Pool is a special area in the Java heap that caches string literals, so identical string literals share the same reference.

text: What is the difference between String, StringBuilder, and StringBuffer in Java?
options: StringBuilder is immutable while String and StringBuffer are both mutable
They are all identical in function and performance with no differences
String is immutable; StringBuilder is mutable; StringBuffer is thread-safe
StringBuffer is always the fastest option among all three implementations
explanation: String is immutable. StringBuilder is mutable and fast but not thread-safe. StringBuffer is mutable and thread-safe but slower due to synchronization.

text: What does the split() method do?
options: Divides a string into an array of parts
Joins multiple strings into one
Converts string to a character array
Removes specified characters from string
explanation: split() divides a string into an array of substrings using a specified delimiter or regular expression.

text: What is Unicode?
options: A type of data encryption algorithm
An extension of the ASCII character set
A universal character encoding standard
A specific font type used in displays
explanation: Unicode is a standard that assigns unique code points to characters from virtually all writing systems worldwide.

text: What is the difference between ASCII and Unicode?
options: ASCII supports many more characters than Unicode ever can
Unicode is the older standard that was developed before ASCII
They are exactly the same encoding standard with no differences
ASCII uses 7 bits for 128 chars; Unicode supports over 143,000
explanation: ASCII encodes 128 characters using 7 bits, while Unicode supports characters from all writing systems using variable-length encoding.

text: What is string encoding (like UTF-8)?
options: Compressing a string to reduce storage size
Encrypting a string for secure transmission
Converting a string value into an integer
Representing Unicode characters as byte sequences
explanation: UTF-8 is a variable-length encoding that represents each Unicode code point as 1 to 4 bytes, backward-compatible with ASCII.

text: What is a string builder pattern and when should you use it?
options: A design pattern for constructing user interface strings
A utility tool for building complex regular expressions
A parser that transforms strings into structured data types
Using a mutable class for efficient repeated concatenation
explanation: When performing many string concatenations in a loop, StringBuilder is more efficient because it avoids creating many intermediate immutable string objects.

text: What is the time complexity of naive string matching?
options: O(n*m)
O(log n)
O(n)
explanation: Naive string matching compares the pattern at each position in the text, resulting in O(n*m) worst-case time complexity.

text: What is the KMP (Knuth-Morris-Pratt) string matching algorithm?
options: A comparison-based sorting algorithm for arrays
A tree traversal algorithm for binary search trees
A linear-time string matcher using a failure function
A hashing algorithm for generating unique hash codes
explanation: KMP achieves O(n+m) string matching by precomputing a partial match table to skip unnecessary character comparisons.

text: What is string interning?
options: Compressing strings to reduce their memory size
Storing one copy of each distinct string value
Encrypting strings for secure data transmission
Storing strings in a temporary memory buffer
explanation: String interning stores a single copy of each unique string value, allowing equality checks via reference comparison instead of character comparison.

text: What is the Rabin-Karp algorithm?
options: A graph traversal algorithm for shortest paths
A string matcher using hashing for efficient search
A comparison-based sorting algorithm for collections
A compression algorithm for reducing data file sizes
explanation: Rabin-Karp uses rolling hash functions to efficiently compare the pattern with substrings of the text, achieving O(n+m) expected time.

text: What is a trie (prefix tree) and how does it relate to strings?
options: A linked list that stores strings in sequential memory order
A standard binary search tree data structure
A hash table that maps string keys to their computed values
A tree where each node represents a character for prefix search
explanation: A trie stores strings character by character in a tree structure, enabling efficient prefix searches, autocomplete, and dictionary lookups.

text: What is the difference between UTF-8, UTF-16, and UTF-32?
options: UTF-32 uses the least amount of storage space per character overall
They are all identical encoding formats with no differences at all
UTF-8 uses 1-4 bytes, UTF-16 uses 2-4 bytes, UTF-32 uses exactly 4
UTF-8 is a fixed-width encoding that always uses exactly one byte
explanation: UTF-8 uses 1-4 bytes (variable), UTF-16 uses 2 or 4 bytes, and UTF-32 uses exactly 4 bytes per character.

text: What is a rope data structure for strings?
options: A contiguous array of individual character values
A hash map that stores character frequency counts
A singly linked list of character nodes
A balanced tree for efficient long string operations
explanation: A rope is a binary tree where leaves contain short strings, enabling efficient concatenation, insertion, and deletion on very long strings.

text: What is the Levenshtein distance?
options: The total length of a string in characters
The count of matching characters between strings
The byte size of a string in memory allocation
The minimum single-character edits between strings
explanation: Levenshtein distance measures the minimum number of edit operations needed to change one string into another, used in spell checking and fuzzy matching.

text: What is the Boyer-Moore string search algorithm's key insight?
options: Start comparing from the beginning of the pattern each time
Use hashing to compare the pattern against every position
Compare every single character without skipping any at all
Start from pattern end and use heuristics to skip sections
explanation: Boyer-Moore compares the pattern from right to left and uses precomputed heuristics to skip large portions of the text.

text: What are surrogate pairs in UTF-16?
options: Duplicate characters stored in the encoding table
Error codes returned by the UTF-16 encoding process
Pairs of 16-bit units for characters beyond the BMP
Paired delimiter characters used in string literals
explanation: Characters with code points above U+FFFF require two 16-bit code units (a high surrogate and a low surrogate) in UTF-16 encoding.

text: What is an array?
options: A loop construct that iterates over data elements
A collection of same-type elements in contiguous memory
A reusable function that performs a specific task
A single variable holding one data value
explanation: An array stores a fixed-size collection of elements of the same type in contiguous memory locations.

text: What is the index of the first element in an array in C/Java?
explanation: In C and Java, array indexing starts at 0, so the first element is at index 0.

text: What is an ArrayList in Java?
options: A doubly linked list of elements
A key-value pair mapping structure
A fixed-size array that cannot change
A dynamic array that grows and shrinks
explanation: ArrayList is a resizable array implementation of the List interface that automatically manages its size.

text: What is a list in Python?
options: A dictionary mapping keys to their associated values
An immutable sequence that cannot be modified at all
A fixed-size array of uniform typed elements
An ordered mutable collection of any element types
explanation: A Python list is an ordered, mutable collection that can store elements of any type and dynamically resize.

text: What happens when you access an array index out of bounds?
options: It returns zero as the default value for any position
It returns null as the default value for the position
It throws an exception or causes undefined behavior
It automatically resizes the array to fit the index
explanation: Accessing an index outside the array's valid range causes an exception in managed languages or undefined behavior in C/C++.

text: What is a dictionary/map?
options: An ordered array with indexed elements
A list of words in alphabetical order
A string type for storing text values
A collection of key-value pair entries
explanation: A dictionary/map stores data as key-value pairs, allowing efficient lookup by key.

text: What is a stack?
options: A LIFO (Last-In First-Out) structure
A sorted list of ordered elements
A random access data structure
A FIFO (First-In First-Out) structure
explanation: A stack is a LIFO data structure where the last element added is the first one removed.

text: What is a queue?
options: A LIFO (Last-In First-Out) structure
A sorted array with indexed elements
A FIFO (First-In First-Out) structure
A hierarchical tree data structure
explanation: A queue is a FIFO data structure where the first element added is the first one removed.

text: What is the difference between an array and a linked list?
options: Arrays can dynamically grow and shrink in size without any overhead
Arrays use contiguous memory with O(1) access; linked lists use nodes
They are functionally identical data structures with no differences
Linked lists are always faster than arrays for every type of operation
explanation: Arrays store elements contiguously enabling O(1) random access; linked lists use nodes connected by pointers enabling O(1) insertion/deletion but O(n) access.

text: What is a set in programming?
options: A sorted array of ordered elements
A specialized type of key-value map
A collection with only unique elements
An array that allows duplicate elements
explanation: A set is a collection that contains no duplicate elements, useful for membership testing and removing duplicates.

text: What is the time complexity of accessing an element by index in an array?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
explanation: Array access by index is O(1) because the memory address is calculated directly from the base address and index.

text: What is a hash table?
options: A balanced binary tree for hierarchical storage
A sorted array of ordered elements in memory
A structure mapping keys to values via hashing
A stack variant that allows random element access
explanation: A hash table uses a hash function to compute an index into an array of buckets, enabling average O(1) lookup, insertion, and deletion.

text: What is a collision in a hash table?
options: A runtime error in the hash function code
When a requested key is not found in map
When the hash table is completely full up
When two keys hash to the same index slot
explanation: A collision occurs when two different keys produce the same hash value, mapping to the same bucket.

text: What is the difference between a singly linked list and a doubly linked list?
options: Singly linked lists always use more memory than doubly linked
There is no meaningful difference between the two list types
Singly linked has next pointer; doubly linked has next and prev
Doubly linked lists are always slower than singly linked lists
explanation: A singly linked list node has a pointer to the next node; a doubly linked list node has pointers to both next and previous nodes.

text: What is a priority queue?
options: A sorted array with indexed element access only
A queue where higher-priority elements dequeue first
A stack with last-in first-out element ordering
A regular first-in first-out queue structure
explanation: A priority queue serves elements based on their priority rather than insertion order, typically implemented using a heap.

text: What is the difference between HashMap and TreeMap in Java?
options: They are exactly the same data structure implementation
HashMap maintains the insertion order of all its entries
TreeMap is always faster than HashMap for all operations
HashMap is unordered O(1); TreeMap is sorted O(log n)
explanation: HashMap uses hashing for O(1) average operations but no ordering; TreeMap uses a red-black tree for O(log n) operations with keys in sorted order.

text: What is a tuple?
options: A mutable list that can change its elements
A key-value map for looking up data by key
An immutable ordered collection of mixed types
A sorted set that maintains element ordering
explanation: A tuple is an immutable, ordered sequence of elements that can be of different types, used to group related values.

text: What is an iterator?
options: An array index used for element access
An object for traversing a collection sequentially
A sorting algorithm for ordering collections
