A type of loop construct for repeating code
explanation: An iterator provides a standard interface for traversing elements of a collection sequentially without exposing its internal structure.

text: What is the difference between a shallow copy and a deep copy of a collection?
options: They are the same operation with identical results in every case
Shallow copy shares element references; deep copy duplicates all
Shallow copy is always slower than deep copy for all collections
Deep copy shares references while shallow copy duplicates objects
explanation: A shallow copy creates a new collection with references to the same objects; a deep copy recursively duplicates all nested objects.

text: What is a deque (double-ended queue)?
options: A regular first-in first-out queue
A queue allowing insertion at both ends
A sorted list with binary search access
A stack variant for ordered elements
explanation: A deque supports insertion and removal of elements from both the front and the rear.

text: What is a bloom filter?
options: A comparison-based sorting algorithm for arrays
A space-efficient probabilistic set membership tester
An image processing filter for visual enhancement
A standard hash table with separate chaining buckets
explanation: A bloom filter uses multiple hash functions and a bit array to test membership with possible false positives but no false negatives.

text: What is a skip list?
options: A filtered array that excludes elements matching a condition
A probabilistic multi-layer linked list for O(log n) search
A list that skips over certain elements during iteration
A type of priority queue that maintains sorted element order
explanation: A skip list uses multiple levels of forward pointers to skip over elements, providing O(log n) average search, insertion, and deletion.

text: What is a concurrent hash map?
options: A hash map using multiple hash functions per key
A thread-safe hash map without full map locking
A distributed database across network nodes only
A regular hash map with no special properties
explanation: ConcurrentHashMap allows concurrent reads and partitioned writes without locking the entire structure.

text: What is the amortized time complexity of adding an element to a dynamic array?
options: O(1) amortized
Always O(n)
O(log n) each
O(n log n) each
explanation: While occasional resizing costs O(n), the cost is spread over many insertions, making the amortized cost O(1) per insertion.

text: What is a persistent data structure?
options: An immutable structure that can never be accessed again
A structure preserving previous versions on modification
A relational database schema structure for queries
A structure saved to persistent disk storage
explanation: A persistent data structure retains all previous versions of itself when modified, enabling access to any historical state.

text: What is a B-tree and where is it commonly used?
options: A self-balancing tree with multiple keys per node for databases
A hash table variant that uses tree-based collision resolution
A standard binary tree with two children per node
A linked list type that organizes data in sequential node chains
explanation: A B-tree is a self-balancing tree where nodes can have multiple keys and children, optimized for disk access and widely used in databases.

text: What is consistent hashing?
options: A type of encryption used for securing sensitive data values
A collision resolution method for hash table bucket overflow
A hashing scheme that minimally redistributes keys on change
Regular hashing with standard hash function methods
explanation: Consistent hashing maps both keys and nodes onto a ring, so adding or removing a node only redistributes keys to/from adjacent nodes.

text: What is a lock-free data structure?
options: An unprotected array that any thread can freely modify
A concurrent structure using atomic operations not locks
A structure with no synchronization protection at all
A read-only data structure that cannot be changed ever
explanation: Lock-free data structures use atomic operations (compare-and-swap) instead of locks, ensuring that at least one thread always makes progress.

text: What is the difference between external and internal iterators?
options: Internal iterators always consume more memory than external iterators
External iterators are always faster than internal iterators in practice
There is no meaningful difference between the two iterator types
External iterators are client-controlled; internal are collection-controlled
explanation: External iterators let the client control iteration; internal iterators let the collection control iteration by applying a function to each element.

text: What is a disjoint set (union-find) data structure?
options: A structure tracking element partitions with union and find
A sorted set that maintains elements in order
A hash set variant that uses separate chaining for entries
A set that has no intersections with any other set at all
explanation: Union-Find tracks elements partitioned into disjoint sets, supporting near O(1) union and find operations with path compression and union by rank.

text: What is a class in OOP?
options: A variable type for storing data
A blueprint for creating objects
A loop construct for iteration
A reusable function for operations
explanation: A class defines the structure (attributes) and behavior (methods) that its objects will have.

text: What is an object in OOP?
options: A standalone function
A class definition in code
A primitive data type
An instance of a class
explanation: An object is a concrete instance of a class, with its own state and behavior.

text: What is encapsulation?
options: Inheriting properties from a parent class definition
A sorting method that orders elements in a collection
A type of iterative loop control structure in code
Bundling data and methods while restricting direct access
explanation: Encapsulation bundles data and methods together while hiding internal details, controlling access through public interfaces.

text: What is inheritance in OOP?
options: Acquiring properties and methods from a parent class
Creating a new object instance from a class
A form of encapsulation that hides internal details
A design pattern for structuring application code
explanation: Inheritance allows a child class to reuse fields and methods of a parent class, promoting code reuse.

text: What is a constructor?
options: A static method belonging to the class itself
A getter method that returns a field value
A destructor that cleans up object state
A special method called when creating an object
explanation: A constructor is automatically invoked when a new object is instantiated, used to set up initial values.

text: What is a method in OOP?
options: A standalone function not tied to any class
A function defined inside a class for objects
A variable that stores data in the program
A data type that defines value constraints
explanation: A method is a function associated with a class that defines behavior and can access the object's attributes.

text: What does the 'this' keyword refer to?
options: The current object instance
A static method reference
The class definition itself
The parent class reference
explanation: 'this' refers to the current object instance, used to access its own fields and methods within the class.

text: What is an access modifier?
options: A keyword controlling member visibility level
A data type for storing numeric values only
A type of loop construct in the language
A constructor for initializing object state
explanation: Access modifiers like public, private, and protected control which parts of the program can access a class's members.

text: What is the 'new' keyword used for?
options: Deleting an existing object from memory
Declaring a new variable in the scope
Defining a new method in the class
Creating a new instance of a class
explanation: The 'new' keyword allocates memory and creates a new instance of a class by invoking its constructor.

text: What is polymorphism?
options: A class relationship defined by inheritance hierarchy
A type of encapsulation hiding internal data details
Different objects responding differently to same method
Having only one single form of behavior always
explanation: Polymorphism allows objects of different classes to be treated uniformly through a common interface while each provides its own implementation.

text: What is the difference between method overloading and method overriding?
options: They are the same concept with different names in different languages
Overloading requires an inheritance hierarchy between the classes involved
Overriding changes the method parameter list in the parent class definition
Overloading: same name different params; overriding: same signature in subclass
explanation: Overloading defines multiple methods with the same name but different parameters; overriding redefines a parent's method with the same signature in a subclass.

text: What is an abstract class?
options: A fully implemented class with all methods defined
A static class that only contains class-level members
A final class that cannot be extended by other classes
A class that cannot be instantiated with abstract methods
explanation: An abstract class cannot be instantiated directly and may contain abstract methods that concrete subclasses must override.

text: What is an interface?
options: A class with full method implementations provided
A contract defining method signatures without bodies
An abstract class with some methods implemented
A type of variable for storing object references
explanation: An interface defines a set of method signatures without implementation; classes implementing the interface must provide the method bodies.

text: What is the difference between composition and inheritance?
options: Composition is always slower than inheritance in performance
Inheritance is an is-a relationship; composition is has-a
They are exactly the same concept with no difference at all
Inheritance is more flexible and loosely coupled overall
explanation: Inheritance creates an 'is-a' relationship; composition creates a 'has-a' relationship, generally offering more flexibility.

text: What is a static method?
options: A method belonging to the class not any specific instance
A constructor method for initializing new object state
A method that can never be overridden by subclasses
A private method accessible only within the same class
explanation: A static method belongs to the class itself, not to instances, and can be invoked without creating an object.

text: What are getter and setter methods?
options: Static methods belonging to the class directly
Methods for controlled field read and write access
Loop constructs for iterating over collections
Constructor types for initializing objects
explanation: Getters return the value of private fields; setters allow controlled modification, enforcing encapsulation.

text: What is the super keyword?
options: A global variable accessible everywhere in code
A reference to parent class for calling its members
The main class that serves as program entry point
A type of access modifier restricting member access
explanation: 'super' refers to the parent class and is used to call the parent's constructor, methods, or access parent fields.

text: What is multiple inheritance and which languages support it?
options: Creating multiple instances of the same class simultaneously
Inheriting from a single parent class in a linear hierarchy
Inheriting from multiple parents; supported by C++ and Python
All programming languages support this feature universally
explanation: Multiple inheritance allows a class to inherit from more than one parent. C++ and Python support it; Java uses interfaces instead.

text: What is the diamond problem?
options: An error related to geometric shapes in rendering code
A memory leak issue caused by circular object references only
An ambiguity when inheriting from classes with common ancestor
A compilation warning about unused variables in the program
explanation: The diamond problem occurs when class D inherits from B and C, both inheriting from A, creating ambiguity about which version of A's members D should use.

text: What is the Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP)?
options: A design pattern for constructing complex object hierarchies
A principle for sorting elements in a collection efficiently
A rule for managing memory allocation in large applications
Subclass objects should be substitutable for superclass objects
explanation: LSP states that subclass objects must be substitutable for their parent class objects without altering the desirable properties of the program.

text: What is the SOLID principle in OOP?
options: A coding style guide for naming conventions in code
A data structure for organizing elements efficiently
Five design principles: SRP, OCP, LSP, ISP, and DIP
A testing framework for validating software behavior
explanation: SOLID comprises five principles for creating maintainable OOP software: SRP, OCP, LSP, ISP, and DIP.

text: What is a virtual method table (vtable)?
options: A hash table for caching method results quickly
A debug table for tracking method execution and logging
A lookup table for resolving dynamic method calls at runtime
A method list defined in the source code of the program
explanation: A vtable is a compiler-generated table of function pointers used at runtime to resolve virtual method calls.

text: What is the difference between association, aggregation, and composition?
options: Association is general; aggregation weak; composition strong ownership
They are all identical concepts with just different names used
Composition is always the better design choice in every single case
Only naming differences exist with no behavioral distinctions at all
explanation: Association is a loose relationship; aggregation implies ownership but parts can exist independently; composition implies the part's lifecycle depends on the whole.

text: What is a mixin?
options: A class that mixes different data types together randomly
A class providing methods to others without being standalone
A type of interface that defines a strict method contract
A structural design pattern for building complex objects
explanation: A mixin provides reusable methods to other classes via multiple inheritance or similar mechanisms, without being intended for standalone instantiation.

text: What is double dispatch and how does it differ from single dispatch?
options: Single dispatch uses two methods while double dispatch uses only one
There is no practical difference between the two dispatch mechanisms
Single dispatch selects by one type; double dispatch by two types
Double dispatch is twice as fast in execution as single dispatch
explanation: Single dispatch resolves the method based on the receiver's type. Double dispatch considers both the receiver and the argument's types.

text: What is metaclass programming?
options: Creating graphical user interfaces programmatically
Writing classes whose instances are themselves classes
A commonly used structural design pattern for code
Programming about programs in general terms
explanation: A metaclass is a class whose instances are classes. Metaclass programming allows customizing class creation, adding behaviors, or enforcing constraints.

text: What is the difference between prototype-based and class-based OOP?
options: Prototype-based OOP is always slower than class-based OOP overall
Class-based uses blueprints; prototype-based clones existing objects
They are the same approach with no meaningful differences at all
Class-based OOP cannot support any form of inheritance at all
explanation: Class-based OOP creates objects from class blueprints; prototype-based OOP creates objects by cloning prototypes and using delegation chains.

text: What is the fragile base class problem?
options: A compilation error from missing method implementations
A memory leak caused by improper object disposal code
A class that is too small to be useful on its own
Base class changes can break derived classes unexpectedly
explanation: The fragile base class problem occurs when modifications to a base class unintentionally break subclasses.

text: What is covariant return type?
options: A generic type parameter used in collection class definitions
Allowing an overriding method to return a more specific subtype
Always returning the exact same type as the parent declares
A type error that occurs when return types do not match up
explanation: Covariant return types allow an overriding method to narrow the return type to a subtype of the original.

text: What is the object slicing problem in C++?
options: Splitting an object into smaller component parts for storage
When assigning derived to base variable, derived data is lost
A memory allocation issue caused by fragmented heap storage
A threading problem from concurrent access to shared objects
explanation: Object slicing occurs when a derived class object is copied to a base class variable by value, losing the derived class's additional data members.

text: What is RAM?
options: Random Access Memory, volatile memory for programs
A programming language for system-level development
A type of hard drive for persistent data storage
Read-Access Module for hardware communication
explanation: RAM (Random Access Memory) is volatile memory that stores data and program code currently being used by the CPU.

text: What is the stack in memory?
options: A memory region storing locals and call info in LIFO
The hard drive used for persistent data storage
A data structure only with no memory role
A type of processor cache for fast data access
explanation: The stack stores local variables, function parameters, and return addresses in a Last-In, First-Out manner.

text: What is the heap in memory?
options: Read-only memory that cannot be written to
Identical to the stack memory region exactly
A sorted data structure for priority access
A memory region for dynamic memory allocation
explanation: The heap is a memory region where dynamically allocated objects reside; memory is allocated and freed in any order.

text: What is a memory leak?
options: A security vulnerability in network protocols
A hardware failure in the memory modules
A type of buffer overflow in array operations
Allocated memory no longer used but not freed
explanation: A memory leak occurs when a program allocates memory but fails to release it after use.

text: What is garbage collection?
options: A sorting algorithm for ordering data values
Deleting files from the disk storage system
Running disk cleanup utilities on the system
Automatic reclamation of unused object memory
explanation: Garbage collection automatically identifies and frees memory that is no longer referenced by the program.

text: What is a pointer?
options: A composite data type definition
A variable storing a memory address
A reusable block of executable code
A variable holding a computed value
explanation: A pointer holds the memory address of another variable, enabling indirect access to data.

text: What is the difference between stack and heap allocation?
options: Stack is automatic and fast with limited size; heap is larger but slower
Heap allocation is always faster than stack allocation in all scenarios
There is no meaningful difference between the two allocation methods
Stack memory is unlimited while heap memory has a very strict size cap
explanation: Stack allocation is automatic, fast, and limited; heap allocation is dynamic, larger, but slower and requires explicit deallocation or garbage collection.

text: What is a null pointer?
options: A pointer to the memory address zero
A pointer that has been deleted already
A pointer to the stack memory region
A pointer to no valid memory location
explanation: A null pointer holds a special value indicating it does not point to any valid object or memory location.

text: What does 'free()' do in C?
options: Frees up disk space on the storage drive
Deallocates previously allocated heap memory
Resets a variable back to its default value
Allocates new memory on the heap for use
explanation: free() releases dynamically allocated memory back to the system.

text: What is a variable's lifetime?
options: The duration the variable exists in memory
Its declared data type in the program
Its identifier name in the source program
Its accessibility scope in the source code
explanation: A variable's lifetime is the period from when memory is allocated for it until that memory is deallocated.

text: What is a dangling pointer?
options: A pointer referencing already freed memory
A pointer to a valid live object in memory
A pointer with a null value assigned to it
A smart pointer that manages object lifetime
explanation: A dangling pointer points to memory that has been freed, leading to undefined behavior if accessed.

text: What is reference counting as a garbage collection strategy?
options: Counting variables in the current scope of code
Tracking reference count and freeing at zero count
A manual memory management technique using counters
Counting the total number of memory blocks allocated
explanation: Reference counting maintains a count of references to each object; when the count drops to zero, the object is immediately deallocated.

text: What is a buffer overflow?
options: Slow memory access due to fragmentation issues
Writing data beyond the allocated buffer boundary
Running out of available system memory completely
A type of memory leak from unreleased resources
explanation: A buffer overflow occurs when data exceeds the buffer's allocated size, overwriting adjacent memory.

text: What is the difference between malloc() and calloc() in C?
options: They are exactly the same function with no differences
malloc initializes all allocated memory bytes to zero
malloc allocates uninitialized; calloc initializes to zero
calloc is always faster than malloc in all scenarios
explanation: malloc() allocates uninitialized memory; calloc() allocates memory for an array and initializes all bytes to zero.

text: What is virtual memory?
options: Shared memory accessible by multiple processes at once
Cloud storage accessible over the internet network
Using disk space to extend memory beyond physical RAM
Encrypted memory for storing sensitive data securely
explanation: Virtual memory maps memory addresses to physical RAM or disk, allowing programs to use more memory than physically available.

text: What is a memory pool?
options: A pre-allocated block for smaller allocations reducing fragmentation
A collection of physical memory hardware modules
A memory leak caused by unreleased dynamically allocated resources
A type of processor cache that stores frequently accessed data values
explanation: A memory pool pre-allocates a large block and serves smaller allocation requests from it, reducing fragmentation and overhead.

text: What is the difference between deep copy and shallow copy regarding memory?
options: Deep copy allocates new memory for nested objects; shallow copies refs
Shallow copy always uses more memory than deep copy for same structure
There is no difference in memory usage between the two copy methods
Deep copy uses less memory than shallow copy by sharing object refs
explanation: Shallow copy copies references, sharing memory for nested objects. Deep copy allocates new memory and recursively duplicates all nested objects.

text: What are smart pointers in C++?
options: Regular raw pointers with no extra features at all
Objects that wrap pointers and manage memory automatically
Pointers that point to function addresses in the program
Pointers that find data faster through optimized algorithms
explanation: Smart pointers (unique_ptr, shared_ptr, weak_ptr) automatically manage the lifetime of dynamically allocated objects, preventing memory leaks.

text: What is memory fragmentation?
options: Memory that has been encrypted for data security
Memory that has been compressed to save space
Corrupted memory caused by hardware failures in RAM
Free memory broken into small non-contiguous blocks
explanation: Memory fragmentation occurs when free memory is scattered in small blocks, potentially preventing large contiguous allocations.

text: What is the RAII pattern?
options: A design pattern for building user interfaces
A type of garbage collection used in managed languages
Tying resource lifetime to object lifetime via destructors
A memory allocation algorithm for dynamic memory pools
explanation: RAII ties resource management to object lifetime: resources are acquired in constructors and released in destructors.

text: What is the mark-and-sweep garbage collection algorithm?
options: Marking files for deletion from the disk system
A disk cleanup algorithm for removing temporary files
A memory allocation method for new object creation
A GC that marks reachable objects then frees unmarked
explanation: Mark-and-sweep traverses from root references to mark reachable objects, then frees all unmarked objects.

text: What is a generational garbage collector?
options: A manual memory manager without automatic collection
A garbage collector for old legacy programs only
A GC dividing objects by age, collecting young often
A reference counting garbage collection implementation
explanation: Generational GC exploits the observation that most objects die young, collecting the young generation more frequently for efficiency.

text: What is the difference between unique_ptr, shared_ptr, and weak_ptr in C++?
options: unique_ptr is exclusive; shared_ptr shares; weak_ptr observes only
They are all the same smart pointer type with no differences
shared_ptr cannot be copied or assigned to another shared_ptr var
unique_ptr is always slower than the other two smart pointer types
explanation: unique_ptr has sole ownership. shared_ptr allows multiple owners via reference counting. weak_ptr observes without preventing deallocation.

text: What is memory-mapped files and how do they work?
options: Files mapped into virtual address space for memory I/O
Files that have been encrypted for secure data storage
Files that are stored only in volatile RAM memory
Files that have been compressed to save disk space used
explanation: Memory-mapped files map file contents into the process's virtual address space, enabling efficient I/O by reading/writing memory addresses.

text: What is a slab allocator?
options: A garbage collector that runs periodically in background
A simple wrapper around the standard malloc function call
A disk allocator that manages file system block assignments
A mechanism pre-allocating and caching object-sized blocks
explanation: A slab allocator pre-allocates memory in slabs of object-sized chunks, reducing fragmentation for frequently created/destroyed objects.

text: What is escape analysis in JVM?
options: Compiler analysis determining if object references escape scope
Network traffic analysis for monitoring data flow patterns
Analyzing program exit codes and termination conditions
Exception analysis for tracking error propagation in code
explanation: Escape analysis determines if an object is only used within a method; if so, it can be allocated on the stack instead of the heap.

text: What is the ABA problem in lock-free memory management?
options: When a location changes A to B to A, making CAS incorrectly succeed
A problem related to the alphabet ordering of values
A variant of buffer overflow caused by writing past array boundaries
A type of memory leak caused by circular reference chains in objects
explanation: The ABA problem occurs when a value changes A->B->A; CAS sees the original value and succeeds incorrectly.

text: What is a write barrier in garbage collection?
options: A mechanism intercepting pointer writes for GC invariants
A memory protection scheme against buffer overflows
A disk write cache for improving I/O performance
A network firewall for filtering traffic
explanation: A write barrier intercepts pointer writes to maintain GC data structures, such as recording cross-generation references.

text: What is Copy-on-Write (COW)?
options: Sharing memory until a write triggers actual copying
Copying files manually between directories on disk
A garbage collection algorithm for reclaiming memory
An error handling technique for runtime exceptions
explanation: COW defers copying memory until a write occurs; multiple references share the same memory, and a copy is made only when modification happens.

text: What is address space layout randomization (ASLR)?
options: Randomizing process memory addresses to prevent attacks
A memory allocation strategy for dynamic array growth
An addressing mode for accessing hardware registers
A sorting algorithm for ordering memory addresses
explanation: ASLR randomizes positions of stack, heap, and libraries in a process's address space, making it harder for attackers to predict memory locations.

text: What is an exception?
options: A function call to a subroutine in the program
An abnormal event disrupting normal program flow
A normal expected program event during execution
A variable type for storing error code values
explanation: An exception is an event during execution that disrupts the normal flow.

text: What is a try-catch block?
options: A function definition with parameters and body
A looping construct for repeated iteration
A construct attempting code and handling exceptions
A conditional statement for branching logic
explanation: A try-catch block executes code in the try section and catches/handles any exceptions in the catch section.

text: What is the purpose of the 'finally' block?
options: To define a new function with a specific name
To terminate the program execution immediately
To execute code regardless of exception outcome
To finalize a variable and prevent changes
explanation: The finally block always executes after try/catch, whether or not an exception was thrown.

text: What is a NullPointerException?
options: A compile-time syntax error in the code
A valid and expected operation result
A warning generated by the code linter
An exception from using a null reference
explanation: A NullPointerException occurs when trying to access a member or method on a reference that is null.

text: What does 'throw' do in exception handling?
options: Ignores an exception and continues running
Explicitly raises and creates an exception
Logs an exception message to the console
Catches an exception from the try block
explanation: The throw keyword explicitly raises an exception, transferring control to the nearest matching catch block.

text: What is an ArithmeticException?
options: A type mismatch between two variable types
A file not found error during I/O access
An exception for illegal arithmetic operations
A syntax error in the source code text
explanation: ArithmeticException is thrown when an illegal arithmetic operation occurs, most commonly division by zero.

text: What is the difference between compile-time and runtime errors?
options: Compile-time errors cause the running program to crash unexpectedly
Runtime errors are detected and caught by the compiler before running
Compile-time errors are caught before execution; runtime during it
There is no meaningful difference between the two error types
explanation: Compile-time errors are detected by the compiler before running. Runtime errors occur during program execution.

text: What is an ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException?
options: A type error from wrong array element types
An exception for accessing an invalid index
A memory leak from unreleased array memory
A valid and expected array access result
explanation: This exception is thrown when trying to access an array element with an index that is negative or beyond the array length.

text: Can you have multiple catch blocks for one try?
options: Yes, to handle different exception types differently
No, only one catch block is allowed per try statement
Only for a maximum of exactly two exception types total
Only in Python, not in any other programming language
explanation: Multiple catch blocks can follow a single try block, each handling a different exception type.

text: What is an error message?
options: A variable name used for storing error data
A text description about what went wrong
A function output returned to calling code
A comment written inside the source code
explanation: An error message is a descriptive text associated with an exception that explains what went wrong.

text: What is the difference between checked and unchecked exceptions in Java?
options: Unchecked exceptions are always more severe than checked exceptions
Checked exceptions are always faster to handle than unchecked ones
Checked must be declared or caught; unchecked RuntimeExceptions do not
There is no meaningful difference between the two exception types
explanation: Checked exceptions must be either caught or declared. Unchecked exceptions don't require explicit handling.

text: What is exception propagation?
options: Unhandled exception moving up the call stack to find a handler
Fixing an exception by catching and logging it
Logging all exceptions to a file for later review and analysis
Throwing multiple exceptions at the same time from one method
explanation: When a method doesn't handle an exception, it propagates up the call stack until a handler is found or the program terminates.

text: What is a custom exception?
options: A user-defined exception class for domain-specific errors
A syntax error detected during the compilation process
A warning generated by the IDE during code development
A built-in exception provided by the language runtime
explanation: Custom exceptions are user-created exception classes for representing specific error conditions in the application domain.

text: What is the 'throws' keyword in Java?
options: A keyword for suppressing exceptions so they are ignored
A mechanism for catching and handling thrown exceptions
It is exactly the same as the throw keyword in Java
A declaration of which checked exceptions a method may throw
explanation: 'throws' in a method signature declares which checked exceptions the method may throw.

text: What is a stack trace?
options: A log file containing all program output and error data
The call stack data structure itself in memory
A memory dump of the entire program state at crash
A report showing the method call chain to an exception
explanation: A stack trace displays the chain of method calls from the point where the exception occurred, aiding in debugging.

text: What is the purpose of try-with-resources in Java?
options: To automatically close resources when the try block finishes
To try executing multiple methods in parallel simultaneously
To retry the try block harder if the first attempt fails
To allocate additional resources for the try block execution
explanation: Try-with-resources automatically closes resources that implement AutoCloseable when the try block exits.

text: What is the difference between Error and Exception in Java?
options: Errors are serious JVM problems; Exceptions are recoverable issues
Exceptions cannot be caught or handled by any catch block
They are the same thing with no meaningful differences at all
Errors are less serious and easier to handle than Exceptions
explanation: Errors represent serious JVM problems. Exceptions represent conditions that applications can reasonably catch and recover from.

text: What is a try-except-else block in Python?
options: An advanced loop construct for iterating with error handling
try runs risky code; except handles; else runs if no exception
Invalid syntax that causes a compilation error
A triple try block for handling three exceptions at once
explanation: In Python, else after except runs only when no exception was raised in the try block.

text: What is exception chaining?
options: Throwing many exceptions at the same time from one method call
Catching exceptions in a specific order from most to least severe
Wrapping one exception inside another to preserve original cause
A linked list of error objects stored in chronological order
explanation: Exception chaining wraps the original exception inside a new one, preserving the full error context for debugging.

text: What is the principle of 'fail fast'?
options: Testing the speed of program execution under various load levels
Detecting and reporting errors as soon as they occur in the program
Making programs crash as quickly as possible for testing purposes
Running programs as quickly as possible to improve the performance
explanation: Fail fast means detecting problems at the earliest possible point and reporting them immediately.

text: What is the difference between fault tolerance and exception handling?
options: Exception handling prevents all errors from ever occurring in the program
Exception handling manages code errors; fault tolerance is system resilience
They are exactly the same concept with no practical differences at all
Fault tolerance is always faster and easier to implement than exceptions
explanation: Exception handling is a programming mechanism for managing errors. Fault tolerance is a broader system design principle for continued operation despite failures.

text: What is an exception safety guarantee?
options: A guarantee that no exceptions will ever occur in the program
Levels of state guarantees after exception: no-throw, strong, basic
A compiler feature that eliminates all runtime exceptions automatically
A testing tool that verifies exception handlers work correctly always
explanation: Exception safety guarantees define what happens to program state when an exception is thrown: no-throw, strong (rollback), basic (valid state), or no guarantee.

text: What is the 'catch-all' anti-pattern?
options: A testing pattern for verifying exception handler correctness
Catching all exceptions generically which hides bugs in code
Catching only specific well-defined exceptions in the code
A recommended design pattern for robust exception handling
explanation: Catching all exceptions with a generic handler masks unexpected errors and makes debugging significantly harder.

text: What is the difference between recoverable and unrecoverable errors?
options: There is no meaningful difference between the two error categories
All errors in every program are always recoverable with proper code
All errors in every program are always unrecoverable without fixes
Recoverable errors can be handled; unrecoverable require termination
explanation: Recoverable errors can be handled gracefully. Unrecoverable errors typically require program termination.

text: What is Result/Either type as an alternative to exceptions?
options: A logging mechanism for recording exception messages
A type representing either success value or error value
A direct replacement for try-catch in all situations
A simple boolean type with true or false values
explanation: Result/Either types encode success or failure in the return type, making error handling explicit and checked at compile time.

text: What is the problem with exceptions and concurrency?
options: Exceptions in one thread may not be visible in another thread
No problems exist between exceptions and concurrency at all
Concurrency completely prevents all exceptions from occurring
Exceptions are inherently thread-safe in all implementations
explanation: In concurrent programming, exceptions in child threads don't automatically propagate to the parent thread.

text: What is a panic in Rust/Go?
options: A regular exception like any other in the language
A warning message that can be safely ignored by developers
An unrecoverable error that unwinds the stack and terminates
A debug log entry recorded during program execution review
explanation: A panic represents an unrecoverable error that unwinds the call stack and terminates the current thread or program.

text: What is structured concurrency's approach to exception handling?
options: Ignoring all exceptions thrown by child tasks completely
Propagating child exceptions to parent and cancelling siblings
Logging exceptions only without any propagation to parents
Catching all exceptions silently without any further action
explanation: Structured concurrency ensures that when a child task fails, its exception propagates to the parent and all sibling tasks are cancelled.

text: What is the difference between resumable and non-resumable exceptions?
options: Resumable allows continuing at throw point; non-resumable unwinds
There is no meaningful difference between the two exception types
Non-resumable exceptions can be resumed after the handler finishes
Resumable exceptions are always slower than non-resumable exceptions
explanation: Non-resumable exceptions unwind the stack. Resumable exceptions allow the handler to fix the issue and resume at the throw point.

text: What is the 'exception specification' problem in C++?
options: Exception specifications are completely reliable and always enforced
They always improve performance of exception handling in all cases
They are required by the C++ standard for every function definition
C++ throw() specs were problematic and replaced by noexcept keyword
explanation: C++ dynamic exception specifications were deprecated because they couldn't be verified at compile time; noexcept is the modern replacement.

text: What is a module in programming?
options: A data structure for storing elements
A variable type for storing data values
A self-contained unit of related code
A type of iterative loop construct
explanation: A module is a file or unit containing related code that can be imported and used by other parts of the program.

text: What is the purpose of the import statement?
options: To include functionality from another module
To delete a module from the project files
To compile code into executable binaries
To export code to other modules or files
explanation: The import statement brings in definitions from another module, making them available in the current file.

text: What is a package in Python?
options: A single Python source code file only
A Python class with methods and attributes
A directory containing modules and __init__.py
A standalone Python function definition
explanation: In Python, a package is a directory containing Python modules and typically an __init__.py file.

text: What is a library in programming?
options: A collection of pre-written reusable code modules
A place for storing reference books and manuals
An integrated development environment application
A single function defined in the source code file
explanation: A library is a collection of pre-written, reusable code organized into modules or packages.

text: What is the difference between a module and a package?
options: They are exactly the same thing with different names
A module is a single file; a package is a collection
A package is smaller in size compared to a module
A module contains multiple packages within itself
explanation: A module is typically a single file; a package is a directory or collection of related modules.

text: What is pip in Python?
options: A general-purpose programming language for development
A compiler that converts Python to machine code binary
A debugger tool for finding errors in Python programs
The package installer for Python that downloads packages
explanation: pip is Python's package manager used to install and manage third-party packages from PyPI.

text: What is npm?
options: A Python-specific development tool only
A database for storing application data values
A compiler for JavaScript source code files
Node Package Manager for JavaScript packages
explanation: npm is the default package manager for Node.js.

text: What is an API?
options: A general-purpose programming language for development
A relational database for storing structured data records
A set of definitions and protocols for software integration
A compiler that translates source code to machine language
explanation: An API defines how software components should interact.

text: What is a namespace?
options: A string type for storing text data values in memory
A file extension that identifies the programming language
A container for identifiers to avoid naming conflicts
A variable name in the source code program
explanation: A namespace provides a scope for identifiers, preventing naming conflicts.

text: What does 'from math import sqrt' do in Python?
options: Creates a new sqrt function in the current file
Imports only the sqrt function from math module
Deletes the sqrt function from the math module
Imports the entire math module with all functions
explanation: This imports only the specified function from the math module.

text: What is dependency management?
options: Managing memory allocation for program variables only
Tracking and maintaining external libraries and versions
Sorting functions in alphabetical order within modules
Managing source code files in a project directory
explanation: Dependency management involves tracking which external libraries a project needs and resolving version conflicts.

text: What is a package.json file?
options: A database schema definition for SQL table structures
A log file recording application events and errors
A Node.js config file listing metadata and dependencies
A generic JSON data file for storing values
explanation: package.json is the manifest file for Node.js projects, defining project metadata, scripts, and dependencies.

text: What is semantic versioning (SemVer)?
options: Alphabetical versioning using letter-based identifiers
Date-based versioning using release date as version
MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH scheme indicating type of changes
Random version numbers assigned without any rules
explanation: SemVer uses MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH: MAJOR for breaking changes, MINOR for features, PATCH for bug fixes.

text: What is a virtual environment in Python?
options: A Docker container with a bundled application inside
An isolated Python environment with its own packages
A cloud-hosted computing environment for deployment
A full virtual machine running a complete OS image
explanation: A virtual environment creates an isolated Python installation, allowing different projects to have different dependencies.

text: What is the difference between static and dynamic linking of libraries?
options: Static includes library at compile time; dynamic loads at runtime
Dynamic linking is always slower than static linking in every case
Static linking uses less memory than dynamic linking at all times
There is no meaningful difference between the two linking methods
explanation: Static linking embeds library code in the executable. Dynamic linking references shared libraries loaded at runtime.

text: What is a circular dependency?
options: When two or more modules depend on each other
A circular buffer data structure for streaming
A loop in the code that repeats indefinitely
A recursive function calling itself repeatedly
explanation: A circular dependency occurs when module A depends on module B and module B depends on module A.

text: What is tree shaking?
options: A data structure operation on binary tree nodes only
A sorting technique that organizes elements by weight
Removing trees from the project code repository
Removing unused dead code from the final build bundle
explanation: Tree shaking eliminates unused code from the final bundle, reducing its size.

text: What is the purpose of a lock file (e.g., package-lock.json)?
options: To lock the project and prevent any code changes
To encrypt installed packages for secure data storage use
To record exact dependency versions for reproducible builds
To prevent anyone from making changes to the source code
explanation: Lock files record the exact resolved version of every dependency for identical installations across machines.

text: What is the difference between devDependencies and dependencies in npm?
options: devDependencies are completely optional and never actually installed
dependencies are operating system packages not application libraries
There is no meaningful difference between the two dependency types
dependencies are for production; devDependencies for development only
explanation: dependencies are packages required to run the application. devDependencies are needed only during development.

text: What is a monorepo?
options: A repository containing only a single source file
A read-only repository that cannot accept any changes
A private repository with restricted access permissions
A single repository containing multiple related projects
explanation: A monorepo houses multiple projects in a single repository, sharing tools and configurations.

text: What is dependency injection?
options: Installing package dependencies from the package registry
A package manager for downloading and installing third-party libs
Providing dependencies from outside rather than creating internally
An import statement for including external module code in file
explanation: Dependency injection provides objects with their dependencies from external sources rather than having them create dependencies internally.

text: What is the OSGi framework?
options: A build tool for compiling and packaging Java applications
A dynamic Java module system supporting runtime bundle mgmt
An operating system for mobile devices and desktops
A testing framework for writing and running automated tests
explanation: OSGi is a Java framework providing a dynamic component model, allowing modules to be managed at runtime.

text: What is the difference between CommonJS and ES Modules?
options: They are identical module systems with no meaningful differences
ES Modules do not support any form of async module loading
CommonJS uses require synchronously; ESM uses import/export
CommonJS is the newer standard developed after ES Modules
explanation: CommonJS loads modules synchronously at runtime. ES Modules are statically analyzable and support tree shaking.

text: What is the diamond dependency problem in package management?
options: Two dependencies requiring different versions of the same package
A class inheritance issue in object-oriented programming code
A circular dependency between two modules in the same project
A build error caused by missing configuration files in project
explanation: The diamond dependency problem occurs when two packages depend on different, incompatible versions of a third package.

text: What is a plugin architecture?
options: A fixed codebase that cannot be extended or modified at all
A build system for compiling and packaging application binaries
A testing framework for automated integration and unit testing
A design allowing runtime extension via external loaded modules
explanation: A plugin architecture defines extension points allowing third-party modules to add functionality without changing the core code.

text: What is the Inversion of Control (IoC) container?
options: A framework component managing object creation and dependencies
A data structure for storing elements in a specific order only
A reversed loop that iterates in backward order through data
A version control system for tracking changes to source code
explanation: An IoC container manages the lifecycle and dependencies of objects, automatically creating and injecting dependencies.

text: What is ABI (Application Binary Interface) compatibility?
options: A network protocol for communication between distributed services
The same as API compatibility with no additional constraints
Binary-level interface ensuring compiled modules interact correctly
A data format for serializing objects to disk or network transfer
explanation: ABI compatibility ensures compiled binaries can work together without recompilation.

text: What is the Service Provider Interface (SPI) pattern?
options: A web service for hosting API endpoints on the internet
A physical network interface for connecting hardware devices
A design where providers implement a service-defined interface
A build tool for automating compilation and deployment tasks
explanation: SPI allows a service to define an interface that third-party implementations fulfill, discovered via mechanisms like Java's ServiceLoader.

text: What is reproducible builds and why is it important for packages?
options: Building projects as fast as possible to save developer time
Building multiple modules in parallel across available cores
Building the same project twice in a row for verification
Ensuring same source always produces bit-identical output binary
explanation: Reproducible builds guarantee identical binary output from the same source, enabling independent verification.

text: What is the difference between peer dependencies and regular dependencies?
options: Regular dependencies are always shared between all project packages
There is no meaningful difference between the two dependency types
Peer dependencies require the consumer to provide a shared version
Peer dependencies are completely optional and never need installing
explanation: Peer dependencies declare that a package expects a specific dependency to be provided by the consuming project.

text: What is a design pattern?
options: A reusable solution template for common software problems
A type of algorithm for data processing
A user interface visual design layout
A programming language for building applications
explanation: A design pattern is a general, reusable solution to a commonly occurring problem in software design.

text: What is recursion?
options: A function calling itself to solve problems
An error in the program source code
A type of iterative loop construct
A data structure for storing elements
explanation: Recursion is when a function calls itself, breaking down a problem into simpler sub-problems.

text: What is a data structure?
options: A way of organizing data for efficient access
A programming language for writing software
A function that performs a specific operation
A database management system for storage
explanation: A data structure is a way of organizing data for efficient operations.

text: What is an event in event-driven programming?
options: An action or occurrence that a program can respond to
A function that performs a specific task when called
A syntax error in the source code of the program
A variable that stores data values during execution
explanation: An event is a signal that something has happened that the program can respond to through event handlers.

text: What is a generic/template in programming?
options: A design pattern for building complex object hierarchies
Code that works with any type specified at compile time
A default value assigned to variables automatically
A runtime check that validates data types dynamically
explanation: Generics/templates allow writing code parameterized by type, enabling type-safe reuse.

text: What is the Singleton pattern?
options: Ensuring a class has only one global instance
Creating many instances of a class at once
An error pattern for handling exceptions
A function pattern for recursive operations
explanation: The Singleton pattern restricts a class to a single instance with global access.

text: What is abstraction in programming?
options: A primitive data type for storing numeric values only
Making code unnecessarily complex and hard to follow
Hiding details while exposing essential features only
A type of error that occurs during program execution
explanation: Abstraction simplifies complex systems by hiding implementation details and exposing only the necessary interface.

text: What is an annotation/decorator?
options: Metadata or wrapper adding behavior without modifying code
A loop construct for iterating over collection elements
A comment in the source code for documentation
A variable that stores a single data value in memory
explanation: Annotations/decorators provide metadata or modify behavior without changing the underlying code.

text: What is refactoring?
options: Adding new features and functionality to the existing codebase
Restructuring code to improve structure without changing behavior
Deleting unused code and removing deprecated function calls
Rewriting the entire codebase from scratch completely
explanation: Refactoring improves code quality and maintainability while preserving functionality.

text: What is the DRY principle?
options: A testing principle for validating dry run output
A moisture management rule for server rooms
Avoid duplicating code by abstracting common logic
A drying algorithm for removing duplicate data
explanation: DRY advocates reducing code duplication by extracting common logic into reusable components.

text: What is the Observer pattern?
options: A logging pattern for recording application events to a file
A security pattern for authenticating users before granting access
A subject notifies dependent observers of state changes automatically
Watching code execute step by step in the debugger tool
explanation: The Observer pattern defines a one-to-many dependency where the subject notifies all observers of state changes.

text: What is the Factory pattern?
options: A singleton variant that creates exactly one object instance
A manufacturing system for producing physical goods
An iterator pattern for traversing collections sequentially
A pattern providing an interface for creating objects flexibly
explanation: The Factory pattern delegates object creation to a factory method, allowing code to work with abstract types.

text: What is the Strategy pattern?
options: A sorting method that orders elements in a specific sequence
A game strategy for winning competitions against opponents
A pattern defining interchangeable algorithms varying independently
A caching technique for storing frequently accessed data values
explanation: The Strategy pattern encapsulates algorithms in separate classes with a common interface, allowing runtime selection.

text: What is the difference between composition and inheritance as design strategies?
options: Composition offers flexibility and loose coupling; inheritance is tighter
They are identical design strategies with no practical differences
Inheritance is always better than composition in every design scenario
Composition is always slower in execution than inheritance-based designs
explanation: Composition provides flexibility and loose coupling. Inheritance provides code reuse but creates tight coupling.

text: What is the Model-View-Controller (MVC) pattern?
options: A database pattern for organizing tables and their relationships
A testing framework for writing and running automated test suites
Separating application into Model (data), View (UI), Controller (logic)
A data structure for organizing elements in memory efficiently
explanation: MVC separates concerns: Model manages data, View handles display, Controller processes user input.

text: What is lazy loading?
options: Loading resources in reverse chronological order
Deferring initialization until actually needed
A slow loading technique for large file downloads
Loading all data and resources at startup time
explanation: Lazy loading postpones resource initialization until it is first needed, improving startup performance.

text: What is the difference between aggregation and composition in UML?
options: Composition implies weaker ownership than aggregation in all designs
Aggregation implies stronger ownership than composition in all cases
Aggregation implies weak ownership; composition implies strong ownership
They are identical concepts with no practical differences at all
explanation: Aggregation means parts can exist without the whole. Composition means parts are destroyed when the whole is destroyed.

text: What is the Iterator pattern?
options: Accessing collection elements sequentially without exposing internals
A search technique for finding elements in a sorted collection
A sorting algorithm for ordering elements in ascending sequence
A loop construct for iterating over elements in arrays
explanation: The Iterator pattern provides a standard interface for traversing different collection types.

text: What is the Decorator pattern?
options: A user interface pattern for visual element styling
A logging tool for recording method execution traces
Adding annotations to class methods for documentation
Dynamically adding responsibilities to objects through wrapping
explanation: The Decorator pattern wraps an object with additional behavior dynamically.

text: What is the Adapter pattern?
options: A data converter for transforming file format encodings
A sorting adapter for converting between sort algorithms
A pattern wrapping one interface to match another interface
A physical power adapter for electrical devices
explanation: The Adapter pattern wraps an existing class with a new interface, allowing incompatible interfaces to collaborate.

text: What is the Visitor pattern and when would you use it?
options: Defining new operations on objects without changing their classes
An authentication pattern for verifying user identity securely
A logging pattern for recording all application events to files
Tracking website visitors for analytics and usage metrics
explanation: The Visitor pattern separates algorithms from objects, enabling new operations without modifying existing classes.

text: What is a monad in functional programming?
options: A single immutable value in the program code
A pattern wrapping values for chaining operations with effects
A primitive data type for storing basic values in memory
A loop construct for iterating through collection elements
explanation: A monad wraps a value in a computational context and provides bind/flatMap to chain operations while managing side effects.

text: What is the difference between a microkernel and a monolithic architecture?
options: Microkernel architecture is always larger in size than monolithic
Monolithic architecture is always more modular than microkernel
Microkernel has minimal core with plugins; monolithic is all-in-one
There is no meaningful difference between the two architecture styles
explanation: Microkernel has a small core with functionality added via plugins. Monolithic contains all functionality in one codebase.

text: What is reactive programming?
options: Procedural programming with sequential statement execution
A paradigm focused on async data streams and change propagation
Programming with user interface reactions and animations
Event-driven programming using callbacks and event listeners
explanation: Reactive programming models computation as reactions to data changes, using observable streams.

text: What is the Command Query Responsibility Segregation (CQRS) pattern?
options: An authentication method for verifying user credentials
A database query for selecting records from tables
A sorting algorithm for ordering elements in collections
Separating read and write operations into different models
explanation: CQRS separates the read model from the write model, allowing independent scaling and optimization.

text: What is a domain-specific language (DSL)?
options: A general-purpose programming language for all domains
A scripting language for automating repetitive tasks
A specialized language designed for a particular domain
An assembly language for low-level hardware programming
explanation: A DSL is tailored to a specific problem domain, offering more expressive notation for domain tasks.

text: What is the actor model?
options: Independent units communicating via asynchronous message passing
A relational database model for organizing table schemas
A user interface pattern for laying out visual components
A design pattern for video game character development
explanation: The actor model treats actors as fundamental units communicating only through asynchronous messages, avoiding shared state.

text: What is aspect-oriented programming (AOP)?
options: A subtype of object-oriented programming methodology
Separating cross-cutting concerns from business logic via aspects
A user interface pattern for organizing visual layout components
A testing approach for validating application behavior thoroughly
explanation: AOP modularizes cross-cutting concerns that cut across multiple classes, weaving them into the code at join points.

text: What is the hexagonal architecture (ports and adapters)?
options: A network topology for connecting distributed system nodes
A six-sided geometric design for user interfaces
Isolating core domain from externals through ports and adapters
A database design for organizing tables and relationships
explanation: Hexagonal architecture defines ports for the core domain and adapters for external concerns, making the core independent of infrastructure.

text: What is algebraic effects as a programming concept?
options: Expressing side effects as values handled by effect handlers separately
An optimization technique for improving numerical computation speed
Mathematical operations performed on algebraic expressions
A specialized data structure for storing algebraic computation results
explanation: Algebraic effects separate effect definition from implementation, allowing functions to perform effects handled by enclosing handlers.

text: What is concurrency in programming?
options: Running only one task at a time sequentially
Handling multiple tasks in overlapping time periods
Exactly the same concept as true parallelism
Strictly sequential execution of all operations
explanation: Concurrency is the ability to manage multiple tasks that can start, run, and complete in overlapping time periods.

text: What is a thread?
options: A reusable function definition block
A type of string data structure
The smallest unit of execution within a process
A data structure for storing elements
explanation: A thread is the smallest sequence of instructions that can be managed independently by a scheduler.

text: What is a process?
options: A function call that executes and then returns
An independent program with its own memory space
A variable that stores data during execution
The same thing as a thread in all respects
explanation: A process is an independent program in execution with its own memory space.

text: What is the difference between concurrency and parallelism?
options: Concurrency always requires multiple CPU cores to function properly
Parallelism is always slower than concurrency in every scenario
They are exactly the same concept with no differences at all
Concurrency manages overlapping tasks; parallelism runs simultaneously
explanation: Concurrency is about dealing with multiple tasks at once (structure); parallelism is about executing multiple tasks simultaneously (execution).

text: What is multithreading?
options: A type of recursion with multiple recursive calls
Using multiple programming languages in one project
Using multiple processors in a distributed system
Running multiple threads within a single process
explanation: Multithreading is the execution of multiple threads within a single process, sharing the same memory space.

text: What is a race condition?
options: A debugging tool for analyzing multi-threaded applications
A bug where outcome depends on non-deterministic thread timing
A performance competition between different threads
A type of exception thrown during concurrent data access
explanation: A race condition occurs when multiple threads access shared data concurrently and the result depends on execution order.

text: What is synchronization?
options: A file operation for reading and writing data safely
Running code statements in sequential order always
A network protocol for data transfer between systems
Coordinating shared resource access to prevent races
explanation: Synchronization coordinates thread access to shared resources to prevent race conditions.

text: What is a mutex?
options: An algorithm for sorting elements in a collection
A primitive data type for storing values
A lock ensuring exclusive resource access for one thread
A function for performing calculations on data values
explanation: A mutex prevents multiple threads from simultaneously accessing a shared resource.

text: What is a deadlock?
options: A memory leak from unreleased dynamically allocated data
A type of infinite loop caused by incorrect conditions
Threads blocked forever waiting for each other resources
A program crash caused by a runtime error in code
explanation: A deadlock occurs when threads are blocked indefinitely because each holds a resource the other needs.

text: What is the main benefit of concurrency?
options: Improved performance and responsiveness of programs
Simpler code that is easier to read and understand
Smaller program binaries with reduced memory footprint
Fewer bugs and errors during program execution overall
explanation: Concurrency improves performance by overlapping I/O with computation and keeps the UI responsive.

text: What is a semaphore?
options: A counter-based primitive controlling shared resource access
A thread type for running background tasks in parallel
A simple flag variable for boolean state tracking
A type of lock for exclusive thread synchronization
explanation: A semaphore maintains a count representing available resources; threads acquire (decrement) or release (increment) it.

text: What is thread safety?
options: A testing concept for validating thread performance
Code functioning correctly under multi-thread access
A debugging feature for analyzing thread behavior
Threads that are guaranteed to never crash or fail
explanation: Thread-safe code produces correct results when accessed by multiple threads concurrently.

text: What is a thread pool?
options: Pre-created worker threads reused for tasks
A memory pool for thread-local storage data
A collection of all threads in the system
A connection pool for database access threads
explanation: A thread pool maintains reusable threads that pick up tasks from a queue, reducing thread creation overhead.

text: What is the producer-consumer problem?
options: A database issue with concurrent read and write transaction conflicts
A business problem related to supply chain management
A network problem caused by packet loss during data transmission
A synchronization problem with shared buffer between producers and consumers
explanation: The producer-consumer problem involves synchronizing producers and consumers sharing a bounded buffer.

text: What is an atomic operation?
options: An indivisible operation completing without interruption
A nuclear physics operation on atomic particles
A file operation that reads and writes data to disk
A database transaction that groups multiple queries
explanation: An atomic operation is indivisible: it either completes entirely or not at all, preventing race conditions.

text: What is the volatile keyword in Java?
options: A final variable that cannot be modified after assignment
A keyword ensuring reads always come from main memory
A variable that changes randomly without any cause
A type of exception thrown during concurrent operations
explanation: volatile ensures reads and writes go directly to main memory, preventing thread-local caching.

text: What is a Future/Promise?
options: An object representing a value available in the future
A type of thread that executes tasks asynchronously
A variable that is declared for use at a later time
A timer that triggers a callback after a set delay
explanation: A Future/Promise is a placeholder for a result that hasn't been computed yet.

text: What is context switching?
options: Saving one thread state and loading another for CPU switching
Changing the scope of a variable from local to global
Changing between different programming languages in a project
Switching between different source code files in an editor
explanation: Context switching saves the current thread's state and restores another's when the CPU switches execution.

text: What is the dining philosophers problem?
options: A sorting problem for ordering elements in a collection
A classic problem illustrating resource allocation and deadlock
A database problem with concurrent transaction conflicts
A food-related algorithm for meal planning optimization
explanation: The dining philosophers problem demonstrates deadlock and resource contention with shared resources.

text: What is async/await?
options: A type of loop for iterating over collection elements
A testing pattern for validating asynchronous behavior
A syntax for writing async code that looks synchronous
A threading model for managing parallel execution of tasks
explanation: async/await is syntactic sugar for working with promises/futures, allowing asynchronous code to be written sequentially.

text: What is the difference between optimistic and pessimistic concurrency control?
options: There is no meaningful difference between the two control approaches
Pessimistic concurrency control never uses any locks on resources
Optimistic concurrency control uses locks to prevent all conflicts
Optimistic assumes no conflicts and validates; pessimistic locks first
explanation: Optimistic allows concurrent access and checks at commit. Pessimistic locks resources before access.

text: What is the Compare-and-Swap (CAS) operation?
options: A file swap operation for exchanging file contents
A string operation for replacing character subsequences
A sorting operation for ordering elements in arrays
An atomic operation comparing and swapping memory values
explanation: CAS atomically checks if a memory location holds an expected value and replaces it if it does.

text: What is the Java Memory Model (JMM)?
options: A memory leak detector for finding unreleased allocations
A garbage collection model for reclaiming unused object memory
A specification defining thread memory interaction and visibility
How Java manages memory allocation for objects on the heap
explanation: The JMM defines rules for thread interaction through shared memory, including visibility guarantees.

text: What is work stealing in thread pool implementations?
options: A security vulnerability where threads access unauthorized data
Interrupting running threads to reassign their execution priority
Idle threads stealing tasks from busy threads for load balancing
Taking another thread source code and executing it directly
explanation: Work stealing lets idle threads take tasks from busy threads' queues, dynamically balancing workload.

text: What is a memory barrier/fence?
options: A hardware instruction enforcing memory operation ordering for CPUs
A memory protection boundary between user and kernel space
A memory limit restricting maximum allocation size for processes
A garbage collection trigger that starts when memory is low
explanation: A memory barrier prevents the CPU from reordering memory operations across it, ensuring visibility.

text: What is the happens-before relationship in concurrency?
options: A scheduling algorithm for determining thread execution priority
Simple chronological ordering of events by their timestamps
A debugging concept for tracing the order of function calls
A guarantee that writes by one action are visible to another
explanation: Happens-before guarantees that memory effects of one operation are visible to another.

text: What is a read-write lock (ReentrantReadWriteLock)?
options: A file lock for preventing concurrent file access
A database lock for serializing transaction execution
A lock allowing multiple readers but exclusive writers
A mutex variant supporting recursive lock acquisition
explanation: A read-write lock allows multiple readers simultaneously but requires exclusive access for writing.

text: What is lock-free programming?
options: A type of race condition caused by missing synchronization
Programming without any synchronization mechanisms at all
Single-threaded programming without any concurrent access
Using atomic operations instead of locks for thread progress
explanation: Lock-free programming uses atomic operations to ensure at least one thread always makes progress.

text: What is the difference between green threads and OS threads?
options: Green threads are runtime-scheduled; OS threads are kernel-managed
Green threads are always faster than OS threads in every scenario
They are exactly the same with no meaningful differences at all
OS threads use less memory than green threads in all situations
explanation: Green threads are scheduled by the language runtime. OS threads are kernel-managed with true parallelism but higher overhead.

text: What is Software Transactional Memory (STM)?
options: A concurrency mechanism using transactions for shared memory with retry
A memory allocation strategy for managing heap memory efficiently
A thread scheduler for determining execution order of all threads
A relational database system for persistent data storage
explanation: STM treats shared memory operations as transactions that can be composed, committed atomically, or retried on conflict.

text: What is debugging?
options: Compiling code into machine language
Writing new source code from scratch
Finding and fixing errors in a program
Running automated tests on the code
explanation: Debugging is the systematic process of identifying, analyzing, and removing errors from software.

text: What is a breakpoint?
options: An error in the source code of the program
A marker where the debugger pauses for inspection
An exit point from a loop during execution flow
The end of a function where control is returned
explanation: A breakpoint is a point where the debugger pauses execution for variable inspection.

text: What is a unit test?
options: A user acceptance test for final validation
Testing the entire application end to end
A test verifying a single isolated code unit
A performance test measuring execution speed
explanation: A unit test tests an individual function or method in isolation.

text: What is a bug?
options: A type of variable storing debug data
A feature that works as intended
A function used for error handling
An error or flaw causing wrong behavior
explanation: A bug is a defect that causes incorrect or unexpected results.

text: What is print/console debugging?
options: Using print statements to display values and flow
An automated process for finding and fixing bugs
A graphical user interface tool for visual debugging
Professional debugging using specialized IDE tools
explanation: Print debugging uses output statements to trace variable values and execution flow.

text: What is a test case?
options: A debug tool for stepping through code line by line
A set of inputs and expected results for verification
A code review process performed by team members
A programming language for writing test scripts
explanation: A test case defines inputs, actions, and expected outcomes to verify specific behavior.

text: What does the term 'step over' mean in debugging?
options: Execute current line and move to next without entering calls
Skip the entire program execution completely
Jump directly to the end of the current function body
Restart the debugging session from the very beginning
explanation: Step over executes the current line completely and stops at the next line in the current function.

text: What is a syntax error?
options: A logic mistake producing incorrect program results
A runtime error that occurs during program execution
A design flaw in the overall application architecture
A violation of language grammar rules at compile time
explanation: A syntax error occurs when code doesn't conform to the language's grammar rules.

text: What is the purpose of an assertion?
options: To create a new variable in the current scope of code
To import a module from an external library into the file
To define a new function with parameters and return type
