To verify a condition is true and fail with message if not
explanation: An assertion checks a condition and throws an error if false, validating assumptions.

text: What is a logic error?
options: An error where program runs but produces incorrect results
A syntax mistake caught during code compilation by compiler
A type error from assigning incompatible values to variables
A compile error that prevents the program from being built
explanation: A logic error produces wrong results because the algorithm or logic is incorrect.

text: What is Test-Driven Development (TDD)?
options: A deployment strategy for releasing code to production
Testing code only after all development is complete
Writing tests before code to drive the design process
A debugging technique for finding runtime exceptions
explanation: TDD follows: write a failing test, write minimal code to pass it, then refactor.

text: What is code coverage?
options: How much source code has been written in total
The percentage of code lines executed during testing
A code review tool for examining code quality metrics
A security measure for protecting source code files
explanation: Code coverage measures what percentage of source code is exercised by the test suite.

text: What is the difference between black-box and white-box testing?
options: There is no meaningful difference between the two testing approaches
Black-box tests functionality without code; white-box tests internals
White-box testing is fully automated while black-box is manual
Black-box testing is always better than white-box in every case
explanation: Black-box validates behavior based on specifications. White-box examines internal code structure and logic paths.

text: What is a mock object in testing?
options: A simulated object mimicking real behavior for tests
A real database connection used in production
A randomly generated object with unpredictable state
A deleted object that has been garbage collected
explanation: A mock simulates the behavior of real dependencies, allowing isolated testing.

text: What is integration testing?
options: Testing interaction between multiple components together
Testing the user interface for visual correctness
Testing individual functions in complete isolation
Testing performance under heavy load conditions
explanation: Integration testing verifies that different modules work correctly together.

text: What is regression testing?
options: Testing for performance regression and slower execution
Re-running tests after changes to ensure nothing broke
Testing only brand new features and functionality
Testing backward compatibility with previous versions
explanation: Regression testing re-executes existing tests to ensure changes haven't introduced new bugs.

text: What is profiling in software optimization?
options: Writing documentation for the application source code
Analyzing execution to identify performance bottlenecks
Formatting code according to style guide conventions
Creating user profiles for application personalization
explanation: Profiling measures execution characteristics to identify performance bottlenecks.

text: What is the difference between stubs and mocks?
options: Mocks are simpler and provide less functionality than stubs do
They are identical testing concepts with no differences at all
Stubs are more complex to implement than mocks in every case
Stubs provide predefined responses; mocks also verify interactions
explanation: Stubs return predefined data. Mocks also verify that they were called correctly.

text: What is a memory profiler?
options: A physical RAM module installed in the computer
A tool monitoring memory usage and detecting leaks
A compiler for translating source code to binaries
A debugger for stepping through code line by line
explanation: A memory profiler tracks memory allocations, usage patterns, and identifies memory leaks.

text: What is the difference between manual and automated testing?
options: There is no practical difference between the two testing methods
Manual testing is done by humans; automated uses scripts and tools
Automated testing is less reliable than manual in every case
Manual testing is faster than automated testing for all suites
explanation: Manual testing involves humans executing test cases. Automated testing uses scripts and tools for speed and repeatability.

text: What is property-based testing?
options: Specifying invariants that hold for all generated inputs
Testing object properties for correct values and types
Testing CSS properties for correct visual rendering
Testing configuration properties for application behavior
explanation: Property-based testing generates random inputs to verify that specified properties always hold.

text: What is fuzzing?
options: Making code intentionally unclear and hard to understand
Feeding random or malformed data to find vulnerabilities
A string operation for making text fuzzy and unreadable
A sorting technique that introduces randomness into order
explanation: Fuzzing generates random or semi-random inputs to find crashes, security holes, and unexpected behavior.

text: What is mutation testing?
options: Testing DNA sequences for mutations in bioinformatics apps
Introducing small code changes to check if tests catch them
Testing genetic algorithms for correct evolution behavior
Testing code changes for backward compatibility with old code
explanation: Mutation testing inserts small changes into source code and checks if the test suite detects them.

text: What is the difference between static and dynamic analysis?
options: Dynamic analysis is performed before compilation of the source code
Static examines code without executing; dynamic examines during execution
There is no meaningful difference between the two analysis approaches
Static analysis is performed at runtime while the program is executing
explanation: Static analysis inspects source code without running it. Dynamic analysis examines behavior during execution.

text: What is a flame graph in performance analysis?
options: An error graph displaying exception frequency over time
A fire hazard visualization for safety monitoring apps
A visualization of call stacks showing CPU time by code path
A heat map showing temperature distribution across hardware
explanation: A flame graph visualizes sampled stack traces, quickly revealing performance hotspots.

text: What is symbolic execution?
options: Executing code with special symbol characters as input data
Executing code with symbolic values to explore all possible paths
A debugging mode that shows variable names instead of values
Running code with special characters to test encoding correctness
explanation: Symbolic execution uses symbolic inputs to explore all feasible execution paths, generating inputs that trigger specific paths or bugs.

text: What is the difference between correctness testing and performance testing?
options: They are exactly the same type of testing with no differences
Correctness verifies behavior; performance measures speed and resources
Correctness testing measures execution speed like performance does
Performance testing checks for bugs just like correctness testing
explanation: Correctness testing ensures correct results. Performance testing measures response times and resource utilization under load.

text: What is chaos engineering?
options: Random testing with no specific goals or expected outcomes
Unstructured development without any planning or methods
Creating intentionally buggy software for entertainment
Intentionally introducing failures to test system resilience
explanation: Chaos engineering deliberately injects failures into production systems to verify they can withstand disruptions.

text: What is taint analysis?
options: Analyzing memory allocation patterns for optimization purposes
Tracking untrusted input flow to find security vulnerabilities
Checking code for bugs using standard debugging tools
Formatting code according to standard style guide conventions
explanation: Taint analysis tracks data from untrusted sources to detect if it reaches sensitive operations without sanitization.

text: What is the difference between benchmarking and profiling?
options: They are identical performance analysis methods with no differences
Benchmarking measures overall metrics; profiling identifies bottlenecks
Profiling gives overall performance metrics; benchmarking is specific
Benchmarking provides more detailed analysis than profiling overall
explanation: Benchmarking measures overall performance for comparison. Profiling breaks down performance per function to locate bottlenecks.

text: What is version control?
options: Tracking and managing code changes over time
Controlling which program version users access
A testing tool for validating software behavior
A deployment tool for releasing code to servers
explanation: Version control tracks all changes to source code, allowing collaboration and reverting to previous versions.

text: What is Git?
options: A distributed version control system for code
A general-purpose programming language
A database for storing application data
A compiler for translating source to binaries
explanation: Git is a distributed version control system that tracks changes in source code.

text: What is a code review?
options: Deleting unused code from the codebase
Having developers examine code before merging
Writing documentation for the application
Running automated tests on the source code
explanation: Code review is the systematic examination of source code by peers to find bugs and improve quality.

text: What is agile development?
options: A programming language designed for rapid application development tasks
An iterative approach emphasizing collaboration and short delivery cycles
A testing method for validating software behavior against requirements
Developing software as fast as possible without any planning at all
explanation: Agile uses iterative sprints, continuous feedback, and adaptive planning to deliver working software incrementally.

text: What is a commit in version control?
options: A deployment to the production environment
A branch created for feature development
Deleting code from the shared repository
A snapshot of changes saved with a message
explanation: A commit records a set of changes to the repository with a descriptive message.

text: What is a branch in Git?
options: An independent line of development from main
A variable used to store temporary values
A copy of a file in the project directory
A function defined in the source code file
explanation: A branch is a separate line of development allowing work without affecting the main codebase.

text: What is a merge in Git?
options: Creating a new commit in the repository
Splitting code into separate source files
Combining changes from one branch into another
Deleting a branch from the repository
explanation: Merging integrates changes from one branch into another.

text: What is documentation in software development?
options: Only code comments written inside source files
Written descriptions of software design and usage
A testing tool for validating software correctness
A deployment process for releasing to production
explanation: Documentation explains the software's purpose, architecture, API, and usage instructions.

text: What is the purpose of code comments?
options: To define variables inside the source file
To make the code execute faster at runtime
To handle errors during program execution
To explain the intent and reasoning in code
explanation: Comments explain why code exists and clarify complex logic, aiding maintenance.

text: What is a pull request?
options: A data request sent to a server for information
A build request to compile and package the application
Pulling code packages from the internet registry
A request to merge changes with review and discussion
explanation: A pull request proposes merging changes, providing a platform for code review and discussion.

text: What is Continuous Integration (CI)?
options: A deployment method for releasing code directly to production servers
Integrating code changes only once at the very end of development
A branching strategy for organizing feature development work in Git
Frequently merging code with automated builds and tests for feedback
explanation: CI involves frequently integrating code with automated builds and tests to catch issues early.

text: What is Continuous Deployment/Delivery (CD)?
options: Automatically deploying or making release-ready every passing change
A testing strategy for validating software behavior before release
A branching model for organizing development work into branches
Manual deployment performed by operations team members only
explanation: Continuous Delivery ensures code is always release-ready; Continuous Deployment automatically pushes passing changes to production.

text: What is the KISS principle?
options: A security principle for protecting sensitive user data
A greeting or expression of affection between people
A testing method for validating software behavior fully
Keep It Simple: avoid unnecessary complexity in design
explanation: KISS advocates simplicity in design, avoiding unnecessary complexity.

text: What is technical debt?
options: Financial debt from purchasing technology equipment
Server costs for hosting applications in the cloud
The cost of future rework from choosing quick solutions
A type of bug caused by incorrect logic in the code
explanation: Technical debt accumulates when shortcuts save time now but require more effort later.

text: What is the YAGNI principle?
options: A testing approach for validating code correctness
A design pattern for creating reusable components
A programming language for building applications
Do not implement functionality until actually needed
explanation: YAGNI advises against adding functionality based on speculation about future needs.

text: What is pair programming?
options: Programming alone without any collaboration at all
Writing duplicate code for redundancy and safety nets
Two developers at one workstation: driver and navigator
Programming with matched pairs of source code files
explanation: Pair programming has two developers working together: the driver writes code while the navigator reviews.

text: What is a sprint in Scrum?
options: A fixed-length iteration for completing prioritized tasks
A deployment window for releasing features to production
A programming technique for optimizing code performance
Running fast to meet project deadlines under pressure
explanation: A sprint is a time-boxed iteration in Scrum methodology for completing prioritized tasks.

text: What is a code smell?
options: Malicious code injected by an attacker into the system
A runtime bug that crashes the application unexpectedly
A surface indication suggesting a deeper design problem
A syntax error detected during the compilation process
explanation: A code smell suggests a deeper design problem, indicating refactoring is needed.

text: What is the difference between waterfall and agile methodologies?
options: Agile has no planning or structure and relies entirely on improvisation
There is no meaningful difference between the two methodologies at all
Waterfall is always faster than agile for delivering software to production
Waterfall is sequential with fixed phases; agile is iterative and flexible
explanation: Waterfall follows a linear sequence. Agile uses iterative cycles with continuous feedback.

text: What is a merge conflict?
options: A deployment error when releasing code to production servers
A runtime error that occurs during program execution in prod
A syntax error caused by incorrect code in the source file
When Git cannot auto-merge because branches changed same code
explanation: A merge conflict occurs when two branches have made different changes to the same lines of code.

text: What is the Twelve-Factor App methodology?
options: Twelve testing phases that every software project must go through
A math-based approach to solving programming problems algorithmically
Twelve best practices for building scalable cloud-native applications
Twelve programming languages that every developer should learn fully
explanation: The Twelve-Factor App defines best practices for building SaaS applications.

text: What is trunk-based development?
options: A database design pattern for normalizing relational table schemas
A branching strategy with single shared branch and short-lived branches
Using tree data structures for organizing hierarchical information
A testing strategy for validating software behavior systematically
explanation: Trunk-based development uses a single shared branch with small, frequent commits and short-lived feature branches.

text: What is the difference between monorepo and polyrepo strategies?
options: Polyrepo is only suitable for small projects with limited source code
Monorepo is always the better choice regardless of project requirements
There is no meaningful difference between the two repository strategies
Monorepo stores all projects in one repo; polyrepo uses separate repos
explanation: Monorepo stores all projects together. Polyrepo uses separate repositories for isolation and independent lifecycles.

text: What is Infrastructure as Code (IaC)?
options: Managing infrastructure through configuration files not manual processes
A programming paradigm for organizing code into functional modules
Writing source code directly on production servers for deployment
A coding standard for formatting and naming conventions in code
explanation: IaC uses configuration files to define and manage infrastructure programmatically.

text: What is the concept of 'shift left' in testing?
options: Moving code directly to production without any testing or review
Left-aligning all source code for consistent formatting readability
Performing testing earlier in the development lifecycle for detection
Moving test files to the left side of the project directory tree
explanation: Shift left moves testing earlier in the development process to catch defects when they are cheaper to fix.

text: What is feature flagging/toggling?
options: A branching strategy for organizing feature development in Git
A testing tool for validating feature behavior before release
Deleting features permanently from the codebase and all traces
Enabling or disabling features at runtime without deploying code
explanation: Feature flags toggle features at runtime without new deployments, enabling gradual rollouts and quick rollbacks.

text: What is the strangler fig pattern for legacy modernization?
options: Gradually replacing legacy systems by building new functionality around
A refactoring tool for automatically restructuring legacy code
A tree-related data structure pattern for hierarchical organization
Deleting all old code at once and replacing with a new system
explanation: The strangler fig pattern incrementally replaces a legacy system by routing new requests to new code.

text: What is observability in software systems?
options: A debugging tool for stepping through code execution line by line
Watching code run step by step in a debugger tool window
Monitoring only server uptime without any additional data collection
Understanding system state from external outputs like logs and metrics
explanation: Observability provides logs, metrics, and traces for understanding and diagnosing issues in complex systems.

text: What is the CAP theorem and how does it affect development practices?
options: A design pattern for organizing code into reusable modular components
In distributed systems, only two of Consistency, Availability, Partition tolerance
A coding principle for writing clean and maintainable source code
A testing theorem for determining the minimum number of test cases
explanation: The CAP theorem states that distributed systems can guarantee at most two of: Consistency, Availability, and Partition tolerance.

text: What is the concept of 'toil' in Site Reliability Engineering (SRE)?
options: Testing software to validate that it meets all specified requirements
Manual repetitive automatable work scaling linearly with service size
Hard work that requires significant effort and long hours from team
Debugging code to find and fix errors in the application source
explanation: In SRE, toil is manual, repetitive work that should be eliminated through automation.

text: What is source code in programming?
options: Human-readable instructions for a program
The memory address of a running process
A hardware signal sent to the processor
The binary output after compilation
explanation: Source code is the human-readable set of instructions written by a programmer.

text: What does a linker do in program building?
options: Checks source code for syntax errors
Allocates memory for runtime variables
Converts code into an intermediate format
Combines object files into an executable
explanation: A linker combines compiled object files and libraries into a single executable program.

text: Which term describes a fixed value written directly in code?
options: Keyword
Operator
Literal
Variable
explanation: A literal is a fixed value written directly in the source code, such as 42 or hello.

text: What is the main purpose of a flowchart?
options: To encrypt data before transmission
To allocate memory during execution
To compile source code into bytecode
To visually represent program logic flow
explanation: A flowchart visually represents the logical flow and steps of a program or algorithm.

text: What does the term debugging mean?
options: Designing the user interface layout
Compiling code into machine language
Writing new features for software
Finding and fixing errors in code
explanation: Debugging is the process of identifying, locating, and correcting errors in a program.

text: Which of these is an example of a markup language?
options: HTML
Python
Java
Ruby
explanation: HTML (HyperText Markup Language) is a markup language used to structure content on the web.

text: What is a runtime environment?
options: A database for storing application data
A text editor for writing source code files
A network protocol for data transmission
Software that provides execution support
explanation: A runtime environment provides the necessary support for executing a program.

text: What is machine code composed of?
options: English keywords and symbols
Flowchart diagram elements
Pseudocode planning statements
Binary digits zeros and ones
explanation: Machine code consists of binary instructions (0s and 1s) that the CPU can directly execute.

text: What is the purpose of comments in code?
options: To speed up program execution time
To declare variables automatically
To handle errors during runtime
To explain code for human readers
explanation: Comments are annotations that explain code purpose, ignored by the compiler or interpreter.

text: What does open source mean for software?
options: Software that runs only on the internet
Software with publicly available source code
Software that requires no installation
Software created by a single developer
explanation: Open source software has its source code publicly available for anyone to view, modify, and distribute.

text: What distinguishes a strongly typed language from a weakly typed one?
options: Strongly typed languages do not support any string operations
Weakly typed languages cannot handle any numeric data types
Strongly typed languages enforce strict rules with fewer casts
Strongly typed languages run faster than weakly typed ones always
explanation: Strongly typed languages enforce strict type rules and restrict implicit type conversions.

text: What is the difference between a logical error and a runtime error?
options: Logical errors crash the program; runtime errors give wrong results
Both types always occur at compile time before program even starts
Logical errors give wrong results; runtime errors halt execution
Runtime errors give wrong output; logical errors are always harmless
explanation: Logical errors cause incorrect results without crashing; runtime errors cause abnormal termination.

text: What is the role of a preprocessor in languages like C?
options: It processes directives like includes and macros before compiling
It manages memory allocation and garbage collection automatically
It links all object files together into one single final binary
It executes the program line by line during the runtime phase
explanation: The preprocessor handles directives such as #include and #define before compilation.

text: What is the key advantage of using an interpreted language?
options: Interpreted languages cannot be used for web applications
Interpreted languages always produce faster executables overall
Interpreted languages enable platform-independent execution
Interpreted languages require a separate linking step always
explanation: Interpreted languages allow immediate execution without separate compilation, enabling portability.

text: What does bytecode represent in languages like Java?
options: The final machine code running directly on hardware
An intermediate code executed by a virtual machine
The original source code written by the programmer
A binary format used only for storing databases
explanation: Bytecode is an intermediate representation that runs on a virtual machine like the JVM.

text: How does just-in-time (JIT) compilation improve performance?
options: It compiles the entire program before any execution begins
It translates bytecode to native code at runtime for speed
It replaces the interpreter with a static linker entirely
It removes all comments and whitespace from source code
explanation: JIT compilation converts bytecode to native machine code during execution for better speed.

text: What is the purpose of a symbol table during compilation?
options: It maps identifiers to their types, scopes, and locations
It stores the graphical icons used in the program interface
It records user input received during program execution
It contains the complete list of syntax errors in the code
explanation: A symbol table stores identifier information including types, scopes, and memory locations.

text: What is the difference between static and dynamic typing?
options: Static typing checks types at compile time; dynamic at runtime
Static typing is only for scripting; dynamic for systems coding
Static typing has no types; dynamic typing has strict types only
Static typing is slower; dynamic typing is always faster overall
explanation: Static typing checks types at compile time; dynamic typing defers type checking to runtime.

text: What does lexical analysis do during compilation?
options: It links external libraries into the final binary
It checks the logic correctness of the algorithm
It optimizes the generated machine code for speed
It breaks source code into tokens for the parser
explanation: Lexical analysis breaks source code into tokens for subsequent parsing.

text: What is a cross-compiler used for?
options: It compiles code for a different target platform
It converts machine code back into source code
It interprets and compiles code at the same time
It compiles two languages into one single program
explanation: A cross-compiler generates executable code for a platform different from the one it runs on.

text: A program compiles without errors but produces incorrect output for certain edge cases. What type of error is this?
options: A runtime error causing immediate termination
A linker error from missing library references
A syntax error caught by the compiler during build
A logical error in the algorithm for edge cases
explanation: Incorrect output without crashes indicates a logical error in the algorithm.

text: In a multi-pass compiler, what is the primary advantage of separating analysis into multiple passes?
options: It allows forward references and better optimization
It makes the compiler run faster by skipping checks
It eliminates the need for any optimization phase
It removes the requirement for a symbol table
explanation: Multiple passes allow handling forward references and performing thorough optimizations.

text: A program works on the developer machine but fails in production. Which factor is most likely the cause?
options: The source code changed itself during the deployment
Environment differences such as OS or library versions
The language behaves differently on each machine
The compiler randomly generates different code each run
explanation: Environmental differences are the most common cause of works-on-my-machine failures.

text: What is the key difference between ahead-of-time (AOT) and just-in-time (JIT) compilation?
options: AOT requires an interpreter; JIT does not need one
JIT always produces slower code than AOT in every case
AOT compiles before execution; JIT compiles during runtime
AOT is only for scripting; JIT only for systems languages
explanation: AOT compiles before execution producing native binaries; JIT compiles during execution.

text: Why might a developer choose a dynamically typed language for rapid prototyping?
options: Dynamic typing makes programs run faster on all hardware
Dynamic typing enables faster iteration by skipping types
Dynamic typing always produces more efficient machine code
Dynamic typing prevents all runtime errors from occurring
explanation: Dynamic typing reduces boilerplate, allowing faster iteration during prototyping.

text: What problem does the halting problem describe in computer science?
options: The difficulty of stopping an infinite loop in a program
The challenge of compiling large programs within limits
The problem of optimizing code for memory constraints
The impossibility of deciding if any program will halt
explanation: The halting problem proves no general algorithm can determine whether any arbitrary program will halt.

text: A language uses type inference to determine variable types. What is the main benefit?
options: It makes the language dynamically typed at runtime
It prevents the compiler from performing optimization
It combines static type safety with reduced verbosity
It eliminates type safety entirely from the language
explanation: Type inference provides compile-time type safety while reducing explicit type annotations.

text: In language design, what does homoiconicity mean?
options: The language can only represent one data type
Code and data share the same representation
The language does not support metaprogramming
The compiler and interpreter are identical
explanation: Homoiconicity means program structure uses the language's own data structures, enabling metaprogramming.

text: What is the primary purpose of an abstract syntax tree (AST) in compilation?
options: To store the final machine code for the target
To handle input and output during execution
To represent hierarchical syntactic structure
To manage memory allocation during runtime
explanation: An AST represents the hierarchical syntactic structure of code for analysis and code generation.

text: A team debates compiled vs interpreted for a real-time system. What is the strongest argument for compiled?
options: Compiled languages always have simpler syntax
Compiled languages are easier for beginners
Compiled languages need no testing before use
Compiled languages produce optimized native code
explanation: Compiled languages produce optimized native code with predictable timing, critical for real-time systems.

text: What is a Boolean data type used to represent?
options: Text strings
Memory addresses
Decimal numbers
True or false
explanation: A Boolean data type represents one of two values: true or false.

text: What is the default value of an uninitialized integer in Java?
options: Undefined
Null
Zero
One
explanation: In Java, uninitialized instance integer variables default to zero.

text: Which data type stores a single character?
options: char
integer
float
string
explanation: The char data type is designed to store a single character value.

text: What does the term type casting mean?
options: Creating a new custom data type
Assigning a name to a constant
Deleting a variable from memory
Converting a value to another type
explanation: Type casting is the process of converting a value from one data type to another.

text: Which data type is used for numbers with decimal points?
options: char
integer
float
boolean
explanation: Float or double data types store numbers with decimal points.

text: What is the difference between a variable and a constant?
options: A variable is global; a constant is always local
A constant uses more memory than a variable
A variable is faster than a constant in speed
A variable can change; a constant cannot change
explanation: A variable can change during execution; a constant remains fixed once assigned.

text: What does null represent in programming?
options: A Boolean false state
The absence of a value
The number zero value
An empty text string
explanation: Null represents the intentional absence of any value or object reference.

text: Which keyword declares a constant in many languages?
options: dim
let
const
var
explanation: The const keyword is used in many languages to declare constants.

text: What is an unsigned integer?
options: An integer with unlimited storage capacity
An integer storing only non-negative values
An integer stored as a floating-point value
An integer that can hold negative values only
explanation: An unsigned integer can only store non-negative values, using all bits for magnitude.

text: What is a string in programming?
options: A sequence of characters
A type of loop structure
A memory address pointer
A numeric calculation result
explanation: A string is a sequence of characters used to represent text data.

text: What is the risk of implicit type conversion in weakly typed languages?
options: It may produce unexpected results by silently converting
It prevents the programmer from using numeric data types
It always causes the program to crash immediately on start
It makes the program significantly faster than explicit casts
explanation: Implicit coercion can silently convert types, producing unexpected hard-to-debug results.

text: Why might a developer choose a double over a float?
options: A double provides greater precision for decimal values
A double is faster to process than a float on all CPUs
A double uses less memory than a float in all cases
A double can store text while a float cannot do that
explanation: A double uses 64 bits vs float's 32 bits, providing greater decimal precision.

text: What is an enumeration (enum) data type used for?
options: Creating dynamic arrays that resize automatically
Defining a set of named constant integer values
Encrypting sensitive string data in the program
Storing floating-point numbers with high precision
explanation: An enum defines named constants, making code more readable and type-safe.

text: What happens when integer overflow occurs?
options: The program automatically switches to a larger type
The OS allocates additional memory for the variable
The compiler prevents the code from being compiled
The value wraps around to the minimum of its range
explanation: Integer overflow causes the value to wrap around to the minimum of the type's range.

text: What is the difference between value types and reference types?
options: Value types require garbage collection; references do not
Value types are slower to access than reference types
Value types store data directly; references store addresses
Reference types cannot be used inside any functions
explanation: Value types store data directly; reference types store memory addresses pointing to data.

text: Why are floating-point numbers sometimes imprecise?
options: Computers cannot perform mathematical operations
Binary representation cannot express all decimal values
Languages intentionally introduce errors for speed
Floating-point numbers are always rounded to integers
explanation: Binary floating-point cannot exactly represent all decimal fractions, leading to precision errors.

text: What is a union data type in languages like C?
options: A type combining two arrays into one structure
A type that converts between different types
A type where all members share the same location
A type that stores multiple values simultaneously
explanation: A union lets different members share the same memory location, holding one value at a time.

text: What is type narrowing in TypeScript?
options: Restricting variables to only hold integer values
Refining a variable type within a conditional
Converting a large data type to a smaller one
Removing type information from variables at runtime
explanation: Type narrowing refines a variable's type to a more specific type within a conditional block.

text: What is the purpose of the void type in programming?
options: To indicate a function returns no value at all
To represent null pointer references in memory
To declare variables with unspecified data type
To store empty string values in memory locations
explanation: The void type indicates that a function does not return any value.

text: How does a BigInteger type differ from a standard integer?
options: BigInteger uses less memory than a standard integer
BigInteger stores decimal fractions unlike standard int
BigInteger can represent arbitrarily large whole numbers
BigInteger is faster but less accurate than standard
explanation: BigInteger types can represent whole numbers of arbitrary size, not limited by fixed bits.

text: A financial app uses float for currency and has rounding errors. What is the recommended fix?
options: Convert all values to strings before performing calculations
Use double type since it eliminates all rounding errors
Increase float precision by adding more decimal places
Use integer types storing values in cents to avoid issues
explanation: Storing currency as integers in smallest unit (cents) avoids floating-point precision issues.

text: What is the concept of type erasure in Java generics?
options: Type information is stored in a separate metadata file
The compiler removes all type info making code untyped
The JVM dynamically creates new types for each generic use
Generic type parameters are replaced with bounds at compile
explanation: Type erasure replaces generic type parameters with their bounds during compilation.

text: A developer encounters NaN in JavaScript. Which operation could produce this result?
options: Multiplying a string that cannot be parsed as a number
Comparing two floating-point numbers with strict equality
Concatenating two numeric strings together into one
Dividing a positive integer by a negative integer value
explanation: Arithmetic on non-numeric strings produces NaN when the string cannot be parsed as a number.

text: Why does comparing floating-point numbers with exact equality often fail?
options: The equality operator does not work with numeric types
Binary representation introduces tiny precision errors
Floating-point numbers are stored as strings internally
Floating-point comparisons are not supported by CPUs
explanation: Binary floating-point representation introduces precision errors making exact comparisons unreliable.

text: In Rust, what is the purpose of the Option<T> type?
options: It provides thread-safe access to shared mutable data
It stores multiple values of different types at once
It safely represents a value that may or may not exist
It optimizes memory allocation for large structures only
explanation: Option<T> represents an optional value (Some or None), eliminating null pointer errors at compile time.

text: What is a phantom type in languages with advanced type systems?
options: A type that cannot be instantiated under any circumstances
A type that stores data in an encrypted format always
A type that automatically converts between other data types
A type existing only at compile time using no runtime data
explanation: Phantom types are type parameters not used at runtime, providing compile-time safety guarantees.

text: A C program assigns a negative signed integer to an unsigned variable. What is the likely outcome?
options: The runtime throws an overflow exception and terminates
The program will refuse to compile due to a type mismatch
The unsigned variable automatically stores zero as default
The value becomes a large positive number via reinterpretation
explanation: Assigning a negative signed value to unsigned reinterprets the bits as a large positive number.

text: What problem do algebraic data types (ADTs) solve in functional programming?
options: They eliminate the need for functions in functional code
They provide automatic serialization to network formats
They optimize memory by compressing data automatically
They model complex data as combinations of sum and product
explanation: ADTs combine sum types and product types to precisely model complex data structures.

text: Why does JavaScript treat typeof null as object instead of null?
options: Because null and objects share the same memory structure
Because null is implemented as an empty object internally
Because of a legacy bug in the original JS implementation
Because the spec intentionally groups null with objects
explanation: typeof null returning object is a well-known bug from the original JavaScript implementation.

text: What is the difference between structural typing and nominal typing?
options: Structural checks type names; nominal checks type shape
Nominal typing does not support inheritance between types
Structural checks type shape; nominal checks type names
Structural typing is slower than nominal in every case
explanation: Structural typing checks type shape (structure); nominal typing requires explicit type name matching.

text: What does the modulus operator (%) return?
options: The quotient of division
The remainder of division
The absolute difference
The product of two values
explanation: The modulus operator returns the remainder after integer division.

text: Which operator is used for logical AND in most languages?
explanation: The && operator performs logical AND, returning true only if both operands are true.

text: What does the increment operator (++) do?
options: Multiplies a value by two
Divides a value by one
Decreases a value by two
Increases a value by one
explanation: The increment operator (++) increases a variable's value by one.

text: What is the assignment operator in most programming languages?
explanation: The single equals sign (=) assigns a value to a variable.

text: Which operator checks if two values are NOT equal?
explanation: The != operator checks whether two values are not equal.

text: What does the logical OR operator (||) return?
options: True if at least one operand is true
False only when first operand is false
The sum of two Boolean operand values
True only if both operands are true
explanation: The logical OR returns true if at least one operand evaluates to true.

text: What type of operator is the greater-than sign (>)?
options: Comparison
Logical
Assignment
Arithmetic
explanation: The greater-than sign is a comparison (relational) operator.

text: What does the NOT operator (!) do to a Boolean value?
options: Doubles the value
Deletes the value
Inverts the value
Converts to string
explanation: The NOT operator inverts a Boolean: true becomes false and false becomes true.

text: Which symbol represents the multiplication operator?
options: asterisk
forward slash
minus sign
plus sign
explanation: The asterisk (*) is the multiplication operator in most programming languages.

text: What is the result of 10 / 3 in integer division?
explanation: In integer division, 10 / 3 equals 3 with the decimal portion truncated.

text: What is the difference between prefix and postfix increment?
options: Prefix decrements and postfix increments the variable
Prefix returns value after increment; postfix returns before
Prefix works on integers only; postfix works on floats
Prefix is faster and postfix is slower in all languages
explanation: Prefix (++x) increments then returns; postfix (x++) returns then increments.

text: What does the ternary operator (?:) do?
options: It performs three separate arithmetic operations
It provides a shorthand for if-else expression
It creates three variables from one declaration
It loops through a collection exactly three times
explanation: The ternary operator is a shorthand for a simple if-else conditional expression.

text: What does the bitwise left shift operator (<<) do?
options: Moves bits left, effectively multiplying by powers of two
Rotates bits in circular pattern within byte boundaries
Moves bits right, effectively dividing by powers of two
Compares two values bit by bit and returns differences
explanation: Left shift moves bits left by specified amount, equivalent to multiplying by 2^n.

text: What is short-circuit evaluation in logical expressions?
options: It skips the second operand when the result is determined
It converts logical expressions into arithmetic for speed
It evaluates all operands regardless of intermediate results
It caches the results of all Boolean operations for reuse
explanation: Short-circuit evaluation skips the second operand when the first determines the result.

text: What is operator overloading in object-oriented programming?
options: Increasing operator precedence beyond default values
Combining multiple operators into one new operator
Defining custom behavior for operators with objects
Using more operators than necessary in expressions
explanation: Operator overloading defines custom behavior for standard operators with user-defined types.

text: What does the null coalescing operator (??) do?
options: It checks if a value is undefined and throws error
It converts null values to empty strings always
It prevents null values from being assigned anywhere
It returns the right operand when left is null
explanation: The null coalescing operator returns the right-hand operand when the left is null or undefined.

text: How does bitwise XOR (^) differ from bitwise OR (|)?
options: XOR returns one only when bits differ; OR either is one
XOR returns one when both bits are one; OR returns zero
XOR works on signed integers only; OR on unsigned ones
XOR does logical operations; OR does arithmetic only
explanation: XOR returns 1 when bits differ; OR returns 1 when either or both bits are 1.

text: What is the purpose of the instanceof operator in Java?
options: It creates a new instance of a class from constructor
It deletes an instance and frees allocated memory
It counts how many instances of a class exist now
It checks if an object is instance of a specific class
explanation: instanceof checks whether an object is an instance of a particular class or interface.

text: What does the spaceship operator (<=>) return?
options: Negative, zero, or positive based on comparison
The average of two numeric values as float result
A new object merging properties from two objects
A Boolean true or false based on strict equality
explanation: The spaceship operator returns -1, 0, or 1 based on less than, equal, or greater than.

text: What is the difference between == and === in JavaScript?
options: Both perform the same comparison with no difference
=== checks value only; == checks value and type
== checks value only; === checks value and type
== is for numbers only; === for strings and objects
explanation: == does loose equality with coercion; === does strict equality checking value and type.

text: A developer uses bitwise AND to check if a number is odd. Which expression correctly identifies odd numbers?
options: (n & 2) == 1 returns true for odd numbers in binary
(n & 1) == 0 returns true because odd clears last bit
(n & 0) == 1 returns true for odd numbers correctly
(n & 1) == 1 returns true because odd last bit is set
explanation: n & 1 isolates the least significant bit; odd numbers have this bit set to 1.

text: In C++, what potential issue arises from overloading the comma operator?
options: It may break the guaranteed left-to-right evaluation
It causes compilation errors in all template-based code
The comma operator cannot be overloaded in C++ standard
It automatically converts all expressions to void type
explanation: Overloading the comma operator can break the built-in sequencing guarantee.

text: What does the unsigned right shift (>>>) do differently from signed right shift (>>)?
options: It shifts bits right and preserves the sign bit in position
It rotates bits right wrapping them around to the left side
It shifts bits left instead of right despite arrow direction
It shifts bits right and fills with zeros regardless of sign
explanation: Unsigned right shift always fills leftmost bits with zeros; signed preserves the sign bit.

text: A Python developer writes 'a is b' vs 'a == b'. When do these give different results?
options: When a and b reference the exact same object in memory
When a and b are the same integer literal used repeatedly
When a and b have equal values but are different objects
When both a and b are None compared in an if statement
explanation: 'is' checks identity (same object); '==' checks equality (same value). Equal values in different objects differ.

text: What is the result of applying De Morgan's law to !(A && B)?
options: (!A || !B) distributes the negation and flips the operator
(!A && !B) negation of each operand combined with AND
(!A && B) negates only the first operand in expression
(A || B) simply removes the negation from the expression
explanation: De Morgan's law: !(A && B) is equivalent to (!A || !B).

text: Why should developers avoid relying on operator precedence in complex expressions?
options: Because parentheses are not allowed with operator precedence
Because complex precedence reduces readability and causes bugs
Because operator precedence varies between every language
Because operator precedence only applies to arithmetic ops
explanation: Complex precedence rules are hard to remember; explicit parentheses improve clarity.

text: What is the purpose of the pipe operator (|>) in functional languages like Elixir?
options: It passes the result of one expression as input to next
It redirects standard output to a file on the system
It creates a new parallel process for each function call
It performs bitwise OR operations on integer operands
explanation: The pipe operator passes the left result as the first argument to the right function.

text: In JavaScript, what is the result of {} + [] when evaluated as a statement?
options: A type error because objects and arrays cannot be added
The string '{}[]' because both operands convert to string
An empty object combined with empty array resulting in NaN
The number 0 because {} is empty block and +[] coerces to 0
explanation: {} is interpreted as empty block, then +[] is unary plus on empty array coercing to 0.

text: What advantage does the walrus operator (:=) provide in Python 3.8+?
options: It performs parallel assignment of multiple variables at once
It creates constants that cannot be reassigned after init
It defines default parameter values for function declarations
It assigns a value and returns it within a single expression
explanation: The walrus operator assigns and returns a value within expressions like conditionals.

text: How does operator precedence interact with associativity when precedence is equal?
options: Associativity determines evaluation order for same precedence
The compiler randomly selects which operator to evaluate first
Operators with equal precedence always evaluate left to right
Operators with equal precedence cannot appear in one expression
explanation: When operators share precedence, associativity (left-to-right or right-to-left) determines order.

text: What does a while loop do in programming?
options: Executes code a fixed number of times
Skips code blocks based on values
Repeats code while condition remains true
Runs code exactly once then stops
explanation: A while loop repeatedly executes code as long as the condition evaluates to true.

text: What is the purpose of the break statement?
options: It restarts the loop from beginning
It pauses execution for specified time
It skips to the next function in code
It exits the current loop or switch
explanation: The break statement immediately exits the innermost loop or switch statement.

text: Which control structure selects between multiple code paths?
options: for loop
while loop
if-else statement
do-while loop
explanation: An if-else statement selects code paths based on whether a condition is true or false.

text: What does the continue statement do in a loop?
options: Ends the loop entirely and exits immediately
Skips the rest of current iteration only
Restarts the loop counter to initial value
Pauses the loop until user provides input
explanation: The continue statement skips remaining code in the current iteration and moves to next.

text: What is a do-while loop?
options: A loop needing no ending condition at all
A loop that checks condition after executing
A loop that runs exactly twice every time
A loop that might never execute its body
explanation: A do-while loop executes the body first then checks the condition, guaranteeing one execution.

text: What does a switch statement do?
options: Switches between two different program files
Transfers control to another function call
Toggles a Boolean variable between states
Compares a value against multiple case labels
explanation: A switch statement compares a value against multiple case labels and executes the match.

text: What is an infinite loop?
options: A loop whose condition never becomes false
A loop that processes infinite data items
A loop iterating exactly one million times
A loop that runs fast but eventually stops
explanation: An infinite loop is one whose termination condition is never met, running indefinitely.

text: What is the purpose of the else clause?
options: It always executes regardless of condition
It executes when the if condition is false
It executes when the if condition is true
It ends the program after the if statement
explanation: The else clause executes when the associated if condition evaluates to false.

text: What are the three parts of a standard for loop?
options: Try, catch, and finally block sections
Input, process, and output statements
Initialization, condition, and update
Declaration, assignment, and return
explanation: A standard for loop contains initialization, condition check, and update expression.

text: What is nesting in control structures?
options: Converting loops into conditional checks
Placing one structure inside another one
Removing unused code from the program
Connecting two programs externally together
explanation: Nesting means placing one control structure inside another control structure.

text: What is the advantage of a for-each loop over a traditional for loop?
options: For-each loops can modify collection size in iteration
For-each loops work only with arrays not other types
For-each loops simplify iteration by abstracting index
For-each loops execute faster than for loops always
explanation: For-each loops abstract away index management, reducing boilerplate and off-by-one errors.

text: What is a sentinel value in loop control?
options: A special value that signals the loop should stop
A flag tracking whether the loop has been started
A variable counting the number of loop iterations
A constant defining maximum loop iteration count
explanation: A sentinel value signals the end of input or the termination condition for a loop.

text: What is fall-through behavior in a switch statement?
options: Execution continues into next case when break missing
The switch exits immediately after matching first case
The switch skips all cases and goes to default
The default case always executes before other cases
explanation: Fall-through occurs when break is missing, causing execution to continue into subsequent cases.

text: What is tail recursion and why is it important?
options: A technique to reverse order of recursive calls only
Recursion at the end of an array for faster access
A recursive call as last action enabling optimization
Recursion that always uses the tail element of list
explanation: Tail recursion places the recursive call last, allowing compilers to optimize into a loop.

text: When should a developer use a do-while loop instead of while?
options: When the loop condition depends on hardware signals
When the loop needs to run a predetermined count
When the loop should never execute if condition false
When the loop body must execute at least one time
explanation: A do-while loop is used when the body must execute at least once before condition check.

text: What is the purpose of labeled break statements in Java?
options: They exit a specific outer loop from nested loops
They add descriptive labels for code documentation
They create named variables within loop block scope
They mark loops for automatic parallel JVM execution
explanation: Labeled break allows exiting a specific outer loop from within nested loops.

text: What is a guard clause in programming?
options: A security check validating user authentication
A try-catch block placed around every function call
A mutex lock preventing concurrent shared data access
An early return handling edge cases at function start
explanation: A guard clause is an early return that handles edge cases at the start of a function.

text: How does pattern matching differ from a traditional switch?
options: Pattern matching is slower and less efficient than switch
Pattern matching can destructure and match complex shapes
Pattern matching cannot have a default case for unmatched
Pattern matching only works with integer and string values
explanation: Pattern matching destructures complex data types and matches structural patterns beyond simple values.

text: What is the risk of deeply nested control structures?
options: They cause undefined behavior in all languages used
They always cause the program to run out of memory
They reduce readability and increase complexity greatly
They prevent the compiler from generating optimized code
explanation: Deep nesting reduces readability and increases cognitive load and likelihood of bugs.

text: What is a loop invariant and why is it useful?
options: A special loop type that cannot be modified in execution
An optimization removing unnecessary variables from loop
A condition true before and after each loop iteration
A constant declared outside the loop that never changes
explanation: A loop invariant holds true before and after each iteration, useful for proving correctness.

text: A function uses recursion with no base case. What will happen during execution?
options: The compiler automatically adds a base case to stop
The call stack overflows causing a stack overflow error
The function returns null after one recursive call
The function loops exactly once then exits normally
explanation: Without a base case, recursion continues indefinitely until stack overflow occurs.

text: In a switch with intentional fall-through, what is the best practice for maintainability?
options: Convert the entire switch to unrelated if statements
Remove all break statements for code consistency
Add explicit comments documenting intentional fall-through
Use goto statements instead of fall-through for clarity
explanation: Documenting intentional fall-through with comments prevents confusion about missing breaks.

text: A developer replaces nested if-else with a strategy pattern. What is the main benefit?
options: Memory usage is reduced because fewer objects are created
The code executes faster because polymorphism is quicker
The number of classes in the codebase decreases greatly
Each strategy is encapsulated making code more extensible
explanation: The strategy pattern encapsulates each behavior, making it easy to add new strategies.

text: What is the time complexity of a triply nested loop where each iterates n times?
options: O(n cubed) because each nesting multiplies by factor n
O(n) because only the innermost loop affects complexity
O(n squared) because the outer loop does not count
O(log n) because nested loops divide problem recursively
explanation: Three nested loops each iterating n times results in O(n^3) time complexity.

text: How does continuation-passing style (CPS) change program control flow?
options: It uses goto statements to jump between code sections
It eliminates all function calls using only loops instead
It converts recursive functions into iterative ones always
It passes a callback representing the next step to each
explanation: CPS transforms functions to take a continuation callback, making control flow explicit.

text: A loop uses floating-point comparison as its termination condition. Why is this risky?
options: The loop will always terminate early before full completion
Floating-point loops always execute one extra iteration
Floating-point numbers cannot be used in loop conditions
Precision errors may prevent termination condition triggering
explanation: Floating-point precision errors can prevent exact equality, potentially causing infinite loops.

text: What is the advantage of iterators over index-based loops for data structures?
options: Iterators use less memory than index-based loops in all cases
Iterators always process elements faster than index access
Iterators prevent any modifications to underlying collection
Iterators provide a uniform interface across data structures
explanation: Iterators provide uniform traversal across different data structures without exposing internals.

text: What problem does the loop-and-a-half pattern solve?
options: It creates loops executing exactly one and a half times
It converts while loops into for loops for performance
It handles loops needing to check condition in middle
It optimizes loops to run at half the time of regular
explanation: The loop-and-a-half pattern handles exit conditions naturally in the middle of the loop body.

text: How do higher-order functions like map and filter replace loops in functional programming?
options: They convert loops into goto statements for faster speed
They apply transformations declaratively without iteration
They eliminate the need for conditional logic in programs
They only work with numeric data not other types at all
explanation: Higher-order functions express data transformations declaratively, replacing explicit iteration.

text: Why might a compiler transform a recursive function into an iterative loop?
options: Because iteration uses more memory improving caching
Because recursive functions always produce wrong output
Because iteration avoids stack overhead and overflow
Because recursion is not supported by modern CPUs
explanation: Converting recursion to iteration eliminates call overhead and prevents potential stack overflow.

text: What is a function in programming?
options: A variable that holds numeric values
A data type that stores text values
A reusable block of code with a name
A control structure for looping code
explanation: A function is a named, reusable block of code that performs a specific task.

text: What is a parameter in a function?
options: A variable that receives input to function
The name given to the function itself
The data type of the function output
The return value of the function call
explanation: A parameter is a variable in the function definition that receives input values.

text: What does the return statement do?
options: It repeats the function from the start
It sends a value back to the caller
It declares a new variable in the scope
It prints output to the screen display
explanation: The return statement exits the function and sends a value back to the caller.

text: What is a function call?
options: Defining a new function in the program
Renaming a function to a new identifier
Executing a function by using its name
Deleting a function from the program
explanation: A function call executes the function by invoking it with its name and arguments.

text: What is the difference between an argument and a parameter?
options: Arguments are types; parameters are values only
They are the same thing with no difference
Arguments are in definition; parameters in calls
Parameters are in definition; arguments in calls
explanation: Parameters are in the function definition; arguments are the actual values passed in calls.

text: What is a void function?
options: A function that cannot be called by others
A function that takes no parameters at all
A function that returns no value to caller
A function that runs only one time ever
explanation: A void function performs actions but does not return any value to the caller.

text: What does the term function signature mean?
options: The comments written above function body
The digital signature used to secure code
The total number of lines in the function
The name, parameters, and return type info
explanation: A function signature includes the function name, parameter types, and return type.

text: What is a built-in function?
options: A function that cannot be modified at all
A function that runs before main function
A function provided by the language itself
A function written by the programmer only
explanation: A built-in function is pre-defined by the programming language or its standard library.

text: What is a recursive function?
options: A function that takes no arguments at all
A function that runs in parallel threads
A function that calls itself to solve tasks
A function that never returns any values
explanation: A recursive function calls itself to break a problem into smaller sub-problems.

text: What is the scope of a local variable inside a function?
options: It is shared across all program functions
It is accessible from anywhere in program
It persists after the function has ended
It exists only within that function body
explanation: A local variable exists only within the function where it is declared.

text: What is the difference between pass-by-value and pass-by-reference?
options: Pass-by-value is faster than pass-by-reference in all cases
Pass-by-value sends a copy; reference sends original location
Both methods behave identically with no differences at all
Pass-by-reference creates a copy of the argument each time
explanation: Pass-by-value sends a copy of the argument; pass-by-reference sends the original's address.

text: What is a lambda function?
options: A small anonymous function defined inline in the code
A function that runs automatically at program startup
A function that cannot accept any parameters at all
A function that can only return Boolean values always
explanation: A lambda is a small anonymous function defined inline, often used for short operations.

text: What is function overloading?
options: Defining multiple functions with same name different params
Replacing an existing function with a new one entirely
Calling a function more times than it can handle
Loading a function into memory before it is needed
explanation: Function overloading defines multiple functions with the same name but different parameter lists.

text: What is a callback function?
options: A function that undoes the actions of another function
A function that calls back the previous function always
A function passed as an argument to another function
A function that only executes during error handling
explanation: A callback is a function passed as an argument to be called at a later point in execution.

text: What is a closure in programming?
options: A function that cannot be called more than once
A function that retains access to its enclosing scope
A function that closes the program when called
A function that blocks all other functions from running
explanation: A closure is a function that retains access to variables from its enclosing scope even after that scope exits.

text: What is a default parameter in a function?
options: A parameter with a preset value used if no arg given
A parameter that overrides all other parameter values
A parameter that must always be provided by caller
A parameter that is automatically deleted after use
explanation: A default parameter has a preset value used when the caller does not provide an argument.

text: What is a higher-order function?
options: A function that requires administrator access to run
A function that takes or returns other functions
A function with more than ten parameters defined
A function that runs with higher priority than others
explanation: A higher-order function takes functions as arguments or returns functions as results.

text: What is the purpose of the main function in C or Java?
options: It handles all error messages in the entire program
It defines all variables used throughout the program
It serves as the entry point for program execution
It manages memory allocation for all data structures
explanation: The main function is the entry point where program execution begins in C and Java.

text: What is a variadic function?
options: A function that can only be called once in program
A function that accepts a variable number of args
A function that changes its name during runtime
A function that returns different types each time
explanation: A variadic function accepts a variable number of arguments, like printf in C.

text: What is a pure function in functional programming?
options: A function written in a pure functional language only
A function that only works with primitive data types
A function with no side effects and deterministic output
A function that has been optimized for maximum speed
explanation: A pure function always returns the same output for given inputs and produces no side effects.

text: A recursive function for Fibonacci has exponential time complexity. How can this be optimized?
options: By converting the function parameters to global variables
By using memoization to cache previously computed values
By adding more recursive calls to speed up computation
By removing the base case to reduce function calls
explanation: Memoization caches results of previous calls, reducing exponential time to linear.

text: What is the Y combinator and what problem does it solve in lambda calculus?
options: It validates the types of arguments passed to functions
It combines two functions into a single optimized one
It enables recursion in languages without named functions
It converts iterative loops into recursive function calls
explanation: The Y combinator enables recursion in anonymous functions within lambda calculus.

text: A function modifies a global variable as a side effect. Why is this considered poor practice?
options: Because side effects always cause the program to crash immediately
Because side effects make functions unpredictable and harder to test
Because global variables use too much memory in programs
Because programming languages prohibit modifying global variables
explanation: Side effects make functions harder to reason about, test, and debug due to hidden state changes.

text: What is the difference between currying and partial application?
options: Currying combines multiple functions; partial splits them into pieces
Currying splits a function into single-arg chain; partial fixes some args
Currying and partial application are exactly the same technique always
Currying only works with two arguments; partial works with any count
explanation: Currying transforms a multi-arg function into a chain of single-arg functions; partial application fixes some arguments.

text: How does tail call optimization (TCO) prevent stack overflow in recursive functions?
options: It moves recursive calls to a separate memory heap location
It converts all recursive calls into asynchronous operations
It limits the depth of recursion to a fixed maximum count
It reuses the current stack frame for the next recursive call
explanation: TCO reuses the current stack frame for tail-position recursive calls, preventing stack growth.

text: What is a thunk in the context of lazy evaluation?
options: A deferred computation wrapped in a zero-argument function
A data structure that stores function parameter information
A compiled machine code fragment stored in memory cache
An error handler that catches exceptions in lazy code blocks
explanation: A thunk is a zero-argument function wrapping a deferred computation, evaluated only when needed.

text: A developer uses function composition f(g(x)). What is the main benefit of this approach?
options: It builds complex operations from simple reusable functions
It eliminates the need for any error handling in code
It guarantees the composed functions execute in parallel
It ensures both functions always return the same data type
explanation: Function composition builds complex transformations by chaining simple, reusable functions together.

text: What is a functor in functional programming?
options: An object that stores multiple functions in an internal array
A function that can only be called once during execution
A special variable that holds a reference to a function pointer
A type that can be mapped over with a structure-preserving function
explanation: A functor is a type implementing a map operation that applies a function while preserving structure.

text: Why are first-class functions important for functional programming paradigms?
options: They enable functions to be passed, returned, and assigned like data
They prevent functions from having any parameters or return values
They allow functions to be stored in databases for persistence
They make all functions execute faster than in other paradigms
explanation: First-class functions can be passed as arguments, returned, and assigned to variables, enabling FP patterns.

text: What problem does the trampolining technique solve for recursive functions?
options: It automatically parallelizes recursive calls across CPU cores
It prevents stack overflow by converting recursion to loop steps
It speeds up recursive calculations by using hardware acceleration
It eliminates the need for base cases in recursive algorithms
explanation: Trampolining converts recursive calls into a loop of thunks, preventing stack overflow without TCO support.

text: What does standard input (stdin) refer to?
options: The file system storage on disk
The network connection to internet
The default input source like keyboard
The screen display output device
explanation: Standard input is the default source of input data, typically the keyboard.

text: What does standard output (stdout) refer to?
options: The printer attached to the computer
The default output destination like screen
The database server for data storage
The keyboard input device
explanation: Standard output is the default destination for output data, typically the screen.

text: What is a file in the context of I/O?
options: A temporary variable in program memory
A named collection of data stored on disk
A network connection between two machines
A function that handles user inputs
explanation: A file is a named collection of data stored persistently on a storage device.

text: What does reading from a file mean?
options: Renaming the file to a new file name
Retrieving data from a file into program
Deleting the file from the file system
Writing new data to the file on disk
explanation: Reading from a file means retrieving stored data from the file into the program's memory.

text: What is the purpose of the print function?
options: To create a new variable in the program
To display output to the screen or console
To save data permanently to a file on disk
To read input from the keyboard device
explanation: The print function displays output to the screen or console for the user to see.

text: What is a file path?
options: The content stored inside a file itself
The location address of a file on system
The date when a file was last modified
The size of a file measured in bytes
explanation: A file path specifies the location of a file in the file system directory structure.

text: What does writing to a file mean?
options: Storing data from program into a file
Deleting all contents of the file only
Moving a file to a different directory
Reading data from the file into memory
explanation: Writing to a file means storing data from the program's memory into a file on disk.

text: What is a console in programming?
options: A database management system for data
A text-based interface for program I/O
A gaming device for entertainment use
A graphical design tool for layouts
explanation: A console is a text-based interface where programs display output and receive input.

text: What does the input function do in Python?
options: It writes data to a file on the disk
It creates a new variable in the code
It displays formatted output on screen
It reads a line of text from the user
explanation: The input function in Python reads a line of text entered by the user from the keyboard.

text: What is the difference between text and binary file modes?
options: Text mode is faster; binary mode is slower always
Text mode is for images; binary mode is for documents
Text mode is read only; binary mode is write only
Text mode handles characters; binary handles raw bytes
explanation: Text mode handles character data with encoding; binary mode handles raw bytes without translation.

text: What is buffered I/O and why is it used?
options: It converts binary data to text format before file output
It compresses files automatically to save disk space used
It stores data in a buffer to reduce system call overhead
It encrypts all data before writing to prevent data loss
explanation: Buffered I/O collects data in a buffer and performs fewer larger system calls for efficiency.

text: What is the purpose of flushing a buffer?
options: To convert buffer contents from binary to text encoding
To clear the buffer and discard all data within it
To force buffered data to be written to its destination
To increase the size of the buffer for more data storage
explanation: Flushing forces all buffered data to be written to the output destination immediately.

text: What is serialization in the context of I/O?
options: Converting objects into a storable or transmittable format
Executing I/O operations in a strict serial sequence
Assigning serial numbers to files for organization
Sorting data in sequential order before processing
explanation: Serialization converts objects into a format suitable for storage or transmission.

text: What is the difference between synchronous and asynchronous I/O?
options: Synchronous blocks until complete; asynchronous does not block
Synchronous only works with network; asynchronous with files
Synchronous is always faster than asynchronous operations
Asynchronous cannot read files only write to them on disk
explanation: Synchronous I/O blocks the program until complete; asynchronous allows continued execution.

text: What is a stream in I/O programming?
options: A sequence of data elements available over time to read
A database connection for querying stored information
A graphical interface for displaying program output data
A fixed-size block of memory for storing data values
explanation: A stream is a sequence of data elements made available over time for reading or writing.

text: What is the purpose of the with statement for file I/O in Python?
options: It prevents other programs from accessing the same file
It converts file contents to uppercase text characters
It creates a new file with specific permissions on disk
It ensures the file is properly closed after operations
explanation: The with statement ensures proper resource cleanup by automatically closing the file.

text: What is CSV format used for?
options: Compressing large files to reduce their disk size
Storing executable program code in text files
