Encrypting sensitive data for secure transmission
Storing tabular data with comma-separated values
explanation: CSV stores tabular data as plain text with values separated by commas.

text: What is the difference between absolute and relative file paths?
options: Absolute paths can only reference files not directories
Absolute starts from root; relative from current directory
Absolute is shorter than relative paths in all cases
Relative paths work across different operating systems only
explanation: Absolute paths start from the root; relative paths start from the current working directory.

text: What is JSON and why is it popular for data interchange?
options: A binary format designed for storing image data on disk
A protocol for encrypting data during network transfer
A programming language for building web applications
A lightweight text format that is easy to read and parse
explanation: JSON is a lightweight, human-readable text format widely used for data interchange.

text: What does the file seek operation do?
options: It creates a backup copy of the file on another disk
It moves the read/write position to a specific location
It searches for a specific string within file content
It locks the file to prevent concurrent access by others
explanation: File seek moves the read/write pointer to a specific position within the file.

text: A program reads a large file line by line instead of loading it all at once. What is the main advantage?
options: It prevents the file from being modified by other processes
Line-by-line reading is always faster than loading at once
It automatically handles character encoding conversion issues
It reduces memory usage by processing one line at a time
explanation: Line-by-line reading keeps memory usage low by only holding one line in memory at a time.

text: What is memory-mapped I/O and when is it beneficial?
options: Allocating extra RAM to speed up sequential file reading
Mapping files into virtual memory for faster random access
Storing all program variables in external files on disk
Converting memory addresses to file paths automatically
explanation: Memory-mapped I/O maps file contents into virtual memory, enabling fast random access patterns.

text: A multi-threaded program writes to the same file simultaneously. What problem can occur?
options: Race conditions cause corrupted or interleaved file data
Each thread creates its own separate copy of the file
The file automatically becomes read-only preventing writes
The operating system duplicates the file for each thread
explanation: Concurrent writes without synchronization cause race conditions, corrupting or interleaving data.

text: What is the difference between blocking and non-blocking I/O in server design?
options: Blocking handles more connections; non-blocking handles fewer
Non-blocking cannot read from network sockets at all ever
Blocking waits for completion; non-blocking returns immediately
Blocking is only for files; non-blocking only for networks
explanation: Blocking I/O waits for completion; non-blocking returns immediately allowing other work.

text: What is the purpose of a write-ahead log (WAL) in database I/O?
options: It logs all read operations for performance monitoring only
It encrypts data before writing to prevent unauthorized access
It compresses write operations to reduce disk space usage
It records changes before applying them for crash recovery
explanation: A WAL records changes before applying them, enabling recovery to a consistent state after crashes.

text: A developer needs to parse a 10GB XML file. Why would a SAX parser be preferred over DOM?
options: DOM parsers cannot handle files containing nested elements
SAX processes XML as a stream without loading it all in memory
SAX is more accurate at parsing XML structure than DOM
SAX automatically validates XML against its schema definition
explanation: SAX parses XML as a stream using minimal memory, while DOM loads the entire document into memory.

text: What is the reactor pattern in asynchronous I/O programming?
options: A technique for converting synchronous code to asynchronous
A single-threaded event loop dispatching I/O events to handlers
A method of buffering all output until program termination
A pattern that creates a new thread for every I/O request
explanation: The reactor pattern uses a single-threaded event loop to demultiplex and dispatch I/O events.

text: How does zero-copy I/O improve performance compared to traditional I/O?
options: It avoids copying data between kernel and user space buffers
It compresses data to zero bytes before transferring it out
It uses zero system calls by directly accessing hardware
It eliminates the need for any disk storage by using RAM only
explanation: Zero-copy avoids unnecessary data copies between kernel and user space, reducing CPU overhead.

text: What is the purpose of the fsync system call?
options: It synchronizes file access between multiple running threads
It converts file system format between different OS types
It synchronizes file timestamps with the system clock value
It ensures file data is physically written to storage device
explanation: fsync ensures that file data and metadata are physically written to the storage device.

text: A program handles both file I/O and network I/O. Why might it use an event-driven architecture?
options: Event-driven guarantees faster disk I/O than any alternative
Event-driven efficiently handles many concurrent I/O sources
Event-driven converts network data to file format automatically
Event-driven eliminates all errors in I/O operations always
explanation: Event-driven architecture efficiently manages many concurrent I/O sources without thread-per-connection overhead.

text: What is string concatenation?
options: Converting string to number
Joining two strings together
Splitting a string into parts
Reversing a string backward
explanation: String concatenation joins two or more strings into a single string.

text: What does the length property of a string return?
options: The number of words inside
The index of last character
The memory size in bytes
The number of characters
explanation: The length property returns the total number of characters in a string.

text: What is a substring?
options: A string used as a variable
A string with no characters
A portion of another string
A string stored in memory
explanation: A substring is a contiguous sequence of characters within another string.

text: What is an empty string?
options: A string that equals null
A string with zero characters
A string containing spaces only
A string holding the digit zero
explanation: An empty string is a string with no characters, represented as double quotes with nothing between.

text: What does converting a string to uppercase mean?
options: Removing all spaces from the string text
Adding exclamation marks after each word
Changing all letters to capital letters
Increasing the font size of the string
explanation: Converting to uppercase changes all lowercase letters to their capital letter equivalents.

text: What is a character index in a string?
options: The total count of characters in string
The numeric position of a character
The ASCII value of the character
The memory address of the character
explanation: A character index is the numeric position of a character within a string, usually starting at 0.

text: What does the trim function do to a string?
options: Converts the string to lowercase only
Removes leading and trailing whitespace
Shortens the string to ten characters
Removes all characters from the string
explanation: The trim function removes whitespace from the beginning and end of a string.

text: What is an escape character in a string?
options: A character that exits the program when found
A character that deletes the previous character
A character that converts text into numeric data
A character starting a special sequence in text
explanation: An escape character starts a special sequence to represent characters like newlines or tabs.

text: What does string comparison do?
options: Counts how many strings exist in program
Joins two strings into one combined string
Converts strings into integer numeric values
Checks if two strings are equal or ordered
explanation: String comparison checks whether two strings are equal or determines their relative ordering.

text: What is the newline character used for?
options: To add a space between two words
To underline the current text line
To delete the current line of text
To move text to the next line down
explanation: The newline character moves the output cursor to the beginning of the next line.

text: What is the difference between mutable and immutable strings?
options: Mutable strings use less memory; immutable use more always
Mutable strings are faster; immutable are always slower
Mutable strings can be changed; immutable cannot after creation
Mutable strings have a fixed length; immutable can grow
explanation: Mutable strings can be modified in place; immutable strings create new strings for any change.

text: What is string interpolation?
options: Converting a string to a different character encoding
Splitting a string by a specific delimiter character
Embedding expressions directly inside a string literal
Comparing two strings character by character position
explanation: String interpolation embeds variable values or expressions directly into a string literal.

text: What is a regular expression used for?
options: Encrypting strings to protect sensitive data values
Converting strings between different data type formats
Defining patterns for searching and matching text
Storing mathematical formulas as text strings
explanation: Regular expressions define text patterns for searching, matching, and manipulating strings.

text: Why is string concatenation in a loop often inefficient?
options: Because each concatenation creates a new string object
Because the loop counter interferes with string length
Because loops cannot process string data type values
Because concatenation requires network access each time
explanation: Each concatenation creates a new string object, causing excessive memory allocation in loops.

text: What is Unicode and why is it important for strings?
options: A standard encoding supporting characters from all languages
A programming language designed for text processing
A compression algorithm that reduces string memory usage
A sorting algorithm designed specifically for text strings
explanation: Unicode is a universal character encoding standard supporting characters from all writing systems.

text: What does the split function do to a string?
options: It converts the string from uppercase to lowercase
It duplicates the string into two identical copies
It removes every other character from the string text
It divides a string into an array using a delimiter
explanation: The split function divides a string into an array of substrings using a specified delimiter.

text: What is the difference between StringBuilder and String in Java?
options: StringBuilder is slower than String for all text operations
StringBuilder is mutable for efficient string modifications
StringBuilder is immutable; String is mutable and changeable
StringBuilder cannot store text only numeric data values
explanation: StringBuilder is mutable, allowing efficient in-place modifications without creating new objects.

text: What is a string pool in Java?
options: A sorting algorithm specifically designed for string arrays
A cache of string literals reused to save memory space
A collection of string utility functions in a library
A data structure for storing strings in sorted order
explanation: The string pool is a cache where Java stores string literals to avoid duplicate object creation.

text: What is the purpose of the replace function on strings?
options: It substitutes occurrences of a pattern with new text
It moves the string to a different memory location
It renames the variable holding the string value
It converts the string from text into binary format
explanation: The replace function substitutes all occurrences of a specified pattern with new text.

text: What is URL encoding for strings?
options: Replacing special characters with percent-encoded values
Compressing URL strings to reduce their total length
Encrypting URL strings to prevent unauthorized access
Converting URLs into string variables in the program
explanation: URL encoding replaces special characters with percent-encoded equivalents for safe URL transmission.

text: A program compares user passwords using == instead of a constant-time comparison. What is the security risk?
options: The program will crash when processing long password strings
The comparison may return incorrect results for all input
The passwords will be stored in plain text in memory always
Timing attacks can reveal password characters one by one
explanation: Non-constant-time comparison leaks password length and characters through timing differences.

text: Why is the KMP algorithm preferred over naive string matching for large texts?
options: KMP requires the pattern to be sorted before searching starts
KMP uses more memory but is simpler to implement correctly
KMP avoids rechecking characters using a failure function table
KMP only works with numeric strings not alphabetic text data
explanation: KMP preprocesses the pattern to build a failure function, avoiding redundant character comparisons.

text: What is the purpose of string interning and when can it cause problems?
options: It translates strings between languages causing encoding issues
It encrypts strings for security but slows down all comparisons
It caches unique strings saving memory but can cause leaks if overused
It compresses strings for storage but prevents substring extraction
explanation: String interning caches unique strings to save memory, but excessive interning can cause memory leaks.

text: How does the Rabin-Karp algorithm use hashing for string matching?
options: It creates a hash table of all possible patterns before searching
It hashes the entire text once and compares with pattern hash
It uses rolling hash to efficiently compare pattern with substrings
It hashes each character individually and sorts them for matching
explanation: Rabin-Karp uses a rolling hash to efficiently compare the pattern hash with text substring hashes.

text: What is a rope data structure and when is it better than a standard string?
options: A rope compresses strings to use minimal memory allocation
A rope converts strings to binary for faster CPU processing
A rope stores strings in encrypted form for better security
A rope is a tree of small strings efficient for large text edits
explanation: A rope is a balanced tree of string fragments, providing efficient insertion, deletion, and concatenation for large texts.

text: What is the difference between UTF-8 and UTF-16 encoding?
options: UTF-8 uses 8 bits per character always; UTF-16 uses 16 always
UTF-8 only supports English; UTF-16 supports all world languages
UTF-8 uses variable 1-4 bytes; UTF-16 uses 2 or 4 byte units
UTF-8 is binary format; UTF-16 is a text-based format only
explanation: UTF-8 uses 1-4 bytes per character; UTF-16 uses 2 or 4 bytes, with different space efficiency tradeoffs.

text: A developer finds that reversing a string containing emoji produces corrupted output. What is the cause?
options: The reversal algorithm has a bug unrelated to character type
Emoji use multi-code-unit sequences broken by naive reversal
Emoji characters are not valid in any programming language
The string library does not support any non-ASCII characters
explanation: Emoji may consist of multiple code units or code points that get split and reordered incorrectly by naive reversal.

text: What is the purpose of normalization forms (NFC, NFD) in Unicode string processing?
options: They ensure equivalent character sequences have canonical form
They convert strings from Unicode to ASCII encoding format
They validate that strings contain only printable characters
They compress Unicode strings to reduce their storage size
explanation: Unicode normalization ensures that equivalent character sequences (like composed vs decomposed) have a consistent canonical form.

text: Why might a trie data structure be preferred for autocomplete over a hash map of strings?
options: Tries automatically sort strings in reverse alphabetical order
Tries use less memory than hash maps for all data sizes
Tries are faster for exact match lookups than hash maps
Tries support efficient prefix-based searching and traversal
explanation: Tries support efficient prefix-based searching, making them ideal for autocomplete where partial matches are needed.

text: What is a homoglyph attack in the context of string security?
options: An attack that overflows string buffers with excess data
An attack that deletes all string data from the database
An attack that encrypts strings making them unreadable
An attack using visually similar characters to deceive users
explanation: Homoglyph attacks use visually similar characters from different scripts to create deceptive strings like fake URLs.

text: What is an array in programming?
options: A collection of elements stored in order
A function that processes numeric data
A single variable that holds one value
A loop that iterates over user input
explanation: An array is an ordered collection of elements, typically of the same type, stored contiguously.

text: What is an array index?
options: The data type of array elements
The position number of an element
The memory size of the full array
The total number of elements in array
explanation: An array index is the numeric position used to access a specific element in the array.

text: What does it mean to traverse an array?
options: To delete all elements from the array
To visit each element of the array once
To sort the array in ascending order
To copy the array to another variable
explanation: Traversing an array means visiting each element exactly once, usually with a loop.

text: What is a two-dimensional array?
options: An array organized in rows and columns
An array that stores only two elements
An array with two different index types
An array that can hold two data types
explanation: A two-dimensional array is organized in rows and columns, like a table or matrix.

text: What is a list in Python?
options: A read-only sequence of characters
A function that returns multiple values
A fixed-size array of integers only
An ordered mutable collection of items
explanation: A Python list is an ordered, mutable collection that can hold items of different types.

text: What does the push operation do on a stack?
options: Searches for an element in the stack
Adds an element to the top of stack
Sorts all elements within the stack
Removes the top element from stack
explanation: Push adds a new element to the top of the stack data structure.

text: What is the difference between a stack and a queue?
options: Both use the same ordering of elements
Stack is LIFO; queue is FIFO ordering
Stack stores numbers; queue stores text
Stack is FIFO; queue is LIFO ordering
explanation: A stack uses LIFO (Last In First Out); a queue uses FIFO (First In First Out) ordering.

text: What is the purpose of a dictionary or map?
options: To store data as key-value pair entries
To count the number of elements in list
To sort data in alphabetical order always
To convert arrays into string format
explanation: A dictionary/map stores data as key-value pairs for efficient lookup by key.

text: What happens when you access an array out of bounds?
options: The array automatically grows in size
An error or undefined behavior occurs
The array resets to its initial state
The first element is returned instead
explanation: Accessing an array out of bounds causes an error or undefined behavior depending on the language.

text: What is a set in programming?
options: A collection of unique elements only
A sorted sequence of text characters
A fixed-size array of numeric values
An ordered list allowing duplicate values
explanation: A set is an unordered collection that contains only unique elements with no duplicates.

text: What is the time complexity of accessing an element by index in an array?
options: O(1) because arrays provide direct index-based access
O(log n) because arrays use binary search internally
O(n squared) because index calculation is quadratic
O(n) because it must search through all elements
explanation: Array index access is O(1) because elements are stored contiguously with direct offset calculation.

text: What is a linked list and how does it differ from an array?
options: A linked list cannot store more than one hundred elements
A linked list is faster for index access than an array
A linked list uses nodes with pointers allowing dynamic size
A linked list stores elements contiguously like an array does
explanation: A linked list uses nodes connected by pointers, allowing dynamic size but losing direct index access.

text: What is a hash map collision and how is it typically resolved?
options: When two values are identical in different hash map entries
When the hash map runs out of memory during insertion
When the hash function produces negative index values
When two keys map to the same bucket needing chain or probe
explanation: A collision occurs when two keys hash to the same bucket, resolved by chaining or open addressing.

text: What is the difference between an ArrayList and a LinkedList in Java?
options: ArrayList stores only strings; LinkedList stores only numbers
ArrayList uses nodes with pointers; LinkedList uses an array
ArrayList provides fast index access; LinkedList fast insertion
ArrayList is immutable; LinkedList is mutable and changeable
explanation: ArrayList provides O(1) index access; LinkedList provides O(1) insertion/deletion at known positions.

text: What is a priority queue?
options: A queue that can only store integer data type elements
A queue where elements are processed in insertion order always
A queue that automatically removes duplicate element entries
A queue where elements are processed based on priority value
explanation: A priority queue processes elements based on priority rather than insertion order.

text: What is the purpose of the Collections.sort() method in Java?
options: It sorts the elements of a list in their natural ordering
It removes all duplicate elements from the given collection
It converts a collection into an array of primitive values
It reverses the order of elements in a collection object
explanation: Collections.sort() sorts list elements in natural or custom comparator-defined order.

text: What is a deque (double-ended queue)?
options: A queue that only allows deletion not insertion operations
A queue allowing insertion and removal at both front and back
A queue that stores exactly two elements at any given time
A queue that automatically sorts elements upon insertion
explanation: A deque allows insertion and removal at both the front and back ends.

text: What is the advantage of a balanced binary search tree over a sorted array?
options: Balanced BST uses less memory than a sorted array always
Balanced BST has faster element access by index position
Balanced BST provides efficient insertion and deletion both
Balanced BST does not require any comparison operations
explanation: A balanced BST maintains O(log n) insertion and deletion while a sorted array requires O(n) for these.

text: What is a sparse array?
options: An array where most elements are default or zero values
An array that stores elements in non-contiguous memory
An array that only contains string type data elements
An array with a fixed small number of total elements
explanation: A sparse array has most elements at default/zero values, often stored efficiently with special structures.

text: What is the difference between a shallow copy and a deep copy of a collection?
options: Deep copies share nested references; shallow copies clone all
Shallow copies are slower to create than deep copies always
Shallow copies share nested references; deep copies clone all
Both shallow and deep copies produce identical independent results
explanation: Shallow copy shares nested object references; deep copy recursively clones all nested objects.

text: A developer uses a HashMap with mutable objects as keys. What problem can occur?
options: The map automatically prevents mutation of all key objects
The map will throw an error when any key is first inserted
Mutable keys cause the map to sort entries in reverse order
Mutating keys changes their hash, making entries unretrievable
explanation: Mutating key objects changes their hash code, making existing entries impossible to find.

text: What is the amortized time complexity of adding an element to a dynamic array (ArrayList)?
options: O(log n) because dynamic arrays use binary search for insert
O(n squared) because each insertion triggers a full array sort
O(n) because every insertion requires copying all elements
O(1) amortized because resizing cost is spread over operations
explanation: Dynamic arrays double capacity when full; the O(n) resize cost amortized over n insertions gives O(1).

text: What is a bloom filter and what tradeoff does it make?
options: A compression technique that trades accuracy for file size
A probabilistic structure allowing false positives not negatives
A sorting algorithm that trades stability for speed
A search algorithm trading memory for faster query execution
explanation: A bloom filter is a space-efficient probabilistic structure that may give false positives but never false negatives.

text: Why is a red-black tree preferred over a simple BST for implementing TreeMap?
options: Red-black trees guarantee O(log n) by maintaining balance always
Red-black trees use less memory than simple BSTs in all cases
Red-black trees do not require any comparison between elements
Simple BSTs cannot store key-value pairs only single values
explanation: Red-black trees guarantee O(log n) operations by maintaining balance, preventing worst-case O(n) degradation.

text: What is the difference between a B-tree and a binary search tree?
options: B-trees cannot be balanced while binary search trees can be
B-trees have multiple children per node optimized for disk I/O
B-trees store only two children per node like binary trees
B-trees only work with string keys not with numeric values
explanation: B-trees allow multiple keys and children per node, minimizing disk reads for database and filesystem use.

text: A concurrent program uses a regular HashMap and encounters data corruption. What is the fix?
options: Use ConcurrentHashMap which provides thread-safe operations
Use a larger initial capacity for the HashMap allocation
Add more entries to the HashMap to prevent hash collisions
Convert the HashMap to a TreeMap for sorted data access
explanation: ConcurrentHashMap provides thread-safe operations using fine-grained locking or lock-free techniques.

text: What is a skip list and what advantage does it offer?
options: A list that skips every other element for faster traversal
A list that automatically removes duplicate elements on insert
A probabilistic structure providing O(log n) search in linked list
A list optimized for storing very large string values only
explanation: A skip list uses multiple levels of linked lists to achieve O(log n) average search, insert, and delete.

text: What is the difference between a weak reference map and a regular map?
options: Weak maps allow garbage collection of keys not strongly referenced
Weak maps are faster for lookup operations than regular maps
Weak maps cannot store null values while regular maps can
Weak maps store values with less precision than regular maps
explanation: Weak reference maps allow keys to be garbage collected when no strong references exist, preventing memory leaks.

text: What is cache-oblivious algorithm design and how does it relate to array processing?
options: It disables caching to ensure consistent performance results
It ignores CPU cache entirely for simpler algorithm design
It fills the CPU cache completely before processing any data
It optimizes memory access patterns without knowing cache size
explanation: Cache-oblivious algorithms optimize memory access patterns without knowing cache size, improving array processing performance.

text: Why does a hash table with a poor hash function degrade to O(n) lookup time?
options: Because many collisions cluster entries into the same bucket
Because poor hash functions produce negative index values
Because poor hash functions prevent the table from resizing
Because the hash table automatically switches to linear search
explanation: A poor hash function causes many collisions, clustering entries in few buckets and degrading to O(n) linear search.

text: What is a class in object-oriented programming?
options: A function that processes data values
A loop structure for iteration
A variable storing numeric data
A blueprint for creating objects
explanation: A class is a blueprint or template that defines the properties and behaviors of objects.

text: What is an object in programming?
options: A type of loop control structure
A keyword reserved by the language
An instance of a class in memory
A comment in the source code text
explanation: An object is a specific instance of a class with its own state and behavior.

text: What is inheritance in OOP?
options: Copying code from one file to another
A class acquiring properties from parent
Renaming a class to a new identifier
Deleting unused classes from program
explanation: Inheritance allows a child class to acquire properties and methods from a parent class.

text: What is encapsulation?
options: Running multiple objects at the same time
Converting objects into primitive data type values
Splitting a class into multiple smaller classes
Bundling data and methods while restricting access
explanation: Encapsulation bundles data and methods together while restricting direct access to internal state.

text: What is a constructor in a class?
options: A function that converts objects to strings
A variable that stores the class name text
A special method called when object is created
A method that deletes the object from memory
explanation: A constructor is a special method automatically called when a new object is created.

text: What is a method in OOP?
options: A comment describing the class purpose
A variable declared inside a class body
A function defined within a class body
A data type used only in class files
explanation: A method is a function defined within a class that defines the behavior of objects.

text: What does the keyword 'this' refer to in a class?
options: The first parameter of any method
The current object instance itself
The main method of the application
The parent class of the current class
explanation: The 'this' keyword refers to the current object instance within a class method.

text: What is polymorphism in OOP?
options: Creating multiple constructors in a class
Deleting objects after they are used once
Storing different data types in one array
Objects taking many forms via shared interface
explanation: Polymorphism allows objects of different classes to be treated through a shared interface.

text: What is an attribute or property of a class?
options: A variable that holds data within a class
A method that performs calculations on data
A class that inherits from another class
A keyword that defines the class access
explanation: An attribute or property is a variable within a class that holds data about the object state.

text: What is an interface in OOP?
options: A tool for debugging object-oriented programs
A type of variable used in class constructors
A contract specifying methods a class must implement
A graphical user interface for the application
explanation: An interface defines a contract of methods that implementing classes must provide.

text: What is the difference between an abstract class and an interface?
options: Abstract classes can have implementation; interfaces cannot always
Abstract classes cannot have fields; interfaces can have fields
Interfaces support multiple inheritance; abstract classes do not
Abstract classes and interfaces are functionally identical always
explanation: Abstract classes can provide partial implementation; interfaces traditionally define only method signatures.

text: What is method overriding in OOP?
options: Creating a method with a different number of parameters
Deleting a parent method from the child class entirely
Defining a method with the same name in a child class
Calling a method from an unrelated class in the program
explanation: Method overriding redefines a parent class method in a child class with the same signature.

text: What is composition in OOP and how does it differ from inheritance?
options: Composition is slower than inheritance in all execution scenarios
Composition and inheritance produce identical code structure always
Composition cannot reuse code from other classes unlike inheritance
Composition uses has-a relationship; inheritance uses is-a relation
explanation: Composition models has-a relationships by containing objects; inheritance models is-a relationships.

text: What is the purpose of access modifiers like public, private, protected?
options: They determine the execution speed of methods in the class
They control the visibility and accessibility of class members
They define the order in which methods are called in class
They specify the data type that a class member can store
explanation: Access modifiers control which parts of the code can access specific class members.

text: What is the Liskov Substitution Principle?
options: Objects should have only one reason to change at any time
Subtypes must be substitutable for their base types safely
A class should be open for extension closed for modification
Classes should depend on abstractions not concrete classes
explanation: LSP states that objects of a subtype should be usable wherever the base type is expected.

text: What is a static method in a class?
options: A method that cannot accept any parameters at all
A method that cannot be called from outside the class
A method belonging to the class itself not instances
A method that runs automatically when program starts
explanation: A static method belongs to the class rather than instances and can be called without an object.

text: What is the diamond problem in multiple inheritance?
options: A class has too many methods causing diamond-shaped UML
A class cannot be instantiated due to circular references
A class inherits from itself creating an infinite loop
A class inherits from two classes sharing a common ancestor
explanation: The diamond problem occurs when a class inherits from two classes that share a common ancestor, causing ambiguity.

text: What is dependency injection and why is it useful?
options: Providing dependencies externally rather than creating inside
Injecting code into a running program for hot patching
Adding extra methods to a class during program execution
Inserting breakpoints into code for debugging at runtime
explanation: Dependency injection provides dependencies from outside a class, improving testability and loose coupling.

text: What is the difference between aggregation and composition?
options: Aggregation has strong ownership; composition has weak ownership
Both aggregation and composition represent identical relationships
Aggregation has weak ownership; composition has strong ownership
Aggregation uses inheritance; composition uses interfaces only
explanation: In aggregation, parts can exist independently; in composition, parts cannot exist without the whole.

text: What is the purpose of the final keyword in Java?
options: It prevents classes from being inherited or methods overridden
It specifies that a variable can only hold final data type
It marks a method as the first one to execute in class
It indicates the method will return the final calculated value
explanation: The final keyword prevents class inheritance, method overriding, or variable reassignment.

text: A developer violates the Single Responsibility Principle by creating a class that handles both data validation and database storage. What is the consequence?
options: The class automatically becomes thread-safe for concurrent use
The compiler will refuse to compile the class with both concerns
The class will execute faster due to fewer method calls needed
Changes to validation logic may unintentionally break storage code
explanation: Violating SRP means changes to one responsibility risk breaking the other, reducing maintainability.

text: What is the visitor pattern and when should it be used?
options: A pattern for creating multiple instances of a single class
A pattern to add operations to objects without modifying classes
A pattern for sorting objects in a collection by their type
A pattern for managing user sessions in web applications
explanation: The visitor pattern adds new operations to object structures without modifying the classes of the elements.

text: How does the SOLID Open/Closed Principle guide class design?
options: Classes should be open for both extension and modification always
Classes should be closed for both extension and modification
Classes should be open for extension and closed for modification
Classes should be open for modification and closed for extension
explanation: The Open/Closed Principle states classes should be extendable without modifying existing source code.

text: What is the difference between covariance and contravariance in generic types?
options: Both covariance and contravariance preserve subtype ordering
Neither covariance nor contravariance affects subtype ordering
Covariance preserves subtype ordering; contravariance reverses it
Covariance reverses subtype ordering; contravariance preserves it
explanation: Covariance preserves the type hierarchy direction; contravariance reverses it for type parameter usage.

text: What is the template method pattern and how does it use inheritance?
options: It generates code templates for creating new class files quickly
It replaces all inheritance with composition for better flexibility
It creates multiple templates of the same class for different uses
It defines algorithm skeleton in base class deferring steps to sub
explanation: The template method defines an algorithm skeleton in the base class, letting subclasses override specific steps.

text: Why should developers prefer composition over inheritance in most cases?
options: Because inheritance is not supported in modern languages anymore
Because composition provides more flexibility and looser coupling
Because composition always uses less memory than inheritance does
Because inheritance cannot be used with interfaces in any language
explanation: Composition provides more flexibility, avoids fragile base class problems, and enables looser coupling.

text: What is double dispatch and how does the visitor pattern implement it?
options: Resolving a method call based on two object types at runtime
Dispatching a method call to two different servers simultaneously
Creating two instances of the same class for parallel processing
Calling the same method twice with different arguments each time
explanation: Double dispatch resolves method calls based on the runtime types of two objects, achieved through visitor accept/visit.

text: What problem does the fragile base class issue describe?
options: Changes to base class may unexpectedly break derived classes
Base classes consume too much memory in the inheritance chain
Base classes cannot have more than one level of inheritance
Derived classes always override every method in the base class
explanation: The fragile base class problem occurs when base class changes inadvertently break derived class behavior.

text: How does the CQRS pattern separate read and write operations in OOP design?
options: It combines all reads and writes into a single unified model
It uses separate models for reading queries and writing commands
It prevents any reading operations during write transactions
It uses different programming languages for reads and writes
explanation: CQRS separates the read model (queries) from the write model (commands) for independent optimization.

text: What is the expression problem and how do OOP and FP approach it differently?
options: Both OOP and FP solve the expression problem in identical ways
OOP easily adds new operations; FP easily adds new types always
Neither OOP nor FP can add new types or operations at all ever
OOP easily adds new types; FP easily adds new operations on types
explanation: OOP makes adding new types easy via subclassing; FP makes adding new operations easy via functions.

text: What is RAM in a computer?
options: Volatile memory for temporary data storage
A processor that executes program instructions
A permanent storage device like hard drive
A network component for internet connection
explanation: RAM (Random Access Memory) is volatile memory used for temporary data storage during program execution.

text: What is the stack in memory management?
options: A region for storing program source code
A region for network data buffering use
A region for dynamically allocated objects
A region for function calls and local vars
explanation: The stack is a memory region used for function call frames and local variable storage.

text: What is the heap in memory management?
options: Memory for dynamic allocation at runtime
Memory for function call stack frames
Memory for operating system kernel data
Memory for storing constant values only
explanation: The heap is a memory region used for dynamically allocated data at runtime.

text: What is garbage collection?
options: Deleting old files from the file system
Defragmenting the hard drive storage
Automatic reclamation of unused memory
Manually freeing unused memory in code
explanation: Garbage collection automatically reclaims memory occupied by objects no longer in use.

text: What is a memory leak?
options: When a program uses too much CPU power
When a program writes to read-only memory
When data is lost during a power outage
When allocated memory is never freed up
explanation: A memory leak occurs when allocated memory is never freed, causing memory consumption to grow over time.

text: What does memory allocation mean?
options: Reserving a block of memory for program use
Compressing data to use less memory space
Transferring memory between two computers
Deleting data from computer memory entirely
explanation: Memory allocation reserves a block of memory for the program to store data.

text: What is a pointer in programming?
options: A variable storing a memory address value
A function that points to another function
A graphical cursor on the screen display
A keyword used to create new variables
explanation: A pointer is a variable that stores the memory address of another variable or object.

text: What is the difference between static and dynamic memory allocation?
options: Static is for objects; dynamic is for integers
Static is at compile time; dynamic is at runtime
Static is slower; dynamic is faster always
Static uses the heap; dynamic uses the stack
explanation: Static allocation occurs at compile time with fixed size; dynamic allocation occurs at runtime with flexible size.

text: What happens when a program runs out of memory?
options: The program automatically frees old memory up
The program switches to using the CPU cache only
The operating system adds more physical RAM chips
The program crashes or throws an out-of-memory error
explanation: When memory is exhausted, the program typically crashes or throws an out-of-memory error.

text: What is a null pointer?
options: A pointer that stores a numeric zero value
A pointer to the largest memory address
A pointer that does not point to anything
A pointer to the first element of array
explanation: A null pointer is a pointer that does not reference any valid memory location.

text: What is a dangling pointer and why is it dangerous?
options: A pointer that takes too long to dereference in code
A pointer that points to the beginning of an allocated block
A pointer that is declared but never used in the program
A pointer referencing freed memory causing undefined behavior
explanation: A dangling pointer references memory that has been freed, leading to undefined behavior when accessed.

text: What is reference counting as a garbage collection strategy?
options: Tracking how many references point to each object in memory
Tracking how many times each function is called at runtime
Counting the total amount of memory allocated by program
Counting how many variables exist in the program scope
explanation: Reference counting tracks how many references point to each object, freeing it when the count reaches zero.

text: What is the difference between stack overflow and heap overflow?
options: Both stack and heap overflow are caused by the same exact problem
Stack overflow uses too much heap; heap overflow uses too much stack
Stack overflow is harmless; heap overflow always crashes program
Stack overflow is from deep recursion; heap from excess allocation
explanation: Stack overflow occurs from deep recursion or large locals; heap overflow from excessive dynamic allocation.

text: What is virtual memory and why do operating systems use it?
options: A type of memory used only by virtual machines and emulators
A memory encryption technique to protect sensitive program data
A technique using disk to extend available memory beyond RAM
Physical RAM that is faster than regular memory modules
explanation: Virtual memory uses disk space to extend available memory beyond physical RAM capacity.

text: What is a memory pool allocation strategy?
options: Pooling memory from multiple computers on a network
Storing all variables in a single continuous memory block
Allocating memory from a single global heap always
Pre-allocating fixed-size blocks for fast repeated allocation
explanation: Memory pools pre-allocate fixed-size blocks, providing fast allocation and deallocation without fragmentation.

text: What is the purpose of smart pointers in C++?
options: They convert pointers into integers for easier comparison
They automatically manage memory ownership and deallocation
They make pointer arithmetic faster on modern CPUs
They prevent pointers from being passed to any functions
explanation: Smart pointers automatically manage memory ownership, deallocating resources when no longer needed.

text: What is memory fragmentation?
options: When free memory exists but is split into small unusable chunks
When memory is completely full with no free space left
When memory hardware develops physical defects over time
When a program accesses memory belonging to another process
explanation: Memory fragmentation occurs when free memory is scattered in small non-contiguous blocks, preventing large allocations.

text: What is a buffer overflow?
options: Writing more data than a buffer can hold overwriting adjacent memory
Reading data from a buffer that has already been fully emptied
Allocating a buffer that is larger than available memory space
Writing less data than a buffer can hold in its capacity
explanation: A buffer overflow writes beyond buffer boundaries, potentially overwriting adjacent memory and causing security vulnerabilities.

text: What is the RAII pattern in C++?
options: A pattern for parallel processing of arrays using multiple threads
A technique for reducing memory usage of string operations
A pattern for implementing recursive algorithms efficiently
Resource acquisition tied to object lifetime via constructor and destructor
explanation: RAII ties resource acquisition to object construction and release to destruction, ensuring automatic cleanup.

text: What is the difference between malloc and new in C++?
options: malloc is faster than new in all performance benchmarks
malloc is for C++ only; new is for C language only
new cannot allocate memory for arrays unlike malloc can
malloc allocates raw memory; new also calls the constructor
explanation: malloc allocates raw memory; new allocates memory and calls the object constructor.

text: A program has a memory leak that only manifests after running for several days. What approach best identifies the leak?
options: Adding more RAM to the server to delay the out-of-memory crash
Using a memory profiler to track allocation growth over time
Reviewing source code manually for every allocation and free
Restarting the program more frequently to prevent the leak
explanation: Memory profilers track allocation patterns over time, identifying objects that grow but are never freed.

text: What is the generational hypothesis in garbage collection?
options: Older objects are more likely to be collected than newer ones
Most objects die young so collecting young generation is efficient
Garbage collection should run only once per program execution
All objects have the same lifetime regardless of creation time
explanation: The generational hypothesis states most objects die young, so focusing GC on young generations is efficient.

text: What is a use-after-free vulnerability and how can it be exploited?
options: Freeing memory twice causing the allocator to add extra memory
Accessing freed memory that may contain attacker-controlled data
Using free software after a trial period has expired completely
Using a variable after its scope ends causing a compiler error
explanation: Use-after-free accesses freed memory that may be reallocated with attacker-controlled data, enabling code execution.

text: How does Rust's ownership system prevent memory safety bugs at compile time?
options: Each value has one owner and memory is freed when owner goes out of scope
It prevents all heap allocation forcing everything onto the stack
It uses garbage collection that runs at compile time instead
It requires programmers to manually free every allocation made
explanation: Rust's ownership system ensures each value has one owner; memory is freed when the owner goes out of scope.

text: What is the difference between mark-and-sweep and mark-and-compact garbage collection?
options: Mark-sweep leaves objects in place; mark-compact moves them together
Mark-sweep moves live objects; mark-compact leaves them in place
Mark-compact cannot handle circular references unlike mark-sweep
Mark-sweep is faster than mark-compact in all possible scenarios
explanation: Mark-and-sweep frees dead objects leaving gaps; mark-and-compact additionally moves live objects together to eliminate fragmentation.

text: What is a slab allocator and when is it used in operating systems?
options: An allocator that only works with very large memory blocks
An allocator that stores data on disk instead of in memory
An allocator caching fixed-size objects for frequent kernel allocations
An allocator that compresses memory to fit more data in RAM
explanation: A slab allocator pre-allocates caches of fixed-size objects for efficient frequent allocation in OS kernels.

text: How does address space layout randomization (ASLR) improve memory security?
options: It encrypts all memory contents to prevent unauthorized reading
It randomizes memory layout making exploit addresses unpredictable
It prevents any program from allocating memory on the heap
It locks memory pages so they cannot be written to by code
explanation: ASLR randomizes the memory layout of processes, making it difficult for attackers to predict target addresses.

text: What is false sharing in multi-threaded programs and how does it affect performance?
options: Threads falsely reporting memory as free when it is still in use
Sharing read-only data between threads causing unnecessary copying
Multiple threads sharing the same variable causing data corruption
Threads on different cores invalidate cache lines they do not truly share
explanation: False sharing occurs when threads on different cores modify variables on the same cache line, causing unnecessary invalidations.

text: What is the purpose of memory-mapped files and how do they differ from traditional file I/O?
options: They compress files in memory to reduce the total memory used
They encrypt files in memory to prevent unauthorized data reading
They store files entirely in CPU registers for faster access
They map file contents to virtual memory allowing direct pointer access
explanation: Memory-mapped files map file contents into virtual address space, allowing direct pointer access instead of read/write calls.

text: What is a garbage collection pause and why is it problematic for real-time applications?
options: A delay caused by the GC writing collection logs to disk
A delay when the GC starts running for the very first time
A stop-the-world pause halting application threads during collection
A pause occurring only when the program first allocates memory
explanation: GC pauses halt application threads during collection, causing unacceptable latency in real-time applications.

text: What is an exception in programming?
options: A normal expected program execution result
An event disrupting normal program flow
A type of variable for storing text
A function for mathematical calculation
explanation: An exception is an event that disrupts the normal flow of program execution.

text: What does the try block do?
options: It tests the program for performance speed
It retries failed operations multiple times
It wraps code that might throw exceptions
It attempts to fix errors automatically
explanation: The try block contains code that might throw an exception during execution.

text: What does the catch block do?
options: It prevents all exceptions from occurring
It handles a specific type of exception
It catches user input from the keyboard
It stops the program from running further
explanation: The catch block handles a specific type of exception that was thrown in the try block.

text: What does the finally block do?
options: It runs only when no exception was thrown
It finalizes variable values permanently
It ends the program after error handling
It executes code regardless of exceptions
explanation: The finally block executes code regardless of whether an exception was thrown or caught.

text: What does throwing an exception mean?
options: Preventing the program from starting at all
Deleting an error from the program memory
Signaling that an error condition has occurred
Converting an error into a warning message
explanation: Throwing an exception signals that an error condition has occurred during execution.

text: What is a NullPointerException?
options: An error from dividing a number by zero
An error from accessing a null reference
An error from invalid string formatting
An error from array index being negative
explanation: A NullPointerException occurs when trying to access a member of a null object reference.

text: What is an ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException?
options: Deleting an element from an empty array
Creating an array with too many elements
Accessing an array with invalid index value
Accessing an array with valid index number
explanation: ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException occurs when accessing an array with an index outside its valid range.

text: What is error handling?
options: The process of creating custom error messages
The process of hiding errors from the user
The process of writing error-free code always
The process of managing and responding to errors
explanation: Error handling is the process of detecting, managing, and responding to errors during execution.

text: What is the difference between an error and an exception?
options: Errors are recoverable; exceptions are not recoverable
Errors are serious system issues; exceptions are recoverable
Both errors and exceptions are exactly the same thing
Exceptions are more serious than errors in all cases
explanation: Errors typically represent serious unrecoverable system issues; exceptions are generally recoverable conditions.

text: What is a stack trace?
options: A list of variables currently stored on stack
A report showing the sequence of method calls
A diagram of the program memory layout
A log of all user inputs during execution
explanation: A stack trace shows the sequence of method calls leading to the point where an exception occurred.

text: What is the difference between checked and unchecked exceptions in Java?
options: Checked exceptions are less severe than unchecked exceptions
Checked exceptions occur at runtime; unchecked at compile time
Both checked and unchecked exceptions behave identically always
Checked must be declared or caught; unchecked are not required
explanation: Checked exceptions must be declared or caught at compile time; unchecked exceptions have no such requirement.

text: What is exception chaining and why is it useful?
options: Catching multiple exceptions in a single catch block always
Throwing the same exception multiple times in succession
Creating a chain of try-catch blocks for different error types
Wrapping an exception in another to preserve the original cause
explanation: Exception chaining wraps an exception inside another, preserving the original cause for debugging.

text: What is the purpose of custom exception classes?
options: They make exceptions run faster than built-in exception types
They automatically fix errors without any developer intervention
They replace all built-in exceptions in the language entirely
They provide domain-specific error types with relevant context
explanation: Custom exceptions provide domain-specific error types with relevant context for better error handling.

text: Why should you avoid using exceptions for normal control flow?
options: Because exceptions prevent the program from being compiled
Because exceptions cannot carry any data or message with them
Because exceptions are expensive and obscure program logic flow
Because exceptions only work inside the main function of program
explanation: Exceptions are expensive to create and throw; using them for control flow obscures logic and hurts performance.

text: What is the difference between throw and throws in Java?
options: throw is for checked exceptions only; throws for unchecked only
throw creates a new exception class; throws catches exceptions
throw raises an exception; throws declares exceptions a method may throw
Both throw and throws perform the exact same operation always
explanation: throw raises an exception; throws declares which exceptions a method might throw in its signature.

text: What is a try-with-resources statement in Java?
options: A try block that limits CPU resources used during execution
A try block that automatically imports required library classes
A try block that automatically closes resources when done
A try block that retries the operation with different data
explanation: Try-with-resources automatically closes AutoCloseable resources when the try block finishes.

text: What is the purpose of logging exceptions?
options: To convert exceptions into warnings that can be safely ignored
To automatically send exceptions to users via email alerts
To prevent exceptions from occurring in future executions
To record error details for debugging and monitoring purposes
explanation: Logging records exception details for debugging, monitoring, and post-mortem analysis.

text: What happens if an exception is thrown but not caught?
options: The exception is automatically converted to a return value
The program terminates and typically displays a stack trace
The exception is silently ignored and execution continues
The program pauses and waits for user input to continue
explanation: An uncaught exception typically causes the program to terminate and display a stack trace.

text: What is a multi-catch block in Java?
options: Nesting multiple catch blocks inside one another deeply
A catch block that runs multiple times for one exception
A single catch block handling multiple exception types
Catching exceptions from multiple try blocks at one time
explanation: A multi-catch block catches multiple exception types in a single catch clause using the pipe operator.

text: What is defensive programming in relation to error handling?
options: Anticipating potential errors and handling them proactively
Only handling errors after they cause the program to crash
Ignoring all possible errors to keep the code base simple
Delegating all error handling to the operating system only
explanation: Defensive programming anticipates potential errors and handles them proactively before they cause failures.

text: A developer wraps every method in try-catch blocks that silently swallow exceptions. What is the consequence?
options: Memory usage decreases because exceptions are not stored
The application runs faster due to fewer exception propagations
The application becomes completely immune to all runtime errors
Errors are hidden making debugging extremely difficult and slow
explanation: Swallowing exceptions hides errors, making bugs extremely difficult to diagnose and fix.

text: What is the difference between fault tolerance and error recovery in system design?
options: Fault tolerance continues despite faults; recovery restores state
Fault tolerance prevents all errors; recovery fixes them after
Fault tolerance is for hardware; recovery is for software only
Both terms mean exactly the same thing in all system contexts
explanation: Fault tolerance keeps the system running despite faults; error recovery restores the system to a valid state.

text: How does the circuit breaker pattern prevent cascading failures in distributed systems?
options: It duplicates every request to ensure at least one succeeds
It physically disconnects failing servers from the network
It stops calling a failing service after threshold allowing recovery
It encrypts all communication to prevent transmission errors
explanation: The circuit breaker stops calls to a failing service after a failure threshold, allowing it time to recover.

text: What is the difference between fail-fast and fail-safe error handling strategies?
options: Fail-fast is for testing; fail-safe is for production environments
Both strategies handle errors in exactly the same manner always
Fail-fast ignores errors; fail-safe reports them immediately
Fail-fast reports errors immediately; fail-safe continues operating
explanation: Fail-fast immediately reports errors for quick detection; fail-safe continues operating despite failures.

text: A microservice architecture experiences a cascading failure. Which pattern best prevents this?
options: Increasing timeout values to give failing services more time
Using bulkhead isolation to contain failures within boundaries
Adding more servers to handle the increased error load
Removing all error handling to let services fail naturally
explanation: Bulkhead isolation contains failures within boundaries, preventing one failing component from taking down others.

text: What is the purpose of structured error handling using Result types instead of exceptions?
options: Result types are faster to execute than exception handling code
Result types prevent any errors from occurring during runtime
Result types make error handling explicit in function signatures
Result types automatically fix errors without developer action
explanation: Result types make error handling explicit in signatures, forcing callers to handle both success and failure cases.

text: How does the retry pattern with exponential backoff improve error handling?
options: It retries immediately without any delay between each attempt
It retries a fixed number of times with constant delay between
It decreases delay between retries to resolve errors faster
It increases delay between retries reducing load on failing service
explanation: Exponential backoff increases delay between retries, reducing load on the failing service and allowing recovery.

text: What is the difference between recoverable and unrecoverable errors in Rust?
options: Recoverable use Result type; unrecoverable use panic macro
Rust does not distinguish between recoverable and unrecoverable
Recoverable use panic macro; unrecoverable use Result type
Both types are handled using the same mechanism in Rust
explanation: Rust uses Result<T,E> for recoverable errors and panic! for unrecoverable errors, making the distinction explicit.

text: What is the supervisor pattern in Erlang/OTP for fault tolerance?
options: A design that prevents any process from ever failing at all
A thread that monitors CPU usage and kills slow processes
A process that monitors child processes and restarts on failure
A function that validates all input before processing it
explanation: The supervisor pattern monitors child processes and automatically restarts them when they fail.

text: Why is it important to include contextual information when throwing custom exceptions?
options: Context helps developers quickly diagnose the root cause of errors
Context information makes exceptions run faster in production
Context reduces the memory footprint of exception objects created
Context automatically fixes the error without human intervention
explanation: Contextual information in exceptions helps developers quickly understand and diagnose the root cause of errors.

text: What is a module in programming?
options: A type of variable for storing numbers
A comment explaining program functionality
A self-contained unit of reusable code
A single line of executable source code
explanation: A module is a self-contained unit of code that can be reused across different programs.

text: What is a package in programming?
options: A collection of related modules grouped together
A compressed file containing program source code
A user interface component for displaying data
A single function within a larger program
explanation: A package is a collection of related modules organized together under a common namespace.

text: What does the import statement do?
options: It exports code to another program file
It deletes unused variables from memory
It compiles the program into machine code
It brings external code into current scope
explanation: The import statement brings external modules or packages into the current file's scope.

text: What is a library in programming?
options: A network protocol for data communication
A tool for designing graphical user interfaces
A database for storing user information data
A collection of pre-written reusable code
explanation: A library is a collection of pre-written, reusable code that provides specific functionality.

text: What is a namespace?
options: A file that lists all variables in the program
A container preventing naming conflicts in code
A variable that stores the program name string
A function that generates random names for vars
explanation: A namespace is a container that prevents naming conflicts by organizing identifiers into separate scopes.

text: What is a dependency in software?
options: A function that runs only in debug build mode
A bug that depends on specific input to occur
A variable that depends on user input value
An external library that a program requires
explanation: A dependency is an external library or module that a program requires to function correctly.

text: What is the purpose of a package manager?
options: To test programs for bugs and performance
To compile source code into executable files
To install and manage software dependencies
To write new code automatically for developers
explanation: A package manager automates installing, updating, and managing software dependencies.

text: What is an API in the context of libraries?
options: The documentation website for the library only
The internal source code of the library files
The set of functions and rules for using library
The version number assigned to the library code
explanation: An API defines the functions, classes, and protocols for interacting with a library.

text: What does exporting a function from a module mean?
options: Copying the function into a separate backup file
Deleting the function from the current module
Making the function available to other modules
Converting the function into a different language
explanation: Exporting makes a function available for other modules to import and use.

text: What is a framework in programming?
options: A testing tool for verifying program correctness
A type of database for storing application data
A single function for processing data values
A structure providing foundation for building apps
explanation: A framework provides a foundational structure and conventions for building applications.

text: What is semantic versioning (SemVer)?
options: Dating versions with the release date as the number
Numbering versions sequentially with single integers
Naming versions with random words for easy recall
A system using MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH format for versions
explanation: Semantic versioning uses MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH to communicate the nature of changes in each release.

text: What is dependency injection in the context of modules?
options: Providing module dependencies externally rather than hardcoding
Injecting malicious code into a module for exploitation
Removing unused dependencies from the project configuration
Automatically downloading dependencies from the internet
explanation: Dependency injection provides dependencies from outside rather than hardcoding them inside modules.

text: What is circular dependency and why is it problematic?
options: When a module depends on too many other external modules
When two modules have functions with the same name defined
When a dependency is updated too frequently breaking code
When module A depends on B and B depends on A causing issues
explanation: Circular dependencies create import loops that can cause initialization failures and tight coupling.

text: What is the difference between a static and dynamic library?
options: Static is linked at compile time; dynamic is linked at runtime
Static is for C only; dynamic is for all other languages only
Static is faster but dynamic uses less total memory always
Static cannot be updated; dynamic updates automatically always
explanation: Static libraries are linked at compile time into the executable; dynamic libraries are loaded at runtime.

text: What is a lockfile in dependency management?
options: A file that limits the number of dependencies allowed
A file that encrypts the dependencies for secure storage
A file that locks the source code from being modified
A file recording exact versions of all installed dependencies
explanation: A lockfile records exact dependency versions to ensure reproducible builds across environments.

text: What is tree shaking in module bundling?
options: Converting modules from one format to another at build
Reorganizing module code into a tree data structure
Sorting modules alphabetically for better organization
Removing unused code from the final bundle to reduce size
explanation: Tree shaking eliminates unused code from the final bundle, reducing application size.

text: What is the difference between CommonJS and ES modules?
options: CommonJS is newer than ES modules in JavaScript evolution
CommonJS uses require/exports; ES modules use import/export
CommonJS uses import/export; ES modules use require/exports
Both systems use identical syntax and behave the same way
explanation: CommonJS uses require/module.exports; ES modules use import/export with static analysis support.

text: What is a monorepo?
options: A single repository containing multiple related projects
A repository containing only one small module of code
A repository that stores only documentation not code
A repository that cannot have more than one contributor
explanation: A monorepo is a single repository containing multiple related projects or packages.

text: What is the purpose of a module bundler like Webpack?
options: It compresses images used in web application projects
It automatically writes unit tests for all module code
It combines multiple modules into optimized output bundles
It deploys applications to production servers directly
explanation: A module bundler combines multiple modules and their dependencies into optimized output files.

text: What is a transitive dependency?
options: A dependency of a dependency that is indirectly required
A dependency that transitions between different versions
A dependency that is optional and not always installed
A dependency that is only used during testing phases
explanation: A transitive dependency is a dependency of one of your direct dependencies, indirectly required by your project.

text: A project has conflicting versions of the same dependency required by two libraries. How is this typically resolved?
options: The project must choose one library and remove the other
Dependency resolution algorithms select a compatible version
Both versions are deleted and the dependency is removed
All dependencies are updated to the latest version available
explanation: Package managers use dependency resolution algorithms to find a compatible version satisfying all constraints.

text: What is the difference between a plugin architecture and a monolithic architecture?
options: Plugins require more memory than monolithic architecture always
Monolithic supports more features than plugin architecture can
Plugins allow extending functionality without modifying core code
Plugins are slower; monolithic is always faster in execution
explanation: Plugin architecture allows extending functionality through loadable components without modifying the core system.

text: What is dependency hell and what strategies mitigate it?
options: Dependencies that are too large consuming excessive disk space
Conflicting version requirements across the dependency graph
Having too few dependencies causing limited functionality
Dependencies that require payment to use in production apps
explanation: Dependency hell refers to conflicting version requirements; lockfiles, SemVer, and virtual environments help mitigate it.

text: How does lazy loading of modules improve application performance?
options: It defers loading modules until they are actually needed
It compresses modules to reduce their memory footprint
It loads all modules at startup to prevent later delays
It caches all modules permanently in browser local storage
explanation: Lazy loading defers module loading until needed, reducing initial load time and memory usage.

text: What is the difference between a micro-frontend architecture and a traditional monolithic frontend?
options: Micro-frontends split UI into independently deployable modules
Monolithic frontends load faster than micro-frontend solutions
Micro-frontends cannot share any state between components
Micro-frontends use only one framework; monolithic uses many
explanation: Micro-frontends decompose the UI into independently developed, deployed, and maintained modules.

text: What is a supply chain attack in the context of package management?
options: Compromising a dependency to inject malicious code downstream
An attack that corrupts the package manager configuration file
An attack that prevents packages from being downloaded online
An attack that targets the physical delivery of hardware
explanation: Supply chain attacks compromise legitimate dependencies to inject malicious code into downstream projects.

text: What is the purpose of code splitting in modern web applications?
options: Separating code comments from executable statements in files
Splitting code across multiple programming languages for speed
Dividing code equally among team members for parallel coding
Breaking application code into chunks loaded on demand for speed
explanation: Code splitting breaks the application into chunks loaded on demand, improving initial load performance.

text: How does the facade pattern simplify complex module interactions?
options: It removes dependencies between modules by duplicating all code
It hides all modules and prevents any external access to them
It converts all module functions into a single large function
It provides a simplified interface to a complex subsystem of modules
explanation: The facade pattern provides a simplified unified interface to a complex set of module interactions.

text: What is the hexagonal architecture and how does it relate to module organization?
options: An architecture using exactly six modules in the application
An architecture isolating core logic from external adapters and ports
An architecture requiring six layers of abstraction in modules
An architecture that arranges modules in a hexagonal grid pattern
explanation: Hexagonal architecture isolates core business logic from external concerns through ports and adapters.

text: What is the difference between hard and soft dependencies in module design?
options: Hard dependencies are faster; soft dependencies are always slower
Hard dependencies are for production; soft are for development only
Hard dependencies are required; soft are optional or gracefully absent
Both hard and soft dependencies behave identically in all cases
explanation: Hard dependencies are required for functionality; soft dependencies are optional and the system degrades gracefully without them.

text: What is recursion in programming?
options: A function that calls itself to solve tasks
A variable that stores multiple data values
A loop that runs a fixed number of times
A technique for sorting array elements fast
explanation: Recursion is when a function calls itself to break a problem into smaller sub-problems.

text: What is a data structure?
options: A way to organize and store data efficiently
A network protocol for transmitting data
A programming language for data analysis
A tool for designing user interface layouts
explanation: A data structure is a way to organize and store data for efficient access and modification.

text: What is a generic type in programming?
options: A type that can only store integer values
A type that cannot be used in functions
A type specific to one programming language
A type parameterized to work with any type
explanation: A generic type is parameterized to work with different data types while maintaining type safety.

text: What is a design pattern?
options: A reusable solution to a common coding problem
A database schema for storing application data
A testing method for finding software bugs
A visual design for user interface screens
explanation: A design pattern is a reusable solution to a commonly occurring problem in software design.

text: What is an iterator?
options: A counter variable used inside for loops
A class that stores a collection of objects
A function that sorts elements in an array
An object that traverses elements one by one
explanation: An iterator is an object that provides sequential access to elements of a collection one at a time.

text: What is a linked list?
options: A sorted array with binary search capability
An array stored in non-contiguous memory blocks
A list of all links on a web page document
A sequence of nodes connected by pointers
explanation: A linked list is a sequence of nodes where each node contains data and a pointer to the next node.

text: What is a binary tree?
options: A tree with exactly two levels of depth total
A tree where each node has at most two children
A tree that stores only binary data zero and one
A tree that can only be traversed in one way
explanation: A binary tree is a tree data structure where each node has at most two children.

text: What is Big O notation used for?
options: Counting the number of lines in source code
Describing algorithm efficiency and scalability
