Determining the memory size of data types used
Measuring the exact runtime in milliseconds
explanation: Big O notation describes the upper bound of an algorithm's time or space complexity as input grows.

text: What is a graph data structure?
options: A set of nodes connected by edges or links
A chart displaying numerical data visually
A database table with rows and column fields
A type of array with two dimensions in it
explanation: A graph is a set of nodes (vertices) connected by edges, representing relationships between entities.

text: What is an abstract class?
options: A class that cannot be instantiated directly
A class with no methods or properties defined
A class that exists only in pseudocode form
A class that is automatically garbage collected
explanation: An abstract class cannot be instantiated directly and may contain abstract methods for subclasses to implement.

text: What is the time complexity of binary search on a sorted array?
options: O(n squared) because it uses nested loops for searching
O(n) because it checks every element in the array
O(1) because it directly accesses the target element
O(log n) because it halves the search space each step
explanation: Binary search has O(log n) complexity because it halves the search space with each comparison.

text: What is memoization and how does it improve performance?
options: It memorizes variable names for faster variable lookup
It logs all function calls to memory for later review
It caches function results to avoid redundant computation
It allocates extra memory to make functions run faster
explanation: Memoization caches the results of expensive function calls, returning cached results for repeated inputs.

text: What is the observer pattern in software design?
options: A pattern where one object observes system memory usage
A pattern where objects subscribe to and receive event updates
A pattern for creating exactly one instance of a class only
A pattern for sorting collections using comparison callbacks
explanation: The observer pattern lets objects subscribe to events and receive notifications when state changes occur.

text: What is dynamic programming?
options: Breaking problems into overlapping subproblems and caching
Creating programs that adapt to user preferences at runtime
Writing code that changes its own source during execution
Programming in a dynamically typed language like Python
explanation: Dynamic programming solves problems by breaking them into overlapping subproblems and caching their results.

text: What is a hash function and what makes a good one?
options: A function that encrypts data using a secret key for security
A function that compresses files to reduce their storage size
A function mapping data to fixed-size values with few collisions
A function that sorts data into alphabetical order always
explanation: A good hash function maps data to fixed-size values with uniform distribution and minimal collisions.

text: What is the singleton pattern?
options: A pattern ensuring a class has only one instance globally
A pattern for connecting a single client to a server only
A pattern that creates multiple instances of different classes
A pattern that limits functions to one parameter at most
explanation: The singleton pattern ensures a class has only one instance and provides a global point of access.

text: What is the factory pattern?
options: A pattern for converting objects between different types
A pattern for destroying objects when no longer needed
A pattern delegating object creation to a factory method
A pattern for mass producing identical objects in batches
explanation: The factory pattern delegates object creation to a factory method, decoupling creation from usage.

text: What is the difference between BFS and DFS graph traversal?
options: BFS is only for trees; DFS is only for general graph structures
BFS explores depth first; DFS explores breadth first always
BFS uses a stack; DFS uses a queue for storing nodes to visit
BFS explores level by level; DFS explores as deep as possible
explanation: BFS explores nodes level by level using a queue; DFS explores as deep as possible using a stack.

text: What is a heap data structure?
options: A complete binary tree satisfying the heap ordering property
A hash table that automatically resizes when it gets full
A linked list sorted in ascending order of element values
A disorganized pile of unsorted data in memory blocks
explanation: A heap is a complete binary tree where each node satisfies the heap property (min-heap or max-heap).

text: What is the strategy pattern?
options: A pattern for choosing between inheritance and composition
A pattern for planning the development timeline of projects
A pattern encapsulating interchangeable algorithms in objects
A pattern that strategically allocates memory for performance
explanation: The strategy pattern encapsulates interchangeable algorithms, allowing them to be selected at runtime.

text: What is the difference between greedy algorithms and dynamic programming approaches?
options: Both approaches always produce identical results for all problems
Greedy makes locally optimal choices; DP considers all subproblems
Greedy always finds the optimal solution; DP finds approximate
DP makes locally optimal choices; greedy considers all subproblems
explanation: Greedy makes locally optimal choices without reconsidering; DP considers all subproblems for global optimality.

text: What is a monad in functional programming and what problem does it solve?
options: A pattern for chaining operations while managing side effects
A single unit of memory allocation in functional programs
A function that converts between different data type formats
A data type that can only hold one value at any given time
explanation: A monad is a pattern for chaining operations while managing context like side effects, optionality, or errors.

text: How does the A* algorithm combine features of Dijkstra and greedy best-first search?
options: A* runs both algorithms separately and picks the better result
A* ignores heuristics and relies only on actual path cost found
A* uses actual cost plus heuristic estimate to guide its search
A* uses Dijkstra for graphs and greedy for trees exclusively
explanation: A* combines actual path cost (Dijkstra) with a heuristic estimate (greedy) for efficient optimal pathfinding.

text: What is the CAP theorem and how does it affect distributed system design?
options: It states only two of consistency, availability, partition tolerance
It applies only to single-machine systems not distributed systems
It guarantees all three properties simultaneously in all systems
It states systems can achieve consistency, availability, and partitions
explanation: CAP theorem states distributed systems can guarantee at most two of consistency, availability, and partition tolerance.

text: What is the difference between a trie and a balanced BST for string operations?
options: Tries cannot store strings with common prefixes unlike BSTs
Tries are slower for prefix queries than balanced BSTs always
Tries provide O(m) lookup by key length; BSTs provide O(m log n)
BSTs provide faster prefix-based searching than tries in all cases
explanation: Tries provide O(m) lookup based on key length with efficient prefix operations; BSTs provide O(m log n).

text: What is the actor model for concurrent programming?
options: A model that uses only one thread for all program operations
A model that prevents all concurrency to avoid race conditions
A model where threads share mutable state through locks
A model where actors communicate through asynchronous messages
explanation: The actor model uses independent actors that communicate through asynchronous message passing, avoiding shared state.

text: What problem does the flyweight pattern solve in software design?
options: It prevents objects from growing beyond a certain memory limit
It shares common state among many objects to reduce memory use
It makes heavy objects lighter by removing unnecessary methods
It converts heavyweight processes into lightweight threads only
explanation: The flyweight pattern shares intrinsic state among many objects, dramatically reducing memory usage.

text: How does consistent hashing improve distributed hash table scalability?
options: It minimizes key redistribution when nodes are added or removed
It sorts all keys alphabetically across all nodes in the system
It eliminates all hash collisions in the distributed hash table
It ensures all hash values are consecutive integers in sequence
explanation: Consistent hashing minimizes key redistribution when nodes join or leave, improving scalability.

text: What is the difference between optimistic and pessimistic concurrency control?
options: Optimistic locks before access; pessimistic assumes no conflict
Optimistic is for reads only; pessimistic is for writes exclusively
Both approaches use identical locking mechanisms for all access
Optimistic assumes no conflict and validates; pessimistic locks first
explanation: Optimistic concurrency assumes no conflict and validates at commit; pessimistic locks resources before access.

text: What is event sourcing and how does it differ from traditional CRUD?
options: Event sourcing uses events only for logging not for state recovery
Event sourcing stores only the current state like CRUD does
Event sourcing stores all state changes as immutable event sequence
Event sourcing deletes old data automatically unlike CRUD approach
explanation: Event sourcing stores all state changes as an immutable sequence of events, unlike CRUD which overwrites state.

text: What is concurrency in programming?
options: Using multiple monitors for one computer
Running one task at a time in sequence
Managing multiple tasks overlapping in time
Connecting multiple computers on a network
explanation: Concurrency is the ability to manage multiple tasks that overlap in time, not necessarily simultaneously.

text: What is a thread in programming?
options: A connection between client and server
A lightweight unit of execution in process
A complete independent running program
A sequence of characters in a string
explanation: A thread is a lightweight unit of execution within a process, sharing memory with other threads.

text: What is parallelism?
options: Writing code in two programming languages
Executing multiple tasks simultaneously on CPUs
Connecting computers in a parallel network
Running tasks one after another in order
explanation: Parallelism is the simultaneous execution of multiple tasks on multiple CPU cores.

text: What is a process in operating systems?
options: A single instruction executed by the CPU
An independent program instance in execution
A file stored on the hard drive disk
A connection between two network devices
explanation: A process is an independent instance of a running program with its own memory space.

text: What is a race condition?
options: A competition between programmers writing code
A performance test comparing two algorithms speed
An error when threads access shared data unsafely
A condition that causes programs to run too fast
explanation: A race condition occurs when multiple threads access shared data without proper synchronization.

text: What is a deadlock?
options: When a program produces incorrect output data
When the CPU overheats from excessive usage
When threads wait for each other indefinitely
When a program runs too slowly for the user
explanation: A deadlock occurs when two or more threads are waiting for each other indefinitely, unable to proceed.

text: What is multithreading?
options: Running multiple threads within one process
Using multiple monitors for programming work
Writing code in multiple languages at once
Storing data in multiple databases together
explanation: Multithreading runs multiple threads within a single process to perform concurrent operations.

text: What does synchronization mean in concurrent programming?
options: Coordinating thread access to shared resources
Converting synchronous code to asynchronous
Copying data between two different computers
Keeping system clocks at the same exact time
explanation: Synchronization coordinates thread access to shared resources to prevent race conditions.

text: What is a mutex?
options: A lock ensuring only one thread accesses resource
A function for mathematical calculations only
A type of data structure for sorting elements
A variable that stores multiple values at once
explanation: A mutex (mutual exclusion) is a lock that ensures only one thread can access a resource at a time.

text: What is the difference between concurrency and parallelism?
options: Concurrency is faster than parallelism in all execution scenarios
Concurrency runs tasks simultaneously; parallelism overlaps them
Both terms mean exactly the same thing in all programming contexts
Concurrency manages overlapping tasks; parallelism runs them at once
explanation: Concurrency manages overlapping tasks (potentially on one core); parallelism executes tasks simultaneously on multiple cores.

text: What is a semaphore and how does it differ from a mutex?
options: A semaphore allows only one thread; a mutex allows multiple
A semaphore is for processes only; a mutex is for threads only
A semaphore allows multiple threads up to a count; mutex allows one
Both semaphores and mutexes behave identically in all situations
explanation: A semaphore allows a specified number of threads to access a resource; a mutex allows only one.

text: What is a thread pool and why is it used?
options: A pool of memory shared between all threads in a program
A data structure for storing thread execution results only
A group of pre-created threads reused for executing tasks
A debugging tool that monitors thread activity in programs
explanation: A thread pool pre-creates reusable threads, avoiding the overhead of creating and destroying threads for each task.

text: What are the four necessary conditions for a deadlock to occur?
options: Speed, memory, disk, and network resource limitations
Compilation, linking, loading, and execution phase errors
Input, processing, output, and storage operation failures
Mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, circular wait
explanation: Deadlock requires mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, and circular wait simultaneously.

text: What is the producer-consumer problem?
options: A problem of converting data between different encoding formats
A problem of coordinating threads that produce and consume data
A problem of allocating memory between different program modules
A problem about optimizing compilation speed of programs
explanation: The producer-consumer problem coordinates threads that add data to a buffer (produce) and remove data (consume).

text: What is an atomic operation?
options: An operation that completes indivisibly without interruption
An operation that uses atomic energy for faster processing
An operation that can be divided into smaller sub-steps
An operation that only works on integer data type values
explanation: An atomic operation completes as a single indivisible step, preventing other threads from seeing intermediate states.

text: What is the difference between preemptive and cooperative multitasking?
options: Both use identical scheduling mechanisms in all operating systems
Preemptive only works on single-core; cooperative on multi-core
Preemptive OS controls scheduling; cooperative tasks yield control
Preemptive is slower; cooperative is faster in all situations
explanation: Preemptive multitasking lets the OS forcibly switch tasks; cooperative requires tasks to voluntarily yield.

text: What is a condition variable used for?
options: Storing the condition of an if-else statement in memory
Checking if a variable meets certain validation requirements
Allowing threads to wait for a specific condition to become true
Defining constant values that cannot be changed during runtime
explanation: A condition variable allows threads to block until a specific condition is signaled by another thread.

text: What is the dining philosophers problem about?
options: Managing memory allocation in object-oriented program design
Optimizing database queries for better application performance
Designing efficient algorithms for sorting large data arrays
Illustrating deadlock and resource contention among processes
explanation: The dining philosophers problem illustrates deadlock and resource contention challenges in concurrent systems.

text: What is thread safety?
options: Code that functions correctly when accessed by multiple threads
A technique for making threads run faster on modern hardware
A security feature that prevents threads from being terminated
Writing code that runs only on a single thread at one time
explanation: Thread safety means code behaves correctly when accessed by multiple threads concurrently.

text: What is the difference between blocking and non-blocking algorithms?
options: Blocking can cause threads to wait; non-blocking guarantee progress
Both types have identical performance in all concurrent scenarios
Blocking algorithms are faster; non-blocking are always slower
Non-blocking algorithms cannot be used with multiple threads
explanation: Blocking algorithms can suspend threads; non-blocking algorithms guarantee system-wide progress.

text: What is lock-free programming and why is it challenging to implement correctly?
options: A technique that prevents any thread from ever being interrupted
A style that avoids all shared memory between concurrent threads
Using atomic operations for thread safety without traditional locks
Programming without any synchronization for maximum speed always
explanation: Lock-free programming uses atomic operations instead of locks, guaranteeing progress but requiring careful memory ordering.

text: What is the ABA problem in lock-free programming?
options: When a thread reads value A but another writes B before commit
When two threads both try to set the same value simultaneously
When atomic operations fail due to hardware memory limitations
When a value changes from A to B to A, making change undetectable
explanation: The ABA problem occurs when a value changes from A to B and back to A, making the change undetectable by CAS operations.

text: How does the fork-join framework improve parallel computation?
options: It recursively splits tasks and merges results for parallelism
It joins multiple programs into one before executing them all
It creates a new thread for every single instruction to execute
It forks the program into separate processes that never rejoin
explanation: Fork-join recursively splits tasks into subtasks, executes them in parallel, and merges the results.

text: What is the happens-before relationship in the Java memory model?
options: A rule preventing any reordering of instructions by the compiler
A requirement that all code must execute in written source order
A guarantee that one action's effects are visible to another action
A constraint requiring all threads to start in a specific order
explanation: Happens-before guarantees that memory effects of one action are visible to another, ensuring correct ordering.

text: What is a memory barrier or fence instruction?
options: A mechanism for allocating memory in a thread-safe manner
A technique for protecting memory from unauthorized access
A hardware limit on the total amount of memory a program uses
An instruction ensuring memory operations complete in order
explanation: A memory barrier ensures that memory operations before the barrier complete before those after it.

text: How does software transactional memory (STM) simplify concurrent programming?
options: It allows composable atomic memory transactions without locks
It automatically detects and removes all race conditions found
It stores all thread data in a transactional database system
It converts multi-threaded code into single-threaded execution
explanation: STM allows composable atomic memory transactions, simplifying concurrent programming without explicit locks.

text: What is the thundering herd problem in concurrent systems?
options: When threads are distributed unevenly across available CPU cores
When a single thread consumes all available CPU resources alone
When many waiting threads wake simultaneously overwhelming resources
When many threads are created causing the system to slow down
explanation: The thundering herd problem occurs when many blocked threads wake simultaneously, overwhelming the resource they compete for.

text: What is work stealing in parallel task scheduling?
options: Idle threads take tasks from busy threads' queues for balance
Threads duplicating work done by other threads for redundancy
Threads stealing CPU time from other threads for faster execution
The scheduler removing low-priority tasks to free up resources
explanation: Work stealing lets idle threads take pending tasks from busy threads' queues, improving load balance.

text: How does the read-copy-update (RCU) mechanism work in concurrent systems?
options: Readers lock data before reading; writers wait for all readers
All operations require exclusive locks for both reads and writes
Readers access data without locks; writers create updated copies
Data is copied to each thread's private memory before any access
explanation: RCU allows lock-free reads while writers create updated copies, synchronizing through grace periods.

text: What is the difference between data parallelism and task parallelism?
options: Both data and task parallelism are identical concepts in practice
Task parallelism applies different operations to same data always
Data parallelism is for CPUs only; task parallelism for GPUs only
Data parallelism applies same operation to different data portions
explanation: Data parallelism applies the same operation to different data portions; task parallelism runs different operations concurrently.

text: What is a unit test?
options: A test of the entire application system
A test of network connectivity speed
A test of a single small code component
A test of the user interface design layout
explanation: A unit test verifies the correctness of a single small component, typically a function or method.

text: What is a breakpoint in debugging?
options: A marker indicating the end of a code block
A point where the code has a syntax error
A point where the program terminates normally
A marker where execution pauses for inspection
explanation: A breakpoint is a marker that pauses program execution at a specific line for inspection.

text: What is a bug in software?
options: An error causing unexpected program behavior
A security update for the application code
A comment left in the source code text
A feature that works exactly as intended
explanation: A bug is an error, flaw, or fault that causes a program to behave unexpectedly.

text: What does a debugger tool do?
options: It compiles source code into machine language
It designs the user interface for applications
It helps find and fix errors in program code
It automatically writes code for the developer
explanation: A debugger helps developers find and fix errors by allowing step-by-step code execution and inspection.

text: What is a test case?
options: The outer casing of a testing hardware device
A folder containing all source code files used
A specific scenario to verify expected behavior
A function that generates random test data sets
explanation: A test case is a specific scenario with inputs and expected outputs used to verify program behavior.

text: What is a syntax error?
options: A violation of programming language grammar rules
An error caused by network connection failure
A mistake in the user interface visual design
An error in the logic of the program algorithm
explanation: A syntax error violates the grammatical rules of the programming language.

text: What does stepping through code mean?
options: Executing code one line at a time to inspect flow
Deleting unused lines of code from the program
Running the entire program as fast as possible
Skipping over sections of code during execution
explanation: Stepping through code executes it one line at a time, allowing developers to inspect program flow.

text: What is a test suite?
options: A software tool for writing source code files
A single test case for one specific function
A room where testing equipment is kept stored
A collection of related test cases grouped together
explanation: A test suite is a collection of related test cases organized together for systematic testing.

text: What is a runtime error?
options: An error that occurs while the program is running
An error that prevents the IDE from opening up
An error in the project configuration file text
An error detected during code compilation phase
explanation: A runtime error occurs during program execution, causing unexpected behavior or crashes.

text: What is the purpose of print debugging?
options: To print the source code on paper for review
To display the final output to the end user
To print test results in a formatted report doc
To add print statements to trace program values
explanation: Print debugging uses print statements to output variable values and trace program execution flow.

text: What is test-driven development (TDD)?
options: Writing tests after all code is complete and deployed
Developing tests and code simultaneously without any plan
Writing tests before code then implementing to pass tests
Testing only the most critical features of the application
explanation: TDD writes tests first, then implements the minimum code needed to pass those tests.

text: What is code coverage in testing?
options: The total lines of code in the entire project codebase
The percentage of code executed during test suite runs
The number of developers who have reviewed the code
The amount of documentation written for the codebase
explanation: Code coverage measures the percentage of code lines, branches, or paths executed during testing.

text: What is an integration test?
options: A test verifying a single function works in isolation
A test ensuring code compiles without any syntax errors
A test measuring the speed of algorithm execution time
A test verifying that multiple components work together
explanation: Integration tests verify that multiple components or modules work correctly together.

text: What is mocking in unit testing?
options: Replacing real dependencies with controlled fake objects
Ridiculing code that is poorly written by developers
Copying production data to use in test environments
Running tests multiple times to check for consistency
explanation: Mocking replaces real dependencies with controlled fake objects to isolate the unit being tested.

text: What is regression testing?
options: Testing the application on different operating systems platforms
Re-running tests to ensure changes have not broken existing code
Testing new features before they are implemented in code
Testing the program under extreme load and stress conditions
explanation: Regression testing re-runs existing tests after changes to ensure nothing previously working is broken.

text: What is a profiler used for in optimization?
options: Measuring performance bottlenecks in program execution
Formatting source code to follow style guidelines set
Writing automated test cases for the program codebase
Translating code from one programming language to another
explanation: A profiler measures program performance, identifying bottlenecks in CPU usage, memory, and execution time.

text: What is the difference between white-box and black-box testing?
options: Both approaches test code in exactly the same way always
White-box tests the UI; black-box tests the backend code
White-box is manual testing; black-box is automated testing
White-box tests with code knowledge; black-box without it
explanation: White-box testing uses internal code knowledge; black-box testing only considers inputs and outputs.

text: What is assertion in testing?
options: A statement that imports external libraries into the code
A statement that declares a variable with a specific type
A statement that verifies a condition is true during testing
A statement that prints debug information to the console
explanation: An assertion verifies that a condition is true, failing the test if the condition is false.

text: What is continuous integration (CI)?
options: Integrating third-party libraries into the project once
Continuously writing code without any breaks or pauses
Automatically building and testing code on every commit
Integrating all code at the end of the development cycle
explanation: CI automatically builds and tests code on every commit, catching integration issues early.

text: What is a flaky test and why is it problematic?
options: A test that always fails consistently every time it runs
A test that only works on the original developer's machine
A test that passes or fails inconsistently without code changes
A test that is too slow to run during the CI pipeline build
explanation: A flaky test passes or fails inconsistently, undermining confidence in the test suite.

text: A test suite has 95% code coverage but critical bugs still reach production. What does this indicate?
options: That code coverage is a useless metric that should be abandoned
That coverage alone does not guarantee test quality or edge cases
That 95% coverage is too low and 100% is always required
That the remaining 5% of uncovered code contains all the bugs
explanation: High coverage does not guarantee quality; tests may lack meaningful assertions or miss edge cases.

text: What is mutation testing and how does it evaluate test suite quality?
options: It tests the program with mutated or corrupted input data files
It modifies the test execution order to find order dependencies
It introduces small code changes to check if tests detect them
It randomly changes test inputs to find flaky test failures
explanation: Mutation testing introduces small code changes (mutants) and checks if tests detect them, evaluating test effectiveness.

text: What is the difference between performance testing and load testing?
options: Performance tests measure speed; load tests measure correctness
Performance measures response under conditions; load tests capacity
Performance testing is manual; load testing is always automated
Both terms describe identical testing activities in all contexts
explanation: Performance testing measures response times under various conditions; load testing specifically tests system capacity limits.

text: A developer uses a memory profiler and finds objects that are referenced but never used. What is this problem called?
options: A segmentation fault caused by accessing invalid memory address
A buffer overflow caused by writing past array boundaries
A logical memory leak where references prevent garbage collection
A stack overflow caused by deep recursive function calls
explanation: A logical memory leak occurs when objects are referenced but never used, preventing garbage collection.

text: What is property-based testing and how does it differ from example-based testing?
options: Property-based verifies invariants with generated inputs randomly
Both approaches use identical methodologies for test generation
Property-based is slower and less effective than example-based
Property-based uses fixed examples; example-based uses random ones
explanation: Property-based testing verifies invariants using randomly generated inputs, covering more cases than fixed examples.

text: What is chaos engineering and how does it improve system reliability?
options: It intentionally injects failures to discover system weaknesses
It randomly deletes source code files to test backup systems
It introduces random code changes to test developer adaptability
It creates chaotic user interfaces to test user error handling
explanation: Chaos engineering intentionally injects failures into production systems to discover and fix weaknesses proactively.

text: What is the difference between static analysis and dynamic analysis?
options: Static runs code to find bugs; dynamic examines code without running
Static examines code without running; dynamic analyzes during execution
Static is for compiled languages only; dynamic for interpreted only
Both static and dynamic analysis require running the program code
explanation: Static analysis examines code without executing it; dynamic analysis monitors behavior during execution.

text: How does delta debugging systematically minimize a failing test case?
options: It deletes random lines until the test no longer demonstrates failure
It adds more test cases until the failure becomes easier to understand
It compares failing test with passing test to find code differences
It systematically removes portions to find minimal failure-inducing input
explanation: Delta debugging systematically removes portions of the failing input to find the minimal subset that triggers the failure.

text: What is the purpose of fuzzing as a testing technique?
options: Generating clear and readable test cases for documentation
Measuring the fuzziness or uncertainty of test result accuracy
Feeding random or malformed input to find crashes and vulnerabilities
Testing the user interface with blurred or fuzzy screen rendering
explanation: Fuzzing feeds random or malformed inputs to programs to discover crashes, hangs, and security vulnerabilities.

text: What is the testing pyramid and what does it recommend for test distribution?
options: More unit tests at base, fewer integration, fewest UI tests at top
More UI tests at top, fewer unit tests at bottom of pyramid
Only unit tests with no integration or end-to-end tests at all
Equal numbers of each test type across all levels of the pyramid
explanation: The testing pyramid recommends many fast unit tests at the base, fewer integration tests, and fewest slow UI tests.

text: What is version control?
options: Limiting the number of code files in project
Controlling access permissions to the server
Controlling which version of language is used
Tracking and managing changes to source code
explanation: Version control tracks and manages changes to source code over time, enabling collaboration.

text: What is a code review?
options: Examining code by peers for quality and issues
An automated test that runs on every commit
A process of deleting unused code from files
A tool that formats source code automatically
explanation: A code review is the practice of having peers examine code for quality, bugs, and adherence to standards.

text: What does DRY stand for in programming?
options: Deploy Run Yield in application lifecycle
Do Repeat Yourself for code clarity
Debug Review Yell in team collaboration
Don't Repeat Yourself in code writing
explanation: DRY stands for Don't Repeat Yourself, a principle to avoid code duplication.

text: What is a branch in version control?
options: A copy of the main code for independent work
A tool for merging two programs into one
A comment added to track code change history
A permanent deletion of code from repository
explanation: A branch is an independent copy of the codebase for developing features without affecting the main code.

text: What is refactoring?
options: Adding new features to existing program code
Restructuring code without changing its behavior
Removing all comments from the source code
Converting code to a different language entirely
explanation: Refactoring improves code structure and readability without changing its external behavior.

text: What is a commit in version control?
options: Running the program to check for any errors
Deleting a file from the code repository
A request to merge code into main branch
A saved snapshot of changes to the codebase
explanation: A commit is a saved snapshot of changes to the codebase in the version control system.

text: What is agile software development?
options: A single long development phase with no feedback
An iterative approach with frequent delivery cycles
A technique for debugging code more efficiently
A method of writing code as fast as possible
explanation: Agile is an iterative development approach emphasizing frequent delivery, collaboration, and adaptation.

text: What is documentation in software development?
options: The user interface of the application system
Written explanations of code and system design
The compiled binary output of the program
The source code of the program itself only
explanation: Documentation provides written explanations of code, APIs, system design, and usage instructions.

text: What is a pull request?
options: A request to review and merge code into a branch
A request to compile the code into executable
A request to delete code from the repository
A request to pull data from a database server
explanation: A pull request proposes code changes for review and merging into a target branch.

text: What is the purpose of coding standards?
options: To limit the number of lines per source file
To make all code run faster on every machine
To prevent any bugs from occurring in programs
To ensure consistent readable code across team
explanation: Coding standards ensure consistent, readable code style across the team for maintainability.

text: What is continuous deployment (CD)?
options: Deploying all code changes at the end of the project
Continuously writing deployment scripts for the team
Deploying code only during scheduled maintenance windows
Automatically deploying every passing build to production
explanation: Continuous deployment automatically deploys every code change that passes the pipeline to production.

text: What is the SOLID principle in software design?
options: A single principle about writing solid unbreakable programs
A principle about making code run on solid-state hardware
Five design principles for maintainable object-oriented code
A principle requiring all code to be stored in solid files
explanation: SOLID is five design principles (SRP, OCP, LSP, ISP, DIP) for creating maintainable OO code.

text: What is technical debt?
options: The monetary cost of purchasing development tools and licenses
The amount of time spent learning new programming technologies
The implied cost of future rework from choosing quick solutions
The storage cost of maintaining large code repositories online
explanation: Technical debt is the implied cost of future rework caused by choosing quick solutions over proper ones.

text: What is a microservice architecture?
options: An architecture using very small functions for all operations
A development practice of writing very concise source code
An architecture breaking an app into small independent services
A very small application with minimal functionality only
explanation: Microservice architecture breaks an application into small, independently deployable services.

text: What is pair programming?
options: Two developers working together on the same code actively
Two programs running in parallel on the same computer
Two developers working on separate tasks simultaneously
Two teams competing to write the same feature faster
explanation: Pair programming has two developers working together on the same code, with one typing and one reviewing.

text: What is the purpose of a linter?
options: To encrypt source code to protect intellectual property
To convert code from one programming language to another
To analyze code for style issues and potential errors found
To compile source code into optimized machine code output
explanation: A linter analyzes code for style violations, potential errors, and adherence to coding standards.

text: What is the difference between monolithic and microservice architectures?
options: Monolithic is one deployable unit; microservices are many separate
Both architectures deploy and scale in exactly the same way
Microservices use one language; monolithic uses many languages
Monolithic is faster; microservices are slower in all cases
explanation: Monolithic deploys as one unit; microservices deploy as many independent services that can scale separately.

text: What is a sprint in Scrum methodology?
options: A fixed-length iteration for completing a set of planned work
A quick fix applied to production code in emergency situations
A code review session held at the end of every work day
A performance test measuring application response time speed
explanation: A sprint is a fixed-length iteration (typically 1-4 weeks) for completing a set of planned work items.

text: What is the purpose of a retrospective in agile development?
options: To assign tasks to team members for the next sprint period
To review the final product before releasing to customers
To reflect on the process and identify areas for improvement
To estimate the effort required for upcoming development work
explanation: A retrospective allows the team to reflect on their process and identify improvements for future sprints.

text: What is infrastructure as code (IaC)?
options: Managing infrastructure through machine-readable definition files
Converting infrastructure diagrams into programming source code
Writing code that runs on physical infrastructure hardware
Coding directly on production servers without version control
explanation: IaC manages infrastructure through machine-readable files, enabling version control and automation.

text: A team has a large codebase with no tests. What is the recommended approach to introduce testing?
options: Write tests for all code at once before making any changes
Hire a separate testing team to write all tests independently
Start by adding tests around code that changes most frequently
Delete all existing code and rewrite it with tests from scratch
explanation: Start by adding tests around frequently changed or critical code, gradually increasing coverage over time.

text: What is the strangler fig pattern for legacy system modernization?
options: Gradually replacing legacy components while keeping system running
Wrapping the legacy system in a new interface without changes
Immediately replacing the entire legacy system with new code
Documenting the legacy system before shutting it down entirely
explanation: The strangler fig pattern gradually replaces legacy components with new ones while keeping the system operational.

text: How does trunk-based development differ from GitFlow?
options: Both approaches use identical branching and merging strategies
Trunk-based uses short-lived branches merging to main frequently
Trunk-based does not use version control; GitFlow requires it
Trunk-based uses long-lived branches; GitFlow uses short-lived ones
explanation: Trunk-based development uses short-lived branches that merge frequently to main, unlike GitFlow's long-lived branches.

text: What is the twelve-factor app methodology?
options: A methodology requiring exactly twelve source code files per app
A set of best practices for building scalable cloud-native apps
A testing methodology requiring twelve types of tests minimum
A security framework with twelve levels of access permissions
explanation: The twelve-factor app defines best practices for building portable, scalable, cloud-native applications.

text: What is observability in software systems and how does it differ from monitoring?
options: Observability infers internal state from outputs; monitoring tracks metrics
Observability and monitoring are identical concepts in practice
Monitoring provides deeper insight than observability in all cases
Observability only tracks errors; monitoring tracks all system events
explanation: Observability infers system internal state from external outputs; monitoring tracks predefined metrics and alerts.

text: What is the purpose of feature flags in software deployment?
options: They indicate which programming language features are being used
They enable toggling features on or off without code deployment
They flag code that contains bugs for developers to fix later
They mark features as complete in the project management tool
explanation: Feature flags enable toggling features on or off in production without deploying new code.

text: How does domain-driven design (DDD) improve complex software projects?
options: It restricts development to a single programming language only
It aligns code structure with business domain using ubiquitous language
It eliminates the need for any testing in the development process
It focuses on optimizing database domain and query performance
explanation: DDD aligns software structure with the business domain, using ubiquitous language for clear communication.

text: What is the difference between horizontal and vertical scaling strategies?
options: Horizontal adds more machines; vertical adds resources to existing
Horizontal adds resources to one machine; vertical adds more machines
Horizontal is for databases only; vertical is for applications only
Both strategies add resources in exactly the same way to systems
explanation: Horizontal scaling adds more machines; vertical scaling adds more resources (CPU, RAM) to existing machines.

text: What is the concept of eventual consistency in distributed systems?
options: All nodes always have the exact same data at every point in time
Only the primary node has consistent data; replicas do not
Data consistency is never achieved in any distributed system
All nodes will converge to the same state given enough time
explanation: Eventual consistency guarantees that all nodes will converge to the same state given sufficient time without new updates.

text: What is the purpose of a service mesh in microservice architectures?
options: It handles service-to-service communication, security, and observability
It combines all microservices into a single monolithic application
It stores all microservice data in a single shared database
It generates source code for new microservices automatically
explanation: A service mesh manages service-to-service communication, security, load balancing, and observability transparently.

text: What is a variable in programming?
options: A named storage location in memory
A hardware component
A type of loop
A function that returns nothing
explanation: A variable is a named storage location in memory that holds a value which can change during program execution.

text: What is the purpose of indentation in Python?
options: It improves readability only
It defines code blocks and scope
It is used for commenting
It speeds up execution
explanation: In Python, indentation is syntactically significant and defines code blocks, unlike languages that use braces.

text: Which of the following is an example of an interpreted language?
options: Assembly
Rust
C
Python
explanation: Python is an interpreted language where code is executed line by line by the interpreter rather than being compiled to machine code first.

text: What is the output of a program?
options: The compiler error messages
The result produced by executing the program
The input given by the user
The source code written by the programmer
explanation: Output is the result or data produced by a program after processing, displayed to the user or saved to a file.

text: What does the term 'syntax' refer to in programming?
options: The rules governing the structure of code
The speed of program execution
The process of finding bugs
The meaning behind the code logic
explanation: Syntax refers to the set of rules that define the correct structure and format of statements in a programming language.

text: What is a binary number system?
options: A system that uses hexadecimal digits
A system based on the number 8
A system that uses digits 0 through 9
A system that uses only 0 and 1
explanation: The binary number system uses only two digits, 0 and 1, and is the fundamental number system used by computers.

text: What is a logic error in a program?
options: An error that crashes the program immediately
An error caught by the compiler
An error where the program runs but produces wrong results
An error caused by missing semicolons
explanation: A logic error occurs when a program runs without crashing but produces incorrect results due to flawed reasoning in the code.

text: What is the role of an operating system for running programs?
options: It designs the user interface of programs
It writes the source code automatically
It compiles all programs into machine code
It manages hardware resources and provides services to programs
explanation: The operating system manages hardware resources like CPU, memory, and I/O devices, and provides services that programs need to execute.

text: What is a keyword in a programming language?
options: Any word used as a variable name
A reserved word with a special meaning in the language
A comment in the source code
A user-defined function name
explanation: Keywords are reserved words that have predefined meanings in a programming language and cannot be used as identifiers.

text: What does the term 'execute' mean in programming?
options: To delete a program from memory
To save a file to disk
To run a program and carry out its instructions
To write source code in an editor
explanation: Executing a program means running it so the computer carries out the instructions specified in the code.

text: What is the difference between a compiler error and a linker error?
options: Linker errors happen before compiler errors in the build process
Compiler errors are warnings while linker errors crash the system
There is no difference between them
Compiler errors occur in syntax checking while linker errors occur when combining object files
explanation: Compiler errors occur during syntax and semantic analysis of individual source files, while linker errors occur when the linker tries to combine object files and resolve external references.

text: What is the purpose of an object file in compilation?
options: It is the final executable program
It contains machine code that has not yet been linked
It stores the original source code
It holds only debug information
explanation: An object file contains compiled machine code from a single source file but has unresolved references that the linker must resolve to create the final executable.

text: What is the difference between procedural and object-oriented programming?
options: Procedural languages cannot handle large programs
OOP cannot use functions at all
Procedural is faster than OOP in all cases
Procedural uses functions to organize code while OOP uses classes and objects
explanation: Procedural programming organizes code around functions and procedures, while OOP organizes code around objects that bundle data and behavior together.

text: What is a semantic error in programming?
options: An error where the code has incorrect punctuation
An error where the code is syntactically valid but logically meaningless
An error caused by using the wrong file extension
An error that prevents compilation due to missing brackets
explanation: A semantic error occurs when code is syntactically correct but does not make logical sense, such as adding a string to a boolean where the operation has no meaningful result.

text: What is the role of a loader in program execution?
options: It loads the executable into memory and prepares it for execution
It links object files together into an executable
It compiles the source code into machine code
It checks the program for syntax errors
explanation: A loader is responsible for loading the executable program into main memory, performing address relocation, and preparing it for execution by the CPU.

text: What is the difference between compiled and managed languages?
options: Managed languages must be interpreted line by line
Compiled languages produce native code while managed languages run on a virtual machine
Compiled languages cannot use garbage collection
Managed languages are always slower than compiled languages
explanation: Compiled languages like C produce native machine code, while managed languages like Java and C# compile to intermediate code that runs on a virtual machine with features like garbage collection.

text: What is an abstract syntax tree (AST) used for?
options: Representing the hierarchical structure of source code during compilation
Drawing flowcharts of the program
Storing the final executable code
Managing memory allocation at runtime
explanation: An AST is a tree representation of the syntactic structure of source code, used by compilers and interpreters to analyze and transform code during compilation.

text: What is the purpose of a garbage-collected runtime environment?
options: To optimize CPU instruction scheduling
To prevent all types of programming errors
To compile code faster than traditional compilers
To automatically reclaim memory that is no longer in use
explanation: A garbage-collected runtime automatically identifies and reclaims memory that is no longer referenced by the program, preventing memory leaks without manual deallocation.

text: What is the difference between a scripting language and a systems language?
options: Scripting languages cannot define functions
Systems languages are always object-oriented
Scripting languages prioritize ease of use while systems languages prioritize performance and control
Scripting languages can only run on web browsers
explanation: Scripting languages like Python prioritize developer productivity and ease of use, while systems languages like C and Rust prioritize performance, memory control, and hardware access.

text: What is the purpose of intermediate representation (IR) in compilers?
options: To display the program output to the user
To serve as a platform-independent form between source code and machine code
To manage the file system during compilation
To encrypt the source code for security
explanation: Intermediate representation is a platform-independent code form used internally by compilers, allowing optimizations to be applied before generating target-specific machine code.

text: How does the LLVM compiler infrastructure enable language-independent optimization?
options: By using a common intermediate representation that multiple language frontends can target
By interpreting all programs at runtime without compilation
By compiling all languages directly to x86 machine code
By requiring all languages to use the same syntax
explanation: LLVM uses a common IR that any language frontend can target, allowing the same optimization passes and code generation backends to be shared across many different programming languages.

text: What is the difference between structural and nominal type equivalence?
options: Structural equivalence is used only in compiled languages
Nominal equivalence ignores all type information
They produce identical results in every programming language
Structural equivalence compares type structure while nominal equivalence compares type names
explanation: Structural type equivalence considers two types equal if they have the same structure, while nominal equivalence requires types to have the same declared name, even if their structures are identical.

text: What is the Church-Turing thesis and its significance for programming?
options: It proves that all programs will eventually halt
It states that any computable function can be computed by a Turing machine
It defines the maximum speed of any computer program
It establishes that compiled code is always faster than interpreted code
explanation: The Church-Turing thesis states that any effectively computable function can be computed by a Turing machine, establishing the theoretical limits of what any programming language or computer can compute.

text: What is the expression problem in programming language design?
options: The challenge of parsing mathematical expressions efficiently
The problem of evaluating boolean expressions with short-circuit logic
The difficulty of adding both new data types and new operations without modifying existing code
The difficulty of handling operator precedence in complex expressions
explanation: The expression problem refers to the challenge of extending a program with both new data types and new operations over those types without modifying existing code, highlighting a tension between OOP and functional approaches.

text: How does dependent typing differ from traditional static typing?
options: Dependent typing eliminates the need for any type annotations
Dependent types only work with integer values
Dependent typing is another name for dynamic typing
Dependent types allow types to depend on runtime values, enabling more precise type specifications
explanation: Dependent types allow types to be parameterized by values, enabling specifications like 'a list of exactly n elements,' which can catch more errors at compile time than traditional static type systems.

text: What is the difference between call-by-name and call-by-need evaluation strategies?
options: Call-by-name re-evaluates arguments each time they are used while call-by-need caches the result after first evaluation
Call-by-need always evaluates arguments before passing them
Call-by-name prevents all side effects in function calls
Call-by-name is the same as call-by-value
explanation: Call-by-name substitutes the unevaluated argument expression each time it is referenced, potentially evaluating it multiple times. Call-by-need (lazy evaluation) evaluates it once and caches the result.

text: What is effect typing and how does it extend traditional type systems?
options: It measures the performance impact of type checking
It tracks computational side effects in the type system alongside value types
It replaces all runtime errors with compile-time type errors
It only applies to visual effects in graphical programming
explanation: Effect typing extends type systems to track side effects like I/O, state mutation, or exceptions, allowing the compiler to enforce purity constraints and reason about program behavior beyond just value types.

text: What is the significance of the lambda calculus as a foundation for programming languages?
options: It establishes the rules for object-oriented inheritance hierarchies
It provides a minimal formal system that can express any computable function using only function abstraction and application
It is a specialized calculus for mathematical computation only
It defines how compilers optimize machine code instructions
explanation: Lambda calculus is a formal system based on function abstraction and application that is Turing-complete, serving as the theoretical foundation for functional programming languages and computation theory.

text: What is gradual typing and what problem does it solve?
options: It allows mixing statically typed and dynamically typed code in the same program
It gradually removes type annotations as the program matures
It automatically converts all types to strings at runtime
It types variables based on the order they are declared
explanation: Gradual typing allows parts of a program to be statically typed and other parts dynamically typed, enabling incremental adoption of type checking in existing dynamically typed codebases.

text: What is the difference between total and partial functions in programming language theory?
options: Total functions are only found in imperative languages
Total functions handle all data types while partial functions handle only one
Partial functions are faster because they skip some computations
Total functions return a result for every possible input while partial functions may not terminate or may be undefined for some inputs
explanation: A total function is defined for all inputs in its domain and always terminates, while a partial function may be undefined for some inputs or may not terminate, which matters for program correctness verification.

text: What is an integer data type used for?
options: Storing true or false values
Storing whole numbers without decimal points
Storing text values
Storing images and multimedia
explanation: The integer data type is used to store whole numbers (positive, negative, or zero) without any fractional or decimal component.

text: What is a floating-point number?
options: A number used only for counting
A number stored as text
A number that is always positive
A number that includes a decimal point
explanation: A floating-point number is a data type that represents numbers with a decimal point, used for real numbers like 3.14 or -0.5.

text: What is the byte data type typically used for?
options: Storing long text strings
Storing very large decimal numbers
Storing date and time values
Storing small integer values in the range 0 to 255
explanation: The byte data type typically stores small integer values, usually in the range 0 to 255, using a single byte (8 bits) of memory.

text: What does initializing a variable mean?
options: Deleting the variable from memory
Assigning a value to a variable for the first time
Changing the data type of a variable
Declaring the variable without a value
explanation: Initializing a variable means giving it an initial value at the time of declaration or shortly after, so it holds a defined value before being used.

text: What is a long data type?
options: A type used exclusively for loop counters
An integer type with a larger range than int
A type that stores only positive numbers
A data type for storing very short strings
explanation: The long data type is an integer type that uses more bits than a standard int, allowing it to store a larger range of whole number values.

text: What is the difference between a literal and a variable?
options: A variable cannot change its value while a literal can
Variables are only used in loops
A literal is a fixed value written directly in code while a variable is a named storage location
Literals are only used for strings
explanation: A literal is a fixed value written directly in the source code (like 42 or 'hello'), while a variable is a named reference to a storage location whose value can change.

text: What is an array data type?
options: A collection of elements of the same type stored in contiguous memory
A single value stored in memory
A type that stores only Boolean values
A special type of function
explanation: An array is a data type that stores a fixed-size collection of elements of the same type in contiguous memory locations, accessed by index.

text: What does the short data type represent?
options: A text string with few characters
A fractional number with limited precision
An integer type with a smaller range than int
A Boolean value that is always false
explanation: The short data type is an integer type that uses fewer bits than int, resulting in a smaller range of values but less memory usage.

text: What is a double data type?
options: An integer type with twice the range of long
A type that doubles the value stored automatically
A type that stores two variables at once
A floating-point type with double the precision of float
explanation: A double is a floating-point data type that uses 64 bits, providing approximately double the precision of a 32-bit float for representing decimal numbers.

text: What is a reference type in programming?
options: A type that can only hold numeric values
A type used only in functional programming
A type that references external files
A type that stores the memory address of data rather than the data itself
explanation: A reference type stores a memory address (reference) pointing to where the actual data is located, rather than containing the data directly in the variable.

text: What is autoboxing in Java?
options: Automatically compiling code without user intervention
Automatically converting primitive types to their wrapper class objects
Automatically resizing arrays when they are full
Automatically casting all types to strings
explanation: Autoboxing is Java's automatic conversion of primitive types (like int) to their corresponding wrapper class objects (like Integer), and vice versa (unboxing).

text: What is the difference between a weakly typed and a strongly typed language?
options: Strongly typed languages cannot perform arithmetic operations
Weakly typed languages are always interpreted
Weakly typed languages do not have data types at all
Weakly typed languages allow implicit type conversions while strongly typed languages restrict them
explanation: Weakly typed languages like JavaScript allow implicit type conversions between different types, while strongly typed languages like Python restrict such conversions and require explicit casting.

text: What is a tuple data type?
options: A type that stores exactly two values
An immutable ordered collection of elements that can have different types
A mutable list of elements of the same type
A type used only for database records
explanation: A tuple is an immutable ordered collection that can hold elements of different types, commonly used to group related values without creating a full class.

text: What is type coercion?
options: Explicitly casting a variable to a new type using syntax
Restricting a variable to a single type permanently
Automatic conversion of a value from one type to another by the language
Removing type information at compile time
explanation: Type coercion is the automatic or implicit conversion of a value from one data type to another, performed by the language runtime, such as converting a number to a string during concatenation.

text: What is the difference between mutable and immutable data types?
options: Mutable types can be changed after creation while immutable types cannot
Mutable types can only store numbers
Mutable types use more memory than immutable types always
Immutable types are only available in functional languages
explanation: Mutable data types allow their values to be modified after creation (like lists in Python), while immutable types create a new object when changed (like strings in Java).

text: What is a generic type parameter?
options: A placeholder for a type that is specified when the class or method is used
A type that is automatically inferred at runtime
A default type assigned when no type is specified
A type that can only hold generic string values
explanation: A generic type parameter (like T in List<T>) is a placeholder that allows classes and methods to operate on different types while maintaining type safety, specified at instantiation or invocation.

text: What is the purpose of the void type?
options: To represent a variable that holds zero
To declare a variable with no type
To create an empty array
To indicate that a function returns no value
explanation: The void type indicates that a function or method does not return any value, serving as a return type declaration for procedures that perform actions without producing a result.

text: What is a tagged union or discriminated union?
options: A type that stores one of several possible types along with a tag indicating which type is active
A type used only in database programming
A type that merges two arrays into one
A type that combines multiple unrelated types randomly
explanation: A tagged union (also called a discriminated union or sum type) stores a value that could be one of several types, along with a tag that indicates which type the value currently holds.

text: Why does 0.1 + 0.2 not equal 0.3 in most programming languages?
options: Because floating-point representation cannot exactly represent some decimal fractions
Because addition is not supported for decimal numbers
Because decimal numbers are stored as strings internally
Because the compiler rounds all numbers to integers
explanation: IEEE 754 floating-point format uses binary fractions, and values like 0.1 and 0.2 cannot be represented exactly in binary, leading to tiny rounding errors in arithmetic.

text: What is the difference between a struct and a class in C#?
options: Structs support inheritance but classes do not
Classes cannot be instantiated while structs can
Structs can have methods but classes cannot
Structs are value types allocated on the stack while classes are reference types allocated on the heap
explanation: In C#, structs are value types typically stored on the stack and copied by value, while classes are reference types stored on the heap and copied by reference.

text: What is a higher-kinded type and which languages support it?
options: A type reserved for advanced mathematical computations
A type that is always at the top of the inheritance hierarchy
A type constructor that takes other type constructors as parameters, supported by Haskell and Scala
A type that can store higher-precision decimal numbers
explanation: Higher-kinded types are type constructors that are parameterized over other type constructors (like Functor over List), enabling powerful abstractions. Languages like Haskell and Scala support them natively.

text: What is the difference between covariance and contravariance in generic type parameters?
options: Covariance applies to input parameters and contravariance to output parameters
They both produce identical behavior in all type systems
Covariance preserves the type ordering while contravariance reverses it
Covariance is used only in dynamically typed languages
explanation: Covariance preserves the subtype relationship (if Dog extends Animal, List<Dog> is a subtype of List<Animal>), while contravariance reverses it, typically applying to function input parameters.

text: What is a refinement type in programming languages?
options: A type that restricts a base type with a predicate specifying allowed values
A type that refines a class through inheritance only
A type that can only be used in pattern matching
A type that has been optimized for faster runtime performance
explanation: A refinement type narrows a base type by adding a predicate constraint, such as specifying that an integer must be positive, enabling more precise compile-time guarantees.

text: How does Rust's ownership model interact with data types to ensure memory safety?
options: Rust uses garbage collection for all heap-allocated types
Rust copies all values automatically without restriction
All data types in Rust are allocated on the stack
Each value has exactly one owner, and the value is dropped when the owner goes out of scope
explanation: Rust's ownership model ensures each value has a single owner at any time, and the value is automatically deallocated when the owner goes out of scope, preventing use-after-free and double-free bugs.

text: What is the difference between existential types and universal types?
options: Existential types are used only for numbers while universal types handle strings
Universal types must be defined at compile time but existential at runtime
They are different names for the same concept
Universal types work for all types (forall) while existential types hide the concrete type (there exists)
explanation: Universal types (forall T) mean the code works for any type the caller chooses, while existential types (exists T) mean some specific type is used internally but hidden from the caller.

text: What is type-level programming and how is it used in practice?
options: Using types only for documentation purposes
Automatically generating types from database schemas
Programming without any type annotations
Writing programs that execute entirely at the type level during compilation to enforce complex constraints
explanation: Type-level programming uses the type system itself as a programming language, performing computations at compile time to enforce complex invariants, commonly used in Haskell and TypeScript.

text: What is the difference between nominal subtyping and structural subtyping for interfaces?
options: Structural subtyping requires classes to declare they implement an interface
Nominal subtyping is only used in interpreted languages
Nominal subtyping requires explicit declaration of subtype relationships while structural subtyping checks compatibility by shape
They produce identical behavior in all object-oriented languages
explanation: Nominal subtyping (Java, C#) requires a class to explicitly declare it implements an interface, while structural subtyping (TypeScript, Go) considers a type compatible if it has the required structure.

text: What is a linear type system and what guarantee does it provide?
options: A type system that arranges all types in a linear hierarchy
A type system that evaluates type expressions in linear time
A type system that only supports linear data structures
A type system that ensures every value is used exactly once, preventing resource leaks
explanation: A linear type system ensures each value is used exactly once, guaranteeing resources like file handles or network connections are properly consumed without duplication or forgetting.

text: What is a GADT (Generalized Algebraic Data Type)?
options: A data type that can only hold algebraic expressions
An algebraic data type where constructors can specify different type parameters for the resulting type
A type alias for complex generic signatures
A standard generic type available in all languages
explanation: GADTs extend algebraic data types by allowing each constructor to produce a different specialization of the result type, enabling more precise type-level reasoning and safer code patterns.

text: What is the bottom type (Nothing/Never) and how is it used in type systems?
options: A type that is the subtype of all types and has no possible values, used for functions that never return
The default type assigned to uninitialized variables
A type used exclusively for error codes
A type representing the smallest possible integer value
explanation: The bottom type (Nothing in Scala, Never in TypeScript) is a subtype of every type with no inhabitants, used for functions that always throw exceptions or never terminate.

text: What is an arithmetic operator?
options: An operator that combines Boolean values
An operator that assigns values to variables
An operator that performs mathematical calculations like addition and subtraction
An operator that compares two values
explanation: Arithmetic operators perform mathematical operations such as addition (+), subtraction (-), multiplication (*), and division (/) on numeric values.

text: What is the result of 15 / 4 in integer arithmetic?
explanation: In integer arithmetic, 15 / 4 equals 3 because the decimal portion is truncated, keeping only the whole number part of the result.

text: What does the less-than-or-equal operator (<=) check?
options: Whether two values are exactly equal
Whether the left value is less than or equal to the right value
Whether the left value is greater than the right value
Whether the left value is strictly less than the right value
explanation: The <= operator returns true if the left operand is less than or equal to the right operand, combining the less-than and equality checks.

text: What does the subtraction operator (-) do?
options: It adds two numbers together
It multiplies two numbers
It divides one number by another
It calculates the difference between two numbers
explanation: The subtraction operator (-) calculates the difference by subtracting the right operand from the left operand.

text: What is the decrement operator (--) used for?
options: Increasing a variable's value by one
Decreasing a variable's value by one
Setting a variable to zero
Doubling a variable's value
explanation: The decrement operator (--) decreases the value of a variable by one, used as a shorthand for variable = variable - 1.

text: What is a comparison operator?
options: An operator that performs string concatenation
An operator that allocates memory
An operator that compares two values and returns a Boolean result
An operator that modifies the value of a variable
explanation: Comparison operators (like ==, !=, <, >) compare two values and return a Boolean (true or false) result indicating the relationship between them.

text: What does the division operator (/) do?
options: It compares two numbers
It finds the remainder of division
It divides the left operand by the right operand
It multiplies two numbers together
explanation: The division operator (/) divides the left operand by the right operand and returns the quotient.

text: What is the compound assignment operator += used for?
options: Comparing whether two values are equal
Adding a value to a variable and storing the result back
Creating a new variable with an initial value
Concatenating two strings permanently
explanation: The += operator adds the right operand to the current value of the left operand and assigns the result back, equivalent to x = x + value.

text: What does the equality operator (==) return?
options: A new variable with the combined value
True if two values are equal, false otherwise
The sum of two values
The larger of two values
explanation: The equality operator (==) compares two values and returns true if they are equal or false if they are not.

text: What is the logical AND operator (&&) used for?
options: Returning true only when both operands are true
Negating a Boolean value
Adding two numbers together
Returning true when at least one operand is true
explanation: The logical AND operator (&&) returns true only when both of its operands are true; if either operand is false, it returns false.

text: What is the difference between logical OR (||) and bitwise OR (|)?
options: Logical OR works on numbers while bitwise OR works on Booleans
They produce identical results for all input types
Bitwise OR short-circuits but logical OR does not
Logical OR works on Boolean values and short-circuits while bitwise OR operates on individual bits
explanation: Logical OR (||) operates on Boolean values and uses short-circuit evaluation (skips the second operand if the first is true), while bitwise OR (|) operates on each bit of integer values independently.

text: What is the purpose of the modulus operator in determining even or odd numbers?
options: If n % 2 equals 1 the number is even, if it equals 0 the number is odd
If n % 3 equals 0 the number is even
If n % 2 equals 0 the number is even, if it equals 1 the number is odd
The modulus operator cannot be used for this purpose
explanation: The expression n % 2 returns the remainder when n is divided by 2. If the remainder is 0, the number is even; if the remainder is 1, the number is odd.

text: What does the bitwise NOT (~) operator do to an integer?
options: It sets all bits to zero
It converts the integer to its absolute value
It flips all the bits, changing 0s to 1s and 1s to 0s
It doubles the value of the integer
explanation: The bitwise NOT (~) operator inverts every bit of the integer, changing each 0 to 1 and each 1 to 0, resulting in the one's complement of the value.

text: What is the optional chaining operator (?.) used for?
options: Creating optional function parameters
Safely accessing nested object properties without throwing if a reference is null
Defining nullable return types
Forcing a null value to throw an exception
explanation: The optional chaining operator (?.) allows safe access to deeply nested properties by returning undefined instead of throwing an error if an intermediate reference is null or undefined.

text: What is the difference between the assignment operator (=) and the equality operator (==)?
options: Neither operator works with numeric values
The assignment operator stores a value in a variable while the equality operator compares two values
They perform the same function in all contexts
The equality operator stores values while the assignment operator compares them
explanation: The assignment operator (=) stores a value in a variable, while the equality operator (==) compares two values and returns a Boolean indicating whether they are equal.
