A BST where each node has a random priority, maintaining BST property on keys and heap property on priorities for probabilistic balance
explanation: A treap assigns random priorities to nodes and maintains BST ordering on keys and heap ordering on priorities. The random priorities provide probabilistic balance, giving expected O(log n) operations.

text: What is the difference between LSM trees and B-trees for write-heavy workloads?
options: LSM trees do not support read operations
They perform identically for all workload types
B-trees are always faster than LSM trees for writes
LSM trees buffer writes in memory and merge sorted runs sequentially, optimizing write throughput, while B-trees update pages in place with random I/O
explanation: LSM trees (Log-Structured Merge trees) buffer writes in memory and flush them as sorted runs, converting random writes to sequential I/O. B-trees update pages in place, generating random I/O but offering faster point reads.

text: What is a HAT-trie and why is it more cache-friendly than a standard trie?
options: It stores multiple characters per node in contiguous arrays, reducing pointer chasing and improving CPU cache utilization
It stores the entire trie in a single array
It hashes all keys before insertion into the trie
It uses hardware acceleration for trie operations
explanation: HAT-tries (Hash Array Mapped Tries) use array-based nodes that store multiple entries contiguously in memory, reducing pointer dereferencing and improving cache locality compared to standard pointer-based tries.

text: What is the difference between wait-free and lock-free concurrent data structures?
options: They provide identical progress guarantees
Wait-free structures are slower than lock-free structures always
Lock-free guarantees system-wide progress while wait-free guarantees per-thread progress in bounded steps
Lock-free structures use locks internally while wait-free does not
explanation: Lock-free data structures guarantee that at least one thread makes progress (no deadlock), while wait-free structures guarantee that every thread completes its operation in a bounded number of steps (no starvation).

text: What is a CRDT (Conflict-free Replicated Data Type) and why is it important for distributed collections?
options: A compressed data type for reducing network bandwidth
A data type that prevents all conflicts by using locks
A data type designed so that concurrent updates on different replicas always converge to the same state without coordination
A data type that replicates data by copying entire collections
explanation: CRDTs are data types mathematically designed so that concurrent updates on different replicas can always be merged deterministically into a consistent state without coordination, enabling eventual consistency in distributed systems.

text: What is a destructor in OOP?
options: A method that copies one object to another
A method that modifies all object properties
A special method called when an object is being destroyed to clean up resources
A method that creates new objects
explanation: A destructor is a special method automatically invoked when an object is being destroyed or garbage collected, used to release resources like file handles or network connections.

text: What is an instance variable?
options: A variable that belongs to a specific object instance of a class
A variable shared by all objects of a class
A variable that cannot change its value
A variable declared outside any class
explanation: An instance variable belongs to a specific object instance and each object has its own copy, as opposed to class/static variables that are shared among all instances.

text: What is method invocation in OOP?
options: Calling a method on an object to execute its code
Deleting a method from a class
Defining a new method inside a class
Copying a method from one class to another
explanation: Method invocation means calling a method on an object (using dot notation like object.method()), triggering the execution of that method's code.

text: What is a subclass or child class?
options: A class that cannot be instantiated
A class that contains only static methods
A class that inherits properties and methods from another class
A class that has no methods
explanation: A subclass (child class) inherits attributes and methods from a superclass (parent class), and can add new features or override inherited behavior.

text: What is the 'private' access modifier?
options: It restricts access to only within the same class
It makes the member accessible from the same package
It allows access from any class in the program
It allows access from subclasses only
explanation: The private access modifier restricts access to the member (field or method) so that it can only be accessed from within the same class where it is declared.

text: What is an abstract method?
options: A method declared without implementation that must be overridden by subclasses
A method that cannot be called
A method that returns abstract data types
A method with a complete implementation
explanation: An abstract method is declared in an abstract class or interface without a body, requiring concrete subclasses to provide the implementation.

text: What is object instantiation?
options: Defining a class in source code
Creating a new object from a class using a constructor
Deleting an object from memory
Copying all methods from one class to another
explanation: Object instantiation is the process of creating a new object (instance) from a class by calling its constructor, allocating memory and initializing the object's state.

text: What is a getter method?
options: A method that retrieves the value of a private field
A method that sets the value of a private field
A method that creates a new field in the class
A method that deletes a field from the object
explanation: A getter method (accessor) returns the value of a private field, providing controlled read access to encapsulated data.

text: What is a setter method?
options: A method that sets or updates the value of a private field with optional validation
A method that removes a field from the object
A method that creates a new class
A method that returns the value of a field
explanation: A setter method (mutator) sets or updates the value of a private field, often including validation logic to ensure the new value is valid before assignment.

text: What is the 'public' access modifier?
options: It allows access only from subclasses
It restricts access to the same class only
It makes the member read-only
It allows access from any class in the program
explanation: The public access modifier allows a member (field or method) to be accessed from any other class in the program, providing the widest level of visibility.

text: What is the difference between an interface and an abstract class in Java?
options: They are identical constructs with different keywords
Abstract classes support multiple inheritance while interfaces do not
Interfaces can have constructors while abstract classes cannot
An interface defines method signatures without state while an abstract class can have both abstract and concrete methods with state
explanation: An interface (before Java 8) defines only method signatures and constants, supporting multiple implementation. An abstract class can have constructors, instance fields, and both abstract and concrete methods.

text: What is the Open/Closed Principle?
options: Software entities should be open for extension but closed for modification
All methods should be public (open) and fields should be private (closed)
Open source code should not be modified by closed source projects
Classes should be open for use and closed for testing
explanation: The Open/Closed Principle states that classes should be open for extension (through inheritance or composition) but closed for modification, allowing new behavior without changing existing code.

text: What is the purpose of the 'protected' access modifier?
options: It makes members accessible only within the same class
It allows access within the same class, subclasses, and same package
It prevents any access to the member
It makes members accessible only through getter methods
explanation: The protected access modifier allows access to the member from within the same class, its subclasses (even in different packages), and other classes in the same package.

text: What is method hiding in Java?
options: Making a method invisible to other classes
Encrypting method implementations for security
Removing a method from a class at runtime
When a subclass defines a static method with the same signature as a static method in the superclass
explanation: Method hiding occurs when a subclass defines a static method with the same signature as a static method in its superclass. Unlike overriding, the method called depends on the reference type, not the object type.

text: What is the Single Responsibility Principle?
options: A program should have only one main class
A class should have only one reason to change, meaning it should have only one responsibility
Each method should have only one parameter
Each class should have only one constructor
explanation: The Single Responsibility Principle states that a class should have only one reason to change, encapsulating a single responsibility, which makes it easier to understand, test, and maintain.

text: What is the difference between shallow copy and deep copy of an object?
options: Shallow copy is always slower than deep copy
They produce identical results for all object types
Deep copy only copies primitive fields
Shallow copy copies only field values (sharing referenced objects) while deep copy recursively copies all referenced objects
explanation: A shallow copy copies field values directly, so reference fields still point to the same objects. A deep copy recursively creates copies of all referenced objects, producing completely independent copies.

text: What is a sealed class in Kotlin or Java?
options: A class that cannot have any methods
A class that restricts which classes can extend it, defining a closed set of subclasses
A class that cannot be imported into other packages
A class that seals its data from garbage collection
explanation: A sealed class restricts its subclasses to a defined set, known at compile time. This enables exhaustive when/switch expressions and models closed type hierarchies.

text: What is the Interface Segregation Principle?
options: All interfaces should be in a separate file
All classes must implement at least one interface
Interfaces should have exactly one method each
Clients should not be forced to depend on methods they do not use
explanation: The Interface Segregation Principle states that no client should be forced to depend on methods it does not use, favoring many small, specific interfaces over one large general-purpose interface.

text: What is the Dependency Inversion Principle?
options: Dependencies should always flow from child to parent classes
Methods should be invoked in reverse order of definition
All dependencies should be inverted to remove circular references
High-level modules should depend on abstractions rather than concrete implementations
explanation: The Dependency Inversion Principle states that high-level modules should not depend on low-level modules; both should depend on abstractions. This decouples components and improves flexibility.

text: What is a data class in Kotlin or a record in Java?
options: A class that stores only static data
A class that stores data in binary format
A class that automatically generates equals, hashCode, toString, and copy methods based on constructor properties
A class that can only be used for database operations
explanation: Data classes (Kotlin) and records (Java) are special class types that automatically generate common methods like equals(), hashCode(), toString(), and copy() based on declared properties, reducing boilerplate.

text: How does the bridge pattern separate abstraction from implementation?
options: By using two separate inheritance hierarchies connected by composition, allowing them to vary independently
By merging all implementations into a single abstract class
By using global variables to connect unrelated classes
By creating a bridge between two interfaces using static methods
explanation: The bridge pattern decouples an abstraction from its implementation by putting them in separate class hierarchies connected via composition, allowing both to evolve independently without affecting each other.

text: What is the Liskov Substitution Principle's impact on exception handling in subclasses?
options: Subclasses must throw the exact same exceptions as the superclass
Subclasses should never throw any exceptions
Subclass methods should not throw broader exceptions than the superclass method specifies to maintain substitutability
The LSP does not apply to exception handling
explanation: LSP requires that subclass methods do not throw broader or new checked exceptions beyond what the superclass method declares, ensuring that code using the superclass type can handle all exceptions from any subclass.

text: What is the difference between the state pattern and the strategy pattern?
options: The state pattern is for concurrency while the strategy pattern is for sorting
They are identical patterns with different names
The state pattern encapsulates state-dependent behavior with transitions between states while the strategy pattern selects an algorithm without transitions
The strategy pattern manages state transitions while the state pattern selects algorithms
explanation: Both patterns use composition and polymorphism, but the state pattern models state-dependent behavior with transitions between states, while the strategy pattern selects among interchangeable algorithms without state transitions.

text: What is the object pool pattern and when is it beneficial?
options: A pattern that distributes objects across multiple servers
A pattern that creates objects in a swimming pool metaphor
A pattern that pools all objects into a single collection for easy access
A pattern that maintains a pool of reusable objects to avoid expensive creation and destruction costs
explanation: The object pool pattern pre-creates and maintains a set of initialized objects ready for use, avoiding the cost of frequent creation and destruction for expensive-to-create objects like database connections or threads.

text: What is the problem with inheritance-based reuse known as the 'yo-yo problem'?
options: When multiple inheritance creates an infinite loop
When understanding a class requires tracing through many levels of inheritance, bouncing up and down the hierarchy
When objects repeatedly create and destroy themselves
When a class inherits from both a parent and grandchild class
explanation: The yo-yo problem occurs when deeply nested inheritance hierarchies force developers to trace method definitions up and down many levels to understand behavior, making the code difficult to follow and maintain.

text: How does the specification pattern encapsulate business rules as recombinable objects?
options: By storing rules as string expressions evaluated at runtime
By hardcoding rules into if-else chains
By writing all business rules as database queries
By representing each business rule as an object with an isSatisfiedBy method that can be combined using AND, OR, and NOT operations
explanation: The specification pattern encapsulates each business rule as an object implementing isSatisfiedBy(candidate), with composite specifications using AND, OR, and NOT, enabling flexible and reusable rule composition.

text: What is the difference between type classes in Haskell and interfaces in Java?
options: Type classes are slower than interfaces
They are identical concepts in different languages
Java interfaces support more features than type classes
Type classes can add behavior to existing types without modifying them while Java interfaces require implementation at class definition time
explanation: Type classes allow retroactive implementation for existing types through instance declarations, adding polymorphic behavior without modifying the original type. Java interfaces must be declared on the class at definition time.

text: What is the memento pattern and how does it support undo functionality?
options: It stores all objects in persistent memory
It creates memory snapshots of the entire application
It memorizes method call history in a static log
It captures an object's internal state in a separate memento object that can be used to restore the state later
explanation: The memento pattern captures an object's internal state in a memento object without violating encapsulation, stored by a caretaker. The originator can restore its state from a memento, enabling undo/redo operations.

text: How does the entity-component-system (ECS) architecture differ from traditional OOP?
options: ECS requires all entities to share the same base class
ECS is just another name for the MVC pattern
ECS uses deep inheritance hierarchies for entity behavior
ECS separates data (components) from behavior (systems) and uses composition over inheritance, favoring cache-friendly memory layouts
explanation: ECS decouples data (components) from behavior (systems), where entities are just IDs with attached components. Systems process components of specific types, enabling cache-friendly data-oriented design and flexible composition.

text: What is the expression problem and how do OOP and functional programming approach it differently?
options: OOP cannot handle any expressions while FP handles all expressions
Both paradigms solve the expression problem identically
FP cannot define new types while OOP cannot define new functions
OOP easily adds new types but struggles to add new operations, while FP easily adds new operations but struggles to add new types
explanation: The expression problem highlights a fundamental tension: OOP easily adds new data types via subclassing but adding operations requires modifying existing classes, while FP easily adds functions but adding types requires modifying existing functions.

text: What is memory deallocation?
options: Allocating additional memory for a program
Compressing memory to save space
Moving data from RAM to the hard drive
Releasing memory that is no longer needed back to the system
explanation: Memory deallocation is the process of releasing previously allocated memory back to the system so it can be reused, either manually (free/delete) or automatically (garbage collection).

text: What is the stack used for in program execution?
options: Storing function call information, local variables, and return addresses
Storing all program files on disk
Running the operating system
Permanently storing user data
explanation: The stack stores function call frames including local variables, parameters, return addresses, and saved registers, growing and shrinking as functions are called and return.

text: Why is the heap used for dynamic memory allocation?
options: Because it automatically cleans up all allocations
Because it is the only available memory region
Because it allows allocating memory of any size at runtime that persists until explicitly freed
Because it is faster than the stack
explanation: The heap allows dynamic memory allocation of arbitrary sizes at runtime, and the allocated memory persists until it is explicitly freed or garbage collected, unlike stack memory which is automatically released when a function returns.

text: What is a stack overflow?
options: When the heap runs out of space
When the stack exceeds its maximum size, typically due to deep or infinite recursion
When a variable stores a value that is too large
When the CPU overheats
explanation: A stack overflow occurs when the call stack exceeds its allocated size, commonly caused by infinite recursion or very deep recursion consuming all available stack space.

text: What does the 'new' keyword do in languages like Java and C++?
options: It allocates memory on the heap and creates a new object
It deletes an existing object from memory
It declares a new variable on the stack
It opens a new file for writing
explanation: The 'new' keyword allocates memory on the heap for a new object, calls the constructor to initialize it, and returns a reference or pointer to the created object.

text: What is automatic memory management?
options: Memory that never needs to be allocated
A system where the runtime environment automatically handles memory allocation and deallocation
Memory that is managed by an external program
A tool for manually tracking memory usage
explanation: Automatic memory management means the language runtime handles allocation and deallocation automatically, typically through garbage collection, freeing developers from manual memory management.

text: What is a reference in memory management?
options: A copy of an object stored in a different location
A comment in the source code
A value that points to or identifies the location of an object in memory
A backup of the original memory
explanation: A reference is a value (like a pointer) that identifies the memory location of an object, allowing indirect access to the object's data and methods.

text: What happens to local variables when a function returns?
options: They are moved to the heap
Their memory on the stack is automatically reclaimed
They become global variables
They are saved to disk permanently
explanation: Local variables are stored on the stack frame of the function. When the function returns, its stack frame is popped, and the memory for all local variables is automatically reclaimed.

text: What is the difference between manual and automatic memory management?
options: Manual is always faster than automatic
Automatic management cannot handle large programs
Manual requires the programmer to allocate and free memory while automatic uses garbage collection
Manual management is only used in interpreted languages
explanation: Manual memory management (C, C++) requires the programmer to explicitly allocate and free memory. Automatic management (Java, Python) uses garbage collection to detect and reclaim unused memory.

text: What is the purpose of the delete keyword in C++?
options: It deletes an entry from a database
It removes a variable declaration from the code
It frees memory that was allocated with new and calls the destructor
It deletes a source code file
explanation: The delete keyword in C++ deallocates memory previously allocated with new, calling the object's destructor before releasing the memory back to the system.

text: What is a memory-mapped file and how does it differ from traditional file I/O?
options: It maps a file directly into the process address space, allowing file access through memory operations instead of read/write calls
It stores file data in a special map data structure
It maps memory addresses to file names
It compresses files to fit in memory
explanation: Memory-mapped files map file content into the process's virtual address space, enabling access through memory pointers instead of explicit read/write system calls, potentially improving performance for random access patterns.

text: What is the difference between strong references and weak references?
options: Weak references cannot be used with any data type
Strong references prevent garbage collection of the referenced object while weak references allow it
Weak references are faster than strong references
Strong references use more memory per reference
explanation: A strong reference prevents the garbage collector from collecting the referenced object. A weak reference does not prevent collection, allowing the object to be reclaimed if no strong references exist.

text: What is memory alignment and why does it matter?
options: Aligning data at memory addresses that are multiples of their size for efficient CPU access
Sorting data in memory alphabetically
Aligning code comments to the same column
Matching memory sizes between different programs
explanation: Memory alignment places data at addresses that are multiples of the data type's size (e.g., 4-byte int at address divisible by 4), enabling efficient CPU access since misaligned access may require multiple memory reads.

text: What is the purpose of the finalize() method in Java?
options: It finalizes the class definition preventing changes
It is called by the garbage collector before reclaiming an object's memory, but its use is discouraged
It permanently saves an object to disk
It marks an object as complete and ready for use
explanation: finalize() is called by the garbage collector before reclaiming an object. However, its use is discouraged because it has unpredictable timing, performance overhead, and can resurrect objects. Java 9+ deprecated it.

text: What is a double-free error?
options: Allocating twice the required memory
Attempting to free memory that has already been freed, causing undefined behavior
Using two different free functions on the same object
Freeing two different objects at the same time
explanation: A double-free error occurs when a program attempts to deallocate memory that has already been freed, leading to undefined behavior, heap corruption, or security vulnerabilities.

text: What is a phantom reference in Java?
options: A reference to an object in another program
A reference that allows determining when an object has been garbage collected without preventing collection
An invisible reference that cannot be accessed
A reference to a deleted object
explanation: A phantom reference is enqueued in a reference queue after the object is garbage collected, allowing cleanup actions. Unlike weak references, the referent is never accessible through a phantom reference.

text: What is the difference between stack allocation and heap allocation in terms of performance?
options: Stack allocation is slower because it checks for overflow
They have identical performance characteristics
Heap allocation is always faster than stack allocation
Stack allocation is faster because it only requires moving the stack pointer, while heap allocation involves finding suitable free blocks
explanation: Stack allocation is a simple pointer increment/decrement operation (O(1)), while heap allocation requires searching for free blocks, updating free lists, and handling fragmentation, making it significantly slower.

text: What is the purpose of soft references in Java?
options: They allow the garbage collector to reclaim objects only when memory is low, useful for caches
They create softly linked objects that cannot be modified
They create references that automatically become null after a timeout
They reference objects stored on soft storage like SSDs
explanation: Soft references are cleared by the garbage collector only when the JVM is running low on memory, making them ideal for implementing memory-sensitive caches that grow when memory is available.

text: What is the concept of ownership in Rust?
options: A design pattern for managing database connections
A system where each value has exactly one variable that owns it, controlling when the value is dropped
A way to assign copyright to code
A security feature that restricts file access
explanation: Rust's ownership system ensures each value has exactly one owner variable. When the owner goes out of scope, the value is dropped (freed). Ownership can be transferred (moved) or borrowed via references.

text: What is memory compaction in garbage collection?
options: Reducing the virtual memory size of a process
Moving live objects together to eliminate fragmentation and create contiguous free space
Compressing data to use less memory
Deleting all objects from memory
explanation: Memory compaction moves live objects together in the heap, eliminating gaps left by freed objects (fragmentation) and creating a large contiguous block of free space for future allocations.

text: What is the tri-color marking algorithm in garbage collection?
options: It colors memory pages for security purposes
It marks objects based on their data type using three categories
It uses three passes to determine which objects to collect
It classifies objects as white (unreachable), gray (to be scanned), and black (scanned) to safely identify garbage during concurrent collection
explanation: The tri-color marking algorithm uses white (potentially unreachable), gray (reachable but children not yet scanned), and black (reachable with all children scanned) to incrementally and safely identify garbage while the program runs.

text: How does the Boehm-Demers-Weiser conservative garbage collector work for C programs?
options: It requires the programmer to annotate all pointers
It only collects objects on the stack
It converts C programs to Java for garbage collection
It treats any value in memory that could be a pointer as a potential reference, preventing collection of possibly-referenced objects
explanation: The Boehm GC is conservative because it scans memory for values that could be valid pointers without type information, treating any word-aligned value pointing into the heap as a potential reference, which may retain some garbage.

text: What is the difference between stop-the-world and concurrent garbage collection?
options: Concurrent GC always takes longer than stop-the-world
Stop-the-world only pauses one thread at a time
They produce identical pause times in all scenarios
Stop-the-world pauses all application threads during collection while concurrent GC runs alongside the application with minimal pauses
explanation: Stop-the-world GC pauses all application threads during the entire collection cycle. Concurrent GC performs most work while the application runs, only requiring brief pauses for synchronization, reducing latency.

text: What is the ZGC garbage collector in Java designed to achieve?
options: Maximum throughput at the expense of longer pauses
Sub-millisecond pause times regardless of heap size by using colored pointers and load barriers
Smaller memory footprint than serial GC
Compatibility with C code through JNI
explanation: ZGC (Z Garbage Collector) achieves consistently low pause times (sub-millisecond) independent of heap size by using colored pointers, load barriers, and concurrent relocation of objects.

text: What is the purpose of Rust's borrow checker?
options: It enforces rules at compile time ensuring references do not outlive the data they point to and preventing data races
It monitors runtime memory usage for debugging
It automatically frees memory when borrowing ends
It checks if the program borrows external libraries correctly
explanation: Rust's borrow checker enforces at compile time that references are always valid, mutable references are exclusive, and data races are impossible, providing memory safety without runtime garbage collection.

text: What is the difference between arena allocation and general-purpose heap allocation?
options: Arena allocation is slower than heap allocation for all use cases
Arena allocation requires garbage collection to function
Arena allocation groups objects with similar lifetimes and frees them all at once, avoiding individual deallocation overhead
General-purpose allocation always uses less memory than arena allocation
explanation: Arena (region-based) allocation groups objects with similar lifetimes, allocating from a contiguous block and freeing the entire arena at once, avoiding per-object deallocation overhead and fragmentation.

text: What is the Cheney's algorithm for copying garbage collection?
options: It copies garbage to a separate memory region for analysis
It creates backup copies of all objects for safety
It uses three memory spaces in a rotating pattern
It uses two equal-sized semi-spaces, copying live objects from one to the other, compacting memory and eliminating fragmentation
explanation: Cheney's algorithm divides memory into two semi-spaces. During GC, it copies all reachable objects from the current space to the other using a breadth-first traversal, then swaps the roles of the spaces.

text: What is the problem of false sharing in concurrent programs and how is it mitigated?
options: When multiple threads allocate memory from the same pool
When different threads modify variables on the same cache line, causing unnecessary cache invalidation and performance degradation
When shared memory is falsely reported as freed
When threads share incorrect data due to synchronization bugs
explanation: False sharing occurs when threads modify different variables that happen to reside on the same CPU cache line, causing cache coherency traffic. It is mitigated by padding variables to separate cache lines.

text: What is the Shenandoah GC's approach to concurrent compaction?
options: It compacts memory only during application idle time
It uses stop-the-world pauses for all compaction operations
It relies on the operating system for compaction
It uses Brooks forwarding pointers to redirect references to relocated objects, enabling compaction concurrent with the application
explanation: Shenandoah GC uses Brooks forwarding pointers (an indirection pointer in each object header) to allow concurrent compaction. When an object is relocated, its forwarding pointer is updated atomically.

text: What is the concept of escape analysis and how does it optimize memory allocation?
options: It analyzes memory escape routes during buffer overflow attacks
It determines when variables escape from their declared scope
It detects when objects escape the program's memory space
It analyzes whether an object escapes the method or thread that created it, enabling stack allocation or elimination for non-escaping objects
explanation: Escape analysis determines whether an object can be accessed outside the method or thread that creates it. Non-escaping objects can be stack-allocated or even eliminated entirely, avoiding heap allocation overhead.

text: What is the purpose of error handling in programming?
options: To speed up program execution
To detect and respond to errors gracefully without crashing the program
To prevent users from running the program
To add more features to the program
explanation: Error handling allows programs to detect problems during execution and respond appropriately, such as displaying a message or retrying an operation, instead of crashing unexpectedly.

text: What is a FileNotFoundException?
options: An exception thrown when a program tries to access a file that does not exist
An error when the file has the wrong extension
An exception when the file system is full
An error when a file is too large to open
explanation: A FileNotFoundException is thrown when a program attempts to open, read, or write to a file that does not exist at the specified path.

text: What is an IllegalArgumentException?
options: An exception when the compiler finds syntax errors
An error when the program has too many arguments
An error when the program runs out of memory
An exception thrown when a method receives an argument that is not valid
explanation: An IllegalArgumentException is thrown when a method receives an argument that is inappropriate or outside the expected range, such as a negative value for a size parameter.

text: What happens if an exception is not caught?
options: The exception is saved to a file for later review
The exception is automatically fixed
The program terminates abnormally and typically displays an error message
The exception is ignored and the program continues
explanation: If an exception is not caught by any exception handler, it propagates up the call stack until it reaches the runtime, which terminates the program and typically prints a stack trace.

text: What is a ClassCastException?
options: An error when a class has too many methods
An exception when a class has duplicate names
An error when a class cannot be loaded
An exception thrown when trying to cast an object to a type it is not an instance of
explanation: A ClassCastException occurs when code attempts to cast an object to a class of which it is not an instance, such as casting a String to an Integer.

text: Can a try block have no catch block?
options: Yes, a try block can exist alone without catch or finally
No, it must have both catch and finally
Yes, if it has a finally block instead
No, every try must have at least one catch
explanation: A try block can omit the catch clause if it includes a finally block, which ensures cleanup code runs regardless of whether an exception occurred.

text: What is a NumberFormatException?
options: An exception when arithmetic operations fail
An exception thrown when trying to convert a non-numeric string to a number
An error when numbers exceed the integer range
An error when formatting numbers for display
explanation: A NumberFormatException is thrown when attempting to convert a string to a numeric type (like Integer.parseInt()) but the string does not contain a valid numeric format.

text: What is the purpose of the 'catch' keyword?
options: It throws a new exception
It defines a block that handles a specific type of exception
It stops the program from running
It creates a new error object
explanation: The catch keyword defines a block of code that handles a specific exception type, executing when that type of exception is thrown within the associated try block.

text: What is an IndexOutOfBoundsException?
options: An exception when too many indices are created
An exception thrown when accessing a collection with an invalid index
An error when the file index is corrupted
An error when a database index is missing
explanation: An IndexOutOfBoundsException is thrown when attempting to access an element in an array, list, or string using an index that is negative or greater than or equal to the size.

text: What is the purpose of the 'raise' keyword in Python?
options: It raises the value of a variable
It increases the stack size
It throws or raises an exception manually
It increases the priority of a process
explanation: The 'raise' keyword in Python is used to manually throw (raise) an exception, either creating a new exception or re-raising a caught exception.

text: What is the difference between try-except-else and try-except-finally in Python?
options: The else block runs when an exception occurs while finally runs when it does not
The else block runs only when no exception occurs while the finally block runs regardless of whether an exception occurred
They are identical in behavior
The else block handles exceptions while finally throws them
explanation: In Python, the else block executes only if no exception was raised in the try block, while the finally block always executes regardless of whether an exception occurred, for cleanup operations.

text: Why is catching generic exceptions (like catch Exception) considered a bad practice?
options: Because generic exceptions take more memory than specific ones
Because generic exceptions are slower to catch than specific ones
Because the compiler does not allow generic exception catches
Because it catches all exceptions including ones the code cannot handle, hiding bugs and unexpected errors
explanation: Catching overly broad exception types masks unexpected errors and bugs that should be detected and fixed, making debugging difficult. Catch specific exceptions to handle only expected error conditions.

text: What is exception filtering in C# (when clause)?
options: Adding a condition to a catch block that must be true for the catch to handle the exception
Filtering out exceptions based on their message text only
Removing exceptions from the program permanently
Blocking certain exception types from being thrown
explanation: Exception filtering (catch when) in C# allows adding a Boolean condition to a catch block. The exception is only caught if the condition evaluates to true, enabling more precise exception handling.

text: What is the purpose of the suppressed exceptions feature in Java?
options: It attaches secondary exceptions that were suppressed during cleanup in try-with-resources to the primary exception
It reduces the number of exceptions a program can throw
It silently ignores all exceptions
It prevents exceptions from being logged
explanation: When a try-with-resources block throws an exception and the close() method also throws, the close exception is added as a suppressed exception to the primary one, preserving both for debugging.

text: What is the difference between assertion errors and exceptions?
options: Assertions are faster than exceptions
Assertions replace the need for all exception handling
Exceptions cannot be caught while assertions can
Assertions check for programmer errors that should never happen while exceptions handle expected error conditions
explanation: Assertions verify conditions that should always be true (programmer errors, invariants), typically disabled in production. Exceptions handle expected error conditions (invalid input, network failure) that the program should recover from.

text: What is the anti-pattern of using exceptions for control flow?
options: Using too few exception handlers in the program
Throwing exceptions from constructors
Catching exceptions and logging them
Using try-catch blocks for normal program flow instead of conditional checks, harming performance and readability
explanation: Using exceptions for normal control flow (like loop termination or conditional branching) is an anti-pattern because exceptions are expensive, intended for exceptional conditions, and make code harder to understand.

text: What is exception wrapping or translation?
options: Wrapping exception handling code in a separate class
Catching a low-level exception and throwing a higher-level exception that is more meaningful to the caller
Converting exception messages to different languages
Converting compile-time errors to runtime exceptions
explanation: Exception wrapping catches a low-level exception and throws a higher-level, more meaningful exception (often with the original as the cause), abstracting implementation details from callers.

text: What is the purpose of the 'finally' block in resource cleanup?
options: It guarantees that cleanup code (like closing files or connections) executes whether or not an exception occurred
It finalizes the data and sends it to the database
It prevents the exception from propagating further
It runs only when no exception is thrown
explanation: The finally block guarantees execution of cleanup code regardless of whether an exception was thrown or caught, ensuring resources like file handles and database connections are properly released.

text: What is the difference between re-throwing and wrapping an exception?
options: Re-throwing is faster while wrapping provides more information
Re-throwing propagates the same exception unchanged while wrapping creates a new exception with the original as the cause
They are identical operations with different names
Re-throwing creates a new exception while wrapping keeps the same one
explanation: Re-throwing (throw without arguments) propagates the original exception with its stack trace intact. Wrapping catches the original and throws a new exception with the original attached as the cause, adding context.

text: What is the role of the getMessage() method on exceptions?
options: It returns a human-readable description of the error that caused the exception
It sends an error message to the user via email
It creates a new exception with a custom message
It translates the exception into a log entry
explanation: getMessage() returns the detail message string of the exception, providing a human-readable description of what went wrong, useful for logging and error reporting.

text: What is the difference between the Result type in Rust and traditional exception handling?
options: Result encodes success or failure in the return type, forcing callers to handle errors explicitly at compile time, while exceptions propagate implicitly
Result types can only represent two types of errors
Exceptions provide better type safety than Result
Result types are slower than exception handling
explanation: Rust's Result<T, E> encodes success (Ok) or failure (Err) in the type system, requiring callers to explicitly handle both cases. Exceptions propagate implicitly up the call stack, making error paths less visible.

text: What is the concept of exception safety in C++ and its three guarantee levels?
options: Low, medium, and high exception severity levels
Compile-time safety, runtime safety, and thread safety
Type safety, memory safety, and null safety
Basic guarantee (no leaks), strong guarantee (transaction-like rollback), and no-throw guarantee (never throws exceptions)
explanation: C++ exception safety defines three levels: basic guarantee (invariants maintained, no leaks), strong guarantee (operation succeeds or state is rolled back completely), and no-throw guarantee (operation never throws).

text: How does Kotlin's approach to checked exceptions differ from Java's?
options: Kotlin uses a different keyword for checked exceptions
Kotlin requires all exceptions to be checked
Kotlin only allows checked exceptions in coroutines
Kotlin does not have checked exceptions, treating all exceptions as unchecked, because studies showed checked exceptions often lead to empty catch blocks
explanation: Kotlin eliminated checked exceptions based on the observation that they often lead to swallowed exceptions (empty catch blocks) in Java. All exceptions in Kotlin are unchecked, simplifying code while relying on good practices.

text: What is the relationship between exceptions and monadic error handling using Either/Result?
options: Monadic error handling replaces all try-catch blocks with recursion
Exceptions are a subset of monadic error handling
Monadic error handling cannot represent multiple error types
Both represent computations that may fail, but monadic error handling makes failure explicit in types and composable through flatMap/bind
explanation: Both mechanisms handle failures, but monadic Either/Result makes errors explicit in the type signature and composable via flatMap/bind, while exceptions are implicit and handled through separate control flow (try-catch).

text: What is the zero-cost exception handling model used in modern C++ compilers?
options: It eliminates all exception-related code at compile time
Exceptions are completely free even when thrown
It replaces exceptions with return codes automatically
It adds no performance overhead when exceptions are not thrown, using table-based stack unwinding only when exceptions occur
explanation: Zero-cost (table-based) exception handling generates lookup tables mapping program counters to cleanup actions. There is no runtime overhead when exceptions are not thrown, but unwinding the stack when an exception occurs uses these tables.

text: What is the problem with exceptions in asynchronous or concurrent code?
options: Exceptions cannot cross thread or async boundaries naturally and may be lost if not properly captured and propagated
Concurrent code cannot throw any exceptions
Exceptions are always thread-safe and work identically in concurrent code
Exceptions in threads automatically propagate to the main thread
explanation: Exceptions thrown in async callbacks, separate threads, or coroutines do not naturally propagate to the calling context. They must be explicitly captured (via Future, CompletableFuture, or structured concurrency) to avoid silent failures.

text: How does structured concurrency improve exception handling compared to unstructured async code?
options: It handles exceptions by restarting failed tasks indefinitely
It ensures that all concurrent tasks are scoped to a parent, and exceptions from child tasks automatically cancel siblings and propagate to the parent
It defers all exceptions until the program exits
It eliminates all exceptions in concurrent code
explanation: Structured concurrency (Kotlin coroutineScope, Java StructuredTaskScope) ensures child tasks are bound to a parent scope. If a child fails, siblings are cancelled and the exception propagates to the parent, preventing orphaned tasks.

text: What is the effect handler approach to exception handling and how does it generalize try-catch?
options: Effect handlers define how effects (including exceptions) are interpreted, allowing the same code to have different error handling strategies depending on the handler
They add hardware-level exception handling support
Effect handlers only work in statically typed languages
Effect handlers replace all exception types with a single error type
explanation: Effect handlers generalize exception handling by separating the raising of effects from their interpretation. The same code can use different handlers for testing, production, or different error recovery strategies.

text: What is the supervisor pattern in actor systems for handling failures?
options: A thread monitor that prevents all exceptions from occurring
A design pattern that replaces all try-catch with logging
A parent actor monitors child actors and applies a restart, stop, or escalate strategy when a child fails with an exception
A senior developer supervises code reviews for exception handling
explanation: In actor systems (like Akka), supervisors monitor child actors. When a child fails, the supervisor applies a strategy: restart the child (reset state), stop it, resume it ignoring the error, or escalate to its own supervisor.

text: What is the purpose of importing a module?
options: To compile the module into machine code
To delete the module from the system
To make the functions and classes defined in the module available in the current file
To create a backup copy of the module
explanation: Importing a module makes its exported functions, classes, and variables accessible in the current file, enabling code reuse without rewriting functionality.

text: What is the __init__.py file used for in Python packages?
options: It creates a backup of the package
It stores initialization data for the program
It marks a directory as a Python package and can contain initialization code
It initializes all variables to zero
explanation: The __init__.py file indicates that a directory should be treated as a Python package. It can be empty or contain package initialization code and specify which modules to export.

text: What is a third-party library?
options: A library created by someone other than the language developers or the project team
A library that requires three imports
A library stored in a third location
A library built into the programming language
explanation: A third-party library is a package or library created by external developers (not the language creators or your team), typically installed through a package manager.

text: What is the purpose of the 'export' keyword in JavaScript modules?
options: It sends data to an external server
It removes a variable from the module
It converts the module to a different format
It makes functions, classes, or variables available for other modules to import
explanation: The export keyword in ES modules makes specified functions, classes, or variables available for other modules to import, controlling what is part of the module's public interface.

text: What is a standard library?
options: A collection of modules and packages included with the programming language by default
A library that can only store standard data types
A physical library where programmers study
A library that follows a specific coding standard
explanation: The standard library is the set of modules and packages that come bundled with a programming language, providing commonly needed functionality like file I/O, data structures, and networking.

text: What is a Maven repository in Java development?
options: A version control system for Java code
A storage location for Java libraries and dependencies that Maven can download automatically
A testing framework for Java modules
A database for storing Java application data
explanation: A Maven repository is a directory of packaged JAR files with metadata. Maven automatically downloads required dependencies from repositories (like Maven Central) based on the project's pom.xml configuration.

text: What does the 'require' function do in Node.js?
options: It imports and loads a module, returning its exported functionality
It checks if a condition is true
It validates that a file exists
It requires user authentication
explanation: The require() function in Node.js (CommonJS) loads a module file, executes it, and returns the module's exports object, making the module's functionality available in the current file.

text: What is the purpose of a .gitignore file in relation to packages?
options: It ignores all errors in the package
It specifies files and directories (like node_modules) that should not be tracked by version control
It hides packages from other developers
It imports Git-related packages
explanation: A .gitignore file specifies patterns for files and directories that Git should not track, commonly used to exclude dependency folders like node_modules/ since they can be reinstalled from the lock file.

text: What is a framework in software development?
options: A single function imported from a library
A debugging tool for tracing program execution
A comprehensive package that provides a structure and set of tools for building applications
A physical frame used to hold a computer
explanation: A framework is a comprehensive software package that provides an application structure, conventions, and tools, dictating the architecture while the developer fills in application-specific logic.

text: What is the difference between a default export and a named export in JavaScript?
options: Named exports can only export functions
Default exports are faster than named exports
A default export allows importing without curly braces while named exports require them
Default exports must be constants
explanation: A default export (export default) allows a single value to be imported with any name without curly braces. Named exports require curly braces and the exact exported name during import.

text: What is the purpose of a requirements.txt file in Python?
options: It lists the hardware requirements for the program
It lists the required test cases
It specifies the Python package dependencies and their versions needed for the project
It contains the project documentation
explanation: requirements.txt lists the Python packages and their version constraints needed by the project, allowing others to install all dependencies with 'pip install -r requirements.txt'.

text: What is the difference between a bundler and a package manager?
options: A package manager only works with frontend code
A package manager installs and manages dependencies while a bundler combines source files into optimized bundles for deployment
A bundler installs packages while a package manager bundles code
They are identical tools with different names
explanation: A package manager (npm, pip) handles downloading, installing, and version management of dependencies. A bundler (webpack, Rollup) combines multiple source files and dependencies into optimized bundles for deployment.

text: What is dependency resolution in package management?
options: Removing unnecessary dependencies from the project
Converting dependencies to a different programming language
Resolving conflicts between developers about which packages to use
The process of determining compatible versions of all direct and transitive dependencies
explanation: Dependency resolution is the process of finding compatible versions for all packages in the dependency graph, including transitive dependencies, ensuring no version conflicts exist.

text: What is the purpose of namespace imports in programming?
options: Importing only private functions from a module
Creating namespace aliases for the operating system
Importing all exports from a module under a single namespace to avoid naming conflicts
Creating new names for existing functions
explanation: Namespace imports (like 'import * as utils from ...') import all exports under a single object, preventing naming collisions and making it clear which module each function comes from.

text: What is the purpose of the peerDependencies field in package.json?
options: It lists peer-reviewed packages only
It defines packages used exclusively during peer programming
It specifies packages that should be provided by the consuming project rather than installed as nested dependencies
It lists packages that are optional for the project
explanation: peerDependencies declare packages that a library expects to be provided by the project using it, avoiding duplicate versions and ensuring compatibility, commonly used for plugins and libraries with shared dependencies.

text: What is lazy loading of modules?
options: Deferring the loading of a module until it is actually needed, reducing initial startup time
Loading modules slowly to reduce CPU usage
Loading only the documentation of a module
Loading modules without importing them
explanation: Lazy loading defers importing a module until the code path that uses it is actually executed, reducing initial startup time and memory usage for modules that may not be needed in every execution.

text: What is the difference between static imports and dynamic imports?
options: Dynamic imports can only load built-in modules
Static imports are resolved at compile time while dynamic imports load modules at runtime based on conditions
Static imports are faster than dynamic imports in all cases
Static imports happen at runtime while dynamic imports happen at compile time
explanation: Static imports are resolved and bundled at compile/build time, while dynamic imports (import() in JS) load modules at runtime, enabling code splitting and conditional loading based on runtime conditions.

text: What is the purpose of a module's public API?
options: The internal implementation details of the module
The set of exported functions, classes, and types that external code can use
The private methods used for testing
The documentation comments in the module
explanation: A module's public API consists of its explicitly exported symbols (functions, classes, types) that external code is intended to use, forming a contract between the module and its consumers.

text: What is the npm audit command used for?
options: Scanning project dependencies for known security vulnerabilities
Checking the financial cost of dependencies
Auditing the code for style violations
Auditing the performance of installed packages
explanation: npm audit scans the project's dependency tree for known security vulnerabilities by checking against a vulnerability database, reporting issues and suggesting fixes or updates.

text: What is the difference between a workspace and a standalone package?
options: Standalone packages cannot have dependencies
Workspaces can only contain two packages
A workspace manages multiple related packages in a single repository with shared dependencies while standalone packages are independent
Workspaces are for development only while packages are for production
explanation: Workspaces (npm/yarn workspaces, Cargo workspaces) manage multiple related packages in a single repository, sharing dependencies and enabling cross-package development, while standalone packages are independent projects.

text: What is the module resolution algorithm in Node.js and how does it find modules?
options: It uses a random search through all directories
It only searches the current working directory
It always looks in the global installation directory first
It searches node_modules directories ascending from the requiring file's location, then global locations, checking for files, directories with index.js, or package.json main fields
explanation: Node.js resolves modules by searching node_modules directories starting from the requiring file's directory and ascending to the root, checking for exact files, directories with index.js, or package.json main entries.

text: What is the purpose of import maps in modern JavaScript?
options: They provide a map visualization of module dependencies
They create documentation maps for modules
They allow controlling how module specifiers are resolved in the browser without a build step
They map imports to different programming languages
explanation: Import maps allow web developers to control how bare module specifiers (like 'import React') resolve to URLs in the browser, enabling the use of npm-style imports without requiring a bundler.

text: What is the difference between side-effect-free and side-effectful module imports?
options: Side-effect-free imports are slower
They produce identical behavior in all bundlers
Side-effectful imports cannot export any values
Side-effect-free imports only use the exported values while side-effectful imports execute code that modifies global state on import
explanation: Side-effect-free modules only export values without modifying global state, enabling tree shaking. Side-effectful modules (like polyfills) execute code with global effects just by being imported, and must be preserved by bundlers.

text: What is the challenge of diamond dependency conflicts in package management?
options: When a package depends on itself recursively
When two packages have identical names in different registries
When a dependency graph forms a perfect diamond shape
When two dependencies require different incompatible versions of the same transitive dependency
explanation: Diamond dependency conflicts occur when package A depends on B and C, which both depend on different incompatible versions of D. Package managers must resolve this by deduplication, version selection, or allowing multiple versions.

text: How does the Java Platform Module System (JPMS) differ from traditional classpath-based loading?
options: Classpath provides stronger encapsulation than JPMS
JPMS uses module declarations to explicitly define dependencies and encapsulated packages, unlike the flat classpath that exposes everything
JPMS is slower than classpath loading
JPMS only works with Java 7 and earlier
explanation: JPMS (Java 9+) uses module-info.java to declare dependencies and control package visibility, providing strong encapsulation. The traditional classpath has no encapsulation boundaries, exposing all public types.

text: What is supply chain security in the context of package management?
options: Protecting against malicious code injected into dependencies through compromised packages or maintainer accounts
Managing the physical supply chain of hardware components
Ensuring packages are delivered in the correct order
Securing the network connection used to download packages
explanation: Supply chain security addresses threats like typosquatting, dependency confusion, and compromised maintainer accounts that could inject malicious code into packages, requiring measures like lockfiles, signatures, and vulnerability scanning.

text: What is the purpose of a Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) for packages?
options: It describes the hardware requirements for the software
It lists the financial cost of each dependency
It provides a comprehensive inventory of all components, libraries, and dependencies in a software project for security and compliance
It documents the development timeline of each package
explanation: An SBOM is a formal record listing all components and dependencies in a software project, enabling vulnerability tracking, license compliance verification, and supply chain risk management.

text: How does Deno's module system differ from Node.js in terms of dependency management?
options: Deno uses the same npm and node_modules system as Node.js
Deno requires all dependencies to be bundled before execution
Deno does not support external modules
Deno imports modules directly from URLs without a centralized package manager or node_modules directory
explanation: Deno imports modules directly from URLs (including from deno.land/x and npm: specifiers), caching them locally without a node_modules directory or centralized package.json, simplifying dependency management.

text: What is the purpose of module federation in webpack 5?
options: Allowing multiple independently deployed applications to share modules at runtime without rebuilding
Federating modules across different government agencies
Merging all modules into a single federated package
Creating federal backups of all modules
explanation: Module federation enables separate webpack builds to share modules at runtime. One application can dynamically load components from another deployed application, enabling micro-frontend architectures without compile-time coupling.

text: What is the difference between vendoring and using a package manager for dependencies?
options: Vendoring copies dependency source code directly into the project repository while package managers download them on demand
Package managers copy all code into the repository
Vendoring only works with interpreted languages
Vendoring is always faster than using a package manager
explanation: Vendoring copies the full source code of dependencies into the project repository, ensuring reproducibility and availability without external services. Package managers download dependencies on demand from registries, saving repository space.

text: What is the Observer pattern used for?
options: Notifying multiple objects when the state of another object changes
Watching for syntax errors in code
Monitoring memory usage in real time
Observing CPU performance metrics
explanation: The Observer pattern defines a one-to-many dependency where when one object (subject) changes state, all its dependent objects (observers) are automatically notified and updated.

text: What is a hash function?
options: A function that validates user passwords
A function that maps input data to a fixed-size output value used for efficient data lookup
A function that encrypts all data permanently
A function that removes duplicate data
explanation: A hash function takes input data and produces a fixed-size output (hash code) that can be used as an index for efficient data storage and retrieval in hash-based data structures.

text: What is an event listener?
options: A hardware device that detects sound
A tool that monitors network events
A function that waits for and responds to a specific event in event-driven programming
A log file that records all system events
explanation: An event listener is a function registered to be called when a specific event occurs, such as a button click or a data change, forming the basis of event-driven programming.

text: What is the MVC pattern?
options: A method of compressing video files
A security protocol for web applications
A version control system for managing code
A pattern that separates an application into Model (data), View (presentation), and Controller (logic)
explanation: Model-View-Controller (MVC) separates an application into three components: Model (data and business logic), View (user interface), and Controller (handles input and updates model/view).

text: What is time complexity?
options: The time needed to compile the program
A measure of how the running time of an algorithm grows relative to input size
The time a program takes to load from disk
The time taken to write the code
explanation: Time complexity describes how the execution time of an algorithm scales as the input size grows, expressed using Big O notation to classify algorithms by their growth rate.

text: What is the Builder pattern and when should it be used?
options: A pattern for building database tables
A pattern that compiles code faster
A pattern for building user interfaces
A pattern that separates object construction from representation, useful for objects with many optional parameters
explanation: The Builder pattern separates the construction of a complex object from its representation, allowing step-by-step creation with optional parameters, making code more readable than constructors with many parameters.

text: What is the Proxy pattern?
options: A pattern that provides a surrogate object that controls access to another object
A pattern that hides all methods of an object
A pattern for configuring network proxies
A pattern that copies objects between servers
explanation: The Proxy pattern provides a surrogate or placeholder object that controls access to another object, useful for lazy loading, access control, logging, or remote object representation.

text: What is memoization in the context of dynamic programming?
options: Caching results of subproblems to avoid redundant computation in recursive algorithms
Storing algorithm pseudocode in comments
Writing notes about algorithm design
Memorizing the source code for the exam
explanation: Memoization in dynamic programming stores the results of solved subproblems in a cache, so when the same subproblem is encountered again, the cached result is returned instead of recomputing it.

text: What is the Chain of Responsibility pattern?
options: A pattern where a request is passed along a chain of handlers until one handles it
A pattern for linking database tables
A pattern for managing responsibility among team members
A pattern that chains function calls together
explanation: The Chain of Responsibility pattern passes a request along a chain of handler objects, where each handler decides to process it or pass it to the next handler, decoupling senders from receivers.

text: What is the difference between space complexity and time complexity?
options: They measure the same thing using different units
Space complexity is always larger than time complexity
Space complexity measures memory usage growth while time complexity measures execution time growth relative to input size
Time complexity only applies to recursive algorithms
explanation: Time complexity describes how execution time grows with input size, while space complexity describes how memory usage grows. An algorithm may trade space for time (caching) or time for space (recomputation).

text: What is the Template Method pattern?
options: A pattern for generating boilerplate code
A pattern that defines the skeleton of an algorithm in a base class, letting subclasses override specific steps
A pattern that templates all method signatures
A pattern for creating HTML templates
explanation: The Template Method pattern defines an algorithm's skeleton in a base class method, with specific steps deferred to subclass implementations through abstract or overridable methods.

text: What is the difference between top-down and bottom-up dynamic programming?
options: They always produce different results
Top-down is always faster than bottom-up
Top-down uses recursion with memoization while bottom-up builds solutions iteratively from smallest subproblems
Bottom-up uses recursion while top-down uses iteration
explanation: Top-down (memoization) starts with the main problem and recursively solves subproblems, caching results. Bottom-up (tabulation) solves smallest subproblems first, building up to the main solution iteratively.

text: What is the Mediator pattern?
options: A pattern that converts between data formats
A pattern for mediating between the CPU and memory
A pattern for middle-tier server components
A pattern that defines an object to encapsulate how a set of objects interact, reducing direct dependencies
explanation: The Mediator pattern centralizes complex communication logic between objects in a mediator object, reducing direct coupling between components so they communicate indirectly through the mediator.

text: What is amortized analysis?
options: A technique that averages the time per operation over a sequence of operations to give a tighter bound than worst-case
Analyzing the amount of memory amortized over program lifetime
Financial analysis of algorithm development costs
Estimating the remaining time for a running algorithm
explanation: Amortized analysis averages the cost of operations over a sequence, showing that while some individual operations may be expensive, the average cost per operation over many operations is lower.

text: What is the difference between the Abstract Factory and Factory Method patterns?
options: Abstract Factory is a simpler version of Factory Method
Abstract Factory creates families of related objects through an interface while Factory Method delegates creation of a single object to subclasses
They are identical patterns used in different programming languages
Factory Method creates multiple objects while Abstract Factory creates one
explanation: Factory Method uses inheritance, with a single method in a base class overridden by subclasses to create one type of object. Abstract Factory uses composition, providing an interface to create families of related objects.

text: How does the CQRS pattern improve scalability in distributed systems?
options: By combining all queries and commands into a single optimized model
By caching all queries on the client side
By separating read and write models, allowing each to be scaled, optimized, and evolved independently
By reducing the number of database tables
explanation: CQRS separates the read model (optimized for queries) from the write model (optimized for commands), allowing independent scaling, different storage technologies, and separate optimization strategies for each.

text: What is the difference between compile-time and runtime metaprogramming?
options: Compile-time metaprogramming is always slower
They both execute at the same phase
Compile-time metaprogramming generates or transforms code during compilation while runtime metaprogramming modifies program structure during execution
Runtime metaprogramming cannot create new functions
explanation: Compile-time metaprogramming (macros, templates, annotation processors) generates or transforms code before execution. Runtime metaprogramming (reflection, dynamic proxies) inspects and modifies program structure during execution.

text: What is the difference between event sourcing and traditional state storage?
options: Event sourcing stores every state change as an immutable event, deriving current state by replaying events, while traditional storage saves only current state
Traditional storage keeps complete history while event sourcing does not
They store data in identical formats
Event sourcing only stores the final state
explanation: Event sourcing persists every state change as an immutable event in an append-only log. Current state is derived by replaying events, providing complete audit trails and temporal queries, unlike traditional CRUD storage.

text: How does the saga pattern manage distributed transactions across microservices?
options: It retries all failed transactions indefinitely
It uses a single distributed lock across all services
It orchestrates a sequence of local transactions with compensating actions to undo completed steps if a later step fails
It rolls back all services using two-phase commit
explanation: The saga pattern breaks a distributed transaction into a sequence of local transactions, each with a compensating action. If a step fails, the saga executes compensating actions for all previously completed steps.

text: What is the difference between the interpreter pattern and an actual language interpreter?
options: The interpreter pattern compiles code while a language interpreter interprets it
The interpreter pattern defines a grammar representation and an interpreter for sentences in that grammar, used for simple DSLs, while a language interpreter is a full program execution engine
A language interpreter is simpler than the interpreter pattern
They are identical in scope and implementation
explanation: The interpreter design pattern represents a grammar as a class hierarchy and interprets sentences by traversing the tree, suitable for simple DSLs. A full language interpreter handles parsing, optimization, and execution of a complete language.

text: What is the purpose of the circuit breaker pattern in distributed systems?
options: It manages electrical circuits in server hardware
It circuits all requests through a central router
It breaks circular dependencies between services
It prevents cascading failures by stopping requests to a failing service and allowing it time to recover
explanation: The circuit breaker pattern monitors failures in remote calls. When failures exceed a threshold, it 'opens' the circuit, immediately failing fast instead of waiting for timeouts, allowing the failing service time to recover.

text: What is the difference between a red-black tree and a 2-3-4 tree?
options: A red-black tree is a binary representation of a 2-3-4 tree where red links represent nodes merged within 2-3-4 tree nodes
Red-black trees have more nodes than 2-3-4 trees for the same data
They are completely unrelated data structures
2-3-4 trees are always faster for all operations
explanation: Red-black trees are binary tree representations of 2-3-4 trees. Red links connect nodes that would be merged in a 2-3-4 node, and the coloring rules maintain the balance invariants of the underlying 2-3-4 structure.

text: What is backpressure in reactive programming and why is it important?
options: Pressure applied to undo the last operation
Network congestion that slows down all requests
Memory pressure from running out of heap space
A mechanism that allows consumers to signal producers to slow down when they cannot keep up with the data flow rate
explanation: Backpressure is a flow control mechanism in reactive streams where downstream consumers signal upstream producers about their processing capacity, preventing buffer overflow and out-of-memory errors in high-throughput systems.

text: What is the difference between optimistic and pessimistic concurrency control in application design?
options: They are identical strategies with different names
Pessimistic control never uses any locks
Optimistic control assumes conflicts are rare and detects them at commit time, while pessimistic control prevents conflicts by acquiring locks before access
Optimistic control is always faster than pessimistic control
explanation: Optimistic concurrency allows concurrent access and checks for conflicts at commit time (using version numbers or timestamps). Pessimistic concurrency acquires locks before access, preventing conflicts but reducing throughput.

text: What is the main advantage of concurrent programming?
options: It reduces the amount of memory needed
It makes source code shorter
It allows programs to perform multiple tasks simultaneously, improving responsiveness and throughput
It eliminates all bugs from the program
explanation: Concurrent programming enables multiple tasks to progress simultaneously, improving responsiveness (UI stays responsive) and throughput (utilizing multiple CPU cores for computation).

text: What is a thread-safe data structure?
options: A data structure that can be safely accessed by multiple threads concurrently without data corruption
A data structure stored in thread-local memory
A data structure that can only be used in single-threaded programs
A data structure that is locked permanently
explanation: A thread-safe data structure can be accessed by multiple threads concurrently without causing data corruption, race conditions, or inconsistent state, typically through internal synchronization.

text: What does the 'join' method do on a thread?
options: It makes the calling thread wait until the target thread finishes execution
It joins the thread to a thread pool
It combines two threads into one
It creates a connection between two threads
