explanation: The KMP algorithm performs efficient string matching by precomputing a failure function that avoids re-examining characters, achieving O(n+m) time complexity.

text: What is the difference between UTF-8 and UTF-16 encoding?
options: UTF-8 uses fixed 8-bit encoding; UTF-16 uses fixed 16-bit encoding
UTF-8 uses variable 1-4 bytes per character; UTF-16 uses 2 or 4 bytes
UTF-16 is backward compatible with ASCII; UTF-8 is not
Both encodings always produce the same number of bytes for any string
explanation: UTF-8 uses 1 to 4 bytes per character and is ASCII-compatible, while UTF-16 uses 2 or 4 bytes per character and is more efficient for non-Latin scripts.

text: What is a rope data structure for strings?
options: A linked list where each node stores a single character
A binary tree of string fragments enabling efficient concatenation and editing
A hash table that maps character positions to their values
A queue that processes string operations in FIFO order
explanation: A rope is a binary tree where each leaf holds a short string fragment, enabling efficient concatenation, insertion, and deletion in very long strings.

text: What is the Rabin-Karp algorithm?
options: A sorting algorithm optimized for string arrays
A string matching algorithm that uses rolling hash functions
A compression algorithm for reducing string data size
An encryption algorithm for protecting string contents
explanation: The Rabin-Karp algorithm uses rolling hash functions for string matching, allowing efficient average-case substring search and multiple pattern matching.

text: What is a trie data structure used for in string processing?
options: Sorting strings by their character frequency distributions
Efficiently storing and searching a set of strings by prefix
Compressing strings using variable-length encoding schemes
Converting strings between different character encoding formats
explanation: A trie is a tree where each path from root to node represents a prefix, enabling efficient storage, search, and prefix-based retrieval of string sets.

text: What is the Levenshtein distance between two strings?
options: The number of characters that appear in both strings
The minimum number of single-character edits to transform one string to another
The difference in length between the two strings
The number of positions where corresponding characters differ
explanation: Levenshtein distance measures the minimum number of single-character insertions, deletions, or substitutions needed to transform one string into another.

text: What is Unicode normalization?
options: Converting all Unicode characters to ASCII equivalents
Converting strings to a canonical form so equivalent sequences compare equal
Removing all non-English characters from Unicode strings
Compressing Unicode strings to use fewer bytes per character
explanation: Unicode normalization converts strings to a canonical form (NFC, NFD, NFKC, or NFKD) so that equivalent character sequences are represented identically.

text: What is the Aho-Corasick algorithm?
options: An algorithm for sorting strings in reverse lexicographic order
A multi-pattern string matching algorithm using a finite automaton
An algorithm for finding the longest palindromic substring
A technique for compressing repeated substrings in a text
explanation: The Aho-Corasick algorithm builds a finite automaton from multiple patterns and searches a text for all pattern occurrences simultaneously in linear time.

text: What is a suffix array in string processing?
options: An array containing the last character of each word in a text
A sorted array of all suffixes of a string enabling fast substring queries
An array that stores the frequency of each character suffix
A data structure for appending characters to the end of strings
explanation: A suffix array is a sorted array of all suffixes of a string, enabling efficient substring search, longest common prefix queries, and other string operations.

text: What is an array in programming?
options: A collection of elements stored at contiguous memory locations
A single variable that stores only one value at a time
A function that processes multiple inputs simultaneously
A control structure for repeating a block of code
explanation: An array is a data structure that stores a fixed-size collection of elements of the same type at contiguous memory locations.

text: What is the index of the first element in most arrays?
options: The size of the array
explanation: In most programming languages, arrays use zero-based indexing, so the first element is at index 0.

text: What is a list in programming?
options: A fixed-size array that cannot grow or shrink
An ordered collection that can dynamically grow and shrink
A set of unique elements with no duplicates allowed
A mapping of keys to their corresponding values
explanation: A list is an ordered collection that can dynamically change its size by adding or removing elements.

text: What is a dictionary or map collection?
options: An ordered list of elements accessed by numeric index
A collection of key-value pairs for efficient data lookup
A set of unique elements sorted in alphabetical order
A stack of elements following last-in-first-out order
explanation: A dictionary or map stores key-value pairs, allowing efficient data retrieval by looking up a key to find its associated value.

text: What is a set collection?
options: An ordered collection that allows duplicate elements
An unordered collection that stores only unique elements
A key-value mapping for storing associated data pairs
A sequence of elements accessed by their numeric index
explanation: A set is a collection that stores only unique elements and does not allow duplicates.

text: What is a multidimensional array?
options: An array that stores elements of multiple different types
An array with more than one index, like rows and columns
An array that can grow and shrink dynamically in size
An array that is shared across multiple functions
explanation: A multidimensional array uses more than one index to access elements, such as a 2D array with rows and columns.

text: What does the push operation do on a stack?
options: Removes the top element from the stack
Adds a new element to the top of the stack
Returns the top element without removing it
Checks if the stack is currently empty
explanation: The push operation adds a new element to the top of the stack.

text: What is a queue data structure?
options: A collection that follows last-in-first-out order
A collection that follows first-in-first-out order
A collection that allows access to any element by index
A collection that stores elements in sorted order
explanation: A queue follows first-in-first-out (FIFO) order, where elements are added at the rear and removed from the front.

text: What is a linked list?
options: An array where elements are stored contiguously in memory
A collection of nodes where each node points to the next
A sorted array with binary search capabilities built in
A hash table that maps keys to array indices
explanation: A linked list is a collection of nodes where each node contains data and a reference (pointer) to the next node in the sequence.

text: What does sorting an array mean?
options: Removing duplicate elements from the array
Arranging elements in a specific order like ascending or descending
Dividing the array into two equal halves
Reversing the positions of all elements in the array
explanation: Sorting arranges the elements of an array in a specified order, such as ascending (smallest to largest) or descending.

text: What is the difference between an array and a linked list in terms of access time?
options: Arrays have O(n) access; linked lists have O(1) access by index
Arrays have O(1) access by index; linked lists have O(n) access by index
Both data structures provide O(1) access time for any element
Both data structures provide O(n) access time for any element
explanation: Arrays provide O(1) random access by index due to contiguous memory, while linked lists require O(n) traversal to reach a specific index.

text: What is a hash table?
options: A sorted array that uses binary search for lookups
A data structure that maps keys to values using a hash function
A tree structure where each node has exactly two children
A queue that prioritizes elements by their insertion time
explanation: A hash table uses a hash function to compute an index into an array of buckets, from which the desired value can be found in average O(1) time.

text: What is a hash collision?
options: When a hash function produces the same output for two different keys
When two hash tables share the same underlying array storage
When a hash function fails to produce any output value
When the hash table runs out of available memory space
explanation: A hash collision occurs when two different keys produce the same hash value, requiring a collision resolution strategy like chaining or open addressing.

text: What is a priority queue?
options: A queue where elements are processed in insertion order only
A queue where elements are served based on their priority level
A queue that only accepts elements of the same data type
A queue with a fixed maximum capacity that cannot grow
explanation: A priority queue serves elements based on their priority rather than their insertion order, with higher-priority elements dequeued first.

text: What is the difference between a stack and a queue?
options: A stack uses FIFO order; a queue uses LIFO order
A stack uses LIFO order; a queue uses FIFO order
Both use FIFO order but with different memory layouts
Both use LIFO order but with different access patterns
explanation: A stack follows Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) order, while a queue follows First-In-First-Out (FIFO) order for adding and removing elements.

text: What is a doubly linked list?
options: A linked list that stores two values in each node
A linked list where each node has pointers to both next and previous nodes
A linked list with exactly two nodes at all times
A linked list where each node is connected to two separate lists
explanation: A doubly linked list has nodes with pointers to both the next and previous nodes, allowing traversal in both directions.

text: What is dynamic array resizing?
options: Sorting an array so that all empty slots are at the end
Automatically allocating a larger array when the current one is full
Reducing the size of an array by removing null elements only
Splitting an array into multiple smaller arrays of fixed sizes
explanation: Dynamic array resizing allocates a larger array (typically double the size) and copies elements when the current capacity is exceeded, amortizing the cost.

text: What is an iterator?
options: A function that creates new collections dynamically at runtime
An object that enables sequential access to elements of a collection
A variable that counts the number of elements in a collection
A sorting algorithm that works only on collection types
explanation: An iterator is an object that provides a way to sequentially access elements of a collection without exposing its underlying representation.

text: What is array slicing?
options: Removing all elements from an array permanently
Extracting a contiguous portion of an array as a new sub-array
Inserting elements at random positions within an array
Converting an array into a linked list data structure
explanation: Array slicing extracts a contiguous portion of an array between specified indices, creating a new sub-array without modifying the original.

text: What is the time complexity of searching in an unsorted array?
options: O(1) constant time in the worst case
O(log n) logarithmic time in the worst case
O(n) linear time in the worst case
O(n log n) linearithmic time in the worst case
explanation: Searching an unsorted array requires checking each element sequentially, resulting in O(n) time complexity in the worst case.

text: What is a bloom filter?
options: A sorting algorithm that filters out duplicate elements
A probabilistic data structure that tests set membership with possible false positives
A compression filter applied to array data before storage
A search algorithm that filters results by relevance score
explanation: A bloom filter is a space-efficient probabilistic structure that can report false positives but never false negatives when testing set membership.

text: What is amortized O(1) time complexity for dynamic array insertion?
options: Every single insertion always takes exactly constant time
The average cost per insertion is constant over a sequence of operations
Insertions alternate between O(1) and O(n) in a fixed pattern
The first insertion is O(1) and all subsequent ones are O(n)
explanation: Amortized O(1) means that while individual insertions may occasionally take O(n) when resizing, the average cost per insertion over many operations is constant.

text: What is a skip list?
options: A list that skips over null elements during traversal
A layered linked list with express lanes for O(log n) average search
A list that removes every other element for space savings
A fixed-size list that rejects elements when full
explanation: A skip list uses multiple layers of linked lists with increasingly sparse nodes, enabling O(log n) average search, insertion, and deletion.

text: What is a concurrent hash map?
options: A hash map that cannot be accessed from multiple threads
A hash map designed for safe concurrent access by multiple threads
A hash map that stores all values in a single global lock
A hash map that copies itself for each thread that accesses it
explanation: A concurrent hash map allows safe concurrent access from multiple threads, typically using fine-grained locking or lock-free techniques for different segments.

text: What is a persistent data structure?
options: A data structure that is saved to disk for long-term storage
A data structure that preserves previous versions when modified
A data structure that cannot be garbage collected by the runtime
A data structure that persists across multiple program executions
explanation: A persistent data structure preserves its previous version when modified, creating a new version while sharing structure with the old one.

text: What is a ring buffer (circular buffer)?
options: A buffer shaped like a ring for visual display purposes
A fixed-size buffer that wraps around, overwriting oldest data when full
A buffer that only stores data in a circular linked list format
A resizable buffer that doubles in size when capacity is reached
explanation: A ring buffer uses a fixed-size array with wrap-around pointers, overwriting the oldest data when full, commonly used for streaming and producer-consumer scenarios.

text: What is consistent hashing?
options: A hashing technique where all keys produce the same hash value
A hashing scheme that minimizes key redistribution when nodes change
A hash function that always produces output of consistent length
A technique for ensuring hash tables never have collisions
explanation: Consistent hashing maps keys and nodes to a ring, so that adding or removing nodes only requires redistributing a minimal number of keys.

text: What is a B-tree and where is it commonly used?
options: A binary tree used for in-memory sorting algorithms
A self-balancing tree with multiple keys per node used in databases and file systems
A tree where every node has exactly two children at all times
A graph structure used for network routing calculations
explanation: A B-tree is a self-balancing tree that can have multiple keys per node and multiple children, optimized for disk-based storage in databases and file systems.

text: What is a lock-free data structure?
options: A data structure that cannot be accessed by multiple threads
A concurrent data structure that uses atomic operations instead of locks
A data structure with no security restrictions on access
A data structure that automatically acquires locks when accessed
explanation: Lock-free data structures use atomic operations like compare-and-swap to ensure thread safety without traditional locks, avoiding deadlock and improving throughput.

text: What is cache-oblivious algorithm design for collections?
options: Designing algorithms that ignore cache entirely for simplicity
Designing algorithms that perform optimally across cache hierarchies without knowing cache size
Designing algorithms that clear the cache before each operation
Designing algorithms that only work with data fully loaded in cache
explanation: Cache-oblivious algorithms are designed to use the memory hierarchy efficiently without needing to know the cache size or line size parameters.

text: What is a class in object-oriented programming?
options: A blueprint that defines properties and behaviors for objects
A single instance of data stored in memory
A function that returns multiple values at once
A loop that iterates over a collection of elements
explanation: A class is a blueprint or template that defines the attributes and methods that its objects will have.

text: What is an object in OOP?
options: A data type used for storing text strings only
An instance of a class with its own state and behavior
A keyword used to declare variables in source code
A mathematical function used for calculations
explanation: An object is a specific instance of a class that has its own values for the attributes defined by the class.

text: What is inheritance in OOP?
options: A mechanism where a class acquires properties of another class
A process of hiding internal data from external access
A method for creating multiple objects simultaneously
A technique for optimizing code execution speed
explanation: Inheritance allows a child class to acquire the properties and methods of a parent class, promoting code reuse.

text: What is encapsulation in OOP?
options: Creating multiple instances from a single class
Bundling data and methods together and restricting direct access
Inheriting methods from a parent class automatically
Overriding a method in a subclass with new behavior
explanation: Encapsulation bundles data and the methods that operate on it together, restricting direct access to internal state through access modifiers.

text: What is polymorphism in OOP?
options: The ability for different classes to respond to the same method differently
The practice of hiding internal implementation details
The process of creating a new class from existing ones
The technique of storing multiple data types in one variable
explanation: Polymorphism allows objects of different classes to respond to the same method call in their own specific way.

text: What is a constructor in OOP?
options: A method that destroys an object when it is no longer needed
A special method called automatically when creating a new object
A method that returns the class name as a string value
A method that converts an object to a primitive data type
explanation: A constructor is a special method that is automatically called when a new object is created, typically used to initialize the object's state.

text: What is method overriding?
options: Defining multiple methods with the same name in one class
Providing a new implementation for an inherited method in a subclass
Calling a parent class method from within a child class
Removing a method from a class at runtime dynamically
explanation: Method overriding occurs when a subclass provides its own implementation of a method already defined in its parent class.

text: What is an interface in OOP?
options: A class that contains only fully implemented methods
A contract that defines method signatures without implementations
A variable that stores a reference to an object
A graphical window displayed to the end user
explanation: An interface defines a contract of method signatures that implementing classes must provide, without including the implementation.

text: What is a static method?
options: A method that can only be called on an instance of the class
A method that belongs to the class itself rather than any instance
A method that cannot return any value to the caller
A method that is automatically called during garbage collection
explanation: A static method belongs to the class rather than to any specific instance and can be called without creating an object.

text: What is abstraction in OOP?
options: Showing all implementation details to the end user
Hiding complex implementation details and showing only essential features
Creating multiple copies of the same class in memory
Converting an object to a string representation
explanation: Abstraction hides complex implementation details and exposes only the essential features and behaviors to the user.

text: What is the difference between an abstract class and an interface?
options: Abstract classes cannot have any methods; interfaces can have all methods
Abstract classes can have implemented methods; interfaces traditionally cannot
Interfaces support constructors; abstract classes do not support them
There is no difference between abstract classes and interfaces
explanation: Abstract classes can contain both abstract and implemented methods, while interfaces traditionally define only method signatures without implementations.

text: What is composition in OOP?
options: A class inheriting behavior from multiple parent classes
Building complex objects by combining simpler objects as fields
Overriding methods from a parent class in the child class
Converting an object to a different class type at runtime
explanation: Composition builds complex objects by including instances of other classes as fields, creating a 'has-a' relationship instead of 'is-a'.

text: What is the Liskov Substitution Principle?
options: A class should have only one reason to change its behavior
Subtype objects should be substitutable for their base type objects
Software entities should be open for extension but closed for modification
High-level modules should not depend on low-level module details
explanation: The Liskov Substitution Principle states that objects of a subtype should be usable anywhere the base type is expected without altering program correctness.

text: What is the difference between aggregation and composition?
options: Aggregation implies ownership; composition does not imply ownership
Composition implies ownership and lifecycle; aggregation does not
Both represent exactly the same type of relationship between objects
Aggregation is used in inheritance; composition in polymorphism
explanation: Composition implies strong ownership where the contained object's lifecycle is tied to the container, while aggregation is a weaker 'has-a' relationship without lifecycle dependency.

text: What is the Single Responsibility Principle?
options: A class should implement exactly one interface only
A class should have only one reason to change
A class should have only one method defined in it
A class should be responsible for creating all its dependencies
explanation: The Single Responsibility Principle states that a class should have only one reason to change, meaning it should have only one job or responsibility.

text: What is the diamond problem in multiple inheritance?
options: A class inheriting from two classes that share a common ancestor causes ambiguity
A class that inherits from exactly four parent classes simultaneously
A method that takes too long to execute due to deep inheritance
A design flaw where a class has too many private fields
explanation: The diamond problem occurs when a class inherits from two classes that share a common ancestor, creating ambiguity about which inherited version of a method to use.

text: What is dependency injection?
options: A class creating all of its own dependencies internally
Providing a class with its dependencies from the outside rather than creating them
Injecting debugging code into production classes at runtime
Removing all external library dependencies from a class
explanation: Dependency injection provides objects with their dependencies from the outside, rather than having them create dependencies internally, improving testability and decoupling.

text: What is the Open/Closed Principle?
options: Software should be open only during development and closed after deployment
Software entities should be open for extension but closed for modification
All class methods must be either public or private exclusively
Source code files should be opened and closed within the same function
explanation: The Open/Closed Principle states that software entities should be open for extension (adding new behavior) but closed for modification (not changing existing code).

text: What is the difference between shallow copy and deep copy of objects?
options: Shallow copy duplicates everything; deep copy only copies references
Shallow copy copies references to nested objects; deep copy duplicates all nested objects
Both create identical independent copies of the original object
Deep copy is faster because it copies less data than shallow copy
explanation: Shallow copy copies only the top-level fields, sharing references to nested objects, while deep copy recursively duplicates all nested objects.

text: What is the purpose of the this or self keyword in OOP?
options: To create a new instance of the current class
To refer to the current object instance within its own methods
To call a static method on the current class
To destroy the current object and free its memory
explanation: The this (or self) keyword refers to the current object instance, allowing access to its own fields and methods within the class.

text: What is the visitor design pattern?
options: A pattern that restricts object creation to a single instance
A pattern that adds new operations to objects without modifying their classes
A pattern that creates objects by copying an existing prototype
A pattern that provides a simplified interface to a complex subsystem
explanation: The visitor pattern lets you add new operations to a class hierarchy without changing the classes, by separating the algorithm from the objects it operates on.

text: What is covariant return type in OOP?
options: A return type that is always the same as the parent class method
An overriding method returning a more specific type than the parent method
A return type that changes dynamically based on the input arguments
A method that returns different types depending on runtime conditions
explanation: Covariant return types allow an overriding method to return a more specific (derived) type than the method in the parent class.

text: What is the SOLID principle of Interface Segregation?
options: All interfaces should be combined into one large universal interface
Clients should not be forced to depend on interfaces they do not use
Interfaces should only contain static methods and constants
Every class must implement all available interfaces in the system
explanation: The Interface Segregation Principle states that clients should not be forced to depend on methods they do not use, favoring many small interfaces over one large one.

text: What is the template method design pattern?
options: A pattern that creates objects using a generic template class
A pattern that defines the skeleton of an algorithm with subclass-specific steps
A pattern that generates source code from predefined templates
A pattern that creates type-safe collections using generics
explanation: The template method pattern defines the overall algorithm structure in a base class, deferring specific steps to subclasses that override designated methods.

text: What is the difference between the Strategy and State patterns?
options: Strategy encapsulates interchangeable algorithms; State encapsulates state-dependent behavior
State encapsulates algorithms; Strategy manages object state transitions
Both patterns serve the exact same purpose with identical implementations
Strategy is for creation; State is for structural organization
explanation: The Strategy pattern encapsulates interchangeable algorithms selected by the client, while the State pattern encapsulates behavior that changes based on the object's internal state.

text: What is mixins in the context of OOP?
options: A technique for combining two separate databases together
Reusable classes that provide methods to other classes without full inheritance
A method for mixing different data types in a single collection
A debugging technique that mixes test code with production code
explanation: Mixins are classes that provide methods to other classes through composition or multiple inheritance, adding functionality without the complexity of full inheritance hierarchies.

text: What is double dispatch?
options: Calling the same method on an object exactly two times
A technique where method selection depends on the runtime types of two objects
Dispatching a message to two different threads simultaneously
Creating two instances of an object in a single statement
explanation: Double dispatch selects the method to call based on the runtime types of both the receiver and the argument, often implemented with the Visitor pattern.

text: What is the Dependency Inversion Principle?
options: Low-level modules should depend directly on high-level modules
High-level modules should depend on abstractions, not concrete implementations
All dependencies should be removed to make classes fully independent
Dependencies should be inverted so that constructors take no parameters
explanation: The Dependency Inversion Principle states that high-level modules should depend on abstractions rather than concrete implementations, and abstractions should not depend on details.

text: What is metaclass programming?
options: Programming that focuses on creating user interface metadata
Using classes whose instances are themselves classes to control class creation
Writing programs that only use primitive data types
Creating abstract classes that cannot have any subclasses
explanation: Metaclass programming involves defining metaclasses (classes of classes) that control how classes are created, customizing class behavior at the type level.

text: What is the Law of Demeter (principle of least knowledge)?
options: Every module should know about every other module in the system
An object should only communicate with its immediate collaborators
All methods must be declared public for maximum accessibility
Classes should always have the maximum number of dependencies
explanation: The Law of Demeter states that an object should only talk to its immediate friends and not to strangers, reducing coupling between components.

text: What is memory allocation?
options: Deleting data from the computer's hard drive
Reserving a portion of memory for use by a program
Compressing data to reduce memory consumption
Transferring data between memory and the processor
explanation: Memory allocation is the process of reserving a block of memory for a program to store data during execution.

text: What is the stack in memory management?
options: A region for dynamically allocated objects with variable lifetimes
A region for local variables and function call data with automatic management
A section of disk storage used for virtual memory swapping
A hardware component that caches frequently accessed data
explanation: The stack is a memory region that stores local variables and function call information, automatically managed in LIFO order.

text: What is the heap in memory management?
options: A memory region for function call data managed automatically
A memory region for dynamically allocated data managed by the programmer
A CPU register used for storing computation results
A sorted data structure for efficient element retrieval
explanation: The heap is a region of memory used for dynamic allocation, where memory is manually allocated and freed by the programmer or garbage collector.

text: What is garbage collection?
options: Manually freeing memory using delete or free functions
Automatic reclamation of memory no longer in use by the program
Compressing files on disk to save storage space
Removing unused source code files from a project
explanation: Garbage collection automatically identifies and reclaims memory that is no longer referenced by the program, preventing memory leaks.

text: What is a memory leak?
options: Memory being accessed faster than the hardware allows
Allocated memory that is never freed and cannot be reclaimed
Data being corrupted during transfer between memory chips
A security vulnerability that exposes memory contents
explanation: A memory leak occurs when allocated memory is no longer needed but is never freed, causing the program to consume increasing amounts of memory.

text: What is a pointer in programming?
options: A variable that stores the memory address of another variable
A function that points to the next line of code to execute
A data type used only for storing floating-point numbers
A control structure that directs program execution flow
explanation: A pointer is a variable that holds the memory address of another variable, allowing indirect access to data.

text: What is a null pointer?
options: A pointer that points to the first byte of available memory
A pointer that does not point to any valid memory location
A pointer that automatically frees the memory it references
A pointer that can hold values of any data type
explanation: A null pointer is a pointer that does not point to any valid object or memory location, often used to indicate the absence of a value.

text: What is static memory allocation?
options: Allocating memory at compile time with a fixed size
Allocating memory at runtime based on user input
Allocating memory that can grow and shrink dynamically
Allocating memory only when the program first starts
explanation: Static memory allocation reserves memory at compile time with a fixed size that does not change during program execution.

text: What is dynamic memory allocation?
options: Allocating fixed memory at compile time for all variables
Allocating memory at runtime as needed during program execution
Permanently storing data on the hard disk drive
Assigning variables to CPU registers for faster processing
explanation: Dynamic memory allocation reserves memory during runtime as needed, allowing the program to request and release memory flexibly.

text: What is a stack overflow error?
options: An error caused by allocating too much heap memory
An error when the call stack exceeds its maximum size limit
An error caused by dividing a number by zero at runtime
An error when a program tries to access a null pointer
explanation: A stack overflow occurs when the call stack exceeds its size limit, often caused by infinite or very deep recursion.

text: What is a dangling pointer?
options: A pointer that points to memory that has already been freed
A pointer that has never been assigned any memory address
A pointer that points to the last element of an array
A pointer that is shared between multiple threads safely
explanation: A dangling pointer references memory that has been deallocated, leading to undefined behavior if the pointer is dereferenced.

text: What is reference counting in garbage collection?
options: Counting the number of variables declared in the program
Tracking how many references point to each object to determine when to free it
Counting the number of times a function is called during execution
Measuring the total amount of memory referenced by the program
explanation: Reference counting tracks the number of references to each object, freeing the object when its count drops to zero.

text: What is the difference between malloc and calloc in C?
options: malloc initializes memory to zero; calloc does not initialize
calloc initializes memory to zero; malloc leaves it uninitialized
Both functions behave identically with no differences at all
malloc allocates on the stack; calloc allocates on the heap
explanation: calloc allocates memory and initializes all bytes to zero, while malloc allocates memory without initializing it, leaving the contents indeterminate.

text: What is memory fragmentation?
options: Breaking memory into equal-sized pages for virtual memory
Having free memory scattered in small blocks that cannot satisfy large allocations
Dividing a program into separate memory-independent modules
Compressing memory contents to create contiguous free space
explanation: Memory fragmentation occurs when free memory is split into small, non-contiguous blocks, making it difficult to allocate large contiguous blocks even though total free memory is sufficient.

text: What is a smart pointer?
options: A pointer that can point to any type of data without casting
An object that manages a pointer with automatic memory deallocation
A pointer that is optimized for faster memory access speed
A pointer that points to multiple memory locations simultaneously
explanation: A smart pointer is an object that wraps a raw pointer and automatically manages memory deallocation, preventing leaks and dangling pointers.

text: What is the difference between unique_ptr and shared_ptr in C++?
options: unique_ptr allows multiple owners; shared_ptr allows only one owner
unique_ptr has exclusive ownership; shared_ptr allows multiple owners with reference counting
Both pointer types have identical ownership semantics and behavior
shared_ptr is for stack memory; unique_ptr is for heap memory
explanation: unique_ptr has exclusive ownership of the managed object, while shared_ptr uses reference counting to allow multiple pointers to share ownership.

text: What is virtual memory?
options: Memory that exists only inside the CPU registers
An abstraction that gives programs the illusion of a large contiguous address space
A type of memory used exclusively for storing virtual machine data
Memory that is shared between all running programs equally
explanation: Virtual memory provides each process with the illusion of a large contiguous address space by mapping virtual addresses to physical memory or disk.

text: What is a buffer overflow?
options: Writing data beyond the allocated boundary of a buffer in memory
A buffer that is too large and wastes memory resources
A technique for speeding up data transfer between devices
An error that occurs when a buffer is empty and read from
explanation: A buffer overflow occurs when data is written beyond the allocated boundary of a buffer, potentially overwriting adjacent memory and causing crashes or security vulnerabilities.

text: What is RAII (Resource Acquisition Is Initialization)?
options: Resources are acquired in a separate initialization phase only
Resource lifetime is tied to object lifetime using constructors and destructors
Resources are initialized lazily only when first accessed at runtime
All resources are acquired at program startup and never released
explanation: RAII ties resource management to object lifetime: resources are acquired in the constructor and released in the destructor, ensuring automatic cleanup.

text: What is memory alignment?
options: Sorting data in memory in alphabetical order for fast search
Placing data at memory addresses that are multiples of the data size
Encrypting memory contents to prevent unauthorized access
Compressing data in memory to fit more into available RAM
explanation: Memory alignment places data at addresses that are multiples of the data's size, allowing the CPU to access it efficiently in fewer operations.

text: What is the mark-and-sweep garbage collection algorithm?
options: An algorithm that counts references to determine when to free objects
An algorithm that marks reachable objects and sweeps unmarked ones as garbage
An algorithm that moves all live objects to one end of memory
An algorithm that immediately frees objects when they go out of scope
explanation: Mark-and-sweep first marks all objects reachable from root references, then sweeps through memory freeing all unmarked (unreachable) objects.

text: What is generational garbage collection?
options: Collecting garbage from each function generation separately
Separating objects by age into generations and collecting younger ones more frequently
Running garbage collection once per program generation lifecycle
Generating new memory blocks to replace garbage-collected ones
explanation: Generational GC divides objects into generations by age, collecting the young generation more frequently since most objects die young (weak generational hypothesis).

text: What is the ownership model in Rust?
options: Multiple variables can own the same data simultaneously at all times
Each value has exactly one owner, and memory is freed when the owner goes out of scope
The garbage collector owns all memory and decides when to free it
The programmer must manually call free for every allocated value
explanation: Rust's ownership model ensures each value has exactly one owner, and memory is automatically freed when the owner goes out of scope, enforced at compile time.

text: What is a memory pool (object pool)?
options: A shared memory space accessible by all programs on the system
A pre-allocated block of memory from which objects are allocated and returned
A pool of threads that manage memory allocation concurrently
A network resource for sharing memory between remote machines
explanation: A memory pool pre-allocates a large block of memory and manages allocation and deallocation within it, reducing fragmentation and allocation overhead.

text: What is copy-on-write (COW) memory optimization?
options: Copying all data immediately when any reference is created
Sharing memory between copies and only duplicating when a write occurs
Writing data to multiple memory locations simultaneously for safety
Copying memory contents to disk before performing write operations
explanation: Copy-on-write shares memory between copies and only creates a separate copy when one of them is modified, saving memory for data that is mostly read.

text: What is the difference between compacting and non-compacting garbage collectors?
options: Compacting GC is faster; non-compacting GC is slower in all cases
Compacting GC moves live objects together to eliminate fragmentation; non-compacting does not
Non-compacting GC moves objects; compacting GC leaves them in place
Both types produce identical memory layouts after collection
explanation: Compacting collectors move live objects together to eliminate memory fragmentation, while non-compacting collectors free memory in place without relocating objects.

text: What is a weak reference?
options: A reference that prevents the garbage collector from freeing the object
A reference that does not prevent the referenced object from being garbage collected
A reference that can only point to primitive data types
A reference that is automatically set to null on every function call
explanation: A weak reference does not prevent the referenced object from being garbage collected, useful for caches and avoiding circular reference memory leaks.

text: What is escape analysis in JVM optimization?
options: Analyzing code for potential security escape vulnerabilities
Determining if an object can be allocated on the stack instead of the heap
Detecting escape characters in string literals during parsing
Analyzing recursion depth to prevent stack overflow errors
explanation: Escape analysis determines whether an object's reference escapes the method scope; if not, the JVM can allocate it on the stack instead of the heap for better performance.

text: What is the slab allocator in operating system memory management?
options: An allocator that divides memory into fixed-size pages only
An allocator that caches freed objects of the same type for efficient reuse
An allocator that allocates memory in continuously increasing block sizes
An allocator that uses a linked list of variable-size free blocks
explanation: The slab allocator pre-allocates memory for objects of specific sizes and caches freed objects for reuse, reducing allocation overhead and fragmentation.

text: What is false sharing in multiprocessor memory?
options: Two threads accessing the same variable without proper locking
Two threads on different cores invalidating each other's cache by accessing adjacent memory
Sharing memory between two processes that should be isolated
A situation where memory appears shared but is actually private
explanation: False sharing occurs when threads on different cores modify variables on the same cache line, causing unnecessary cache invalidation and performance degradation.

text: What is an exception in programming?
options: A normal event that occurs during every program execution
An unexpected event that disrupts the normal flow of execution
A special type of loop that runs exceptionally fast
A function that handles user input validation logic
explanation: An exception is an unexpected event or error condition that disrupts the normal flow of program execution.

text: What is a try-catch block used for?
options: Defining a function that may throw errors
Wrapping code that may throw exceptions and handling them
Creating a loop that retries failed operations
Declaring variables that might have invalid values
explanation: A try-catch block wraps code that might throw an exception in the try section and handles the exception in the catch section.

text: What does the throw keyword do?
options: Catches an exception that was previously raised
Signals an exception to be handled by a catch block
Deletes an object from memory immediately
Returns a value from a function to the caller
explanation: The throw keyword raises or signals an exception, transferring control to the nearest matching catch block.

text: What is the finally block used for?
options: Executing code only when an exception is thrown
Executing cleanup code regardless of whether an exception occurred
Defining the last function in a program file
Terminating the program after an unrecoverable error
explanation: The finally block contains code that always executes after the try-catch, regardless of whether an exception was thrown, typically used for cleanup.

text: What is a runtime exception?
options: An error detected at compile time before execution
An exception that occurs during program execution
An error in the source code formatting or style
An exception that only happens during testing
explanation: A runtime exception occurs during program execution, such as dividing by zero or accessing an invalid array index.

text: What is a NullPointerException?
options: An error when a program runs out of available memory
An exception caused by accessing a member through a null reference
An error when a pointer exceeds the array bounds
An exception thrown when dividing a number by zero
explanation: A NullPointerException occurs when the program attempts to use a reference that points to null, such as calling a method on a null object.

text: What is an IndexOutOfBoundsException?
options: An exception when a variable exceeds its maximum value
An exception when accessing an array with an invalid index
An exception when a loop counter becomes negative
An exception when too many functions are called recursively
explanation: An IndexOutOfBoundsException occurs when a program tries to access an array or collection element using an index that is outside its valid range.

text: What is a custom exception?
options: An exception that can never be caught by a catch block
A user-defined exception class for application-specific errors
An exception provided by the programming language standard library
An exception that only occurs in production environments
explanation: A custom exception is a user-defined exception class created to represent specific error conditions in the application.

text: What is exception propagation?
options: Handling an exception at the exact point where it occurs
An exception passing up the call stack until a handler is found
Creating multiple exceptions from a single error condition
Converting one type of exception to a different type
explanation: Exception propagation is the process of an unhandled exception traveling up the call stack until it finds a matching catch block or terminates the program.

text: What is an ArithmeticException?
options: An exception for invalid string formatting operations
An exception for illegal arithmetic operations like division by zero
An exception for running out of available heap memory
An exception for accessing a file that does not exist
explanation: An ArithmeticException occurs when an illegal arithmetic operation is performed, such as integer division by zero.

text: What is the difference between checked and unchecked exceptions in Java?
options: Checked exceptions occur at runtime; unchecked at compile time
Checked must be declared or caught; unchecked do not need to be declared
Unchecked exceptions must always be caught; checked never need catching
Both types must always be explicitly caught in a try-catch block
explanation: Checked exceptions must be declared in the method signature or caught in a try-catch block, while unchecked exceptions (RuntimeException subclasses) do not require this.

text: What is exception chaining?
options: Catching multiple unrelated exceptions in a single catch block
Wrapping one exception as the cause of another to preserve context
Throwing the same exception multiple times in a row
Linking catch blocks together without any try blocks
explanation: Exception chaining wraps a caught exception as the cause of a new exception, preserving the original error context while throwing a more appropriate exception.

text: What is a try-with-resources statement in Java?
options: A try block that retries the operation using different resources
A try block that automatically closes declared resources after completion
A try block that limits the resources a program can consume
A try block that shares resources between multiple catch blocks
explanation: Try-with-resources automatically closes resources (like streams and connections) declared in the try statement, even if an exception occurs.

text: When should you use exceptions versus return codes for error handling?
options: Return codes should always be used instead of exceptions
Exceptions for unexpected errors; return codes for expected conditions
Exceptions should only be used for input validation checks
Return codes and exceptions should never be used in the same program
explanation: Exceptions are best for unexpected error conditions that disrupt normal flow, while return codes suit expected conditions that the caller can handle directly.

text: What is a stack trace in exception handling?
options: A list of all variables stored on the call stack
A report showing the sequence of method calls leading to the exception
A trace of memory allocations made on the stack
A log of all exceptions thrown during program execution
explanation: A stack trace shows the sequence of method calls from the program entry point to the point where the exception was thrown, aiding in debugging.

text: What is the purpose of multiple catch blocks?
options: To catch the same exception type multiple times for redundancy
To handle different exception types with specific recovery logic
To increase the speed of exception handling during execution
To convert all exceptions to a single unified exception type
explanation: Multiple catch blocks allow different exception types to be handled with specific recovery or logging logic appropriate to each error condition.

text: What is a sentinel return value as an alternative to exceptions?
options: A return value that indicates the function completed successfully
A special return value like -1 or null that indicates an error occurred
A value returned by the finally block after exception handling
A default value assigned to uninitialized variables by the runtime
explanation: A sentinel return value is a special value (like -1, null, or None) returned by a function to indicate an error condition instead of throwing an exception.

text: What is defensive programming in relation to error handling?
options: Writing code that attacks potential security vulnerabilities
Writing code that anticipates and handles potential error conditions proactively
Removing all error handling code to simplify the program
Using only system-level exceptions and avoiding custom exceptions
explanation: Defensive programming anticipates potential errors by adding checks, validations, and safeguards to prevent problems before they cause exceptions.

text: What is the difference between Error and Exception in Java?
options: Errors are recoverable; Exceptions are always unrecoverable
Errors indicate serious problems not meant to be caught; Exceptions are meant to be handled
Exceptions are more severe than Errors in all cases
Both are identical classes with no practical difference
explanation: Errors represent serious problems like OutOfMemoryError that programs typically should not try to handle, while Exceptions represent conditions that programs can reasonably catch and recover from.

text: What is an assertion in programming?
options: A statement that always evaluates to true during execution
A check that verifies a condition is true and fails if it is false
A type of comment used to document expected behavior
A variable that stores the result of a boolean expression
explanation: An assertion checks that a condition holds true at a specific point in the code, and if the condition is false, it terminates the program with an error message.

text: What is the Result type pattern (Either/Result) as an alternative to exceptions?
options: A design pattern that uses global variables to track error states
A type that encapsulates either a success value or an error value explicitly
A pattern that logs all results to a file for later analysis
A technique that retries operations automatically until they succeed
explanation: The Result/Either type encapsulates either a success value or an error value, making error handling explicit in the type system without using exceptions.

text: What are the performance implications of exception handling?
options: Exceptions have zero overhead in all programming language implementations
Try blocks are cheap but throwing and catching exceptions is relatively expensive
Every line inside a try block runs significantly slower than outside
Exception handling is always faster than using error return codes
explanation: Modern exception implementations have low overhead for try blocks (zero-cost on the happy path), but the actual throw-and-catch mechanism involves stack unwinding and is relatively expensive.

text: What is exception safety in C++?
options: Ensuring that no exceptions are ever thrown by the program code
Guaranteeing program state remains valid when exceptions are thrown
Using only try-catch blocks and never using error return codes
Disabling exception support in the compiler for safety
explanation: Exception safety guarantees that program invariants are maintained, resources are not leaked, and the program remains in a valid state when exceptions occur.

text: What is the strong exception guarantee?
options: An operation succeeds completely or has no observable effect on state
An operation always throws an exception if any error occurs
An operation is guaranteed to never throw any exception at all
An operation catches all exceptions and converts them to warnings
explanation: The strong exception guarantee means that if an operation throws an exception, the program state is rolled back to what it was before the operation began.

text: What is structured error handling using monadic error types?
options: Using deeply nested try-catch blocks for every possible error
Chaining operations that may fail using types like Maybe/Option and Result/Either
Structuring code so that all errors are handled by a single global handler
Defining error codes in a structured enumeration type only
explanation: Monadic error handling uses types like Maybe/Option and Result/Either to chain operations that may fail, propagating errors through the type system instead of exceptions.

text: What is panic and recover in Go's error handling model?
options: Panic throws a checked exception; recover catches it in a catch block
Panic stops normal execution; recover in a deferred function regains control
Panic and recover are identical to try and catch in Java
Panic logs an error message; recover restarts the entire program
explanation: In Go, panic stops normal execution and begins unwinding the stack, while recover called in a deferred function can catch the panic and regain control.

text: What is the noexcept specifier in C++?
options: A keyword that automatically catches all exceptions within a function
A specifier indicating a function guarantees it will not throw exceptions
A modifier that disables exception handling for the entire program
A directive that converts exceptions to error return codes
explanation: The noexcept specifier declares that a function will not throw exceptions, enabling compiler optimizations and causing std::terminate if an exception does escape.

text: What is the difference between resumption and termination semantics for exceptions?
options: Resumption continues from the throw point; termination unwinds the stack
Termination continues from the throw point; resumption unwinds the stack
Both approaches always unwind the stack and never resume
Both approaches always continue execution from the throw point
explanation: Resumption semantics allow execution to continue from the point where the exception was thrown after handling it, while termination semantics unwind the stack and never return to the throw point.

text: What is exception filtering in languages like C# and VB.NET?
options: Removing all exception-related code during compilation for performance
Catching an exception only if a specified condition is true, using when clauses
Filtering exceptions to send them to different log files by type
Preventing certain exception types from ever being thrown in code
explanation: Exception filtering allows a catch block to specify a condition (when clause) that must be true for the exception to be caught, enabling more precise handling.

text: What is the zero-cost exception model?
options: An exception model where no exceptions are ever thrown by any code
A model with no overhead on the normal path but higher cost when exceptions occur
A model that eliminates all costs associated with exception handling entirely
A model where exception handling is replaced by error return codes
explanation: The zero-cost exception model (table-based) has no performance overhead on the normal execution path, but incurs higher cost when exceptions are actually thrown due to table lookup and stack unwinding.

text: What is a module in programming?
options: A single line of executable code in a program
A self-contained unit of code that encapsulates related functionality
A type of variable used for storing global state
A hardware component connected to the computer system
explanation: A module is a self-contained unit of code that groups related functions, classes, and variables together for organization and reuse.

text: What is a package in programming?
options: A compressed archive of source code files only
A collection of related modules organized in a directory structure
A tool for debugging code in an integrated environment
A single function exported from a library
explanation: A package is a collection of related modules organized together, often in a directory hierarchy, for distribution and namespace management.

text: What does the import statement do?
options: Exports functions from the current module to other modules
Brings functionality from another module into the current scope
Deletes a module from the project permanently
Compiles a module into standalone machine code
explanation: The import statement brings functions, classes, or variables from another module into the current scope for use.

text: What is a namespace?
options: The physical file path where source code is stored
A container that holds identifiers to avoid naming conflicts
A special variable that stores the program name
A function that generates unique identifier names
explanation: A namespace is a container that holds identifiers like function and variable names, preventing naming conflicts between different parts of a program.

text: What is a library in programming?
options: A tool for writing documentation about source code
A collection of precompiled code that provides reusable functionality
A text editor designed for writing source code files
A database that stores program execution logs
explanation: A library is a collection of prewritten and precompiled code that provides reusable functions and classes for common tasks.

text: What is a package manager?
options: A tool for compiling source code into executable programs
A tool for installing, updating, and managing software dependencies
A tool for formatting code according to style guidelines
A tool for monitoring program performance at runtime
explanation: A package manager automates the process of installing, upgrading, configuring, and removing software packages and their dependencies.

text: What is the purpose of exporting from a module?
options: Making specific functions or variables available to other modules
Sending data from a program to an external hardware device
Converting module code to a different programming language
Removing unused code from a module to reduce file size
explanation: Exporting makes specific functions, classes, or variables from a module accessible to other modules that import them.

text: What is a dependency in software development?
options: A bug that depends on specific input to reproduce
An external library or module that a project requires to function
A function that depends on global variables for its behavior
A test case that depends on other tests passing first
explanation: A dependency is an external library, module, or package that a software project requires to compile or run correctly.

text: What is an API (Application Programming Interface)?
options: A graphical interface that users interact with directly
A set of defined methods and protocols for building and interacting with software
A programming language used to create mobile applications
A type of database for storing application data
explanation: An API defines a set of methods, protocols, and tools that specify how software components should interact with each other.

text: What is a header file in C/C++?
options: The first line of executable code in a source file
A file containing declarations of functions and types shared between source files
A compiled binary file included in the final executable
A file that stores the program's configuration settings
explanation: A header file contains function declarations, type definitions, and macros that are shared between multiple source files through the #include directive.

text: What is the difference between static and dynamic linking?
options: Static linking happens at runtime; dynamic linking at compile time
Static linking includes library code in the executable; dynamic linking loads it at runtime
Both approaches produce identical executable files in all cases
Dynamic linking is only used for standard library functions
explanation: Static linking copies library code directly into the executable at compile time, while dynamic linking loads shared libraries at runtime, keeping the executable smaller.

text: What is semantic versioning (SemVer)?
options: A system for naming version branches in source code repositories
A versioning scheme using MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH to convey change significance
A tool for automatically generating version numbers from git commits
A method for comparing the quality of different software versions
explanation: Semantic versioning uses MAJOR.MINOR.PATCH format where major increments indicate breaking changes, minor for new features, and patch for bug fixes.

text: What is dependency injection versus dependency lookup?
options: Injection pushes dependencies in; lookup has the component pull dependencies
Lookup pushes dependencies in; injection has the component pull dependencies
Both terms describe the same mechanism with no practical difference
Injection is for testing only; lookup is for production environments
explanation: Dependency injection provides dependencies to a component from outside, while dependency lookup has the component actively request its dependencies from a container.

text: What is circular dependency between modules?
options: A module that depends on every other module in the project
Two or more modules that depend on each other forming a cycle
A dependency that is only needed during certain phases of execution
A module that is imported but never actually used in the code
explanation: A circular dependency occurs when two or more modules depend on each other, creating a cycle that can cause initialization problems and tight coupling.

text: What is a lock file in package management?
options: A file that prevents other developers from modifying the source code
A file that records the exact versions of all dependencies installed
A file that locks the project from accepting any new dependencies
A file that restricts which packages can be imported by the code
explanation: A lock file records the exact versions of all installed dependencies, ensuring consistent installations across different environments and developers.

text: What is the difference between a framework and a library?
options: A framework calls your code; a library is called by your code
A library calls your code; a framework is called by your code
Both are identical in structure and usage in all projects
Frameworks are only for web development; libraries for desktop apps
explanation: A framework provides the overall structure and calls your code (inversion of control), while a library provides functions that your code calls as needed.

text: What is tree shaking in module bundling?
options: Reorganizing the module dependency tree for better readability
Eliminating unused code from the final bundle by analyzing imports
Loading all modules at program startup to improve runtime speed
Randomly selecting which modules to include in the build
explanation: Tree shaking is an optimization that analyzes import/export relationships to remove unused code from the final bundle, reducing the output size.

text: What is lazy loading of modules?
options: Loading all modules at application startup for faster access
Loading modules only when they are actually needed during execution
Loading modules in alphabetical order of their file names
Disabling module loading entirely for smaller application size
explanation: Lazy loading defers the loading of a module until it is actually needed, reducing initial startup time and memory usage.

text: What is a transitive dependency?
options: A dependency that is explicitly listed in the project configuration
A dependency required by one of the project's direct dependencies
A dependency that is only used during the testing phase
A dependency that transitions between active and inactive states
explanation: A transitive dependency is a package that is not directly required by the project but is needed by one of the project's direct dependencies.

text: What is the purpose of an __init__.py file in Python packages?
options: It contains the main entry point of the Python application
It marks a directory as a Python package and can initialize the package
It stores configuration settings for the Python interpreter
It defines the minimum Python version required by the project
explanation: The __init__.py file marks a directory as a Python package, enabling module imports from it, and can contain initialization code for the package.

text: What is the diamond dependency problem in package management?
options: A dependency graph shaped like a diamond causing version conflicts
Having exactly four dependencies that all depend on each other
A package that requires a diamond-level paid subscription to use
A circular dependency between four packages in the project
explanation: The diamond dependency problem occurs when two dependencies both require different versions of a shared dependency, creating potential version conflicts.

text: What is the module pattern for encapsulation in JavaScript?
options: Using global variables to share data between all scripts on a page
Using closures and IIFEs to create private scope and expose a public API
Defining all functions as methods on the window object
Using HTML script tags to separate JavaScript modules
explanation: The module pattern uses closures and immediately invoked function expressions (IIFEs) to create private scope, exposing only a controlled public API.

text: What is the difference between CommonJS and ES modules?
options: CommonJS uses import/export; ES modules use require/module.exports
CommonJS uses require/module.exports synchronously; ES modules use import/export with static analysis
Both module systems have identical syntax and loading behavior
ES modules are only available in browser environments
explanation: CommonJS uses require() for synchronous loading and module.exports, while ES modules use import/export keywords with static analysis enabling tree shaking.

text: What is the OSGi module system in Java?
options: A simple file packaging format for distributing Java applications
A dynamic module system that manages component lifecycles and versioned dependencies
A tool for converting Java code to other programming languages
A testing framework for validating module interactions
explanation: OSGi is a dynamic module system for Java that manages bundle (module) lifecycles, versioned dependencies, and service registration at runtime.

text: What is the Java Platform Module System (JPMS) introduced in Java 9?
options: A system for converting Java code to native machine code modules
A system for strong encapsulation and explicit module dependencies using module-info.java
A system for automatically generating module documentation
A system for compiling Java modules into JavaScript
explanation: JPMS provides strong encapsulation by allowing modules to declare which packages they export and which modules they depend on through module-info.java.

text: What is dependency resolution in package managers?
options: Removing all dependencies from a project to make it self-contained
Finding compatible versions of all required packages satisfying all constraints
Converting dependencies from one package manager format to another
Documenting all dependencies in a human-readable report
explanation: Dependency resolution is the process of finding a set of package versions that satisfies all version constraints from direct and transitive dependencies simultaneously.

text: What is ABI (Application Binary Interface) compatibility?
options: Compatibility between source code written in different programming languages
Compatibility at the binary level allowing compiled modules to work together
Compatibility between different operating system user interfaces
Compatibility between different versions of a source code editor
explanation: ABI compatibility ensures that compiled binary modules can interface correctly, covering calling conventions, data layout, and symbol naming at the machine code level.

text: What is a monorepo approach to module organization?
options: Storing each module in its own separate repository on the server
Storing all related modules and packages in a single version-controlled repository
Using only one module for the entire application with no dependencies
A repository that only accepts contributions from a single developer
explanation: A monorepo keeps all related modules and packages in a single repository, simplifying dependency management and cross-module changes at the cost of repository size.

text: What is the Inversion of Control (IoC) container?
options: A data structure that stores elements in reverse order
A framework component that manages object creation and dependency wiring
A container that inverts the priority of queued elements
A virtual machine that runs code in reverse execution order
explanation: An IoC container manages the creation, configuration, and wiring of application objects and their dependencies, implementing dependency injection automatically.

text: What is the plugin architecture pattern?
options: A pattern where all code must be compiled into a single binary file
A pattern where a core system loads extensions at runtime to add functionality
A pattern for compressing application code into smaller modules
A pattern that restricts the number of modules in an application
explanation: The plugin architecture defines extension points in a core system that allow external modules (plugins) to be loaded at runtime, adding functionality without modifying the core.

text: What is recursion in programming?
options: A technique where a function calls itself to solve a problem
A loop that iterates through a collection of elements
A method for organizing code into separate modules
A process of converting source code to machine code
explanation: Recursion is a technique where a function calls itself to break down a problem into smaller subproblems until a base case is reached.

text: What is a design pattern in software engineering?
options: A graphical layout for user interface design
A reusable solution to a commonly occurring problem in software design
A specific programming language syntax rule
A template for creating database table structures
explanation: A design pattern is a general reusable solution to a commonly occurring problem within a given context in software design.

text: What is the Singleton design pattern?
options: A pattern that creates multiple instances of a class
A pattern that ensures a class has only one instance
A pattern that separates data from behavior completely
A pattern that connects two incompatible interfaces
explanation: The Singleton pattern ensures that a class has only one instance and provides a global point of access to it.

text: What is generic programming?
options: Writing code that only works with one specific data type
Writing code that works with any data type using type parameters
Writing code without any variables or data types defined
Writing code that generates random output each time
explanation: Generic programming uses type parameters to write code that works with any data type while maintaining type safety.

text: What is an event-driven programming model?
options: Programs execute statements strictly from top to bottom
Program flow is determined by events such as user actions or messages
Programs run all functions simultaneously in parallel
Programs process all data in a single batch operation
explanation: Event-driven programming determines the flow of execution based on events like user input, timer triggers, or messages from other programs.

text: What is an iterator pattern?
options: A pattern that creates objects without specifying their exact class
A pattern that provides sequential access to collection elements
A pattern that converts one interface to another compatible one
A pattern that defines a family of interchangeable algorithms
explanation: The iterator pattern provides a way to access elements of a collection sequentially without exposing its underlying representation.

text: What is the Observer design pattern?
options: A pattern where one object watches for file system changes
A pattern where objects are notified when a subject changes state
A pattern that observes memory usage during program execution
A pattern for monitoring network traffic between servers
explanation: The Observer pattern defines a one-to-many dependency where all dependent objects (observers) are notified when the subject's state changes.

text: What is the Factory design pattern?
options: A pattern that deletes objects when they are no longer needed
A pattern that creates objects without specifying the exact class to create
A pattern that converts objects from one format to another
A pattern that restricts access to certain object methods
explanation: The Factory pattern provides an interface for creating objects without specifying the exact class, letting subclasses or configuration determine the type.

text: What is reflection in programming?
options: Mirroring data between two separate databases
The ability of a program to examine and modify its own structure at runtime
Reversing the execution order of statements in a program
Creating a backup copy of the program source code
explanation: Reflection allows a program to inspect and modify its own structure, such as examining classes, methods, and fields at runtime.

text: What is the MVC (Model-View-Controller) pattern?
options: A pattern that merges all code into a single monolithic file
A pattern that separates data, presentation, and input handling concerns
A pattern for encrypting data before storing it in a database
A pattern for managing concurrent access to shared resources
explanation: MVC separates an application into Model (data/logic), View (presentation), and Controller (input handling), promoting separation of concerns.

text: What is the Decorator design pattern?
options: A pattern that adds visual decorations to user interface elements
A pattern that dynamically adds responsibilities to objects without subclassing
A pattern that removes unnecessary features from an object at runtime
A pattern that creates a decorated copy of an existing database table
explanation: The Decorator pattern wraps an object to dynamically add new responsibilities or behaviors without modifying the original class or using subclassing.

text: What is metaprogramming?
options: Writing documentation about how a program works
Writing programs that generate, manipulate, or transform other programs
Debugging programs using a special meta-debugging tool
Creating programs that only run in virtual environments
explanation: Metaprogramming involves writing code that generates, manipulates, or transforms other code, either at compile time (macros) or at runtime (reflection).

text: What is the Adapter design pattern?
options: A pattern that adapts the screen size for different devices
A pattern that converts one interface to another that clients expect
A pattern that creates adapters between hardware and software
A pattern that adjusts program performance based on system load
explanation: The Adapter pattern converts the interface of a class into another interface that clients expect, enabling classes with incompatible interfaces to work together.

text: What is the difference between compile-time and runtime polymorphism?
options: Compile-time uses method overriding; runtime uses method overloading
Compile-time uses method overloading; runtime uses method overriding via virtual dispatch
Both types of polymorphism are resolved at the exact same stage
Compile-time polymorphism does not exist in any programming language
explanation: Compile-time polymorphism is achieved through method overloading and generics resolved at compile time, while runtime polymorphism uses method overriding with virtual dispatch.

text: What is the Command design pattern?
options: A pattern that executes system shell commands from within a program
A pattern that encapsulates a request as an object enabling undo and queuing
A pattern that sends commands to a remote server for processing
A pattern that creates a command-line interface for the application
explanation: The Command pattern encapsulates a request as an object, allowing parameterization, queuing, logging, and undoable operations.
