text: What is the Proxy design pattern?
options: A pattern that creates a backup copy of an object for recovery
A pattern that provides a surrogate object controlling access to another object
A pattern that sends network requests through a proxy server
A pattern that simplifies complex algorithms into a single function
explanation: The Proxy pattern provides a placeholder or surrogate object that controls access to another object, useful for lazy loading, access control, and logging.

text: What is aspect-oriented programming (AOP)?
options: Programming that focuses on the visual aspects of user interfaces
Separating cross-cutting concerns like logging from core business logic
Programming from multiple perspectives or angles simultaneously
Optimizing specific aspects of program performance at compile time
explanation: AOP separates cross-cutting concerns (like logging, security, and transactions) from core business logic, weaving them in at specified join points.

text: What is the Builder design pattern?
options: A pattern for building user interfaces from predefined components
A pattern for constructing complex objects step by step with a fluent API
A pattern for compiling source code into executable binaries
A pattern for building database schemas from class definitions
explanation: The Builder pattern constructs complex objects step by step, allowing the same construction process to create different representations.

text: What are annotations or decorators in code?
options: Comments that describe how a function should be used
Metadata attached to code elements that can influence behavior at compile or runtime
Visual formatting applied to source code for readability
Test cases written alongside the source code they validate
explanation: Annotations or decorators are metadata attached to classes, methods, or fields that provide information to the compiler or runtime, influencing behavior.

text: What is the Facade design pattern?
options: A pattern that exposes all internal details of a subsystem publicly
A pattern that provides a simplified interface to a complex subsystem
A pattern that creates multiple facades for different user roles
A pattern that encrypts the external interface of a system
explanation: The Facade pattern provides a simplified, unified interface to a set of interfaces in a complex subsystem, making it easier to use.

text: What is the CQRS (Command Query Responsibility Segregation) pattern?
options: Using the same model for both reading and writing all data
Separating read and write operations into different models for scalability
Querying a database using commands instead of standard SQL syntax
Segregating command-line arguments from graphical user inputs
explanation: CQRS separates the read model from the write model, allowing each to be optimized independently for different scalability and performance requirements.

text: What is the Actor model in programming?
options: A model where objects inherit behavior from actor base classes
A concurrency model where actors communicate via asynchronous message passing
A model for simulating real-world actors in video game engines
A design pattern for creating theatrical user interface animations
explanation: The Actor model treats actors as the fundamental unit of computation; each actor has private state and communicates exclusively through asynchronous message passing.

text: What is the Flyweight design pattern?
options: A pattern for creating lightweight user interface components
A pattern that shares common state among many objects to reduce memory usage
A pattern for compressing data to reduce file size on disk
A pattern that creates objects with minimal method definitions
explanation: The Flyweight pattern shares intrinsic (common) state among many objects while keeping extrinsic (unique) state external, significantly reducing memory usage.

text: What is event sourcing?
options: Sourcing events from external third-party services via API calls
Storing all state changes as a sequence of immutable events rather than current state
Creating events dynamically from source code annotations at runtime
Distributing event handling across multiple source code files
explanation: Event sourcing persists the state of an entity as a sequence of immutable domain events, enabling complete audit trails and state reconstruction at any point in time.

text: What is the difference between a macro and a function?
options: Macros are expanded at compile time; functions are called at runtime
Functions are expanded at compile time; macros are called at runtime
Both are processed at exactly the same stage of compilation
Macros can only contain a single expression; functions cannot
explanation: Macros are expanded by the preprocessor or compiler at compile time, performing text or AST substitution, while functions are called at runtime with their own stack frame.

text: What is the hexagonal architecture (ports and adapters)?
options: An architecture with exactly six modules connected in a ring pattern
An architecture that isolates core logic from external systems using ports and adapters
An architecture optimized for systems with six or fewer microservices
An architecture that uses hexagonal data structures for storage
explanation: Hexagonal architecture isolates the core business logic from external systems by defining ports (interfaces) and adapters (implementations), enabling easy substitution.

text: What is type-driven development?
options: Developing applications that only use primitive data types
Using the type system to guide design and encode business rules at compile time
A development process driven by typing speed measurements
Developing type definitions before writing any test cases
explanation: Type-driven development uses expressive type systems to encode business rules and invariants, letting the compiler verify correctness and guide the design.

text: What is the expression problem in programming language design?
options: The difficulty of parsing complex mathematical expressions in code
The challenge of extending both types and operations without modifying existing code
The problem of evaluating expressions with too many nested parentheses
The inability to use expressions inside conditional statements
explanation: The expression problem is the challenge of adding both new data types and new operations to a system without modifying existing code, which OOP and FP each solve partially.

text: What is the concept of algebraic effects?
options: Mathematical operations applied to algebraic data types
A mechanism for managing side effects through handlers without monads
Algebraic transformations applied to source code during optimization
A pattern for computing mathematical effects of algorithm complexity
explanation: Algebraic effects provide a structured way to manage side effects by separating the description of effects from their handling, offering an alternative to monads.

text: What is the difference between nominal and structural subtyping in advanced type systems?
options: Nominal subtyping checks structure; structural checks declared relationships
Nominal requires explicit declarations; structural matches based on shape compatibility
Both approaches produce identical type checking behavior in practice
Structural subtyping is only used in statically typed languages
explanation: Nominal subtyping requires explicit type declarations (extends/implements), while structural subtyping considers types compatible if they have the same shape regardless of declarations.

text: What is concurrency in programming?
options: Running only one task at a time in a sequential order
Managing multiple tasks that can make progress within overlapping time periods
Compiling multiple source files into a single executable
Storing multiple values in a single variable
explanation: Concurrency is the ability to manage multiple tasks whose execution can overlap in time, though they may not execute simultaneously.

text: What is a thread in programming?
options: A physical wire connecting two hardware components
The smallest unit of execution that can be scheduled by the operating system
A sequence of characters stored in a string variable
A type of network connection between two computers
explanation: A thread is the smallest unit of execution within a process that can be independently scheduled and run by the operating system.

text: What is the difference between concurrency and parallelism?
options: Concurrency requires multiple CPUs; parallelism does not require them
Concurrency deals with managing tasks; parallelism executes them simultaneously
Both terms describe exactly the same concept in programming
Parallelism is managing tasks; concurrency is executing simultaneously
explanation: Concurrency is about structuring code to handle multiple tasks that can overlap, while parallelism is about executing multiple tasks literally at the same time on multiple cores.

text: What is a process in computing?
options: A single function within a program's source code
An instance of a running program with its own memory space
A data type used for storing process-related information
A special kind of loop used in system programming
explanation: A process is an instance of a running program with its own independent memory space, managed by the operating system.

text: What is a race condition?
options: A competition between two programs to finish first
A bug where the outcome depends on the unpredictable timing of operations
An error that occurs when a program runs too fast
A condition that checks which thread is currently active
explanation: A race condition occurs when the behavior of a program depends on the relative timing of concurrent operations, leading to unpredictable and incorrect results.

text: What is a mutex (mutual exclusion)?
options: A data structure for storing multiple values together
A synchronization mechanism that allows only one thread to access a resource at a time
A technique for running two functions mutually in parallel
A type of exception thrown during concurrent execution
explanation: A mutex is a synchronization primitive that ensures only one thread can access a shared resource at a time, preventing race conditions.

text: What is a deadlock?
options: A program that has terminated normally after completing its tasks
A situation where two or more threads are stuck waiting for each other
A lock that has expired and needs to be renewed
A debugging technique for freezing program execution
explanation: A deadlock occurs when two or more threads are each waiting for the other to release a resource, resulting in all of them being permanently blocked.

text: What is thread synchronization?
options: Running all threads at exactly the same speed on the CPU
Coordinating the execution of threads to ensure correct access to shared data
Starting all threads at the exact same time precisely
Converting single-threaded code to multi-threaded code
explanation: Thread synchronization coordinates the execution of multiple threads to ensure they access shared resources correctly without data corruption.

text: What is an atomic operation?
options: An operation that can be interrupted at any point by other threads
An operation that completes entirely without interruption from other threads
An operation that uses the smallest possible amount of memory
An operation that breaks a task into the smallest possible units
explanation: An atomic operation executes as a single indivisible unit that cannot be interrupted by other threads, ensuring consistency.

text: What is a thread pool?
options: A collection of threads that are reused for executing multiple tasks
A single thread that handles all concurrent tasks sequentially
A pool of memory reserved exclusively for thread stack storage
A debugging tool for monitoring thread behavior at runtime
explanation: A thread pool maintains a collection of pre-created threads that are reused to execute tasks, avoiding the overhead of creating new threads for each task.

text: What is a semaphore?
options: A flag that indicates whether a thread has completed its task
A synchronization primitive that controls access using a counter
A data structure for ordering threads by their priority level
A signal sent from one process to another over the network
explanation: A semaphore uses a counter to control access to a shared resource, allowing a specified number of threads to access the resource concurrently.

text: What is the producer-consumer problem?
options: A problem where producers create too many objects for memory to handle
A synchronization problem where producers add to a buffer and consumers remove from it
A design issue where consumers cannot understand the producer's API
A networking problem where data is lost between sender and receiver
explanation: The producer-consumer problem involves coordinating threads where producers add data to a shared buffer and consumers remove data, requiring synchronization.

text: What is a Future or Promise in concurrent programming?
options: A variable that will be assigned a value in the next version of the code
A placeholder for a result that will be available asynchronously in the future
A promise that the program will complete within a specified time
A function scheduled to run at a specific future date and time
explanation: A Future or Promise represents a value that may not yet be available but will be resolved asynchronously, allowing non-blocking concurrent code.

text: What is a critical section?
options: The most important function in the entire application
A code segment that accesses shared resources and must not execute concurrently
A section of code that is critical for program startup only
A debugging breakpoint set at a critical location in code
explanation: A critical section is a portion of code that accesses shared resources and must be executed by only one thread at a time to prevent data corruption.

text: What is the async/await pattern?
options: A pattern where all operations run synchronously one after another
A pattern for writing asynchronous code that looks like synchronous code
A pattern for synchronizing the system clock across multiple threads
A pattern that pauses the entire application until a task completes
explanation: Async/await allows writing asynchronous code in a sequential style, where await suspends the function until the asynchronous operation completes without blocking the thread.

text: What is thread starvation?
options: A thread that uses too much memory and starves other resources
A thread that is perpetually denied access to resources it needs to proceed
A thread that has completed all its tasks and has nothing to do
A thread that starts but immediately terminates without executing
explanation: Thread starvation occurs when a thread is perpetually unable to gain access to a shared resource because other threads continually acquire it first.

text: What is a condition variable in threading?
options: A boolean variable that determines if a thread should run
A synchronization primitive that allows threads to wait until a condition is signaled
A variable that stores the current state of thread execution
A counter that tracks the number of active threads in a pool
explanation: A condition variable allows threads to suspend execution and wait until another thread signals that a particular condition has been met.

text: What is the difference between cooperative and preemptive multitasking?
options: Cooperative tasks are scheduled by the OS; preemptive tasks yield voluntarily
Cooperative tasks yield voluntarily; preemptive tasks are interrupted by the scheduler
Both approaches let the operating system control all task switching
Preemptive tasks run slower because they must cooperate with other tasks
explanation: In cooperative multitasking, tasks voluntarily yield control, while in preemptive multitasking, the OS scheduler can interrupt and switch tasks at any time.

text: What is a read-write lock?
options: A lock that prevents both reading and writing simultaneously always
A lock that allows multiple readers or one exclusive writer at a time
A lock that only works with read-only data structures
A lock that converts read operations into write operations
explanation: A read-write lock allows multiple threads to read simultaneously but requires exclusive access for writing, improving performance for read-heavy workloads.

text: What is the event loop in single-threaded concurrency models?
options: A loop that creates new events for user interface rendering
A loop that continuously checks for and dispatches events or callbacks
A loop that iterates over all threads in the application
A loop that generates random events for stress testing
explanation: An event loop continuously checks for pending events and dispatches their callbacks, enabling concurrency on a single thread, as used in Node.js and browser JavaScript.

text: What is the compare-and-swap (CAS) operation?
options: An operation that compares two arrays and swaps their positions in memory
An atomic operation that updates a value only if it matches an expected value
A sorting algorithm that compares and swaps adjacent elements
A technique for swapping two threads' execution contexts
explanation: CAS is an atomic hardware operation that compares a memory location to an expected value and only updates it if they match, forming the basis of lock-free algorithms.

text: What is the happens-before relationship in the Java memory model?
options: A physical ordering of instructions in the source code file
A guarantee that memory writes by one action are visible to another action
The order in which threads are created during program startup
A constraint that prevents threads from executing in parallel
explanation: The happens-before relationship guarantees that memory writes by one specific action are visible to another specific action, ensuring proper ordering in concurrent programs.

text: What is a livelock?
options: A situation where threads are deadlocked but can be manually unlocked
A situation where threads keep changing state in response to each other without progress
A thread that remains active and never terminates during execution
A lock that is held indefinitely by a single thread without release
explanation: A livelock occurs when threads continuously change state in response to each other but make no actual progress, unlike deadlock where threads are blocked.

text: What is the ABA problem in lock-free programming?
options: An algorithm that runs in O(ABA) time complexity
A value changing from A to B and back to A, fooling a CAS operation
Three threads (A, B, A) competing for the same resource
An encoding problem with characters A, B, and A in strings
explanation: The ABA problem occurs when a CAS check succeeds because a value changed from A to B and back to A, missing intervening modifications that may have invalidated assumptions.

text: What is software transactional memory (STM)?
options: Memory allocated specifically for database transaction processing
A concurrency control mechanism that uses transactions for shared memory access
Software that monitors memory usage and generates transaction reports
A technique for storing transactions in memory before writing to disk
explanation: STM manages concurrent access to shared memory using transactions that either commit atomically or retry, similar to database transactions, simplifying concurrent programming.

text: What is the fork-join concurrency model?
options: A model where one process forks into two and they never rejoin
A model that splits tasks into subtasks and combines their results
A model for forking a repository and joining it with another
A model where threads fork from the main process and join the kernel
explanation: The fork-join model recursively splits a task into smaller subtasks (fork), processes them in parallel, and then combines the results (join).

text: What is memory ordering and memory barriers?
options: The physical arrangement of memory chips on the motherboard
Instructions that enforce ordering constraints on memory operations across threads
Sorting data in memory in ascending or descending order
The priority order in which memory allocation requests are processed
explanation: Memory barriers are instructions that enforce ordering constraints on memory read/write operations, preventing the CPU and compiler from reordering operations across the barrier.

text: What is the difference between green threads and OS threads?
options: Green threads are managed by the runtime; OS threads are managed by the kernel
OS threads are managed by the runtime; green threads by the kernel
Both types are always managed by the operating system kernel
Green threads use more memory than OS threads in all cases
explanation: Green threads are managed by the language runtime or virtual machine in user space, while OS threads are managed by the operating system kernel with hardware support.

text: What is the dining philosophers problem?
options: An algorithm for optimizing database query performance
A classic concurrency problem illustrating deadlock with shared resources
A data structure problem involving circular linked lists
A sorting problem where philosophers must order their arguments
explanation: The dining philosophers problem illustrates deadlock and resource contention, where philosophers (threads) sharing forks (resources) can deadlock if each holds one fork and waits for another.

text: What is work stealing in thread pool schedulers?
options: Threads stealing CPU time from other processes in the system
Idle threads taking tasks from the queues of busy threads for load balancing
Removing completed work items from the task queue permanently
Assigning all work to a single thread while others remain idle
explanation: Work stealing allows idle threads in a pool to take (steal) pending tasks from the queues of busy threads, achieving dynamic load balancing.

text: What is a unit test?
options: A test that verifies the entire application works as a whole
A test that verifies a single small piece of code in isolation
A test that measures the performance of the application
A test that checks the user interface layout and design
explanation: A unit test verifies that a small, isolated unit of code (like a function or method) works correctly with given inputs.

text: What is a breakpoint in debugging?
options: A point where the program crashes due to an error
A marker that pauses program execution at a specific line for inspection
A boundary between two modules in the source code
A point where a loop terminates its execution
explanation: A breakpoint is a marker set by the developer that pauses program execution at a specific line, allowing inspection of variables and program state.

text: What is test-driven development (TDD)?
options: Writing all tests after the code is fully implemented and deployed
Writing tests before writing the code that makes them pass
Testing only the driver components of the application
Developing code without any testing at all for speed
explanation: TDD is a development approach where tests are written first, then the minimum code is written to pass the tests, followed by refactoring.

text: What is an assertion in testing?
options: A comment explaining what a test should verify
A statement that checks if an expected condition is true in a test
A declaration of all variables used in a test function
A method for importing test data from external files
explanation: An assertion is a statement in a test that checks whether a specific condition is true, causing the test to fail if the condition is false.

text: What is a test case?
options: A container that holds all tests for the entire application
A specific set of inputs and expected outputs to verify code behavior
A case statement used inside a switch block for testing
A tool for generating random test data automatically
explanation: A test case defines specific inputs, execution conditions, and expected results to verify that a piece of code behaves correctly.

text: What is a debugger tool?
options: A tool that automatically fixes all bugs in source code
A tool that allows stepping through code and inspecting program state
A tool that compiles code into a debug-only binary format
A tool that generates documentation from source code comments
explanation: A debugger is a tool that allows developers to step through code execution, inspect variables, set breakpoints, and examine the call stack.

text: What is a bug in software?
options: A feature that works exactly as intended by the developer
An error or flaw that causes incorrect or unexpected behavior
A performance optimization applied to the source code
A security feature that protects the program from attacks
explanation: A bug is an error, flaw, or unintended behavior in software that causes it to produce incorrect results or behave unexpectedly.

text: What is logging in software development?
options: Recording user credentials for authentication purposes
Recording program events and data during execution for diagnostics
Creating a log cabin simulation in a game application
Removing old source code files from the project history
explanation: Logging records information about program events, errors, and data during execution, helping developers diagnose issues and monitor application behavior.

text: What is regression testing?
options: Testing to verify new features work correctly for the first time
Retesting to ensure existing features still work after code changes
Testing the program with extremely large data sets for performance
Testing the program on older hardware and operating systems
explanation: Regression testing verifies that previously working features continue to function correctly after new code changes, bug fixes, or enhancements.

text: What is a test suite?
options: A single test that covers all possible scenarios in the application
A collection of related test cases grouped together for execution
A suite of tools used for writing source code in an IDE
A report generated after running all performance benchmarks
explanation: A test suite is a collection of related test cases organized together, typically testing a specific module, feature, or aspect of the application.

text: What is integration testing?
options: Testing individual functions in complete isolation from others
Testing how multiple components or modules work together
Testing the user interface layout on different screen sizes
Testing only the database queries and stored procedures
explanation: Integration testing verifies that multiple components or modules work together correctly, testing the interactions and interfaces between them.

text: What is code coverage in testing?
options: The number of developers who have reviewed the source code
A measure of how much source code is executed by the test suite
The total number of test cases written for the application
The percentage of bugs found before the software is released
explanation: Code coverage measures the percentage of source code lines, branches, or paths that are executed when the test suite runs.

text: What is a mock object in testing?
options: A real object used directly in production testing environments
A simulated object that mimics the behavior of a real dependency
An object that generates random data for stress testing
An object that mocks the user by displaying error messages
explanation: A mock object simulates the behavior of a real dependency, allowing unit tests to run in isolation without requiring actual external services or components.

text: What is boundary value analysis in testing?
options: Testing only the most common input values used by typical users
Testing at the edges of input ranges where errors are most likely
Analyzing the boundaries between different source code modules
Measuring the maximum value a variable can hold in memory
explanation: Boundary value analysis focuses testing on the edges of input ranges (minimum, maximum, just inside, just outside), where errors are statistically most likely to occur.

text: What is the difference between black-box and white-box testing?
options: Black-box tests internal code structure; white-box tests external behavior
Black-box tests external behavior without seeing code; white-box tests internal structure
Both approaches test the exact same aspects of the application
White-box testing does not require access to the source code
explanation: Black-box testing examines external behavior without knowledge of internal code, while white-box testing examines internal code structure and logic.

text: What is a test fixture?
options: A tool for fixing broken tests in the test suite
A fixed state or environment set up before tests run
A physical device used for hardware testing only
A fixture in the IDE for running tests automatically
explanation: A test fixture is the fixed state, configuration, or environment prepared before tests run, including setup and teardown of test data and dependencies.

text: What is static analysis in code quality?
options: Running the program and observing its behavior during execution
Analyzing source code without executing it to find potential issues
Measuring how fast static variables are accessed at runtime
Testing the application only with static input data
explanation: Static analysis examines source code without executing it, detecting potential bugs, security vulnerabilities, and code quality issues through automated tools.

text: What is the red-green-refactor cycle in TDD?
options: A color coding scheme for classifying bugs by severity level
Writing a failing test, making it pass, then improving the code structure
Using traffic light colors to indicate test execution progress
Refactoring code three times in red, green, and blue phases
explanation: Red-green-refactor is the TDD cycle: write a failing test (red), write minimal code to pass (green), then refactor to improve code quality while keeping tests passing.

text: What is a test stub?
options: A test that has been abandoned and is no longer maintained
A simple implementation that returns predetermined responses for testing
The first test written in a new test suite project
A placeholder for a test that has not been implemented yet
explanation: A test stub provides a simple, predetermined response to calls made during testing, replacing a real component to isolate the code under test.

text: What is profiling a program?
options: Creating a user profile for each person who uses the software
Measuring program performance to identify bottlenecks and resource usage
Writing a detailed profile document describing the software features
Comparing the program against a standard security profile
explanation: Profiling measures a program's runtime characteristics such as execution time, memory usage, and function call frequency to identify performance bottlenecks.

text: What is mutation testing?
options: Testing software for compatibility with mutated hardware configurations
Introducing small changes to code to evaluate how well tests detect them
Testing software with randomly generated inputs for robustness
Modifying test cases to improve their execution speed
explanation: Mutation testing introduces small deliberate changes (mutants) to the source code and checks whether existing tests detect them, measuring test suite effectiveness.

text: What is property-based testing?
options: Testing object properties and their getter/setter methods
Generating random inputs and verifying that general properties always hold
Testing CSS properties for correct rendering in web applications
Testing only the public properties of a class interface
explanation: Property-based testing generates random inputs and verifies that specified properties or invariants hold for all generated cases, finding edge cases human testers miss.

text: What is fuzzing in software testing?
options: Making code intentionally unclear to test readability
Providing random, malformed, or unexpected inputs to find vulnerabilities
Testing software in a fuzzy logic computing environment
Gradually increasing test complexity from simple to complex
explanation: Fuzzing automatically generates random, malformed, or unexpected inputs to a program to discover security vulnerabilities, crashes, and assertion failures.

text: What is symbolic execution in program analysis?
options: Executing a program using symbols instead of actual values to explore paths
Using symbolic names for all variables instead of numeric addresses
Executing only the symbolic links in a file system during testing
Running a program in a symbolic debugger with breakpoints
explanation: Symbolic execution uses symbolic values instead of concrete inputs to explore all possible execution paths, generating constraints that can be solved to find bugs.

text: What is the difference between stubs, mocks, and fakes in testing?
options: All three terms are identical and interchangeable in all testing contexts
Stubs return fixed data; mocks verify interactions; fakes have working implementations
Mocks return fixed data; stubs verify interactions; fakes do nothing at all
Fakes return fixed data; stubs verify interactions; mocks have implementations
explanation: Stubs provide canned responses, mocks verify that expected interactions occurred, and fakes are simplified working implementations of a dependency.

text: What is chaos engineering?
options: Writing chaotic and unstructured code to test robustness
Deliberately injecting failures into systems to test resilience
Randomly deleting source code files to test version control
Using random algorithms instead of deterministic ones
explanation: Chaos engineering deliberately injects failures (network issues, server crashes, latency) into production or staging systems to verify that the system can withstand and recover from disruptions.

text: What is concolic testing?
options: Testing concurrent programs for deadlock and race condition bugs
Combining concrete and symbolic execution to explore program paths systematically
Testing console-based applications using automated input scripts
A testing method that combines unit tests with integration tests
explanation: Concolic testing combines concrete execution with symbolic execution, using real inputs while collecting symbolic constraints to systematically explore different execution paths.

text: What is delta debugging?
options: Debugging by comparing the differences between two program versions
Systematically minimizing a failure-inducing input to find the smallest failing case
Debugging only the changes made in the most recent code commit
A debugging method that uses delta encoding for log compression
explanation: Delta debugging systematically minimizes a failure-inducing input or change set to find the smallest possible input that still triggers the failure.

text: What is the difference between branch coverage and path coverage?
options: Branch coverage tests all possible execution paths; path coverage tests all branches
Branch coverage tests each branch direction; path coverage tests every possible path combination
Both coverage metrics measure exactly the same thing in practice
Path coverage is easier to achieve than branch coverage in all cases
explanation: Branch coverage ensures every branch direction is tested at least once, while path coverage requires testing every unique combination of branches through the code, which is exponentially harder.

text: What is a test oracle in software testing theory?
options: A database system used to store test results permanently
A mechanism for determining whether a test has passed or failed
A senior tester who manually verifies all test outcomes
A prediction model that estimates how many bugs remain
explanation: A test oracle is any mechanism for determining whether the actual output of a test matches the expected output, essential for automated test verdict decisions.

text: What is version control?
options: A system that tracks changes to files over time
A tool for controlling the version of the programming language used
A process for numbering software releases sequentially
A method for controlling access to different software versions
explanation: Version control is a system that records changes to files over time, allowing developers to track history, revert changes, and collaborate.

text: What is code review?
options: A process where developers examine each other's code for quality
An automated tool that checks code for syntax errors only
A meeting where managers review the project's timeline
A report generated by the compiler listing all warnings
explanation: Code review is a practice where one or more developers examine another developer's code to identify bugs, improve quality, and share knowledge.

text: What is refactoring?
options: Adding new features to the existing codebase quickly
Restructuring existing code without changing its external behavior
Removing all comments from the source code for clarity
Converting code from one programming language to another
explanation: Refactoring improves the internal structure and readability of code without changing what it does externally, making it easier to maintain.

text: What is the DRY principle?
options: Debug, Refactor, Yield in the development cycle
Do not Repeat Yourself to avoid code duplication
Deploy, Run, Yank for continuous integration processes
Design, Review, Yield for software architecture planning
explanation: DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself) is a principle that aims to reduce duplication of code by extracting common logic into reusable abstractions.

text: What is a code smell?
options: A virus that corrupts source code files on disk
An indication in code that suggests a deeper design problem
A compiler optimization that removes unused code
A feature that adds an unpleasant odor to program output
explanation: A code smell is a surface-level indication in source code that suggests a deeper problem, such as overly long methods, duplicated code, or excessive complexity.

text: What is continuous integration (CI)?
options: Continuously writing new code without stopping for breaks
Frequently merging code changes and automatically building and testing
Integrating multiple programming languages in a single project
Continuously running the application in production without downtime
explanation: Continuous integration is the practice of frequently merging code changes into a shared repository, with automated builds and tests to detect issues early.

text: What is the KISS principle in software development?
options: Key Information Security Standard for safe programming
Keep It Simple, Stupid, favoring simplicity over complexity
Knowledge Is Shared Systematically among team members
Keep Integrating Software Systems for continuous delivery
explanation: KISS (Keep It Simple, Stupid) is a design principle that favors simplicity, stating that systems work best when kept simple rather than made complex.

text: What is pair programming?
options: Two developers working on separate tasks at the same time
Two developers working together at one workstation on the same code
Pairing a developer with a tester for each feature
Writing code in pairs of functions that call each other
explanation: Pair programming involves two developers working together at one workstation, with one writing code (driver) and the other reviewing (navigator).

text: What is a pull request in collaborative development?
options: A request to download the latest code from the repository
A proposal to merge code changes for review before integration
A request to pull a feature from the product backlog
A command that pulls code from a remote server to local
explanation: A pull request is a proposal to merge code changes from one branch into another, allowing team members to review, discuss, and approve the changes.

text: What is technical debt?
options: The financial cost of purchasing development tools and licenses
The implied cost of rework caused by choosing quick solutions over better approaches
The amount of time spent learning new technologies for a project
The number of unresolved issues in a bug tracking system
explanation: Technical debt is the implied cost of future rework caused by choosing an easy or quick solution now instead of a better approach that would take longer.

text: What is the YAGNI principle?
options: You Always Get New Ideas during code review sessions
You Aren't Gonna Need It, avoiding premature feature implementation
Your Application Gets Naturally Improved over time
You Always Generate New Iterations for testing purposes
explanation: YAGNI (You Aren't Gonna Need It) advises against implementing features until they are actually needed, avoiding unnecessary complexity.

text: What is the purpose of a linter?
options: To compile source code into an executable binary file
To analyze code for potential errors, style issues, and suspicious patterns
To compress source code files for efficient storage
To generate user documentation from source code comments
explanation: A linter statically analyzes source code to flag potential errors, style violations, and suspicious patterns, helping maintain code quality and consistency.

text: What is continuous deployment versus continuous delivery?
options: Both terms describe exactly the same practice with no difference
Continuous delivery prepares releases for manual approval; deployment automates to production
Continuous deployment requires manual approval; delivery is fully automated
Continuous delivery deploys only to staging; deployment deploys to development
explanation: Continuous delivery ensures code is always ready for release with manual approval for production, while continuous deployment automatically releases every change that passes tests.

text: What is the boy scout rule in software development?
options: Always write code outdoors for better focus and creativity
Leave the code cleaner than you found it when making changes
Only use natural language for naming variables and functions
Always work in teams of exactly three developers per feature
explanation: The boy scout rule states that developers should leave the codebase in a better state than they found it, making small improvements whenever they touch existing code.

text: What is the difference between a branch and a fork in version control?
options: A branch is in the same repository; a fork creates a separate copy of the repository
A fork is in the same repository; a branch creates a separate copy of the repository
Both terms describe exactly the same operation in version control
Branches are for code; forks are for documentation only
explanation: A branch creates a separate line of development within the same repository, while a fork creates an entirely separate copy of the repository.

text: What is the principle of least astonishment?
options: Software should surprise users with unexpected features regularly
Software should behave in ways that users and developers would reasonably expect
Developers should be astonished by how simple the code looks
The least experienced developer should always review the code
explanation: The principle of least astonishment states that software should behave in ways consistent with what users and developers would reasonably expect, minimizing surprise.

text: What is a feature flag (feature toggle)?
options: A flag variable that indicates if any bug is present in the code
A mechanism to enable or disable features without deploying new code
A flag that marks code as ready for production deployment
A toggle switch in the user interface for accessibility settings
explanation: A feature flag allows features to be turned on or off at runtime without deploying new code, enabling gradual rollouts, A/B testing, and quick rollbacks.

text: What is the composition over inheritance principle?
options: Inheritance should always be preferred over composition in all designs
Favoring object composition for code reuse over class inheritance hierarchies
Composing classes should inherit from at least three parent classes
Inheritance and composition should never be used in the same project
explanation: Composition over inheritance suggests using object composition (has-a) for code reuse instead of deep inheritance hierarchies (is-a), leading to more flexible designs.

text: What is semantic commit messaging?
options: Writing commit messages in multiple human languages for global teams
Using structured prefixes like feat, fix, and chore to categorize commits
Including the full source code diff in every commit message
Writing commit messages using only emoji symbols
explanation: Semantic commit messages use structured prefixes (feat:, fix:, chore:, etc.) to categorize commits, making the history clear and enabling automated changelog generation.

text: What is code documentation best practice?
options: Document every single line of code with a detailed comment
Write self-documenting code and comment only the why, not the what
Never write any comments because code should speak for itself
Copy the source code into a separate document with explanations
explanation: Best practice is to write clear, self-documenting code with meaningful names, and add comments only to explain why something is done, not what the code does.

text: What is trunk-based development?
options: A strategy where each developer works on a separate long-lived branch
A strategy where all developers commit to a single shared branch frequently
A strategy for managing multiple production releases simultaneously
A strategy where code is developed on trunk-shaped directory structures
explanation: Trunk-based development has all developers commit small, frequent changes to a single shared branch (trunk/main), using feature flags instead of long-lived feature branches.

text: What is the Strangler Fig pattern for legacy code migration?
options: Rewriting the entire legacy system from scratch in a single release
Gradually replacing legacy components by routing traffic to new implementations
Removing unused code from the legacy system until nothing remains
Wrapping the legacy system in a new user interface layer only
explanation: The Strangler Fig pattern gradually replaces legacy system components by intercepting requests and routing them to new implementations, eventually replacing the entire system.

text: What is the concept of immutable infrastructure?
options: Infrastructure that is manually patched and updated in place
Infrastructure components that are replaced entirely rather than modified
Infrastructure that cannot be deployed or changed after initial setup
Infrastructure that uses only immutable data structures in code
explanation: Immutable infrastructure treats servers as disposable: instead of updating existing servers, new ones are built with the desired state, and old ones are destroyed.

text: What is the concept of observability in modern software systems?
options: The ability to observe source code in an IDE during development
The ability to understand a system's internal state from its external outputs
The practice of watching developers as they write code for training
The ability to observe competitors' software for feature comparison
explanation: Observability is the ability to understand a system's internal state from its external outputs (logs, metrics, traces), enabling effective debugging and monitoring.

text: What is the difference between horizontal and vertical scaling?
options: Horizontal scaling adds more powerful hardware; vertical adds more machines
Horizontal scaling adds more machines; vertical upgrades existing hardware
Both approaches involve adding identical machines to the infrastructure
Vertical scaling distributes load; horizontal concentrates resources
explanation: Horizontal scaling adds more machines to distribute the load, while vertical scaling upgrades the existing machine's CPU, memory, or storage capacity.

text: What is the twelve-factor app methodology?
options: A methodology requiring applications to have exactly twelve source files
A set of best practices for building scalable, maintainable cloud applications
A security framework with twelve levels of access control
A development cycle with twelve sequential phases from start to finish
explanation: The twelve-factor app methodology defines best practices for building modern cloud-native applications, covering aspects like configuration, dependencies, and disposability.

text: What is the concept of eventual consistency in distributed systems?
options: All nodes always see the same data at the exact same instant
All replicas converge to the same state given enough time without new updates
Data is eventually deleted from all nodes after a timeout period
Consistency is never achieved in distributed systems under any conditions
explanation: Eventual consistency guarantees that if no new updates are made, all replicas will eventually converge to the same consistent state, trading immediate consistency for availability.

text: What is the CAP theorem?
options: A theorem stating that all distributed systems are perfectly consistent
A theorem stating a distributed system can guarantee at most two of consistency, availability, and partition tolerance
A theorem about the maximum capacity of network connections
A theorem that proves concurrent access problems are unsolvable
explanation: The CAP theorem states that a distributed system can simultaneously guarantee at most two of three properties: consistency, availability, and partition tolerance.

text: What is the concept of infrastructure as code (IaC)?
options: Writing application code that runs directly on bare metal hardware
Managing and provisioning infrastructure through machine-readable configuration files
Storing infrastructure passwords and credentials in source code
Replacing all infrastructure with software-only simulation environments
explanation: Infrastructure as Code manages and provisions computing infrastructure through machine-readable definition files rather than manual configuration, enabling version control and reproducibility.

text: What is the concept of service mesh in microservice architectures?
options: A mesh network topology for connecting physical servers together
A dedicated infrastructure layer for handling service-to-service communication
A testing framework for validating microservice API contracts
A database mesh that distributes data across multiple service instances
explanation: A service mesh is a dedicated infrastructure layer that handles service-to-service communication, providing features like load balancing, encryption, and observability transparently.
